#### humility, knowledge, false ego
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/t-Oa1FsWlxg?t=1373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "More and more, we become knowledgeable. The more and more, we become humble."
**Summary:** The speaker reflects on the story of Louis Pasteur, emphasizing that true knowledge leads to humility, contrasting it with the pride of the half-baked. He shares an anecdote about Newton's humility in his last days, highlighting the vastness of unexplored knowledge.

So, when we are half-baked, we naturally feel that we are on top of the world, we are proud. More and more, we become knowledgeable. The more and more, we become humble. In fact, it is said that scientists like Newton were quite humble. Newton was once asked, you know, when he was in the last days of his life. So somebody approached Newton and said, I am sure when you take your last breath, you will die. You will be leaving this body with lot of satisfaction. With the sense of accomplishment that you have done it. So many accomplishments to your credit. The world will remember you for your accomplishment. You will die as a proud man. So Newton said, what have I done? What have I accomplished? What have I fathomed? What have I discovered? I am like a small boy standing on the shore of mighty ocean. And the whole ocean lies in front of me completely unexplored. Imagine, big ocean and a child is standing on the ocean. He says, I am like a child standing on the shore of a mighty big ocean. And the whole ocean lies in front of me completely unexplored. So it is because of false ego, because of hankara, because of ignorance. We forget our constitutional position and we become puffed up. We become proud. And we also as conditioned souls are no exception.

---
#### conditioned souls, Krishna consciousness, promise
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/t-Oa1FsWlxg?t=1479
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pride cannot touch them. Whereas we all are fallible. We all get puffed up."
**Summary:** The speaker acknowledges that conditioned souls are prone to pride and artificial humility, contrasting them with pure devotees like Prabhupada. He then returns to the original verse, emphasizing the importance of fulfilling one's promise.

We are not pure devotees. People like devotees like Prabhupada, they are completely Krishna conscious. Pride cannot touch them. Whereas we all are fallible. We all get puffed up. Somebody praises us. Artificially we say, no, no, no, it's okay, it's okay. Deep within, ah, great. You are a big collector, yes. So, it's a journey. This purification also cannot happen overnight. Slowly as we become Krishna conscious, example is given when the sun rises, all the darkness slowly gradually dissipates. As the sun of Krishna consciousness will rise, genuine Krishna consciousness, the more and more we realize our insignificant position as amsa of the Lord, the more and more all the ahankara, all the darkness which is there, covering our consciousness will get dissipated. So here it is said, the Lord therefore challenged the demon not to go away, but to fulfill his promised word and kill him. One may be proud, yet he does not deserve a seat in an assembly, if he fails to fulfill his promise.

---
#### destiny, God's plan, king and minister
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/t-Oa1FsWlxg?t=1554
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Whatever is happening is as per the will of the Lord, sanction of the Lord, it's for our good."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces a story about a king and his minister to illustrate the concept of destiny and God's plan. The king is proud and mocks the minister's belief that everything happens for good, setting the stage for a series of events that will test their beliefs.

One time there was a king, and he had a minister. So invariably the king was puffed up. Power corrupts, they say. And the minister, relatively, was quite God-fearing, was always believing in God, believing in destiny, whatever is happening is as per the will of the Lord, sanction of the Lord, it's for our good. So he was having that belief. And invariably when the king and the minister would have a conversation, the king would mock at the minister, what are you talking about? Everything is happening for good, God is all good. Whatever is destined will happen in our life. So he would make fun of his minister. But the minister, because he was conscious of the Lord, he would always maintain his stand, yes, God is all good. Whatever is happening is for our good. Just like Krishna says, I am the well-wisher of all living entities. So this minister also would emphatically say, yes, whatever God is doing is for our good. So one day the king was cutting an apple. And unfortunately, because maybe his attention was somewhere else, while cutting the apple he cut his finger. And it got badly cut. Blood started oozing out. And the minister was there. He says, what has happened? See this. And now I know that everything happens for good. Whatever God is doing is for good. What is good in this? My finger has got cut.

---
#### God's plan, tribals, minister's wisdom
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/t-Oa1FsWlxg?t=1689
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The Goddess will not be pleased if we offer somebody who is not complete. He is incomplete. Finger is not there."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, describing how the king, angered by the minister's words, imprisons him. The king then gets captured by tribals who intend to sacrifice him, but he is spared because of his cut finger. Upon returning, the king realizes the truth in the minister's words.

So the minister says, the Lord has a higher plan. Everything happens for good. So the king got wild. You nonsense. My finger has got cut. God has a higher plan. He took that, ordered his minister, put him behind the bars. So he was imprisoned. So the king went out on the forest for hunting. And he somehow lost his way. And he was captured by some tribals, Aborigines. And they took captive. They bound him and took him. And finally he was presented, you know, the whole village, the chief, village chief was there. And there was some puja of some Kali was happening there. And the chief of the village, he was very happy when all his, you know, so-called soldiers brought king, tied. Because they wanted to Narabali, offer a human being in sacrifice to Goddess. So he was happy. So nice. Goddess will be very pleased today. It's not an ordinary man. It's not an ordinary man whom you're going to sacrifice. It's going to be a king. Because he was in royal robes. And so they untied him. And they brought him close to the Kali. And about to kill him, suddenly the chief said, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. So they all got up. What happened? Says, no, we cannot offer him. So they were curious. He says, see, his finger is cut. The Goddess will not be pleased if we offer somebody who is not complete. He is incomplete. Finger is not there. So what to do? Okay, useless fellow, go. They relieved him. So the king comes back to the kingdom and, you know, he is now recollecting the words of the minister. You know, whatever happens, God has a higher plan. If his finger was not there, if finger was there, today he would have been chopped off. So now having realized his minister's word, he felt sorry that what he has done to the minister. And he, you know, says, okay, release him. So the minister comes. Says, now I understand what you said. God has a higher plan. Even my finger getting cut, there was a higher plan of the Lord.

---
#### surrender, faith, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/t-Oa1FsWlxg?t=1871
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything which is happening is for our good."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story, revealing that even the minister's imprisonment was part of God's plan, as it saved him from being sacrificed in place of the king. He emphasizes the importance of surrender, faith, and gratitude in Krishna consciousness, even amidst difficulties.

By the way, do you think that I imprisoned you, that was also a higher plan of the Lord? That is also for your good? He says, yes. He says, how? He said, had you not imprisoned me, I would have invariably accompanied you to the forest. And having realized that your finger is not there, I would have been Narabali. So God is great. Everything which is happening is for our good. So when we are devotees of the Lord, we are naturally conscious of the Lord, His protection, our dependence on Him. Whatever the Lord takes us through, we take it as a blessing. Sometimes we may be able to understand, sometimes we may not be able to understand. A devotee that way never rebels. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, maro bhi raakho bhi jo ichcho to maro. This consciousness naturally, stems from a pure Krishna consciousness understanding. Whatever is happening, if we are so long surrendered to the Lord, trying to please our Lord, trying to serve Him, trying to follow this process which will purify us, whatever ups and downs we may go through, even as devotees life, having faith that Krishna has a higher plan, we may encounter some difficulties, some challenges, somebody, you know, not treating us well. We are here for purification. We are not here for a cozy, comfortable life, everyone is good, everything is good. In one lecture we heard that, if we become super peaceful, we all want to be peaceful, no problems, so that we can peacefully practice Krishna. If we become super peaceful, first thing we want to do is, we will forget Krishna. So that way, let's take whatever is unfolding in our lives in stride, be grateful to Krishna, Prabhupada, for Divya Gyan, the knowledge what they have given us, and pursue this process, practice this process very sincerely. We stop here. Granth Raj, Srimad Bhagavatam, Kishla Prabhupada.

---

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-02-04 | SB 3.18.16-19 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### anger, detachment, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=215
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What meets our eyes is a superficial understanding."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the apparent contradiction of the Lord exhibiting anger when the Bhagavad Gita advises controlling the senses and mind, and not being attached to results.

So here in these verses, the battle between Lord Varaha and Hiranyaksha is being described. There was keen rivalry between the two combatants. Both had sustained injuries on their bodies from the blows of each other's pointed maces, and each grew more and more enraged. So in the previous verses we are seeing how both the Lord and Hiranyaksha were angry at each other. Even the Lord became very angry. The personality of Godhead now exhibited his anger and rushed to meet the demon who bit his lip in rage. For someone who is totally new to Krishna Consciousness, this can be bewildering. How can the Lord get angry? Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita Himself says there are three gates leading to hell: lust, anger and greed. And here Lord Himself is getting angry. This is Jaisa Achar and then Prachar. So here the Lord is getting angry and grew more and more enraged at the smell of blood of His person. In His eagerness to win, this we see. Okay, actually this can be expected, after all He was a demon. Eagerness to win. And Krishna also says you have the right to do your duty but do not be attached to the results. On one side the Lord says control the senses, control the mind. From the very beginning control the senses and then He marries 16,108 queens. So people sometimes even ask this question: Why the Lord married so many queens? From their understanding, was He not satisfied with one wife? Why did He dance with the gopis? That's why Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita says, what meets our eyes is a superficial understanding.

---
#### knowledge, parampara, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=372
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Not to get angry is very easy. A stone also does not get angry. But to get angry at the right time, in the right circumstances, on the right person, to the right degree, is not very easy."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of understanding scriptures through parampara to avoid misconceptions, and explains that anger has its place when used appropriately.

That's why Krishna says, it's a most confidential knowledge. Tatvatah means in truth. What we can understand just by reading like a novel or a comic or any other book, will not be tatvatah understanding. It will not be the real understanding. That's why Krishna says one who understands in tatvatah, in truth, he gets eligible for liberation from the cycle of birth and death. He goes back to the kingdom of God. So how do we understand? Why the Lord who is... And we also understand saintly people are expected to be tolerant, not get provoked. They should be peaceful. They should be equipoised. They should be forgiving. These are all qualities of devotees and Lord should also exhibit these qualities. And here we are saying that the Lord is fighting and Lord is getting angry and is beating Hiranyaksha and is eager to win, to defeat Hiranyaksha. So how do we understand this? So as I mentioned, if we have not studied the scriptures in a parampara, a parampara understanding, just like Prabhupada is writing the purports, when we read those purports and understand them, all our misconceptions and wrong understandings slowly can get wiped away. Typical understanding is a saintly person means he should never get angry. That's why many times people say Hinduism, because of all this tolerance and ahimsa, that's why these Mohammedans invaded India and we were just simply tolerant and they ransacked the temples, destroyed the temples. And we were all talking about ahimsa, we should be tolerant. Actually that's not the correct understanding. Wherever anger is required, it has to be exhibited. Wherever ahimsa is required, it has to be done. The whole Bhagavad Gita was spoken by Lord to Arjuna to inspire him to fight. Sometimes it can be bewildering what to tolerate, what not to tolerate, when to get angry, when not to get angry. There is a very famous quote which says, not to get angry is very easy. If someone decides I'll not get angry, relatively it's easy. A stone also does not get angry. But to get angry at the right time, in the right circumstances, on the right person, to the right degree, is not very easy. In other words, there is place of anger, there is place for every emotion.

---
#### emotions, devotion, superficiality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=579
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone worships Hanuman as a great devotee of the Lord, and he got angry, and he burned the Lanka, and still we glorify him, still we worship him."
**Summary:** This segment explains that emotions originate from the Lord and have their place, citing Hanuman burning Lanka as an example, and criticizes superficial understanding of scriptures.

The emotions what we exhibit, originally is there in the Lord. Lord is a person, and person means emotions, feelings. And every emotion has a place. Just like Hanuman got angry, and he burned the Lanka. He was a great devotee of the Lord. Everyone worships Hanuman. He worships Hanuman, as a great devotee of the Lord. And he got angry, and he burned the Lanka. And still we glorify him, still we worship him. And still we accept him as a great devotee of the Lord. So many times, people have a very superficial understanding of our scriptures. In fact, many people who come up with these arguments, may not have even read the scriptures. Or if they would have read, they would have read very superficially, something written by some author, and they miss out the correct understanding. One time there was a family, who was visiting Afghanistan. Indian family. They had gone to meet some of their friends and acquaintances. So, they were visiting Afghanistan for the first time, and as you all know, it's an area which is strived with lot of terrorism, lot of violence. So this family was going in a car, and suddenly they were stopped, on the highway. And two men, with guns, stopped the car, and said, come out. So they were completely, you know, panic stricken, fearful, what's going to happen, fearful of death. Imagine, on the highway, deserted highway, somebody stops the car, and comes with a gun. So their heart started pounding. So anyway, they had no option, they came out of the car, and, the so-called terrorist, asked that person, what is your religion?

---
#### religion, misunderstanding, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=772
**Verse References:** BG 4.7
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Had he read Quran, he wouldn't be a terrorist."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about a Hindu family in Afghanistan who were stopped by terrorists, and the father recited a verse from the Bhagavad Gita to save them, highlighting the misunderstanding and chaos caused by fanaticism in the name of religion.

So this person, although he was Hindu, from Indian, from India, but fearful that they are not going to spare if he says I am a Hindu. So he spontaneously says, I am a Muslim. So the terrorist, how can he believe? Look like Indian, look like, so he said, can you recite a verse from Quran? So this person is a Hindu, how does he know Quran? Viva voice, they say, just to test your knowledge. So this person, you know, where does he remember Quran? Started, yada yadahi dharmasya glanir bhavati bharata, somehow, spontaneously, spoke out that verse, and the terrorist, okay, okay, go. So he got into the car, and went off. So, when they started off, the wife said, hey, why did you say you are a Muslim? And he asked you to quote Quran, and you said, yada yadahi, you quoted a verse from the Bhagavad Gita. Why did you do that? He said, had he read Quran, he wouldn't be a terrorist. It's a fact that many times people who are fighting in the name of religion, in fact, you ask anybody, I ask this question quite often, how many of you feel that today the world is in chaos because of religion? 95% of people raise their hands. I've asked this question in many, many gatherings, many, many forums. How many of you feel that today because of in the name of religion, people are fighting, the world is in a mess. If you look objectively also, all the fights which are going on in Syria, in Afghanistan, Iraq, all these things, it's all stemming from, you know, these differences, Israel, Palestine. So, how many of you feel that the world is in chaos because of religion? Everyone, except for few people, they raise their hands. People see that people are fighting in the name of religion. Religion, which is supposed to bring about peace, harmony, universal brotherhood, actually is doing just the opposite. India got divided, like Prabhupada says in the Bhagavatam, earlier it was all Bharat-Varsha, it got divided in the name of religion. Hindustan and Pakistan. People are seeing that countries are getting divided, countries are fighting, in the name of religion. Actually, the fact is, it is not because of religion.

---
#### fanaticism, religion, parampara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=912
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Without dharma, it is nothing but polished animalism."
**Summary:** This segment argues that the world's problems stem from a fanatic understanding of religion, not religion itself, and emphasizes the importance of understanding scriptures through parampara.

It's because of fanatic understanding of religion. Where do people have Tatvataha understanding? First and foremost, people have not read the Bhagavad Gita, not read the Quran, not read the Bible, even if they have read the Bible, or Quran, or whatever it is, it is superficial understanding. Just like, if you read and don't understand in Parampara, what is the understanding we are getting? The Lord is getting angry. And anybody can justify that, anybody can get angry, because we are following, we are devotees of the Lord. So, what has happened is, people with vested interest have fanned the religious sentiments of ignoble people, gullible people, ignorant people, people who are jobless, people who are illiterate, and has created chaos in the whole thing. Many of the so-called jihadis, they may be illiterate otherwise. Just like, we had that terrorist attack in India. They were paid some amount, the family would be paid few lakhs of rupees, and, you know, they were asked to participate in this jihad. So, in one sense, it is a very sorry state of affairs, but the result is not that you do away with religion. It says, just like a cataract, when the eyes have cataract, and the vision becomes blurred, the solution is not to pluck out the eyes. Na rahega baans, na bajegi baansuri. They say, pluck out the eyes, no more cataract, no more blurred vision. But that's not the solution. You remove the cataract, and your vision is restored. So dharma, or religion, has a very important place, because it helps us lead a meaningful life. Without dharma, it is nothing but polished animalism. So, when we understanding in parampara, we are fortunate to understand from a bona fide spiritual master, we can get a clear understanding, and not get bewildered when we read such things.

---
#### tolerance, anger, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=1073
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One should be ready to tolerate all insults to one's own self, but when Krishna or his pure devotee is blasphemed, a genuine devotee becomes angry and acts like fire against the offenders."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Lord Chaitanya's anger towards Jagai and Madhai, emphasizing the principle of tolerating insults to oneself but becoming angry when Krishna or his devotees are blasphemed.

Prabhupada says, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu became angry. Although he says, one who does not deserve respect also, maana dena, respect. Tolerant than a tree. Humbler than a blade of grass. Anybody tramples, it just stands up. Tree, you cut it, it gives you wood. You shake it, it gives you fruits. Tolerant, does not repel back. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, who is preaching that, is saying, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu became angry with the miscreant brothers, Jagai and Madai, who blasphemed and struck Nithyananda Prabhu. In his Sastastaka, Lord Chaitanya wrote, one should be humbler than the grass, and more tolerant than the tree. One may then ask, why the Lord exhibited his anger? The same thing here. Lord one side is saying, three gates leading to hell, and other side, he himself is getting angry. Lord Chaitanya is saying, humbler than a blade of grass, and then he is becoming angry, and Jagai and Madai. The point is that one should be ready to tolerate all insults to one's own self.

---
#### emotions, knowledge, tolerance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=1230
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every emotion has a place, but with the right knowledge, Tattvata understanding, we can have a proper understanding where and when to apply that emotion."
**Summary:** This segment reiterates that every emotion has a place when guided by proper knowledge, and emphasizes the importance of tolerating personal insults while becoming angry at blasphemy against Krishna or his devotees.

This is the principle. Sometimes we can also get bewildered. No, no, we should get angry. Little bit somebody provoked, angry. And justify, Lord also got angry. The principle is, the point is that one should be ready to tolerate all insults to one's own self. My ego gets hurt, I should tolerate. Somebody speaks something, I feel bad, I tolerate. I become provoked, I tolerate. One should be ready to tolerate all insults to one's own self. But when Krishna or his pure devotee is blasphemed, a genuine devotee becomes angry and acts like fire against the offenders. Here Mother Earth, she is also a devotee, and Hiranaksha, and the Lord, just like Lord Chaitanya became angry. Jagai and Madai, they hit Nithyananda Prabhu, and the Lord became angry. So every emotion has a place, but with the right knowledge, Tattvata understanding, we can have a proper understanding where and when to apply that emotion. Just like Krishna said, dharma aviruto kamo asmi, even karma has a place. dharma aviruto, like that, all emotions, if it is not dharma aviruto, in the sense, if it is in line with dharmic principles, have a place. If you always remember Krishna, by your activities, and seeing Krishna everywhere, even in the heart of demoniac persons, then anger will never overcome you. Now how do we tolerate? Suppose somebody is, you know, in the middle of the night, in the middle of the night, in the middle of the night, in the middle of the night, he is, you know, provoking us, he is making our life miserable, or he is speaking some sarcastic words, and I am getting affected. So the solution is, if you are always remembering Krishna, and seeing Krishna everywhere, even in the heart of demoniac people, then anger will never overcome you. Being purified of false pride, many times we get angry because of false pride, because of ego. And then we justify that so and so is responsible for my anger. Actually, if we look introspect, it is because our ego got hurt, our self-respect, our esteem got affected. Maybe itch chali jayegi, what will people think about me? But occasionally, if there is good reason, you may have to become angry, just to chastise the evil doers and blasphemers. So specially when we are young, we will require some guidance. Superficial understanding is not to get angry. So Prabhupada says, if there is good reason, you may have to become angry, just to chastise the evil doers and blasphemers. Because we are living in a material world, where everyone is in one sense diseased. Everyone has ego, everyone has pride, everyone is there to put down others. So sometimes, we may have to get angry, to chastise the evil doers, and the blasphemers.

---
#### anger, tolerance, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=1377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anger can be controlled, we cannot stop anger altogether. But if we simply become angry with those who blaspheme the Lord, or the devotees of the Lord, we can control our anger, in Krishna consciousness."
**Summary:** This segment explains how to control anger in Krishna consciousness by directing it towards blasphemers of the Lord and his devotees, while tolerating personal insults.

Anger can be controlled, we cannot stop anger altogether. But if we simply become angry with those who blaspheme the Lord, or the devotees of the Lord, we can control our anger, in Krishna consciousness. When insignificant persons use rough words to cast false, angry accusations against saintly persons, their fruitless words do not disturb the great personalities. So if it is, mind you here, angry accusations against saintly person, personal attack, just like here it is said, all insults to one's own self, saintly persons, they do not get disturbed. They act like, but when required, evil doers, blasphemers, blasphemers of devotees, blasphemers of the Lord, they have the right to become angry, they should become angry. Sometimes, when we are living in day to day life, we will get upset, we will get, somebody will speak some things, bit critical, unnecessarily, will pass some remarks, as we said, we all are conditioned souls, we are not pure devotees, where we have risen above all this. So when, now here it is said, a saintly person should tolerate, should not get disturbed. Now what if we get disturbed? We will get disturbed. Only when our consciousness has risen to a certain level, when we are advanced in Krishna consciousness, saintly person, great personalities, we will not be affected. Suppose somebody has insulted us, and we get angry, we get affected, we get worked up, and the result of that is, many times, we can either retaliate, or sometime, if it is, let's say a senior devotee, or somebody else, we can't retaliate, deep within, we have grudges, hatred, ill feeling, can't do anything, but then, inside, keep brewing, anger, hatred. In fact, it is said that if a devotee has that kind of a consciousness, he is hurting himself.

---
#### forgiveness, anger, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=1536
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "People don't expect that saintly person should get provoked, and lose temper. That's a natural understanding."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about being insulted while collecting donations, and how he reacted with anger, losing the donation, illustrating the importance of forgiveness and tolerance for devotees.

So, how to keep our heart clean? We will get affected, we will get, so to say, somebody insults us, somebody speaks harsh words. One time, I went out to meet somebody, many years back, when I used to go out for life membership. So, we went to a donor's place, and I was telling him, he had agreed to become a member, and I was supposed to go and collect the membership amount. He was ready, he was having a cheque book in his hand. He was about to write the cheque, and I was sitting, I took out the receipt book, and the person sitting next to me, his friend, he was also there, in his office. So, that person said, what do you do whole day? So, I told him that we get up in the morning, and we have the sadhana, and then we have different services and all that. Then that person suddenly switched tone and said, do you know what is a parasite? So, it took me some time to understand what is he up to? He said, I understand. Do you know what is a parasite? I said, yeah, I understand. How are you different from a parasite? He asked. Parasite is basically a person, parasite in a body, lives on the other person's blood, or other person's this thing. So, what he was trying to indicate is that we are parasites, we don't do any meaningful work, we beg. We just do little bit of ringing the bell, while our time, and beg people, and live at the cost of others. So, moment I heard that, how are you different from parasite? I became angry. I said, how can you call out so, and I tried justifying, you know, no, you don't know how much we are building a big temple, it's a public temple, so many people will come, temple is required, and all those things. While all this anger discussion was going on, the person took the checkbook, and kept it inside. It was in a drawer. And then he said, Swamiji will see later on. That's it. People don't expect that saintly person should get provoked, and lose temper. That's a natural understanding. And in one sense, that's the understanding which people have. But, we cannot go to other extreme. Now here, this was a blasphemy to me. How are you different from a parasite? Ideal would have been to just tolerate. Just, after all he is ignorant, he has his own understanding of who the devotees are. Maybe he is an atheist. Simply tolerate, and somehow, without offending him, Lord's service should happen. Somehow intelligently, if I had added, he would have even given the check. So sometimes, we will get affected, so to say, we will get, momentarily lose our temper, because we are conditioned souls, we are not pure devotees. But the worst thing is, we brew anger within us.

---
#### forgiveness, anger, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-ZjWL2a5c6E?t=1755
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Just like the monkeys, they can go free, if they just drop the banana. So likewise, many times, we just tightly hold on to that anger, resentment, hatred, ill feeling against someone, you have to let go."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of forgiveness and letting go of anger, using the analogy of monkeys trapped by their attachment to bananas, and concludes with a reminder to get angry when the Lord and his devotees are blasphemed.

We stop talking to someone. Like here it says, the lips in rage, he bit his lip in rage, took up his mace again, and began to repeatedly brandish, you know, anger within. So it is said that for a devotee, it is very important to also cultivate a quality, and that quality is shama. Shama means forgiveness. Just like here it says, if one remembers that the Lord, seeing Krishna everywhere, even in the heart of a demoniac person, the person who has insulted you, if you are Krishna conscious, genuine Krishna conscious, anger will not overcome you. Being purified of all false pride. So it is said that sometimes when people have to catch monkeys, wild monkeys in jungles, they take cages, and they put it in the forest, and inside the cages, they put something which the monkeys like. We all know monkeys like bananas and, so big big bananas, bunches they will keep inside those cages, and they leave them, and they leave them in wild forest. What they expect is that once the monkeys come inside the cage to eat the bananas, and they will drop the lever, and the cage will get closed. So the monkeys, one or two times they got trapped, but you know, we all learn from experience, monkeys are also that way quite shrewd. So the monkeys saw that, you know, this is a trap for us. So instead of going inside the cage to eat the bananas, they came on the side of the cage, put the hand inside the cage, and they were trying to take the bananas. So that even if the lever goes in, they will get the bananas also without getting trapped. But then it so happened that people started observing, those who had put those cages, the monkeys would hold big big bananas in their hand, will try to pull it out, but the cage ribs were there, the bars were there, they won't be able to pull out the banana, because bananas were quite big. And they are desperate to eat the bananas, and they will not leave the bananas. Trying, trying, trying, somehow not leaving the banana, and finally they will be caught. So the learning from this is, just like the monkeys, they can go free, if they just drop the banana. So likewise, many times, we just tightly hold on to that anger, resentment, hatred, ill feeling against someone, you have to let go. We will be peaceful. We have to also analyze, most of the time, that hatred, ill feeling is because I have got insulted. Somebody has hurt me. All of us are conditioned souls, we all are diseased. Jesus says, Father forgive them, for they know not what they are doing. Everyone is acting in lower modes, we all get overpowered. Drop it. We will momentarily get angry. Beg forgiveness, ask forgiveness, let go. Keep your heart clean. Keep your consciousness clean. So if we have that, now having said that, we have to avoid the other extreme. Other extreme is every time let go. If the Lord and His devotees are blasphemed, we have to get angry. If I get insulted, I tolerate. If unknowingly, unintentionally, in spite of this, I get provoked, I get angry, I use harsh words, I beg forgiveness. I let go. So we will stop here. Granth Raj, Srimad Bhagavatam Ki, Srila Prabhupada.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-03-30 | SB 3.19.24 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Krishna, demoniac mentality, inconceivable potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He did not know that God is greatest of the great and the smallest of the small."
**Summary:** This segment explains the meaning of Keshava, Krishna's inconceivable potencies, and the futility of trying to capture the Absolute with material power.

In this verse, the Lord is addressed as Keshava because he killed the demon Keshi in the beginning of creation. Keshava is another name of Krishna. Krishna is the origin of all incarnations and is confirmed in the Brahma-samhita that Govinda, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the cause of all causes, exists simultaneously in a different incarnation and expansions. The demon's attempt to measure the Supreme Personality of Godhead is significant. The demon wanted to embrace him with his arms, thinking that with his limited arms he could capture the Absolute by material power. He did not know that God is greatest of the great and the smallest of the small.

---
#### devotion, demoniac mentality, universal form
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=66
**Verse References:** BG 9.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord, Keshava, or the Personality of Godhead, Krishna, is not bound by any measurement of our calculation."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how the Lord can manifest in both the universal form and within the hearts of his devotees, emphasizing that He is unapproachable by demoniac activities.

No one can capture the Supreme Lord or bring him under his control. But the demoniac persons always attempt to measure the length and the breadth of the Supreme Lord. By his inconsiderate potency, the Lord can become the universal form, as explained in the Bhagavad Gita, and at the same time he can remain within the box of his devotees as his worshipable Deity. There are many devotees who keep a statue of the Lord in a small box and carry it with them everywhere. Every morning they worship the Lord in the box. The Supreme Lord, Keshava, or the Personality of Godhead, Krishna, is not bound by any measurement of our calculation. He can remain with his devotee in any suitable form, yet he is unapproachable by any amount of demoniac activities.

---
#### Hiranyaksha, Krishna, inconceivable potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=110
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But to his amazement, when he tried doing that, when he embraced the Lord tightly in order to crush him, to his great amazement, he found the Lord standing outside the circle of his arms."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the story of Hiranyaksha's attempt to kill Krishna, highlighting the Lord's inconceivable potencies and the demon's ultimate defeat.

So this chapter named, The Killing of Demon Hiranyaksha, and here we are seeing how in the fight between the demon and the Lord, finally the demon is getting defeated. When the demon saw his magic forces dispelled all his powers, all his whatever ammunition he had, he tried everything to defeat the Lord, finally, when he came in the presence of the Supreme Lord, personality of Godhead, Keshava, full of rage, as a last attempt, he wanted to embrace him, but with a different intention. Out of rage, he wanted to embrace him with his arm in order to crush him, so that he can embrace the Lord and embrace so tightly that the Lord is crushed and killed. But to his amazement, when he tried doing that, when he embraced the Lord tightly in order to crush him, to his great amazement, he found the Lord standing outside the circle of his arms.

Our actions may exhibit that we still have lording tendency, we still have enjoying tendency, but fundamentally yes we accept Krishna's to Bhagwan Swayam. He is the enjoyer, He is the proprietor, He is the Lord, He is the master. He is the supreme controller.

#### devotee, good qualities, faults
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=463
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Mahat has the ability to see some good in everyone."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Vishwanath Chakravarti Thakur's classification of good people, starting with the 'Mahat' who can see good in everyone and believes faults can be transformed.

Vishwanath Chakravarti Thakur in one of his books mentioned about four kinds of good and bad people. It's very nice where he analyzes that there are four kinds of good people. One, although broad category is a good man or a bad man, he is a devotee or he is a demon, but he further classifies a devotee and a demoniac person. The first category is mahat. Vishwanath mahat sevaya. Mahat, he is a great person. And what is the quality of a mahat? A Mahat, these all first four categories are of, you know, people who have started becoming devotees, started developing good qualities. Mahat, a great person has ability to see some good in everyone. We all know in this world, we all have a good sense. In this world, we We encounter different kinds of people, some very bad people, some very wicked people, cruel people and sometimes very charitably disposed, very pious, very good, very kind, noble souls. All kinds of people are there in this world and sometimes it is very bewildering. A Mahat has the ability to see some good in everyone. They also perceive fault in others. Fault is also there. But they consider that these faults can eventually be transformed by certain methods into good qualities. It should be noted that Mahat will never reject or condemn a faulty person, but thinks only how that person can be benefited, can be transformed.

#### devotee, good qualities, faults, Mahattara, Mahattama
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=586
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Mahattara also sees good and bad qualities in others, but focuses on and praises the good quality."
**Summary:** This segment continues the classification of good people, describing 'Mahattara' who praises good qualities and 'Mahattama' who magnifies good qualities while ignoring faults.

In other words, he sees mainly the good qualities, although he is able to see the faults, but he has that confidence. That by following certain process, some principles, he can be transformed. And those faults can be rectified and transformed into good qualities. Such a person who has this kind of a mentality, he focuses on the good, he sees the fault, but he has the confidence that that can be rectified and transformed into a nice person, this is a quality of a Mahat. Higher than Mahat is Mahattara. A greater person. First is great person, Mahat. Greater person, Mahattara. Mahattara also sees good and bad qualities in others, but focuses on and praises the good quality. He gives an example just like a merchant. He is a materialistic person. He is very busy transacting, making money. A typical businessman wants to increase his wealth. Now, a Mahattara sees that a businessman very enthusiastically doing business. Although he may see him as a sense gratifier, ultimately he is materialistic. He wants to make more profit for himself and enjoy the profit. But he notices that he is treating his customers so nicely. And because he is so gentle in his dealings, he is worthy of deliverance. So, in other words, he praises the good quality. Although he sees the fault, he magnifies the good qualities. Then, Vishnu Chakravidh Thakur, even higher than Mahattara is Mahattama, especially great person. Even greater than that person. A Mahattama. A Mahattama sees whatever good qualities others have, he magnifies them, not seeing the even smallest of faults. He refuses to focus on the faults. He sees good qualities and he magnifies them. If he sees 80% good, 20% fault, he ignores the 20% and the 80%, he magnifies it to 100%. In other words, he focuses only on the good.

make the required profits. They are trying to make some formula. If you follow the formula, this will be the desired results. Nothing but lording tendency. Five factors of action, Krishna says, the ultimate, the chief is the super soul. That is completely out of the equation. So this demoniac mentality eventually, although we may control for a while, we may aggrandize, we may become wealthy, we may accumulate some riches, but ultimately Krishna says, finally, even if we don't surrender to the Lord, we don't recognize the Lord, finally, mithyu harvas chaham, sarva haras chaham, finally everyone has to surrender at the point of death. Even that, demoniac people are not able to recognize. That's why everyone is so busy accumulating wealth, making properties, more and more, gratifying the senses. It's very unfortunate. One time there was a wealthy man who was sitting in the balcony and he saw there was an ant crossing the balcony from one side of the balcony to the other side.

#### intelligence, materialism, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ARRiIBi5l7I?t=1723
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All that is so small, so insignificant, it has some analytical skills. It can sense there is a danger."
**Summary:** This segment begins a story about a wealthy man observing an ant, highlighting the ant's intelligence and ability to overcome obstacles.

So he was very carefully noticing this ant and this ant, I am sure all of us have watched, they also have intelligence. If you try to put a hand, obstruct the path, they will turn left, turn right, somehow try to evade. Immediately they are able to sense there is an obstacle. All that is so small, so insignificant, it has some analytical skills. It can sense there is a danger. You put a hand, it will turn left, you put hand there also it will turn the other side. If you keep hand, it will start moving fast, it will turn back. It senses that now somebody is obstructing path either ways. So this person was carefully observing and he was really amazed that such a tiny creature is having the ability to comprehend, analyze, explore, to some extent predict. He was able to predict. He was able to understand and predict danger. It would make twists and turns and keep moving. And that ant was carrying some kind of a leaf. Then comes in the floor a very big crack, must be an old house. There was a very big crack. Imagine the crack was there and this ant is some 1 or 2 millimeter long. Imagine the crack is 8 mm. 8 mm. No way this ant could have crossed the crack. So this person became little more curious to understand what will the ant do now. Because if you obstruct, you can go left, you can go right. Now there is an 8 mm crack and this ant is just 1 mm or 2 mm. What will the ant do? He was curious to know. The ant came, sensed that there is a crack, there is an obstruction. It was carrying a

#### gratitude, devotional service, blessings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=600
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Out of gratitude that we are receiving so much, the minimum what we can do is express our gratitude, be grateful, have an attitude of gratitude, and the natural consequence of that gratitude would be, let me render some service, devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of gratitude for the blessings received and how it naturally leads to devotional service, cautioning against taking these blessings for granted.

Out of gratitude that we are receiving so much, the minimum what we can do is express our gratitude, be grateful, have an attitude of gratitude, and the natural consequence of that gratitude would be, let me render some service, devotional service. Because the Lord is so great, so kind, so merciful, so magnanimous, He is giving me so many things, let me do something for Him, some menial service for Him. Only an ungrateful soul, a person who does not recognize, will wonder why I should do anything in return, even as disciples. As devotees, we have to be very, very careful that sometimes we can take all the blessings what we have for granted. We are getting nice prasadam, we are getting an opportunity to associate with devotees, we are staying in the ashram, the atmosphere is so congenial for spiritual growth.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=661

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

We have regular classes, morning till 9.30, we are all involved in various spiritual practices. Now, after this, we will go, we will have prasadam. Prasadam is ready. So we can take this for granted, and sometimes we can even start cribbing, or so to say complaining about small, small things, lacunas we might find in our lives, in the system. In fact, by default, as conditioned souls, we are ungrateful. Conditioned soul means that natural, loving attitude, loving service to the Lord is covered. Jivera swaroopo hai nityera krishna das. That attitude, somehow, because of contamination, because of conditioning, because of having overpowered by the lower modes, rajo guna and tamo guna, that's covered. Sometimes we even start rebelling, and demanding, and even complaining about injustice. So as a natural, we are conditioned to be ungrateful. That is conditional life.

Even karmas have realized that the attitude of gratitude is very, very important if a person has to be happy, and successful in life. Sometimes again because of conditioning we are lazy, we are reluctant, we shrug off from the services. Very expensive to visit them at that time, who are given the service and are porridge, the prasadam will be ready, hot, cooked, offered. Just to sit down and have prasadam. You go to a shop, you buy something, you take something and you give in return. You give your money in return. #### karma, desire, cycle of action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1013
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "And all the Jeevas, if you see in the material world, we are caught up in a web or mesh of actions and reactions."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of karma, highlighting how desires lead to actions, which create reactions, trapping living entities in an endless cycle of enjoyment and suffering.

In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna mentions about the five truths, Jeeva, Prakriti, Kala, Karma and Ishvara. And of all these things, all are eternal except for karma. Prabhupada explains why karma is not eternal because we act and based on our actions, we have some reactions. And all the Jeevas, if you see in the material world, we are caught up in a web or mesh of actions and reactions. We desire, to fulfill that desire, we act, act independent of the Lord, completely oblivious of the presence of the Lord, forgetful of the Lord. We want to enjoy independently and then we act, we desire, based on that we create karmas, based on that there are reactions. And then we enjoy or suffer those reactions and further it creates the reactions, it's like an endless cycle, it's like a bhava, it's like a whirlwind.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1115
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Why karma is called, is not eternal? Because by proper guidance, by culture of Krishna consciousness, the actions and the subsequent reactions can be changed."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how karma is not eternal because through Krishna consciousness, actions and reactions can be changed, leading to liberation from material existence by purifying desires and performing the right actions.

Rather than wings or threads, we think it is this simple, but we filled it with religious bribery. protector of the verfieckpaul. To give an example, let's say a butcher, already he's sinful based on that he has got the life of a butcher and he is committing more sins. In that life he's committing more sin. It's like a negative spiral going down and now in a material by a large people are in the negative spiral. Already there is sinful based on that they have got the bodies and in that the body devoid of Krishna consciousness with no knowledge of the worship of the Divinest being deivy 2. service, they are further getting entangled. And based on that entanglement, based on desires which are unfulfilled, that jiva is getting life after life different bodies. And it's an endless cycle. Why karma is called, is not eternal? Because by proper guidance, by culture of Krishna consciousness, the actions and the subsequent reactions can be changed. We act because of false ego in conditional stage. We have different desires. We have misgivings, I am the body, I am the enjoyer, we completely forgetful of the Lord. By culture In the nature of Krishna consciousness we can purify those desires, do the right kind of actions, and liberate ourselves from this material existence.

#### devotional service, purification, dependence on Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1186
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Right actions are nothing but devotional service. Purified actions are nothing but devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker defines right actions as devotional service, emphasizing that the process of bhakti yoga aims to remove contamination and cultivate gratitude, recognition of the Lord's blessings, and exclusive dependence on Him.

What are right actions? Right actions are nothing but devotional service. Purified actions are nothing but devotional service. Jīvera svarūpa hoye nityara kṛṣṇa dāsa. That's our svarūpa, purified state. Only in contaminated stage we are forgetful of Lord. Only in a contaminated condition stage we think that we are the enjoyer. When you remove that contamination, the natural what remains is to serve the Lord. So the whole process of bhakti yoga, what we are following, is to remove this contamination. To come to a purified stage. Purified stage means there is a gratitude. There is a recognition that, yes, I am blessed by so many things. The Lord is my master. He is giving me so many things. And when there is recognition, when there is gratitude, when there is dependence on the Lord, just like you are saying, we will be completely exclusively dependent on the Lord. Right now we have made so many arrangements for our protection. Why we are not dependent on the Lord? Why we are forgetful of the Lord? Because we have faith in our own abilities, own mechanisms, own protection, safeguards, what we have built. The more and more we get freed from that conditioning, we become purified, the more and more we exclusively depend on the Lord.

#### devotional service, faith, story
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1276
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We have faith in the Lord's protection. We have the conviction that Lord is the master. I am a servant."
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the importance of faith in the Lord's protection and service through a story of rats in a dilapidated hut being flooded, where all perish.

We have faith in the Lord's protection. We have the conviction that Lord is the master. I am a servant. And by devotional service, by practice of bhakti yoga, we can get out of this conditioning. One time it so happened that there was a dilapidated hut, and in that hut there were many rat holes. It was on a bank of a river. And the rats would, in the daytime, come out of those holes and move around. Sometimes go to nearby shops, collect those items, whatever they like to eat, and then come back. It so happened that one day it rained very heavily, poured very heavily, just like we had that recently in Shardham. So the river got inundated, started overflowing, and the water entered that hut, inundated the entire hut, and all the rat holes were filled with water. Those rats somehow tried surviving, but since they were caught unaware, and practically all the rat holes were filled with water, all of them died.

#### analogy, spiritual master, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1374
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "And one of the rats, which was struggling somehow to remain afloat and survive, got pushed by a wave and landed upon that log."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, describing how one rat survived by clinging to a log of wood, drawing a parallel to our condition in the material world.

Luckily, while all this havoc was happening, on that river there was one log of wood, big, huge log of wood that was going downstream. And one of the rats, which was struggling somehow to remain afloat and survive, got pushed by a wave and landed upon that log. While there was, water was gushing through and, the whole place was getting inundated with water, but somehow this log was floating, and this rat, completely drenched, was sitting on top of the log, flowing downstream. And luckily this log striked some bank, and this mouse, which was sitting on top of the log, got somehow pushed onto the land and saved himself, got saved. This is a very crude example, there is no one-on-one analogy, but nevertheless, to drive home the point, there is some similarity to what is our condition.

Kala will flow and even people, mirtyu haras sarvacaham, Krishna says, everything will be destroyed, everything will end in destruction. #### spiritual master, devotional service, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1522
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If we take shelter of a pure devotee, take shelter of his lotus feet, even though there is destruction all around us... we can get saved."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the necessity of taking shelter of a pure devotee, comparing it to getting into a boat led by an expert sailor, to escape the cycle of birth and death and return to the kingdom of God.

When a person is drowning, he cannot save himself just by swimming nicely. We require an external help. We require someone who can come and pick us up. And sometimes it can be out of compassion. Sometimes when tragedies happen, a lot of people who have that seva bhava, who want to help people, they go to such areas. They rush to these areas on humanitarian grounds to save people. So Lord is very magnanimous, very merciful, just like here we saw that he came to protect Gajendra. He himself comes and sends his pure devotees to pick up, to reclaim the conditioned soul which is being floored by Samsara Sagara, inundated in Samsara Sagara. And this rat somehow got saved, swimming on that log. If we take shelter of a pure devotee, take shelter of his lotus feet, even though there is destruction all around us, birth, death, punarapi janma, punarapi marna, the Samsara Sagara, we are also in that Sagara, we can get saved. That is what the process of devotional service is. Get into the boat, which is, so to say, led by the spiritual master, an expert sailor, who can take us to the right destination. And what is the destination? Back to our original home, the kingdom of God. And if we don't take shelter of devotional service, don't take shelter of a pure devotee of the Lord, just like those rats, we'll all be killed, vanquished, by Kala. And again, based on our desires, again, punarapi janma, punarapi marna, that goes on.

Ears have a natural inclination to hear about the Lord, hear the glories of the Lord, hear kirtans, hear the holy name. Nose has a natural inclination to smell something aromatic. The lotus feet of the Lord, the flowers which are offered to the Lord, the incense which is offered to the Lord, you know, we have the sticks which are offered to the Lord. #### false ego, conditioned life, plunderer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1743
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In a conditioned stage what happens is, it is important for us to understand, there comes a plunderer between the soul and the intelligence."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the conditioned state, explaining how false ego acts as a plunderer between the soul and intelligence, leading to contaminated intelligence, uncontrolled mind, and untamed senses.

This is a normal stage. In a conditioned stage what happens is, it is important for us to understand, there comes a plunderer between the soul and the intelligence. What is the name of that plunderer? That plunderer is called the false ego. The false ego, the main function is identification, desires. Ego is just like saying, what is ego? Your identity. False ego means wrong identity. False ego plunderer means identity that I am the enjoyer. I am this body. And when this false ego comes, then the intelligence picks up the desire from this ego, false ego, gives that to the mind, mind to the senses, and there is havoc in the system. The senses which are meant to serve the Lord start acting in a way which is detrimental to the self-interest of the soul. The intelligence becomes contaminated. The mind becomes uncontrolled. The senses become untamed. This is our present state. This is the meaning of conditioned life. The root cause is what? The false ego. What is the false ego? I am the enjoyer. There is no Lord.

#### devotion, false ego, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1846
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains how devotion transforms false ego into real ego, leading to gratitude and recognition of Krishna as the real enjoyer and master, contrasting it with the rebelliousness and ungratefulness associated with false ego.

I have to take care of myself. So through the process of devotion, what is happening is, slowly our false ego is getting transformed into real ego. What is real ego? Jivera Swaroopa Hohe Nityera Krishna Das Krishna is the real enjoyer. He is the master. Everything what I have, my abilities, my skills, my talents, is coming from the Lord. In a purified sense, naturally that feeling of gratitude, that gratitude is there. In that stage where we are overpowered by false ego, naturally we will be rebellious. We will always be ungrateful. #### devotional service, purification, positive spiral
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=1938
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the moment we take up the process of devotional service, we start becoming devotees, what we are getting into is a positive spiral."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the positive spiral of devotional service, where engaging in bhakti yoga purifies the false ego, leading to increased motivation and inspiration to render more devotional service, ultimately resulting in a completely purified ego and surrender to the Lord.

So slowly, through the process of devotional service, by doing devotional service, by the process of bhakti yoga, the false ego gets purified. And when the false ego gets purified, we do more bhakti yoga. Because we do more bhakti yoga, the false ego gets purified. And because of purification of false ego, we do more devotional activities. This is a positive spiral. So the moment we take up the process of devotional service, we start becoming devotees, what we are getting into is a positive spiral. What is positive spiral? We have false ego. We engage in devotional service. We hear, chant, glorify. It purifies the ego. Because the ego gets purified, it could be temporarily. Because of that purified ego, we are motivated, we are inspired. Just like today, we are reading in this verse, what grateful man would be who would not render devotional service. We get motivated, inspired to render devotional service. Because of that, the ego gets more purified. And it goes on in a cyclic manner, till the point where the ego is completely purified. Pure devotee means completely purified ego, completely surrendered to the Supreme Lord, completely thinking of the Lord always.

#### false ego, negative spiral, process of purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W4SK4xkXIYU?t=2026

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Depends again how well we take the medicine, how sincerely we follow the process."

And the reverse also is possible. If we lose the association of devotees, if we stop taking the medicine, if we stop chanting the holy names, the false ego becomes thicker and thicker and thicker. Because of that thick false, many times we ask people, why don't you come to the temple? When he will call, we will come. The time has to come. You ask a rich man, why don't you give some donation? He has to give, then I will give. He may have big, big cars parked in his portico, in his house. Lord has to give, then I will give. Lot of people have said like that. Lord has to give. You tell your Lord to give, then we will give. So it's like the dust which is setting on the false ego, becoming thicker and thicker and thicker. So that way we are very grateful to Shri Prabhupada, we are very blessed. Although we have false ego, we are not purified. Although we are conditioned soul, we are blessed that we are having a process. Depends again how well we take the medicine, how sincerely we follow the process. The process is there which will purify us and slowly that good qualities, gratitude, rendering devotional service, understanding that Krishna is my master, dependence on Krishna, depending on Krishna for protection, all those good qualities will start manifesting in our system.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-07-07 | SB 3.25.3 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Anukirtaya, Internal Potency, Description
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CcyCWSidAqo?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagwan, the supreme personality of Godhead, has no material body, but he can assume any kind of body by his supreme will."
**Summary:** This segment defines Anukirtaya as following the description of the Lord's pastimes manifested by his internal potency, as requested by Shaunaka Rishi to Suta Goswami.

The word Anukirtaya is very significant. Anukirtaya means to follow the description, not to create a concocted mental description, but to follow. So, Shaunaka Rishi requested Suta Goswami to describe what he had actually heard from his spiritual master, Shukadeva Goswami, about the transcendental pastimes of the Lord manifested by his internal potency. Bhagwan, the supreme personality of Godhead, has no material body, but he can assume any kind of body by his supreme will. That is made possible by his internal potency. So, these are the three things.

---
#### Fiction, Reality, Illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CcyCWSidAqo?t=75
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "You get transported into another world."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the absorption people find in fiction with the potential for real absorption in the pastimes of the Lord, noting that fiction provides only temporary relief from the anxieties of life.

These are the words spoken by Shaunaka Rishi, who is asking Suta Goswami, please describe all the activities and pastimes of the personality of Godhead, who is full of self-desire, and who assumes all these activities by his internal potency. Prabhupada mentions, Anukirtaya means to follow the description, and not to create a concocted mental description, but to follow. Invariably when I travel, I see people reading some books, The Flight or otherwise. And sometimes when I strike a conversation with a person sitting next to me or otherwise, I invariably find the book what they are reading is some fiction novel. So one time I asked a person, what kind of books do you like, what kind of books do you read? She said, I like fiction books. In my life till now I haven't read any fiction books. So I was curious, so I asked him. Why it interests you, why do you read fiction books, you know it's a fiction, it's not a reality. What interests you, why do you read fiction books. So he told me an very interesting thing. He says, you get transported into another world. It appears as if you have gone into another world and you forget your present world actually then I was introspecting and people get transported into another world definitely you forget the realities of life everyone is bogged down by so many anxieties that's why people also watch movies people go to the cinema theater watch a movie they get so engrossed in the movie and the movies are so quote-unquote nicely made directed or produced that completely captures a person's attention and for that two hours you're completely absorbed in that particular world you relate with those characters and when you come back people also say it's a stress buster it's constantly mind is analyzing producing thoughts thinking of the past thinking of the future when you go and watch a movie you break that circuit yes it gives momentary relief just like people drink but then it doesn't address the problem at the root momentarily you forget momentarily you get transported into another world but back again again you're back to your so instead of addressing the root cause instead of looking for real source of happiness today's world the lot of quick fix solution people are seeking momentarily it does give them relief but far far away from finding the real solution.

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that unlike fiction, the pastimes of the Lord are real and should be understood systematically, not superficially, to avoid misconceptions about the Lord's forms and activities.

Unlike fiction books unless unlike these days movies are there there's a movie very popular now called Angry Birds there was a video game and on that they have made billions of dollars have been spent on that movie Angry Birds fictitious characters unlike that all the characters although such a contradictory thing such historical pastimes of the Lord they're called mythology whereas very educated intelligent people knowing that these are all fictitious characters they they are very eager to watch some of you may be knowing Harry Potter became very famous few years back and I was speaking to someone people would queue up outside the bookstore they would pre-order the books and the day the book was being released people would queue up the bookstores will open two hours early in the morning so nine o'clock it'll open at seven o'clock and people would queue up again it's all fiction whereas here what Shonaka Rishi is asking Shruthi Goswami is please describe to us the pastimes of the Supreme Lord what you have heard from your spiritual master Shukadev Goswami so these pastimes are not concoctions these are all not fiction many times when we hear about Lord's pastime like Lord descending in the form boar form or keshav dhrita meena sharira fish form tortoise form it appears it's all fiction how can Lord okay we understand we have faith in Lord but how can Lord descend in the form of a fish or a tortoise so again avajananti ma mudha Krishna says janma karma chame divyam evam yo veti tatvataha that's why just to conclude by hearing superficially one can lead to a very wrong and unnerving understanding that's why systematically the bhagavata describes about even the leelas of the Lord sometimes is mentioned in the tenth canto before we understand the leelas of the Lord we have to understand who the Lord is just like Prabhupad in the Puppeteer is mentioning he can assume any kind of body by a supreme will this is made possible by his internal potency.

#### Illusion, Movies, Understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CcyCWSidAqo?t=520
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Actually, if we see a shooting of a movie, it's all to shoot one scene, cut, cut, retake, cut, cut, and finally all this cut, cut, retakes."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of movie-making to illustrate how illusion is created through disjointed scenes stitched together, contrasting this with the need for genuine understanding.

When I was young, maybe three, four, four years, four, five years, so when we would go to watch some, we were staying in a township, so invariably on a Saturday or Sunday, they would screen some movies. Those days, VCRs and all that were not there, so those film roles. So invariably. In the colony, the township, on weekends, they would screen such movies. And whenever I would watch, imagine five years old, boy, not have complete understanding of the world. Whenever I would watch the movie, I clearly remember, invariably that movie would have some songs, hero and heroine, on top of the mountains, and then suddenly heroes start singing. And then, so I used to wonder, on top of the mountain, hero is singing, how his voice is coming from, how this orchestra, sound, band, everything is coming. He is on top of a mountain, a small child, he will think like that. Now, grown-ups also, it doesn't strike them, lip-syncing and all those things, top of a mountain, distant. Sometime we do video recording, we have to really struggle hard. So that sound comes properly. Wind is blowing, and then he's in a deep in a forest, he's singing, and along with that, a band is there, some guitar is there, some, you know, all that musical instruments are there. So I used to wonder how this musical sound is coming. Actually, if we see a shooting of a movie, it's all to shoot one scene, cut, cut, retake, cut, cut, and finally all this cut, cut, retakes. They all start. They're all stitched together, and, you know, experts sit together and weave it together, and finally the movie comes out, and it brings about a great illusion. We get absorbed in the plot. Actually, if we see the movie, cut, cut, cut, one scene, a hero will come and slap, and say, why did you hit me? You know, it's all disjointed scenes. It doesn't make any sense, but once it is stitched together, it creates an illusion. We get so absorbed in the plot, in the scene.

#### Illusory Energy, Desire, Devotional Service

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

So illusory energy, why it is? Because we want to enjoy, independent of Krishna. We don't want to surrender to Krishna. We don't want to, ichcha, dvesha, samutthena, dvandamohena, bharata. We want, we have different desires, ichcha and dvesha, envy of the Lord. So we have been sent here to try it out. And such souls who are rebellious of the Lord, till they get purified, they shouldn't be purified. They shouldn't be disturbing the Lord and His devotees and His wonderful pastimes. So this illusory energy keeps them within this material world. And only when we express our desires, maam eva ye prapadyante, purify, tapo divyam shudhyet brahma saukhyam tu anantam, when we start purifying our life, expressing our desires by doing devotional service that Krishna, I want you, I want to serve you, enough of service of my senses, mind, maya, then her grip on us starts diminishing. This is sum and substance of what we are doing, devotional service. maam eva ye prapadyante, mayam etam tarantit. Krishna, the maya energy, which is energy of the Lord, releases the grip on us. And when the Lord says, I want you, I want you, I want to serve you, enough of service of my senses, mind, maya, then her grip on us starts diminishing. When the grip is completely loosened, then we go back to Krishna.

#### Non-essentials, Schemes, Debt
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CcyCWSidAqo?t=1217
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Keep them busy in non-essentials of life and invent innumerable schemes."
**Summary:** The story continues with the plan to keep people busy with non-essentials, invent schemes to occupy their minds, and tempt them to spend and borrow, leading to a debt cycle.

Everyone is busy. In Mumbai, I generally get up a little early. Still you will see the roads are full. Up and down. Just see from the window. You can't make out it is 3 o'clock. 4 o'clock in the night. Constantly. Trains start at 5 o'clock in the morning, packed. And till 12 o'clock in the night, packed. Keep them busy in non-essentials of life and invent innumerable schemes. And recently the government is proposing that malls, cinema theatres, 24 hours it should be open. Earlier there was a regulation. That people, you know, 24 hours it should be open. All these bars, this thing, there was a regulation. And there was a big debate going on. Now they are liberalizing. 24 hours, malls will be open and people are excited. 3 o'clock you can go and shop in the morning. At least now they'll get up at 3 o'clock. Keep them busy in the non-essentials of life and invent innumerable schemes to occupy their mind. Tempt them to spend, spend, spend and borrow, borrow and borrow. Many times people who are working in the corporate world, they have four, five credit cards or even more sometimes. And why they get it? Because they're earning some salaries or the banks offer because that's the way they make money by giving credit and on that interest. So initially when you get the credit card, you feel good because you can go to the shop, you can buy whatever you want, swipe the card and all that thing. But then it so happens that many times because senses are untamed, uncontrolled, unlimited desires, we get tempted. We also as devotees, many times we get tempted and this today's world, there's a lot of allurements. So invariably people overspend. So every credit card has some limit. You reach that limit and then you have to pay the money till you further use it, for you to use further. So if they have four, five credit cards, they, you know, touch the limits of all the cards. And they get into a debt cycle. The first day of the month when the salary comes, the entire salary practically goes in paying for the credit card bills and things like that.

Although we have great opportunity to hear about the Lord, talk about the Lord, when we meet each other, discuss about the past times of the Lord, but because we are conditioned, because we have natural inclination, Prabhupada says, vacho vegam, manasa vegam, jiva vegam, tongue, constantly, the moment we get an opportunity, we start talking.
