## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-07-28 | SB 3.25.8 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### conditioned life, sense gratification, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=210
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So it's a very scary world, and these days, an average child, seven hours in a week, he spends in front of a television."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the conditioned state of living entities in the material world, highlighting the pervasive influence of sense gratification and the illusion created by marketing and media.

And here it is also mentioned, I have attained after many, many births, by and large in this material world, the living entities are in a conditioned stage, in darkness, overpowered by ignorance. In the spiritual world, everyone is situated in their constitutional position, enlightened, in knowledge, serving the Supreme Lord. So in this material world, after many, many lifetimes, bahunam janmanam ante, labdhuva sudurlabham idam sahubham mama ante, which means after many, many births, labdhuva, we get human birth, and after many, many births, one comes to the understanding, vasudevam sarvam iti, and that is purely because of the mercy of a bona fide spiritual master, who brings us to light, and removes the darkness which is covering us. So here Prabhupada mentions, the disciple or conditioned soul is put into this darkness region of ignorance, therefore is entangled in the material existence of sense gratification. So Prabhupada also mentions there are two sides, one is the conditioned side, and the other side is life of freedom. In conditioned life, we are in ignorance. We are in ignorance, and entangled in the material existence of sense gratification. We are in Kali Yuga, and in present time, we see Kali Yuga is in full force. Sense gratification is very, very rampant. In fact, for most of the people, that's the goal of life. If we see today's newspapers, it's full of advertisements alluring people, with different kinds of allurements, different kinds of deals, different types of, so to say, propositions to gratify the senses. Sometimes full, full pages of ads, like for example, there was one ad, billion day, billion day sale or something, billion sale. Some four, five full pages ad, and different kinds of deals, how you can get a product at half the price, or discounted price, special price. And there are different competitors who are competing, how to get customers, who are competing, how to get customers into their fold, how to increase their business. And billions of dollars are being spent. It's said that top 100 advertisers are spending close to about 15 billion dollars in different kinds of campaigns. And I think I've shared this earlier, these days, marketing and sales experts, in fact they have degrees on that. People go through two years, three years courses, how the mind of a customer can be pulled into buying the product. And there are many, many intelligent people, people from IIMs and top colleges, who sit and strategize different campaigns. In one sense, we don't even know how gullible we are, how helplessly, without our knowledge, we get influenced. In other words, sometimes we use the word brainwashing, although we use it in a wrong context. You know, when people come to spiritual life, come to ISKCON, and they hear philosophy, and then there is some transformation in their heart, so people say it's brainwashing. But actually, it's a fact that the majority of people are getting brainwashed. If you actually have to go by the word brainwashing, brainwashing means you're getting influenced helplessly. You lose your original individuality. So it's a very scary world, and these days, an average child, seven hours in a week, he spends in front of a television. And different kinds of commercials, different kinds of advertising, what all is going on in his head, what all worldview he's forming based on those advertisements which people have strategized to influence our minds. Krishna also says, those who are on this path, their mind, their intelligence, is resolute. So in one sense, it's a very scary world, it's a very precarious condition. We are very very fortunate, we are very very blessed that we are in a very very, so to say, protected environment. We don't even have an option to watch a television. Otherwise, it's very alluring, it's very very tempting. You may even go for seeing something like news or something, but then you get pulled into so many things. And a lot of people, even so-called Karmis say that it is the biggest distraction in the household these days.

#### spiritual practice, freedom, spiritual master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=654
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So in that sense, it's a great fortune that we are practicing spiritual life where five hours, six hours, especially the sadhana time, we can completely focus in remembering the lord."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the fortune of practicing spiritual life, especially the opportunity to focus on remembering the Lord, and highlights the role of the spiritual master in guiding disciples from ignorance to enlightenment.

So in that sense, it's a great fortune that we are practicing spiritual life where five hours, six hours, especially the sadhana time, we can completely focus in remembering the lord, thinking about the lord, chanting his holy name, doing kirtans, hearing about him, bhagavatam class, which is not at all possible in the outside world. At best many people are able to just finish their rounds if they are really genuine and serious in spirituality. Sometimes people start off with 16 rounds but because of circumstances so many commitments, so many responsibilities, they gradually come down eight rounds, four rounds. So this is the life of ignorance, life of bondage, life, conditioned life full of gratification of senses. So Prabhupada says the spiritual master is therefore worshipped as one who delivers the disciple to the other side with the light of knowledge. The word paragram, para also means uspar. I think it comes from that word uspar, uspar leke jana. Uspar means the other side. Gam means to go, to take. The word paragam is very significant. Paragram refers to one who can take the disciple to the other side. So what spiritual master does is conditioned soul to the other side completely engrossed and gratifying the senses, completely ignorant of the purpose of human form of life pulls that disciple and takes him to the other side which is other side of light, other side of enlightenment, other side of knowledge.

---
#### bhakti yoga, process, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=788
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So without even bothering to control our mind and senses, not that sometimes people say should I give up this, should I give up that, should I give up all non-veg, should I give up smoking everything and then I can chant Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, generally we say no."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the challenging Ashtanga Yoga with the merciful Bhakti Yoga process, emphasizing that in Bhakti Yoga, one starts with serving the Lord, even with an untamed mind and senses, and gradually develops detachment from sense gratification.

Now it is very important for us to understand what process we are following, what process Shri Prabhupada has given us. Even in the Bhagavad Gita Krishna talks about Ashtanga Yoga which yama, niyama, asana, control of mind, control of senses, sitting in rigid posture. He tells Arjuna and Arjuna as we know he expresses his inability to follow that process. And that was 5000 years back. So mind is very turbulent, mind is very restless and Krishna himself is recommending to control the mind you go to secluded place sit in a rigid posture yama, niyama, pranayama, asana, dharana and finally one will come to a sage where dhyana one will be able to focus his mind, withdraw from the senses and focus his mind on the Supreme Lord. It could be after many many lifetimes. In Satya Yuga it used to be after many many thousands of years. Lifespan was longer. So out of causeless mercy what in Kali Yuga we are getting the process by Shri Prabhupada or Acharyas are giving us the Bhakti Yoga process where instead of following all the strict rules and regulations we start with the end. End is what even though our mind and senses are completely untamed we are in a conditioned stage we are in ignorance we start with the end that is to serve the Supreme Lord which is our constitutional position Jivera Swaroopahoe Nityera Krishna Das. So without even bothering to control our mind and senses not that sometimes people say should I give up this, should I give up that should I give up all non-veg should I give up smoking everything and then I can chant Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. Generally we say no you can have a desire to give up Hare Krishna Mantra. One day it may so happen that if you are continuing to chant you automatically will develop a distaste and a desire to give it up. That's our process. Same is the case with Bhakti Yoga. It is not that we are all great Yogis in terms of controlling the mind, controlling the senses still we see our senses are many times they overpower us. We still have inclination to gratify our senses. Still our mind is we can understand it is not fully in our control it is untamed. Again it is not a binary logic zero or one. So we start with the end that is to serve the Supreme Lord.

---
#### service, purification, higher taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=976
**Verse References:** BG 3.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We start serving the master of the senses, the Lord with our senses and gradually what happens is our desire to serve our senses, desire to gratify the senses automatically becomes distasteful, we lose taste for it, we develop a higher taste and it automatically is pushed out of the system."
**Summary:** This segment explains how serving the Lord with our senses gradually diminishes the desire for sense gratification, leading to purification and the development of a higher taste, contrasting it with the strict control methods of other yogic practices.

We start serving the master of the senses, the Lord with our senses and gradually what happens is our desire to serve our senses, desire to gratify the senses automatically becomes distasteful, we lose taste for it, we develop a higher taste and it automatically is pushed out of the system. Otherwise the other process involves strict control of the senses, strict control of the mind then Dhyana and finally one reaches a stage where one gets linked or connected with the Supreme Lord again link means what Jivera Swaroopa Hoey Nittyarak and then you have to serve. It is not that just get linked understanding that this is my constitutional position I am servant of Supreme Lord ultimately we have to serve and we are very fortunate very blessed. We are starting with the end, serving the Supreme Lord, which is our constitutional position, and all the lower forms of service automatically gets, so to say, eliminated from our life, gradually. And that is what purification is all about. So, in Vedic times, people, knowing that we have a natural desire for gratifying our senses, people naturally would, knowing that ultimate goal of life is to purify the mind, purify the senses, the whole life was so organized, you know, in one sense, if you could say, the philosophy of life was simple living, high thinking. Yes, we need to eat something. To maintain this body. There is a difference between eat to live, and live to eat. Yes, we cannot completely give up eating. And Krishna also does not forbid that you should not eat, you should eat only some bland food, without any salt. Yajna shishtasana santo mucyante sarvakil bishay. We can eat, we can offer to the Lord, purify, sanctify the foodstuff. And we can eat. And eat the remnants as prasadam. So, the life was so organized, simple living, high thinking.

---
#### simple living, high thinking, purpose of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=1135
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So you meet a person and then, You ask him, where are you going? So he looks at you, looks completely blanked out, and says, I don't know."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the Vedic principle of simple living and high thinking with the modern tendency of simply living without purpose, using the analogy of a passenger on a train who doesn't know his origin, destination, or purpose.

I was talking to someone on this line, in one of the sessions. So, then I asked them, in Vedic times, life was simple living, high thinking. What do you feel in present time, our life is, or people in general? What kind of philosophy are we? What kind of philosophy are we practicing? So, different people gave different answers. One answer was quite interesting. So, one person said, simply living, no thinking. Simple living, high thinking. Present time, it's simply living. Actually, in one sense, without this higher purpose in life, it's simply living. So, a person is living while being a person. A person lives while being a person. So, the ultimate goal is to live your life, and to live also. I'd like to give some examples of that. There is a person sitting next to you, and it's a long journey. Sometimes it so happens, in the journey, we strike a conversation with the person sitting next to us, or in the same compartment. So, you meet a person and then, you meet a person and then, you meet a person and then, You ask him, where are you going? So he looks at you, looks completely blanked out, and says, I don't know. He says, where did you board the train? He looks and says, I don't know. He says, where are you heading? Where will you get down? Again, thinks for a while and says, I don't know. Why are you traveling? Looks, I don't know. If you come across such a person who gives this kind of answer, what will you think about that person? I don't know. What will you think about that person? He is mad. Don't know where he got up. Don't know where he is traveling. Don't know where he is going to get down. Why he is traveling, he doesn't know. Actually, in one sense, this is applicable to all of us in the material world, who are in conditioned stage. Where we boarded the train, in a sense, when we came in this world, where were we? Where we boarded? We don't know. Where we are going to get down, we don't know. What happens after death, we don't know. Why we are traveling? Simply traveling. Simply living. So in that sense, there is some kind of a madness. Pramatta. Nunam pramatta kurute vikarma. Pramatta, this is madness. Again, this madness we don't realize unless we come to life.

#### ignorance, illusion, human form of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=1333
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Big big intellectuals, big big intelligent, it doesn't strike them, where were we before birth? What happens to us after death?"
**Summary:** This segment discusses how ignorance and illusion prevent people from contemplating fundamental questions about life, death, and the purpose of existence, emphasizing the importance of inquiring into these matters in the human form of life.

We don't realize. Big big intellectuals, big big intelligent, it doesn't strike them, where were we before birth? What happens to us after death? These questions are, so to say, completely not ignored. For many people, it is irrelevant. It's not irrelevant for a person who is traveling, where he has to get down, what happens to him after he gets down, it's not irrelevant. Where he boarded the train, the train is not irrelevant. Why he is traveling is not irrelevant. There is a purpose for all travel. Nobody travels purposely. Only a madman travels purposely. We are traveling in this world. We are embarked on this journey. This life is like a journey. What is the purpose? So conditioned state means we are completely oblivious of these fundamental realities of life. And we are not even bothered about it. This is the meaning of Maya or illusion. Tamasi ma jyotir gama. We are in tamas, deep darkness. These questions don't even strike us. Forget about seeking answers to these questions. Athato brahma jagat. Now that you have got human form of life, try to inquire, Who am I? What is the purpose of my existence? So, in the Vedic times, people were automatically, because they were guided by the Vedas, because they were guided by well qualified brahmanas, kings who were administering the kingdom based on their guidance, the whole by and large was simple living. People would keep their lives simple, bare necessities. One time it happened, a person from the United States, they came to India and you know, he was looking out for a yogi. And so he was going around asking people where to find a yogi. So finally he was told about a yogi and he went looking for him, found his address, got his house, and then reached his place and rang the bell. The door opened. He was welcomed inside.

#### detachment, awareness, traveler
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=1471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So the yogi said, so am I."
**Summary:** This segment tells a story about an American seeking a yogi in India, illustrating the Vedic understanding of life as a journey and the importance of detachment from material possessions.

So he introduced himself and as soon as he entered the house, he saw the house is completely empty. There is a bookshelf where all this Ramayana, Mahabharata, Granthas are there. And there is one mat, straw mat on the floor and one lamp on the side. So this person was quite surprised that there is no furniture in the house. So he asked the yogiji, where is your furniture? This person asked. So the yogi asked this man, a foreigner, he says, where is your furniture? He says, my furniture? I am a traveller. So the yogi said, so am I. So in other words, in the Vedic time, people were quite aware that this life is not just like a traveller. He is moving, he knows that wherever he is staying, it is not his permanent place. So, one day he has to move on. Knowing that even if we are on a journey, we don't get totally attached to the compartment in which we are travelling, the seat we are seated in. Journey is going to end, we have to disembark. It is not meaningless for a person who is enlightened to be detached. Automatic Jnana and Vairagya, they go hand in hand. A person who is enlightened, who can see that this life is temporary, will automatically be detached. Just like Pariksit Maharaj, he knew that when the time came, he didn't by hook or crook try to elongate his life, counter the curse which the Brahmana boy had given. He simply accepted that and prepared himself and died a glorious death, remembering the Lord, hearing about the Lord. So, that was the awareness which everyone had. Ultimately, it is about realizing God, developing our relationship with Him, remembering Him, executing our worldly duties, and finally, Anta Narayana Smriti, how we can die remembering the Supreme Lord. So,

#### conditioning, ignorance, spiritual master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9RWXaUYmybY?t=1660
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because of ignorance we gratified the sense of thinking that that will give me happiness and we did that again and again again and again again and again again and again till it became a habit and today even after we have the knowledge that I should not be doing this I should not be still I am helplessly acting at times because of habits because of conditioning that's the meaning of conditioning you've got conditioned to act in a particular way so what we are doing as sadhakas we are breaking that conditioning we have different anarthas different habits wrong habits we are trying to break those habits by cultivating good habits so that's why Prabhupada says in spirituality we have to be patient we have to be determined we have to be realistic sometimes we realize in spite of me chanting in spite of my doing all the things why I am still getting attracted why still I am getting helplessly so to say forced to gratify my senses in fact this is a question which Arjuna also asked where Krishna says it is lust only so the root cause is ignorance we are simply suffering because of ignorance by the instruction of the spiritual master the darkness of ignorance is removed"
**Summary:** This segment explains that the root cause of sense gratification is ignorance, which leads to conditioning and habitual actions. The role of the spiritual master is to remove this darkness of ignorance, enabling disciples to move towards freedom.

stage of life because of ignorance we gratify our senses no doubt many times because of having acted for so many times in so many lifetimes we have got habituated but the root cause is ignorance today still we may gratify the senses it may not be because of ignorance we may realize that it is wrong but we are conditioned to act in that way root cause is ignorance because of ignorance we gratified the sense of thinking that that will give me happiness and we did that again and again again and again again and again again and again till it became a habit and today even after we have the knowledge that I should not be doing this I should not be still I am helplessly acting at times because of habits because of conditioning that's the meaning of conditioning you've got conditioned to act in a particular way so what we are doing as sadhakas we are breaking that conditioning we have different anarthas different habits wrong habits we are trying to break those habits by cultivating good habits so that's why Prabhupada says in spirituality we have to be patient we have to be determined we have to be realistic sometimes we realize in spite of me chanting in spite of my doing all the things why I am still getting attracted why still I am getting helplessly so to say forced to gratify my senses in fact this is a question which Arjuna also asked where Krishna says it is lust only so the root cause is ignorance we are simply suffering because of ignorance by the instruction of the spiritual master the darkness of ignorance is removed

**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the rarity of human birth, especially in Bharatvarsha, and the even rarer fortune of finding a bona fide spiritual master and the process to cross over the ocean of nescience.

and thus the disciple is unable to go to the side of freedom in fact everyone is wanting freedom but they don't know what freedom is see how people with so enthusiasm we celebrate Independence Day even if you see typically on 15th August now this month we're going to have 15th August you'll see if you're going on the roads you'll see people with tricolors selling and people having it on their vehicles and you know celebrating Independence Day so as a conditioned soul we want independence we want freedom but a spiritual master actually takes us to the side of light and the side of freedom the real freedom It is stated in the Bhagavad Gita that after many many births one surrenders to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Similarly, if after many many births one is able to find a bona fide spiritual master and surrender to such a bona fide representative of Krishna, one can be taken to the side of light. See, if we look how fortunate we are, after many many births we get human birth. And we see in human life there are so many people who have no clue of even God. That birth also is rare. Even human birth is rare. And then on top of that, birth in Bharatvarsha, Dharma Bhoomi, that's even more rare. And then even though in India all kinds of people are there. Then some people are little pious. They may not be religious, they are pious. They may be little God-fearing. And then out of them a subset, a small set is religious. They may, religious in the sense they may religiously go to the temple but it's more of a, to assist or to enable their gratification of senses. Lord is used as a means to an end. The end is gratification of senses. And out of such religious people there are people who are, you know, started worshipping Lord, glorifying the Lord. And the initial part I told is, Karmakant is completely interested in gratification of senses. So that way, We are one in the billions of souls who has come to Krishna, who has got a bona fide spiritual master. We've got the process, if we follow, we can cross over this ocean of nascence, come to light. Only thing which is left for us is to just diligently and sincerely follow. Rest everything what is required to cross this ocean of nascence, to come to light is all provided.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-08-11 | SB 3.25.10 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### false ego, maya, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NrhYHjxWvts?t=570
**Verse References:** BG 9.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our idea is to get out of maya without surrender to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a book cover to illustrate the difficulty of separating the soul from the body due to false ego, emphasizing that liberation requires energy, specifically the chanting of the holy names, and surrender to Krishna, as techniques and mental jugglery alone are insufficient to overcome maya.

And in another class we heard that just like two things are joined, for example in this book, this cover of the book, hardboard, is joined to the book. Actually they are two separate things. When the book is made, the pages are bound together and then this hardcover is pasted. So if actually we want to separate the pages, we require some energy, some force. If we want to remove the cover, if we just simply desire to remove, it won't remove because it is bound. All the pages are stitched together. And if we want to segregate, if that's an assignment that we have to separate all the pages, we have to separate the pages. We require some force, some energy. If we can quantify that force, even though it is little energy, you may tear the pages or you may separate it out, it requires some energy. So likewise this soul which is identified with the body because of false ego, if we want to separate, then we require some energy. And that energy comes from, especially in this age of Kali, it is said, harir nama harir nama harir nama eva kevalam. People are trying different means, different methods to be happy. A lot of people have identified that because of attachment, because of desires, we are suffering. And people have also, over the years, prescribed various solutions. How we can be happy, how we can purify the desires, how we can get rid of ego, jealousy, pride, anger. Marketplace is full of such books. But this false ego is so thick. So, just like Devotu here is praying to the Lord, it's not possible for the conditioned soul on its own, through his mind, through his intelligence, through his understanding, free himself from the false ego. No mental jugglery, no mental psyching up, I am not the body, can free one from this false ego. That's why Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita says. Somebody was asking me, is there any technique, especially when we deal with maya, opposite sex. The answer is, actually there is no technique. Because when the maya attacks, the first thing is, we'll forget the technique. And there are many many traps, gross and subtle. Never ever we can be confident of a technique, saying that this technique is applicable, now it's working. Because if it works with situation A, you will confront situation B, which is much more subtler, much more finer, and technique A will not work. Our idea is to get out of maya without surrender to Krishna. Because that's what we have come for. We want solution minus Krishna. We want happiness minus Krishna. We want peace minus Krishna.

---
#### surrender, free will, discrimination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NrhYHjxWvts?t=814
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna, you only can free me. There is no alternative."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that after many lifetimes of struggle, one realizes that only Krishna can free them from maya. He quotes verses emphasizing the rarity of such realization and addresses the apparent contradiction of the Lord being both a well-wisher and the source of forgetfulness, attachment, and detachment.

And the whole process is, we will try different thing, this way, that way, this way, that way, bahunan janmana mante jnanvan mam prapadyant. After many many years of struggle, one will come to a situation, Krishna, you only can free me. There is no alternative. So then finally, bahunan janmana mante jnanvan, a person who is sufficiently knowledgeable, who is sufficiently experienced, who has sufficiently tried this way, that way, mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti, that Krishna, you are the only one, you are the only one who can free me. Samahatma durlabha. That atma who has come to this understanding, this realization, is very rare. That's why Krishna says, Manushya naam sahasreshu kashchitadati siddhayi. There are many many people who are trying to get freed of false ego, but minus Krishna. They have faith in their endeavors minus Krishna. Yatataam api siddhayi. Vedaa, dhe venddhanam, kashchin mam veti, tatvataam, and out of those who have perfected their lives, so called by yogic methods of different kinds of processes, one will actually come to the understanding that Krishna, you are the samam bohm, you are the actual absolute truth. So Devahuti here is very fortunate that she has understood this and says that, I am engaged by your maya, So Prabhupada in the purport mentions that the Lord is there in everyone's heart, sarvasse chaham hrithi sanivisto. Even forgetfulness of Lord, attachment to the body and bodily relation actually also comes from the Lord. It is little perplexing to understand on one side the Lord is said to be surdham sarvabhutanam, He is our well-wisher. On the other side, it is also said that the forgetfulness also is coming from the Lord. Matas smritir jnanam apohunam cha. Apohunam means forgetfulness. This identification with the body and bodily relation is also coming from the Lord. Detachment from the body and bodily relation. It is also coming from the Lord. That's why devotees are pleading or praying to Kapiladeva for giving that detachment. So the controlling point actually is the Lord. Forgetfulness also comes from Him. Remembrance also comes from Him. Attachment also comes from Him. Detachment also comes from Him. So the question here is, does this mean that the Lord discriminates? Someone He is giving attachment to the body and bodily relationship because of which He is suffering. And someone He is blessing with detachment, freedom from false ego because of which He is blissful. Does this mean that the Lord discriminates by engaging one in His devotional service and another in sense gratification?

---
#### free will, desire, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NrhYHjxWvts?t=1031
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is very merciful. Actually, when we want to enjoy, then the Lord gives him facility to enjoy."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that the Lord does not discriminate, but rather facilitates the desires of the living entity based on their free will. He explains that when one desires to enjoy separately from the Lord, the Lord provides the means through maya, which covers the real consciousness. He then shares a story about Prabhupada addressing devotees' concerns about past sinful activities and the time it will take to purify themselves, emphasizing Krishna's readiness to purify us instantly, but the obstacle is our own deep-rooted desires to enjoy independently of Krishna.

If that were true, it would be incongruity on the part of Supreme Lord, but that is not the actual fact. from the worksheet, et cetera derives by Charities. So what's the reason why somebody is engaged in devotional service with thick false ego, and somebody is apparently free from false ego, engaged in the service of the Lord. The answer is, the way we have exercised or exercising our free will. As soon as the living entity forgets his constitutional position of eternal servitorship of the Lordver, and wants to exist kicking wouldn't mind Ayya. can give him life. He wants to enjoy himself by sense gratification. So the Lord is very merciful. Actually, when we want to enjoy, then the Lord gives him facility to enjoy. Because he wants to enjoy, bhoga vansha kare, to facilitate that enjoyment through another energy of the Lord, maya, which is agent of the Lord, covers the real consciousness. The capture by maya is the consciousness of false identification with the body and bodily attachment, and attachment for the person. These are the possessions of the body. These are the activities of maya. And since maya is also an agent of the Lord, it is indirectly the action of the Lord. And Krishna says, mamma maya durat teya. It's very, very difficult to surmount. Yesterday somebody sent us a video on WhatsApp. Shri Prabhupada is asked, some devotees are asking, Shri Prabhupada, we have done sinful activities for many, many lifetimes. We are so impure, we have false ego. You know, basically, some insubstances. How many lifetimes will it take for us to purify? The way Devotee is praying and the way we are discussing now, false ego is very thick, and because of, we all have enjoying tendencies. Where is the hope for us? How many lifetimes will it take? Suddenly we see something immediately, enjoying tendency, crops up. So Prabhupada says, one minute. Then he, in one sense he says, have you not read Gita? What does Krishna say in the Gita? Mam eva prapadyante maya metam tarantite. And then he says, masucha. Surrender unto me. Mam ekam sharanam rajaham tam sarvapapebhyo mokshesham masucha. So from Krishna's point of view, for us, for him to purify us of the false ego, like Krishna is the controlling point, for him to free us from the false ego, to give us detachment, he is ready. It takes no time for him. In a moment he can bestow us with that freedom. In a moment he can free us from the false ego. Sometime we think that... So the problem is, it is our desires. Deep-rooted desires to enjoy minus Krishna. Hope that I will be able to enjoy minus Krishna. And we are not willing to give up that hope. We are not willing to give up that desire.

#### surrender, chanting, kali yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NrhYHjxWvts?t=1274
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Chant anyway."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the need to give up all other plans for happiness and surrender to Krishna, despite the difficulty in relinquishing subtle desires. He highlights the Lord's unlimited power and renunciation, making purification easy for Him, but the challenge lies in our willingness to surrender. He then discusses the process of devotional service, including anartha-nivritti, and the importance of chanting the holy names in Kali Yuga, even without taste or attachment, quoting Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's prayer about lacking taste for chanting.

That's why Krishna says, sarva-dharman parityajya. We have so many so-called hopes, so many plans to be happy, to be enjoyed. And Krishna is saying, parityajya, give up. But we are not willing to give up. Grossly we may give up, but subtly still we have desire to be happy minus Krishna. So here, as we all know, one of the qualities of the Lord is, He is all-powerful. He is all-renounced. Unlimited strength, unlimited fame, unlimited renunciation. So detachment, freedom from false ego, for us, it's not very difficult from the Lord's perspective. In a moment He can purify us. But are we ready to surrender to the Lord? So therefore, we have to follow a process. We are conditioned soul. Even the hope for enjoyment, the desires won't suddenly vanish. Anartha-nivritti takes time. Bhajan-kriya, anartha-nivritti, tato, asakti, nishtha, ruchi, all that takes time. So as I mentioned, we require some energy, some strength, even to remove this binding. So that strength in Kali Yuga is coming from the chanting of the holy names. But unfortunately, even Chaitanya Mahaprabhu prays durdaiva midrisham nanu raga I have no attachment, I have no taste for chanting. The medicine is there, but we have no inclination to take the medicine. So there is one very nice article. Many times when we are chanting, our minds are wandering, many kinds of thoughts are coming. Sometimes the chanting is mechanical. Sometimes we wonder, I am chanting for so many years, nothing is happening. Sometimes it can be very uninspiring to see that I have so many anarthas, so much lusty thoughts are there, and in spite of I trying my best, still I am getting defeated again and again. So this is a very nice article which says that your mind is wandering all over the universe when you chant. Otherwise, many times it doesn't wander. Only during chanting time it wanders more all over the universe. Chant anyway. Your mind is wandering to the past and future when you chant. Chant anyway. You are not able to concentrate on Krishna's name while you chant. Chant anyway. You have no taste for chanting. Chant anyway. Because initially, just like Prabhupada gives example, jaundice patient, for him sugar candy is very bitter because he is a diseased state. So the holy name also is very bitter for a conditioned soul. There is no taste. It is mechanical. So chant anyway. Medicine may not be very pleasing, but we have to take the medicine. You have lusty desires. Chant anyway. You are making offenses while chanting. Chant anyway. You are not praying to Krishna to help you chant better. Chant anyway. You often chant late at night. You do not pray very early. Chant anyway. So basically it describes you know our present state.

#### chanting, purification, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NrhYHjxWvts?t=1737
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord's holy name, when we chant, when we inundate our consciousness, with the Lord's holy name, it's like putting fresh water inside the glass."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the analogy of muddy water, explaining that while meditation can provide temporary peace, the impurities remain. He emphasizes that chanting the Lord's holy name is like adding fresh water to the glass, washing away the impurities and ultimately purifying the consciousness. He quotes a verse describing how hearing Bhagavatam and chanting the holy names destroys all misconceptions and attachments in the heart.

So as a result all the impurities, all the disturbances slowly start settling down. And a person actually starts experiencing clarity of thought, peace of mind, freedom from anxieties. And nowhere Krishna is in picture. Krishna is completely out of picture and you can experience. But point to remember, point to, although there is a momentary experience of peace and happiness, but the dust is still there. Dirt is still there. It's settled down. False ego is still there. Dirt is still there. It's settled down. False ego is still there. Dirt is still there. It's settled down. I am the body is still there. I am the body is still there. Bodily relation, attachment is still there. In fact false ego increases more. I am the body. I am a peaceful man. So we give a demonstration. If you put a spoon and stir it again, again the dust is back. Dust has not gone out. It's still there. It's settled down. The water is apparently pure on the top. So if we want freedom from false ego by mental gymnastics, it will not help. The Lord's holy name, when we chant, when we inundate our consciousness, with the Lord's holy name, it's like putting fresh water inside the glass. So finally we conclude this saying that, how do we make the water pure? If we keep it undisturbed, the water, the dust will settle down, but it's still there. If you want to make water pure, you have to add fresh water. And all the dirty water flows out, and finally the glass is left with pure water. Naśṭa-prāyaśva-vadreṣu nityaṁ bhāgavata-saṁhaṁ. nityaṁ bhāgavata-saṁhaṁ. When we hear bhāgavatam, chant the holy names, when we hear all this, puṇya-śravaṇa-kīrtanā, hṛda-antastho abhadrāṇi, all the dirty things which is there in the heart, all misconceptions, all wrong identifications, all attachments, vidhunoti surat satām, all that gets completely destroyed. So conditioned soul does not want to chant, but that's the process. When we chant, when we hear bhāgavatam, when we hear puṇya-śravaṇa-kīrtanā, hear Kṛṣṇa kathā, that actually has the potency, has the power to go inside our ears and dispel all darkness, all illusion, all misgivings, drive away māyā from our lives. No penance is equal to it, and nothing is as potent or powerful as the holy name.

**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating the importance of chanting the holy names, even when it feels monotonous or mechanical, as it is the means to get strength from the Lord, be freed from false ego and anarthas, and ultimately experience real sat, chit, and ananda. praying to Lord Kapila, and how do we get freed from false ego? By taking shelter of the Lord, by pleading to the Lord, by getting strength from the Lord, jnana bala kriya, and when we chant the holy names of the Lord, we get that strength, right now we don't have taste for the holy name, it is very monotonous, mechanical, but acharyas are saying, chant anyway, take the medicine anyway, it is bitter, take it anyway, and a day will come when we will get cured, and we will start relishing the Lord's holy name, and a day will come when we will get freed from all this false ego, all this anarthas, all this misgivings, and then, as a soul, standing in its true glory, we will experience real sat, chit, and ananda, then the bliss what we will be experiencing, it says, brahma bhuta prasannatma, we will feel free from all kinds of hankering, lamentation, for today, as of today, it is all idealistic for us, but Krishna is saying, that state is possible, prasannatma, completely joyful, there is no hankering, there is no lamentation, so, we have a wonderful process, if we follow this, the goal what we can achieve, the state of consciousness what we can achieve is something very glorious, and that should motivate us to follow the process sincerely, and take this medicine wholeheartedly.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-09-13 | SB 3.25.12 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### bodily concept of life, sense gratification, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=570
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone in this world is under the clutches of this bodily concept of life."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how the bodily concept of life leads to sense gratification, frustration, anger, and ultimately, entanglement in karma.

The modes which are influencing us based on the conception of life are, so to say, triggering different kinds of thoughts, different kinds of impulses, and we helplessly are acting based on those impulses. So when we are in the bodily concept of life, or material concept of life, naturally the thoughts would be of, I am the body, and since our natural desire is to seek happiness, anandamayo bhyasat, I am the body, so the only definition of pleasure is to seek happiness through our senses, or sense gratification. And since the bottom line, the common definition of pleasure is to seek happiness through our senses, or sense gratification, the common denominator in all our lives is we all seek happiness. Everyone is engaged in different activities. Ultimately, it's to seek happiness. So naturally, in the bodily concept of life, sense gratification becomes rampant. That becomes the goal of life. Eat, drink, and be merry. That becomes the philosophy of life. And invariably, the soul comes under the clutches of karma. More sense gratification, krodha, and when sense gratification is obstructed or is not fulfilled, because you cannot get whatever you want, so then krodha comes, frustration comes, anger comes, krodha, dbhavati, sammoha, sammoha, smriti, vibrama, buddhinasho. So one gets into that. The connective spiral from the bodily concept, sense gratification, sense gratification, not necessarily what you desire will be met. So frustration comes, one gets caught in this whirlpool of frustration, anger, unlimited desires. Another symptom of this bodily concept of life is we will hanker for name, fame, adoration, puja, putishtha, labha. Our thoughts would trigger us to act in ways whereby we will seek name, fame, and adoration. Actually, if you see this description, everyone in this world is under the clutches of this bodily concept of life. That's why everyone wants to be famous, wants to gratify the senses. The whole advancement is how we can titillate our senses more and more in much more gorgeous ways, much more elaborate ways, much more sophisticated ways.

---
#### self-realization, transcendental life, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=754
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The desire to be liberated from the conditional life is the same as the desire to be liberated from the conditional life."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the bodily concept of life with the spiritual concept, emphasizing the soul's entanglement in material existence and the desire for liberation.

And here we have an understanding which Devotee is explaining who is interested in self-realization, in spiritual concept of life, in transcendental realization. The symptom of this transcendentalist or those who are interested in self-realization is they understand that we are the soul, Aham Brahmasmi. And this soul is now entangled in this material existence, caught in this cycle of birth and death, Punarapi janma, punarapi marna. Just like a fish outside water, no matter what we give to that fish, no matter what arrangements we make for the fish to be happy, it cannot be happy, because that is not its original habitat. That's not what it's meant to be. So we as living beings, spiritual sparks, mamayi vamsho jiva loke, are meant to be in association of Krishna, serving Krishna in the spiritual world. That is our constitutional position. And here in this material world, engaged in sense gratification, we will get frustrated. And bahunam janmanam ante, after many many lifetimes, one will realize, although many people are getting frustrated, but they don't have a clue as to what is the solution. That's why Prabhupada says, punas punas charvita charvanam, chewing the chewed. Although there is no taste, still they have no clue of a higher existence. So here Prabhupada says, one who does not care for these transcendental necessities of life, it's a necessity for the soul, for the soul to be happy, for us to be blissful, for us to get what we are actually looking for, that is happiness. The soul has to be happy. That is happiness. The soul has a necessity of transcendental life. But unfortunately, if one simply engages like an animal in eating, sleeping, fearing, and mating, he has spoiled his life. Lord Kapila is very much satisfied by his mother's questions because the answer stimulates one's desire for liberation from the conditional life of material existence. Now I was telling about how perfect questions, perfect answers, we had no clue that we are in material existence. We were also busy making plans for sense gratification. If we look back, just a few years back, before we came in touch with Prabhupada and his books, we also very much had our own plans of life, no clue of anything higher in life. So what happens is, when we hear such conversation, the mind is filled with the desire to be liberated from the conditional life. The desire to be liberated from the conditional life is the same as the desire to be liberated from the conditional life.

#### Krishna consciousness, desire, Prabhupada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=941
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we read the pure words, Prabhupada's purports, then the desire got triggered."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how hearing pure words, especially Prabhupada's purports, triggers the desire for liberation and how such questions are called apavarga vardhanam.

Sometimes people ask, Swamiji, how did you come to Krishna consciousness? How did you join the temple? Sometimes, deep within we take the credit, yeah, we were smart enough to understand and figure out, yes, this is the goal of life. I read book and I understood that this is the purpose of life. Actually, that's not the case. When we read the pure words, Prabhupada's purports, then the desire got triggered. Stimulates one's desire for liberation from conditional life of material existence. Such questions are called apavarga vardhanam. So Prabhupada also says this material existence is called pavarga. And spiritual existence, spiritual life is called apavarga vardhanam. Vardhana means to increase. Apavarga. That which nullifies pavarga.

#### material existence, illusion, time
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=1004
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although we have advanced, although we have come up with gadgets which can potentially save us time, we are much more entangled, much more busier."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of pavarga, highlighting the hard work, fear, and ultimately death that characterize material existence, contrasting it with the potential for time-saving advancements that paradoxically lead to greater entanglement.

Pavarga is pa means parishrama. Everyone in this material world is working very very hard. In fact, compared to what man was working 10 years back, or 20 years back, or 30 years back, we see people are working much harder. Although on one side, we have modern means of communication, transportation, and so much advancement. What used to take months, now you can do it in days. What used to take days, now you can do it in minutes. I remember when I was in college, so we had to, you know, parents expect that you call. Those days, you know, STD call. And STD was not so easily available. So I remember, if I have to make a call to my parents back home, just, they were staying in a place just 200 kilometers from Kurukshetra. So I would travel about four and a half kilometers to make that STD call, STD booth. And I would go early in the morning before 6 a.m. Just to make the call would get up early. Anyone knows why? Why before 6 a.m.? Because there was pulse, you know. In the morning time, every eight seconds is one pulse. So that means, if one pulse is one rupee, so eight seconds is equal to, between 6 a.m. to 9 a.m., it was half. Sixteen seconds is equal to one pulse. Sorry, four seconds is one pulse. So it becomes double the rate. And the peak time, that is after 9 a.m. when the office is open, two seconds is equal to one pulse. And when you go all the way before 6 o'clock, the line will be there, people standing, because everyone wants to make call before 6 o'clock. And once the call gets started, your heartbeat also starts, because every pulse, tuk, tuk, it's like counter is on. You can see that thing, two rupees, four rupees, five rupees, haan, thika hai, thika hai, achcha, ho gaya, haan, ho gaya, five rupees, six rupees. And today you see the liberty, people don't even realize that they're making an international call. You don't have to travel to make a call. So in one sense, we have saved that time. I mean, if you look really like, this is just one crude example, like that, there's so many things. If you have to print something, a book, I remember in 92, 93, if you have to just make a color print of the book, just to see how the colors will come like, it was a very elaborate process. Just to take a print and to see how the colors would, it would cost 4,000 rupees in those days. They actually would make the plates. They actually would do an offset printing in a smaller, you know printing a smaller, this thing and, then you can use, yes be very confident this is what I'm going to get. And for DTP, practically they is to cut the tracing paper, and paste it on the sheet and then make the plates. Very tedious process. If you have to print a book, means it's like a big project. But now, we see you know, copy paste, Control-C, Coral draw, coral draw to this thing and you can immediately get the PDF file, go to the printer, it gets printed and you want to print 40 books, in next 4 hours you can print a book. So on one side we see that how we have advanced which is meant to save time, our time and that time we can use in much better ways but unfortunately in today this is the meaning of Maya or illusion, although we have advanced, although we have come up with gadgets which can potentially save us time, we are much more entangled, much more busier.

#### devotional activities, spiritual interest, eternal
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=1479
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who have actual spiritual interest are called sat or devotees, satamprasangat."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts pavarga with apavarga vardhana, explaining that devotional and spiritual activities nullify pavarga, and that those with genuine spiritual interest are called sat, or devotees.

So, dharmasya hi apavargasya, or apavarga vardhana means these devotional activities or spiritual activities, or transcendental realization, which Devotee is interested in, is apavarga vardhana. It actually nullifies pavarga. Sometimes people wonder, devotees, they don't work very hard. Prabhupada gives example many times. But still, the temples are there. Krishna has blessed them with all the resources. Prabhupada says there are so many vehicles going out daily for book distribution. Not necessarily devotees are working hard, like the people outside, but still Krishna is supplying. Those who have actual spiritual interest are called sat or devotees, satamprasangat. Sat means that which exists eternally. And asat means that which is not eternal. Unless one is situated on the spiritual platform, he is not sat, he is asat. One time there was a person, he used to sell hats, hat seller. So he was having a bundle of hats and was passing through a village. He walked for quite some time and then he became tired. It was quite hot, summer days. And then he decided, let me take rest underneath a tree. And as soon as he sat down, because he was tired, he fell asleep. And all the hats what he had in a bundle was kept next to him. And suddenly the monkeys from the tree, they came down. And each one of them picked up a hat and climbed the tree. So when he woke up, first thing he saw, where is the bundle of hats? So he was very surprised. Where is the bundle of hats? And then he looks up, all the monkeys all over the tree sat and sitting with the hats on their heads. So then this person somehow remembered this monkey is the imitate. So he started scratching his head. So the monkeys also started scratching his head. And after scratching his head for a while, he took the hat, which was he also had a hat. He put it on the floor and all the monkeys after scratching that through all the hat on the floor. So all the hats came down. It was raining hats. So he was very happy. Quickly, he picked up the hands and went off. And then he came back home and he was very happy that, you know, how he cheated the monkeys and finally got his hats back. He was very happy with that. And he would tell all his family members and years rolled by his grandson. You know, when he was young, he would tell this story very proudly. This is what I did and see this how the monkeys actually took all the hats and how I actually tricked them and I got all the hats back.

---
#### imitation, devotees, grandfather
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=1672

So grandson also would hear and you know, would say our grandfather was so intelligent. So one day it so happened now it was a time this grandson became a hat seller. And he also actually was passing through a village one day he also became tired to cut the story start. He also slept. He also had a bundle of had monkeys again came monkeys also took the hat and monkeys went and sat on the top of the tree. So when he got up, he was disappointed but fortunately he remembered what had happened to his grandfather what the grandfather had told him. So deep within he was quite happy. So he did like this. So the monkeys also did like this. Then he threw his hat down. None of the monkeys threw his hat down. And one of the big monkeys came down with the hat on his head and slapped him. And he said, Do you think only you have grandfather?

#### devotees, imitation, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_PXHREvqCw8?t=1774
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anu Karan means you imitate the activities of great devotees and Anu Charan means to follow in the footsteps."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the difference between Anu Karan (blind imitation) and Anu Charan (following in the footsteps), emphasizing the importance of genuinely following great devotees for liberation.

So the reason why I thought I'll share this story is here. We have examples of great devotees like devotee, who is interested in transcendental realization and is asking wonderful questions from Kapiladev. Kapiladev many times as devotees like we hear from Prabhupada that how Raghunath Das Kuswami was eating every alternate day so Prabhupada mentions about two things one is Anu Karan and one is Anu Charan. Anu Charan means following in the footsteps if we blindly imitate these great devotees Prabhupada also explained that how many times followers of Lord Shiva they drink ganja and things like that saying that Lord Shiva also took or many devotees of Krishna might dance Ras Leela because Krishna also danced so Anu Karan means you imitate the activities of great devotees and Anu Charan means to follow in the footsteps so here we are very fortunate we are learning what kind of questions these great devotees are asking what kind of answers great devotees are giving in response to such questions and when we relish these kind of conversations and try to follow in the footsteps not imitate try to follow in the footsteps of these great devotees our path for liberation also opens what is called here is Apavarga Vardhanam that also happens in our case so we are very very blessed very very fortunate that we have an opportunity to hear this kind of conversations daily it's big luxury for many devotees, but many devotees who are even practicing Krishna Consciousness back home not necessarily they'll get time to hear Bhagava or read Prabhupada Books daily. Some people are very sincere at best they are able to complete their 16 rounds even that sometimes becomes a challenge. So all of us we get a very good opportunity everyday every morning become we the whole morning program. And... So we should realize our good fortune, be grateful to Prabhupāda for giving us this, so to say, good fortune, and steadily progress on this path. We'll stop here.
