## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-10-12 | SB 3.25.15 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### discomfort, conditioned life, human form of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=599
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But in human form of life we have the ability and opportunity to find solution to the actual problem, actual discomfort of the material body."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the temporary nature of material solutions to discomfort and emphasizes the unique opportunity in human life to address the fundamental problem of repeated birth and death.

Temporary comforts. But unfortunately, in conditioned life, completely bewildered, we are completely lost sight of the real problems, the real discomforts. We all are facing some discomforts and we are trying to find solutions, but those solutions are of temporary discomforts, which Krishna says will come and go, like the changing seasons, summer and winter. But the real discomfort for which nobody is anxious about or is trying to find a solution, which Krishna says is a subject of knowledge. Because life after life we are caught up in the cycle of birth and death and taking material bodies we have to face all the discomforts for which we are finding solutions. But in human form of life we have the ability and opportunity to find solution to the actual problem, actual discomfort of the material body.

---
#### suffering, materialism, conditional life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=676
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So life has become hell. Swamiji, I wonder, what's the use of living like this?"
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of an influential man who, despite his wealth and status, suffers immensely from physical pain, illustrating that material possessions do not guarantee happiness in conditional life.

That the living entity which is implicated in the measures of these modes conditioned and taking birth again and again. There was one gentleman who helped us in the initial days of Akshaya Patra. He was a very influential man. There is a committee at the national level which is chaired by the finance minister. And he was part of that committee and many financial policies are reviewed more so with respect to taxation in that committee. And since he was very very influential and was very impressed by the Akshaya Patra thing, he would invariably speak to some industrialist, influential people. And he helped us a lot. He would have such, since he was in an influential position, he could call anybody, any industrialist, he could even call let's say Mr Mukesh Ambani. Because financial policies which the government makes impacts all businesses. So they will have some relations. So he introduced some industrialists to us in the initial days. And somehow, you know, we got busy and he got busy and some of the connection for some time got lost. And then one day, he calls a devotee and says, Swamiji, when you come to Delhi, please try to visit my place. And the voice was very very gloomy, sad. So the devotees tried to inquire, how are you, long time we haven't met, surely we will definitely come to your place when we visit Delhi. And it so happened that next few days when the devotees were visiting Delhi, they visited his place. Mind you, we are talking about not an ordinary person. Sometimes, you know, when ordinary person faces some challenges in life, he is not able to find solution because he doesn't have money, cannot afford treatment, already he is in a financial mess. Here we have an example of a person who is very influential among the, you know, top brass in the country, makes national level additions. So the devotees visited his house, the door was opened, some servant came and they invited the devotees inside. And this person then comes in, limping, very very slowly. And then the devotees, he sits in the drawing room on the sofa. And some of the devotees who visited him could not, you know, make out, just few years back he was so much charged up, so much enthusiastic, full of enthusiasm. And here he is completely weak, frail, old. So then, in course of the discussion, he says, Swamiji, he holds the hand and says, Swamiji, you all are very close to Krishna. You all pray so much. When you pray, please pray that I die very soon. So it was like, never expected these kind of words from a person. So he said, what happened? Why are you saying so? He says, Swamiji, what to say, in the last few years my life has become a hell. He is living in a big palatial house in Delhi. Servants are there, everything is there, all amenities are there. So my life has become a hell. So he says, somehow I have this severe backache. Tried all kinds of treatments, continuously it pains. Can't sleep, can't sit. Any time of the day it pains. And now I am living for last few years on painkillers. And now a time has come, even the painkillers have become ineffective. Because the body gets used to. That's why many times the doctors prescribe a higher dose. And because of these painkillers which I have been taking every day, 8, 10, 12 tablets, all the organs are now, because of side effects, after all it's chemical, it's not, the body can't take it for a short while. So he says, because of that I have gastric problems, all kinds of digestion problems. So life has become hell. Swamiji, I wonder, what's the use of living like this? So these are the examples of how sometimes we get a feeling that yes, if somebody is rich, he can, he is that way blessed, he can afford treatment, he can get admitted in the best of hospitals, he can have, you know. But conditional life doesn't spare anybody. Prakriti sthani karshati. It's an illusion which makes us, no doubt, money has a place, money can afford you it. Just like they say, money can buy medicines, but cannot buy health. Some sayings are there, money can buy home, money can buy house, but not home. So there's a difference between the two. Lot of people living in palatial houses, but there is no homeliness. There is no cordial relationship between the family members. Fights are going on. So this is the meaning of conditional life, which irrespective of how much bank balance you have, what status you have in the society, you have to go through.

---
#### liberation, Krishna consciousness, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1079
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the same cheta, the consciousness is transferred to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna, or when one becomes Krishna conscious, he is on the path of liberation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna invites us to find a solution in the Gita, emphasizing that by attaching our consciousness to the Supreme, we can open the doors to liberation and transcend the struggles of conditional life.

So Krishna is inviting us in the Gita, giving us a solution how we can, as living entity, we are his amshas, we have a glorious position to enjoy in association of the Lord, to enjoy transcendental pleasure, real pleasure, and unfortunately, clouded by ignorance, we have settled down with such inglorious position. Deha apta kalatra adishu atma sainishu asat swapi. We are so complacent, thinking that I am safe because we have a sainiya around us. Deha apta, good body, apta, relatives, friends, family members, acquaintances, well-wishers. Kalatra adishu atma sainishu asat swapi. This atma sainiya is asat. So here in this verse is saying, instead of being complacent, living a conditional life, but when the same consciousness is attached to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, one is situated in the consciousness of liberation. So one is conditional life, which is full of struggle, and when we start attaching our consciousness, which is attached now to material pleasure, material sense objects, when we start attaching that same consciousness to the Supreme, then doors for liberation opens. Slowly the conditional life, the conditioning which is there, the modes which are influencing us, pulling us down, starts losing their grip on the conditioned soul. When the same cheta, the consciousness is transferred to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna, or when one becomes Krishna conscious, he is on the path of liberation.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, maya, devotional practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1201
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna consciousness, susukkum kartum avyayam, means when a person is Krishna conscious, dependent on Krishna, remembering Krishna, then he will be, one of the symptoms would be he will be happy."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the transition phase of practicing Krishna consciousness, highlighting the purification of consciousness and the experience of liberation from maya, emphasizing that a symptom of Krishna consciousness is happiness and dependence on Krishna.

So we are now in a transition phase, actually if we analyze. We are not fully Krishna conscious. We are not like Prabhupada, sent person, always acting for Krishna's pleasure, always remembering Krishna. And to some extent we realize that we are in maya. By Prabhupada's mercy we have started practicing principles of Krishna consciousness. Neither we are fully Krishna conscious, nor we are completely conditioned. Slowly by practicing these principles, we are elevating from the lower modes, and our consciousness is getting purified. The more and more we get purified, the more and more we will feel liberated. More and more we will feel free from the clutches of maya, clutches of material existence. That's why it is said, a devotee, how do we know if we are also struggling, we are not happy, full of anxiety, then that means we are in clutches of maya. Krishna consciousness, susukkum kartum avyayam, means when a person is Krishna conscious, dependent on Krishna, remembering Krishna, then he will be, one of the symptoms would be he will be happy. Whatever is happening, Krishna is there, Krishna is taking care, Krishna is taking me through this. This reminds me, I think one time we have shared this example.

---
#### humility, lessons, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1310
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This pencil I am giving to you with five important lessons. And I am going to share those lessons with you. Every time you see this pencil, remember those lessons. And if you follow these lessons in your life, you will come out to be a very, very successful person."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to narrate a story about a grandmother giving her grandson a pencil with five important lessons for a successful life.

I will repeat it again, it is very relevant to what we are discussing today. One time a young boy goes to his grandmother's place. She was staying in some small town during his summer vacations. Unfortunately grandmother can't spend much time with him. She was probably a writer and she was busy throughout the day writing some manuscripts, some books. Always scribbling something on the paper. So every time when he would clamor for attention, she would say, surely, surely, I will definitely spend time. And days rolled by. Time came when he had to now leave. So the grandmother also was feeling very sorry that she could not spend quality time with grandson who had come on vacation. But to compensate for whatever she could not do for the grandson, he says, definitely I should give him some memorable gift. And she called the grandson and says, I am going to give you a very, very memorable gift. This will stay with you for the rest of your life, this important gift. So the grandson got excited. Maybe she is going to give me a bag of chocolates. Something which everyone has his own idea of what is important. So grandmother all along had been scribbling on paper with a pencil. She says, this pencil which is very dear to me, I have been using for many, many years now. I want to gift it to you. So the grandson was, what will I do with this pencil? Dirty looking pencil. She says, this pencil I am giving to you with five important lessons. And I am going to share those lessons with you. Every time you see this pencil, remember those lessons. And if you follow these lessons in your life, you will come out to be a very, very successful person. The child became inquisitive and she started telling. The first lesson we can learn from the pencil is that although the pencil writes many, many things, like she said, the manuscripts, this is eventually going to get transformed into a book. It will be sold in huge numbers. Although the pencil writes many, many things, but at no point, pencil takes the credit. It always remembers that it is in the hand of a writer.

---
#### humility, instrument, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas a devotee should always remember, I am just a nimitta, I am just an instrument. The Lord is the real doer."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the first lesson from the pencil story: to remember that we are instruments of the Lord and to give credit to Him for our abilities and accomplishments, rather than taking pride in our actions.

So we as devotees also many times will do wonderful things. Krishna says, I supply whatever devotee lacks, provide what the devotee lacks, and maintain what the devotee has. And sometimes it happens seamlessly. You require, in one sense, transcendental vision. It requires some element of humility to realize this. Otherwise, ahankara vimudatma kartaham iti manyate. Whole world is very convinced that, yes, I am doing it. And it makes sense because they can literally see that I spoke, I wrote, I did it, I convinced him. So vimudatma kartaham iti manyate. It is very rampant, everyone thinking, just like, you know, after the match happens, then somebody is given man of the match or somebody is given some prize and the interview happens. No, I thought, yes, I should bat very patiently, I should not hit, I should be there for the rest of the whole thing. All the people who bat, they think like this only. And then he will glorify, I thought like this, and then I thought, no, no, I should. So everyone is convinced to the core that, yes, I did it. Because of me, it happened. So whereas a devotee should always remember, I am just a nimitta, I am just an instrument. The Lord is the real doer. Which is not some psyching up, it is actually a reality. All the abilities what we have, all the strength we have, all the skills we have, is coming from the Lord. Trillion brain cells we have, all of them are interacting coherently, producing some thoughts. Are we conscious control? Are we in grip of things? No. If something wonderful happens, we take the credit. Whereas Krishna consciousness means giving credit to the Lord. Consciousness of the Lord. He says when one is transferred to the Supreme Person, when consciousness is transferred, where always one is conscious that the Lord is the doer, the Lord is the giver, everything is, I am His Amsa, I am just Kshetra Gna, Kshetra belongs to Him, He is the proprietor. Bhukta Ram Yajna Tapasam Sarva Loka Maheswam. So this is first thing.

---
#### surrender, faith, correction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1632
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is in control. Krishna is protecting me. He is sharpening me. He is taking me through this thing so that I can come out as better devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the second and third lessons from the pencil story: to accept difficult situations as the Lord's way of sharpening us and to be humble enough to accept correction.

Second thing, she says, the pencil allows from time to time, sharpened. So we take the pencil, put it in the sharpener and sharpen it. The pencil doesn't rebel. Likewise, the Lord also, when we are practicing the principles of Krishna consciousness, Lord will take us through some situations which might be difficult for us to comprehend. Why me? Why it has to happen to me? I have surrendered to the Lord. I have done so much sacrifice. Krishna says He will protect me. What is happening to me? So devotee, one of the principles of surrender is, He is Avasya Rakshabhi Krishna. He has that faith. Krishna is in control. Krishna is protecting me. He is sharpening me. He is taking me through this thing so that I can come out as better devotee. So that Anarthanevrtti happens. Third thing, she says, the pencil always allows, by chance the pencil writes something, it allows an eraser to erase what it has written and correct. So we as devotees also should be humble enough to be corrected.

---
#### humility, introspection, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1724
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "No matter what the size, shape, color of the pencil is, what is really important, what actually is important is what is inside, not the externals."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the third lesson, emphasizing the importance of humility and introspection, and introduces the fourth lesson from the pencil story: that inner qualities are more important than external appearances.

Many times we are very obstinate. We are very, very rigid about our opinions. We are very sure many times that what I am thinking or what I am doing is right thing. But if we little bit introspect, when we were young, we were thinking in one way. When we grew up, our thinking changed. What we thought as very, very important, now if we look back, we realize how immature I was, how inexperienced I was, how I was thinking like that. We had some goals in life. We were very, so to say, passionate about achieving those goals. If I look back and say this was, what was I thinking? So as we grow, as we become more experienced, as we become more mature, we will realize that even my thinking, my opinions were wrong. And sometimes we are not humble enough to accept that. We are very rigid about our thinking, rigid about our opinion. And even if someone corrects it, even if someone points out some lacuna, some shortcoming in our perception, in our thinking, we are not willing to accept. Fourth thing the pencil teaches, no doubt these days we get pencils of different color, shape, size. If you see, you know, go to a particular stationery shop, you will see different kinds of colors of pencils, shape, sizes. Chhota beam pencil, you will have little Krishna pencil. No matter what the size, shape, color of the pencil is, what is really important, what actually is important is what is inside, not the externals. So likewise, as devotees are in the conditional life, we are very externally focused. All related to the body, complexion, beauty, bank balance. But what really matters is what is inside. Jesus said, what profit the man if he wins the whole world but loses his own soul.

---
#### soul, responsibility, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1866
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So likewise, as devotees, we have to be very cautious, careful, discriminate and act responsibly."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of the soul over the body and introduces the fifth lesson from the pencil story: to act responsibly, as everything leaves a mark, and to be mindful of the laws of karma.

What is really important is the soul. Prabhupada says when the soul leaves his body, this wonderful body which we have, you know, cosmetics, all beauty saloons, kept it so nicely, worked, you know, keep it healthy, it's burnt, it's buried, it's cremated. What is really important is the soul. And you see how conditional life means we are completely oblivious of the soul, completely busy taking care of the body. Body pleasures. And the last thing we can learn from the pencil is that it always leaves a mark. So likewise, as devotees, we have to be very cautious, careful, discriminate and act responsibly. Conditional life means acting recklessly. I don't care. They openly say, just do it. It will be seen. These days, you know, we all have heard newspapers, molestations, rapes, it's on the rise. Sometimes I wonder, you know, they know the consequences. But that madness which sets in for that momentary pleasure, a person is willing to do some, such a heinous thing, knowing very well that he will be caught. And life will be messed up. So everything leaves a mark. We know the stringent laws of nature, the laws of karma. So devotees, we are all very, we should be very, very, act responsibly, act with discretion, with caution. We should be careful we don't commit vaishnava pradha. We don't commit offenses. Constantly some kind of introspection, constantly some kind of checking should happen.

---
#### introspection, improvement, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hDKYhs-kxfw?t=1999
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So if we are always in the mode of introspecting, correcting ourselves, improving ourselves, improving internally, improving our consciousness, remembering Krishna, then in fact we can be so busy that we have no time to point out others' mistakes."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the importance of living responsibly, introspecting, correcting oneself, and improving internally, drawing a parallel with internal auditors who point out areas for improvement, and reiterating that transferring consciousness to the Supreme Personality of Godhead opens the path for liberation.

So when we lead a life responsibly, trying our best, sincerely following the principles, wherever pointed out that there is some shortcoming in my behavior, in my attitude, I try to, rather than justifying it, I try to correct myself. I try to improve myself. Yesterday we were having some discussion regarding, some related to Akshaya Patra. So in the discussion it came up that, you know, generally every company, every organization has some. And this gentleman, he is a very experienced person. He comes from Mumbai with me. He has been, you know, head of many big companies, Colgate. So he was telling Swamiji, when these internal auditors come, and they point out some mistake, we all try, we don't take them seriously. Many times we think that they are pointing faults. He says, actually I have realized over many, many years, I have learned the hard way, actually they are our friends. They are pointing out our improvement areas. They are pointing out the weaknesses which we have to improve on. But people treat them like enemies. People treat them that they have come to point mistakes. It all depends on how you perceive. He says, if we, if we treat them like they have come, they are our friends, they are well-wishers, they are coming and correcting us, they are pointing out what is lacking in us, in our operations, in our procedures, in our processes. And if we take their suggestions seriously and improve, then we can do very well. So same is the case as devotees when we, somebody points out some shortcoming. Rather than justifying, conditional life means we justify. He says, no, it's not true. How can you say that? And then we in turn point your mistake, the other person's mistake. What right do you have to speak like this? Have you seen your, this thing? So, if we are always in the mode of introspecting, correcting ourselves, improving ourselves, improving internally, improving our consciousness, remembering Krishna, then in fact we can be so busy that we have no time to point out others' mistakes. We have no time for fault finding. Many times when we have fault finding, we are very externally focused, we are not internally focused. No doubt as authorities or some as leaders, sometimes it is a, it's a thankless job to point out faults, as a service to the other devotee. But we all like this service very much. Right from the beginning stage, we want to do this service. So if with this spirit, if we live, practice the principles of Krishna consciousness, here it says, when it is transferred to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, then one becomes Krishna conscious and his path for liberation opens up. So let's stop here.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2016-12-01 | SB 3.25.22 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### renunciation, devotional service, spiritual progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, to genuinely progress in spiritual life, it is very important that we cultivate spiritual knowledge and renunciation."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of renunciation and detachment from worldly connections for spiritual progress, emphasizing the development of knowledge and detachment through devotional service.

As soon as he takes his birth in the world, a person who has so many responsibilities and obligations to the public, to the demigods, to the great sages, to the general living beings, to his parents, to his family forefathers, and to many others. When he gives up all such obligations for the sake of the service of the Supreme Lord, he is not punished for such renunciation of obligation. But if for sense gratification a person renounces all such obligations, he is punished by the law of God. So here it is said, such a sadhu engages in staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation. Now, which sadhu is being referred to? The previous verse talks about what are the symptoms of a sadhu. The symptoms of a sadhu are that he is tolerant, merciful, and friendly to all living entities. He has no enemies, he is peaceful, he abides by the scriptures, and all his characteristics are sublime. So what does this sadhu do with such symptoms? For the sake of the service of the Lord, he renounces all other connections, such as family relationships and friendly acquaintances within the world. Actually, to genuinely progress in spiritual life, it is very important that we cultivate spiritual knowledge and renunciation.

---
#### sacrifice, devotion, family obligations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=360
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is supreme and I want to serve him, even at the cost of not serving them."
**Summary:** This segment explores the conflict between family obligations and devotion to Krishna, highlighting the difficulty others may have in understanding a devotee's choice to prioritize serving the Lord, even at the expense of familial duties.

So here it is said, there is a very famous verse, which all of us have heard many, many times, Prabhupada quoting. It's there in the Gita also. devarshi bhutaptaninam pitranam nakinkaram nayam rinicharajan. We all are indebted. Prabhupada is right. He is right here. That when a person takes birth, a person has so many responsibilities and obligations. devarshi, to the devas, to the rishis, the sages. bhutapta, to the public, to the general living entities, ninanam, to the parents, to the family members, the forefathers. But it is said that if one, ya sarvatma sharanam sharanyam, if one surrenders to Mukunda, who is worthy of surrender, who is our supreme father, who is our eternal father, then although one might not be able to fulfill those obligations, Prabhupada writes in the book, but he is not punished for such renunciations. But he is not punished for such renunciations. That's why many times when devotees join, it is very difficult for the family members, his friends, other members to understand how he can be so callous, how he can be so reckless. Sometimes he may be the only son in the family. And it is beyond their comprehension. It defies all logic, all rational. And that's why people even use the word, he's been brainwashed. Because from their perspective, it doesn't make any sense. A child who has been raised by the parents, they've invested love, affection, money, got him educated. Now is the time when he should be taking care of them, staying with them. And he says, no, Krishna is supreme and I want to serve him. Even at the cost of not serving them. So many times devotees take this bold step.

---
#### sacrifice, national duty, spiritual duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=526
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although they may have back home, young wife, young children, maybe only one daughter, but the person is willing to take the risk for the sake of the country."
**Summary:** The speaker draws a parallel between soldiers sacrificing their family interests for the sake of their country and devotees sacrificing worldly attachments for the service of the Lord, highlighting the societal respect given to national duty while spiritual duty is often misunderstood.

Even in this material world, we have examples of people who sacrifice their family interest for higher cause. Many times we hear these days, the India-Pakistan border, there's a lot of tension. And we hear sometimes some jawans getting killed. Or recently there was an attack on the army camp. And some jawans got killed. And then we hear in newspaper, how the body was brought back to the village. And the family members are crying and all that. Now the person who is there in the army, posted in the battlefield in the border areas, he knows that he's risking his life for the country. Constantly there is tension and terrorism. The opportunity of meeting death for the sake of the country is much higher. Although death can come anywhere, anytime, to anyone. But it is dangerous. Because day in and day out you are exposed to an environment where casualties can happen. Now a person knowing that very well, a person agrees or rather could be, somebody may say it's a job, after all it's a livelihood. But for some people, for them it's there in their blood, I want to serve my motherland. For the sake of my nation, I am willing to lay down my life. And such people willingly go and take all risk to protect their motherland, to protect their country, to fight for their nation. Although they may have back home, young wife, young children, maybe only one daughter. But the person is willing to take the risk for the sake of the country. And if a person gets killed, nobody says that he was callous. Nobody says that he was reckless, he left his family. He should have taken care of the family because people understand that fighting for the country is a higher cause. In fact, they say that he is a martyr. He sacrificed his life. He celebrated. His funeral happens with military honors. People salute him for his sacrifice. So here we see an example of the interest of the nation is higher than the interest of the family. And a person who, for the sake of country's interest, sacrificed the interest of the family, of his children, he is respected. He has not said that he has abnegated his responsibilities.

---
#### sacrifice, varnashrama, spiritual knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=725
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Just like the country requires engineers, the country requires doctors, architects, military men, army men, police people, all likewise, country also requires people who are well versed in the scriptures."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the lack of understanding in today's world regarding sacrifice for the service of the Lord, comparing it to the societal appreciation for those who serve the country, and highlighting the importance of Brahmanas in guiding society towards spiritual goals within the varnashrama system.

So same way, unfortunately, just like these people can appreciate, but in today's world, not many people have that understanding of sacrificing for the service of the Lord. That's why this is difficult for people to comprehend. Just like the country requires engineers, the country requires doctors, architects, military men, army men, police people, all likewise, country also requires people who are well versed in the scriptures. Prabhupada gives example. This is the duty of the Brahmanas who, because they are learned, because they are enlightened, because they are educated, they can guide the administrators, the Kshatriyas, in ruling the kingdom. That's why in Varanashrama system, even though everyone was doing their respective duties, may not be directly engaged in devotional service. Avaishyas may be just doing their business, or workers may just be doing some work, but because the overall society was guided by the Brahmanas, everyone, by doing their respective duties, prescribed duties, was moving gradually in the right direction. And that is towards the ultimate goal of life, which is to surrender to Krishna, God-realization or self-realization. So that's why, you see, this renunciation was celebrated in Vedic times. Unfortunately, because this knowledge is not prevalent in present-day society, these concepts are not understood by people in general. Therefore, renunciation does not have much value. So here it is said, for the sake of the Lord, He renounces all His family connections, such as family relationships and friendly acquaintances within the world.

---
#### maya, attachment, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=870
**Verse References:** BG 7.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Again, we can start developing attachment. Again, start getting involved. Again, getting, so to say, meddling with all the affairs in the family. And then slowly, one gets pulled back."
**Summary:** This segment cautions against subtle traps of maya within devotional service, where devotees may become lenient and re-develop attachments, emphasizing the importance of adhering to Prabhupada's instructions in context and avoiding entanglement in worldly affairs.

Many times, when we come to devotional service also, we have to be a little alert. Krishna says in the Gita, deviesha gunamayi mamamaya dhuratyaya. And as we all have heard, maya has many traps, gross and subtle. Sometimes a person with all good intentions might come to serve the Lord, and might even so-called cut off his relationships with a single-minded focus, serve the Lord. But slowly, because Prabhupada has made an institution, because people from all walks of life, as Prabhupada started the movement, different devotees from different walks of life, from different backgrounds, from different cultures, from different countries, embraced Krishna consciousness, Prabhupada was at times lenient. Again, we cannot take His instructions in isolation from case to case. Many times, when we read Prabhupada's books, we can take the instructions of Prabhupada out of context. But Prabhupada being an Acharya, according to time, place and circumstances, sometimes devotees may feel that apparently the instructions are contradictory. It's not. According to time, place and circumstances, keeping the principles intact, the application of the principles was done. So, sometimes we can become more lenient, and again, we can start developing attachment. Again, start getting involved. Again, getting, so to say, meddling with all the affairs in the family. And then slowly, one gets pulled back. It has happened in the past, it can happen to anyone, if we are not careful.

---
#### surrender, obligation, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1021
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When he gives up all such obligation for the sake of the service of the Lord, he is not punished for the renunciation of obligation."
**Summary:** This segment reiterates the principle that surrendering to Krishna allows one to be free from the karmic consequences of renouncing worldly obligations, emphasizing the importance of faith in the Lord's protection.

So here it is said, as soon as he takes birth, he has obligation. One may even rationalize that, yes, I have obligation. They're dependent on me. There's no one to take care. But then here it is said, when he gives up all such obligation for the sake of the service of the Lord, he is not punished for the renunciation of obligation. And, six principles of surrender, one of the principle is, avashyarakshivya krishna, evishvasa palana. One should have the strong conviction that the Lord will protect. So here it is said, such a sadhu engages in staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation.

---
#### devotional service, deviation, practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1070
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sometimes we can make small deviation, and then, we get accustomed to that deviation, and then it becomes little more, and a person can go completely tangent."
**Summary:** This segment warns against small deviations in devotional service that can lead to larger departures from the path, emphasizing the need for sincere practice and cultivation of knowledge and detachment.

Sometimes we can make small deviation, and then, we get accustomed to that deviation, and then it becomes little more, and a person can go completely tangent. So even this renunciation, jnana, vairagya, one has to very sincerely practice the principles, cultivate jnana and vairagya. One time in a particular city, there came one archer. He was very expert in shooting. So in a big maidan, all the people gathered, and he was supposed to exhibit his archery skills. So, he would take a bow, the arrow, and then, right in front there was a board with all the circles, typically in archery you have, and he started shooting. And all the arrows would go and hit the bull's eye. And then just to show his expertise, he would sometime turn towards left and shoot, again he'll go and hit the center. There was one person in the gathering, in the crowd, who whenever he would see that the arrow has gone and hit the target, he would say, nothing great about it. Nothing great about it. And all others in the public were completely amazed. And they were cheering him and, you know, big round of applause. And he was also quite enthused when people are cheering up, you know, and there's a big rasa there. So then, sometimes he would turn his back and again shoot, again it'll hit, people will again cheer him up. So he was, that was going on. But every time, this particular person said, nothing great about it. So what? Nothing great about it. So what?

---
#### practice, habit, skill
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1200
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "After all, it's just a practice. You have practiced it many, many times. Now it has become, you know, it's become a habitual thing to shoot."
**Summary:** The story of the archer and the milkman illustrates that repeated actions, even seemingly impressive skills, become habitual through practice, emphasizing the importance of consistent effort in any endeavor.

And, unfortunately, the person who was shooting, he was hearing all this. Again he would try to show something, at least, this time he should not say nothing great about it. He closed his eyes and shot. And again, nothing great about it. So what? So, you know, he got a little worked up. After all display of his skills, he came to this man and he said, why are you saying nothing great about it? Nothing great about it. Why are you saying that? So he said, so what? After all, it's just a practice. You have practiced it many, many times. Now it has become, you know, it's become a habitual thing to shoot. So what? So this person said, no, I mean, he was trying to rationalize that he's talented. He has such, such a skill. So this person said, you come to my place. So he was basically a milkman. And, he invited to his house, made him sit. His wife came, he boiled the milk on the stove. And generally milkman, you know, when they pour the milk on a glass, typically some devotees also do in the ashram. So from the top, he poured the milk right from the can, the milk poured in the small little glass. And then from the glass back to the pan, again from the pan to the glass. And this time increased the distance. Again, the milk will fall. And again, he'll put in the pan. Again, it'll fall. And this person was surprised. And not one drop of milk fell from the pan out of the glass. So he asked him, can you do this? This archer who was shooting the arrows, can you do it? So he said, it's difficult. So how do you do it? He said, just to practice. Just practice. Just practice. Right from my childhood I've been doing it. Now I can close my eyes and do it. And then I actually took a pan and did it.

#### habit, practice, devotional principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1344
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whenever we act, do something, it's like a small thread in that big rope. And then if we repeatedly do, it becomes a habit, habitual response."
**Summary:** This segment explains how repeated actions become ingrained habits, comparing them to threads forming a rope, and emphasizes the importance of following devotional principles to instill renunciation and detachment.

So, many times we don't realize the acts which we do repeatedly, it becomes ingrained into our mind-body system and it becomes a habitual response. And something which we do time and again, we are not, they say, just like habit, it's like small little threads in the rope. You know, they're small threads which are intertwined and then finally it goes and makes a big, huge, thick rope. So like that, whenever we act, do something, it's like a small thread in that big rope. And then if we repeatedly do, it becomes a habit, habitual response. So why Prabhupada has given us all these principles, the principles of devotional service, the four regulative principles, some element of tapasya, the fasting on some particular days, this all so that renunciation, vairagya, gets instilled in our mind-body system.

---
#### devotional service, fault-finding, habit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1426
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though there may be hundred good things to be happy about, a person would automatically, by default, pushed by his nature, will find fault."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of consistent devotional service and warns against the habit of fault-finding, explaining how repeated complaining can become ingrained as a negative nature.

Sometimes we may not even understand why we are supposed to do, why every day get up in the morning. That's why it says, such a sadhu engages in staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation. Prabhupada uses the word suyantritau. Suyantritau means no discrepancy. Just like a watch, the machine, the machine removes very systematically, no discrepancy. So if one practices, and sometimes we have to be also careful. Some devotees, you know, by nature we have the habit of finding faults or complaining. And then we practice complaining. Every time we find something, we complain, or we find a fault. And quite surprisingly, it becomes the nature. Because we have complained so often, we have found faults so often, fault finding becomes a nature. It gets ingrained. Even though there may be hundred good things to be happy about, a person would automatically, by default, pushed by his nature, will find fault.

---
#### sadhana bhakti, chanting, perseverance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1510
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But all these things are having cumulative effect."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the cumulative effect of consistent sadhana bhakti, such as chanting and attending prayers, even when immediate results are not apparent, and encourages staunch devotional service with faith in the guru's instructions and the holy name.

That's why actions done repeatedly, Prabhupada said, day after day, we are chanting Hare Krishna Mantra. Day after day, we are singing Guru Puja. Day after day, we are attending Mangala Aarti and all the prayers. Day after day. Day after day, we are going and bowing down in front of the deities. Day after day, we are taking Charanamrit. Many times, seen in isolation, one might wonder why nothing is happening. But all these things are having cumulative effect. We all have heard about a person trying to hit the rock. One time he hit, there was no crack in the rock. Second time, third time, fourth time, fifth time, tenth time, fifteenth time, nothing was happening to the rock. So a person said, what's the use? I am hitting so often, nothing is happening to the rock. But the twenty-first time he hit, the rock split into half. So even our heart, actually is very very hard-hearted, steel-framed. Prabhupada says, if we chant the holy names of the Lord, if tears don't fly from our eyes, if our hairs don't stand. So we are right now, because of so many anarthas, so much of conditioning, so many past bad habits. We are doing Sadhana Bhakti. Sadhana Bhakti. Day after day, day after day, chanting, day after day, you know, hearing Bhagavatam class and all those things. It might appear that it is not having an effect. It might appear that not much change is happening. But if we do it staunchly, it says, staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation, with the faith in Guru's instructions, in the holy name.

---
#### chanting, offenses, complacency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1635
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Then one should really understand that he is in a dangerous condition."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of attentive chanting and warns against complacency regarding offenses during chanting, emphasizing that a lack of concern about distractions and sleepiness during chanting indicates a dangerous spiritual condition.

Sometimes we might even chant offensively, a lot of offenses. It is said that when we chant, it is it is sometimes okay, not okay in the sense we get distracted, we start talking to others, or on a particular day, we may not be very attentive while chanting. Or we may end up talking to others. Because conditioned soul has lot of anarthas, attachment for other things. Holy name is not very pleasing. It is understandable in a conditioned stage. But if a devotee, day after day, he enjoys talking, enjoys, you know, gossiping, enjoys discussing something while chanting, and if the chanting is not okay, if while chanting we are feeling sleepy, and he is not bothered about it, that's a very big dangerous position. One day, maybe not feeling well, feeling sleepy, not feeling okay, consciousness is down, it's understandable. We end up doing prajalpa, we end up talking, we end up discussing something, we end up laughing, we end up doing something while chanting. Some days, chanting may not be okay. Some day, our hearing will not be okay, we are fully sleepy during class. One day, two days, it's okay. But if day after day, and we are losing interest, and day after day, while chanting, we are doing all this talking and discussion, and we are completely complacent about it, it doesn't bother us. It doesn't, what am I doing? This, this question doesn't come in our mind. Then one should really understand that he is in a dangerous condition.

---
#### detachment, rasa, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=1754
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we don't get rasa in chanting, hearing, bhagavatam, association of devotees, in devotional activities, if we don't get rasa there, we are conditioned soul, we want rasa."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of finding rasa in devotional activities to avoid being drawn back to worldly attachments, and encourages diligent adherence to devotional principles for progressive detachment and attachment to the Lord.

And here it is said that such a devotee who has severed his relationship with the family members, with the friends, with acquaintances, because there is some rasa there. If we don't get rasa in chanting, hearing, bhagavatam, association of devotees, in devotional activities, if we don't get rasa there, we are conditioned soul, we want rasa. So where do we find rasa? Back in that relationship. Prabhupada uses harsh word. He says sometimes a worm in the stool comes out of the stool and again goes back. Because it's conditioned and finds that is much more comfortable. So it's very instructive. For the sake of the Lord, he renounces all other connections such as family relationships and friendly acquaintances within the world. It's applicable to all of us. For the sake of the service of the Lord, that's what we have done. And we have to be very careful. We have to diligently follow the principles and do what such a sadhu is supposed to do. And what is that? Engage in staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation. If we do that, then day after day, suyantrita, we are following all the principles, then that jnana, vairagya, the detachment will be progressive in that state. And a time will come when we will completely be detached from all the material things and be fully attached to the Lord. And that is what we should be aspiring for. Stop here.

---

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2017-01-12 | SB 3.25.27 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### happiness, self-realization, modes of material nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=630
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, living entity is intrinsically happy."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of Shankaracharya looking for his lost key under a street light to illustrate how we often seek happiness in the external world instead of looking within.

So we should remember our good fortune. So here it is said that when one engages in these nine devotional activities, so our, as devotees, our endeavor should be, every moment should be engaged in these nine activities, one of these nine activities. Either we are hearing, or we are chanting, or we are remembering, or we are serving, or we are praying. And when one does that, he will be free from the influence of the modes of material nature. Modes of material nature is like an imprisonment. Yesterday we talked about the bodily concept of life is an imprisonment. The modes of material nature force us to get into, come down from the spiritual platform to the material platform. When one starts thinking in terms of I and mine, body and the associated bodily relatives. One time, Shankaracharya, he was looking for a key outside his house, in the street, underneath a street light. He had lost the key. And he was desperately trying to find out where. And one of his disciples came back to the ashram and saw Shankaracharya himself, kneeling and looking for the key, underneath the street light. So naturally, he also said, let me also, he came to know that he has lost the key. So he also started searching for the key. And it went on for some time, 10 minutes, 15 minutes. And then the disciple, you know, asked Shankaracharya, do you remember? Do you remember where you lost the key? You know, it is natural when we, this question comes in mind. Do you remember vaguely, where do you remember? So Shankaracharya, yeah, yeah, yeah, I remember. It was somewhere inside, next to the bed. So the disciple was a little amused. He says, you lost the key inside, near the bed. And why are you searching here? So he said, inside, because the oil got over in the lamp, it became very dark. So I thought, let me search the key outside, where there is light. So the disciple, you know, was really, how can, I mean, he didn't say, but it was apparent from his looks. How can he be so foolish? Lost the key inside and is looking for the key outside, in the light. So Shankaracharya just smiled at, when the disciple was, you know, wonderstruck with this answer. So Shankaracharya smiled. And he said, isn't this the case with all of us? This is a very important lesson. Actually, living entity is intrinsically happy. Sat, chit, and ananda. He doesn't need anything else. He is amsa of the Lord, in association with the Lord. He is completely happy. Joyful. Experiencing blissful relationship, reciprocal relationship with the Lord. That is the quality of the jiva. Actually, all of us, we are not happy with the jiva. We are looking for happiness in the external world through sense gratification and so many other elaborate arrangements for that happiness. Hoping against hope that I will be happy by this arrangement, by that arrangement, by this achievement, by this accomplishment. And that's why the goalposts keep changing. That's why nobody is happy. Krishna in the Gita says, third chapter, when one is self-realized, when he is illumined from within, when he is self-satisfied, he is happy within, he has no duty to perform.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, service, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=920
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna consciousness, which is nothing but the jiva realizing that I am amsha of the Lord, I am eternally servant of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how to achieve intrinsic happiness by freeing oneself from the modes of material nature and engaging in the service of the Supreme Lord, developing Krishna consciousness and realizing one's eternal relationship with Krishna.

And how do we find that happiness? How do we be situated in that intrinsic state of sat, chit, and ananda? When we are free from the modes of material nature and when we are serving the Supreme Lord. Thus, by engaging in the service of the, not by engaging in the service of modes of material nature, by developing Krishna consciousness, which is nothing but the jiva realizing that I am amsha of the Lord, I am eternally servant of the Lord, jiveyera swaroopahoye nityera krishnadasa, knowledge in renunciation. Naturally, when we are free from the modes of material nature, we are serving the Supreme Lord. Naturally, when one develops this knowledge that I am amsha of the Lord, my duty is to serve the Lord, naturally, jnana will result in vairagya. Automatically, we will not serve false masters. When we realize my true master is Krishna, I am amsha of him, my duty is to serve him, automatically, we will not want to serve false masters. In which the mind is always fixed in devotional service, so I have brought in the Supreme Lord in my life. Compared to the Supreme person I have regarded, one achieves my association in this very life. So we all are in this business, this business of reviving our, achieving our lost happiness. And how the process is very simple, we have to revive our Krishna consciousness.

---
#### happiness, modes of material nature, self-reflection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=1018
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The answer is, it's within you."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that true happiness comes from within and is not dependent on external circumstances, urging devotees to look inward and address their own anarthas rather than blaming others for their unhappiness.

So Prabhupāda says a devotee, if he has an opportunity to serve, be in association of devotees, and take little prasādam, he should be happy. If we are not happy, that means we are under the influence of the modes. No doubt, it's a fact that we cannot suddenly get out of the modes of material nature. We are conditioned for many, many lifetimes. It will take. We are in the process of purification. But, otherwise, for our happiness, we don't need elaborate arrangements, we don't need some big things to happen, we don't need big accomplishments, we don't need name, fame, adoration, position. Intensically, that happiness is there. And if we are not experiencing, we should understand, sometimes it becomes obvious that I have anarthas, I am lusty. Give factor in that. Sometimes we blame somebody else outside. I am not happy, not because of him, not because of this, not because of that. I am happy because I have anarthas. I am still in bodily concept. Still, I am overpowered by modes of material nature. Then, in one sense, it is empowering because otherwise, we blame others and empower others. They are controlling me. Because of them, I am unhappy. Because of this, I am not experiencing happiness. The answer is, it's within you.

---
#### devotion, sacrifice, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=1109
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Simply love Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the story of Srila Prabhupada's visit to London despite his deteriorating health, highlighting his deep compassion for his disciples and his unwavering dedication to spreading Krishna consciousness, culminating in his simple yet profound message: "Simply love Krishna."

Shila Prabhupada once, this was in 1977, he was in Vrindavan. And as we know, his health was not quite good. And he was taking some kind of Ayurvedic treatment. And his health was generally deteriorating. It was, you know, from day to day, it was becoming back to worse. So, the Kaviraj who was treating him had advised him complete rest. He should not travel because even till 1977, he was travelling. So, I don't remember the month, there was a Rathayatra in London. And by then, there were hundreds and thousands of devotees. And each one of them wanting to have darshan of Prabhupada, have a glimpse of Prabhupada, hear from Prabhupada. So, they had organized a very, very big Rathayatra festival in London. And all the devotees there, the leaders there, they were calling Prabhupada. They were writing letters and calling Prabhupada that, Please, Prabhupada, come. It will be very inspiring for devotees to have you here. The whole festival will be surcharged when you are here. And here, Kaviraj is saying that you should not be moving, you should not be travelling. So, one side, there was a pressure for them to come. And the secretaries and the senior devotees who were attending Prabhupada, they were always some kind of a pushback. No, no, no, Prabhupada cannot come. Prabhupada's health is not okay. Prabhupada is advised not to travel. And the devotees, please, please, and then one of the devotees called up and said, Even if he cannot speak, let him just come. Just by seeing him, just by having a glimpse of him, the devotees will be enthused. Let him just come. Let him not speak. Let him not travel. Just let him come. So, when Prabhupada came to know that devotees are so eagerly waiting for him to come, he decides, okay, I'll go. Imagine, Kaviraj is saying, you cannot come. You cannot travel. You should take bed rest. He says, I'll travel. So, Prabhupada went to London. This was the last time. And from the airport, Prabhupada was taken to Bhaktivedanta Manor in the car. Finally, from the car, because Prabhupada could not walk, so a palanquin was brought, some kind of a palanquin. Prabhupada was shifted from the car into the palanquin. And the palanquin was brought into the temple hall. And as soon as the palanquin was brought, Prabhupada came in. And as usual, Govindam Adipurusham song was played. So, Prabhupada was very weak. You know, with some support, he was sitting on the palanquin, hand folded. And while the Govindam Adipurusham song was going on, profusely tears were flowing from his eyes. And the devotees who were carrying the palanquin, looking back, continuously tears were gushing through the eyes of Prabhupada, even his kurta became completely wet. And that song got over, so Prabhupada indicated again. Again that song was played. And Prabhupada is just completely absorbed, thinking, seeing the deities having darshan. And tears are flowing from his eyes. So the condition was that Prabhupada would not speak. That was agreed upon. Prabhupada would just come, so that devotees can have darshan and, you know, they are enthused. That was the condition. But nevertheless, some devotee brought the mike. Maybe Prabhupada wants to speak something. So Prabhupada got the mike. The mike was brought before his mouth, hands folded. Tears flowing from his eyes. And he says, simply love Krishna. Again, tears are flowing and the mike was taken off. These are three words Prabhupada spoke, all the way he went to London. And simply love Krishna.

---
#### realization, hearing, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=1393
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Hearing or associating with devotees is the most important function for self-realization."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of hearing from realized souls and associating with devotees, highlighting that purity and realization are more impactful than eloquent words in touching others' hearts and conquering the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

Now, it is said that when a pure devotee speaks, just like here it is said, if one meekly and submissively hears about the activities of the Lord from a realized soul, we can also speak big, big lectures. No transformation will happen. A pure devotee speaks just few words. It evokes transcendental emotions. I spoke the same pastime last Sunday in Mumbai. And one person, after the session, he said, I am very touched. This particular, this thing, that is, purity is the force. So here it says, when one hears about the activities of the Lord from a realized soul, he will be able to conquer the Supreme Personality of Godhead, who is unconquerable by any other process. Hearing or associating with devotees is the most important function for self-realization. Hearing and associating with devotees from a realized soul. Everyone can say, depend on Krishna, remember Krishna. We should serve Krishna. But what is going on in our heart? What is our realization? To that extent, we will be able, Krishna shakti vina nahi tare pravartanat. What's going on in our heart? So that's why for a devotee, it's not just preaching alone. Janma sarthaka kari karopakaar. To the extent we are realized, to the extent we have imbibed those instructions, what's going on in our heart, to that extent we will be able to touch others. And for that, we don't require bombastic words, we don't require eloquent speaking, we require realizations. If one meekly and submissively hears about the activities of the Lord from a realized soul, he will be able to conquer the Supreme Personality of Godhead, who is unconquerable by any other process. Hearing or associating with devotees is the most important function for self-realization.

---
#### sadhana, alertness, sincerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=1546
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But if it is happening day after day, there is something really wrong. And we have to do something."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of being cautious and sincere during morning sadhana, such as chanting and hearing Bhagavatam, and taking corrective action if one's practice becomes lax or inattentive.

That's why we should be very, very cautious when we do our morning sadhana, chanting, reading or hearing bhagavatam. One day it's okay, maybe we are tired, we are sleepy. One day it's okay, maybe we couldn't chant properly, the mind is agitated. But if it is happening day after day, day after day, and it doesn't affect me, I am not bothered about it. Sometimes devotees get into that, maybe day after day, or chanting time, feeling sleepy and then talking to someone. One should do something. One day it's okay, maybe we are not in control of our body, the body is tired or whatever it is. But if it is happening day after day, there is something really wrong. And we have to do something. Yena, tena, prakara, rena. Somehow or the other. Even if we have to do some material gymnastics. In terms of washing our face, or do something, or breathing, or whatever it is. Because these are the foundational building blocks. Hearing from a realized soul, hearing the holy name of the Lord, hearing the Lord who has come in the form of His holy name. And unless we have this spiritual strength, here it is said, modes of material nature are waiting. And we have observed, it is on a day to day basis, one day chanting is not okay, the whole day you will see your mind is powerful. It is like the medicine. So devotees have to be very conscientious, very alert, very sincere. Sometimes very serious.

---
#### purification, Krishna consciousness, determination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gcPh25wiDlE?t=1667
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In this very lifetime we can purify."
**Summary:** This segment encourages devotees to understand the overall purpose of their practice, which is purification and reviving Krishna consciousness, and to be determined and sincere in their efforts to achieve this goal in this very lifetime, especially considering the rapidly changing and increasingly challenging world.

We also, because we have been asked to do it, we do it. We should understand, many times, now I realize, when I was studying, did my engineering, computer science. We were just simply studying the books. There was no overall picture. You know, you are going depth of networking and computer organization. One simple question I was asked in an interview. How does a computer work? I fumbled giving the answer. Because sometimes you lose the overall purpose of what we are doing. We are here in the process of purification. Anarthanivritti. To revive our lost Krishna consciousness. And when we revive that, Krishna says, one achieves my association in this very life. For I am the Supreme. And it is not like we have to wait for many, many lifetimes. If we are sincere, if we are conscientious, if we are very determined, we are always in the mode of how I can do better, how I can do better, how I can improve. In this very lifetime we can purify. In another lecture Prabhupada says, you know, we know the world today is very different from what it was 20 years back. And the world 20 years back is very different from what it was 40 years back. The world is rapidly changing. The changes which has happened in the last 10 years has not happened in the last century. And the changes are not very pleasant as far as practice of Krishna consciousness is concerned. Imagine if we don't finish this process in this life and we again take birth with the Reliance Jio, unlimited browsing, streaming live videos. There is no guarantee that we will, the Maya's attraction, the allurements will be much, much higher. So that way we should be very inspired that this very lifetime Krishna is saying here, my association in this very lifetime for I am the Supreme Personality. You have got it. So we will stop here.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2017-02-16 | SB 3.25.31-32 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Lust, False Ego, Sense Gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CxvZZv_G-DE?t=538
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lusty intelligence makes the spirit soul acquire a false ego and thus identify with the mind and the body, and thus the spirit soul becomes addicted to sense enjoyment, thinking this as the true enjoyment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how lust, untamed senses, and a lusty intelligence lead to false ego and addiction to sense gratification, which is mistaken for true happiness.

Lust starts with the senses, untamed mind, untamed senses. The senses in the mind, when they start indulging in sense gratification, even the intelligence department becomes lusty. Then Prabhupada mentioned a very interesting thing: lusty intelligence, which is the next-door neighbor. Intelligence is the next-door neighbor of the spirit soul. Lusty intelligence makes the spirit soul acquire a false ego and thus identify with the mind and the body, and thus the spirit soul becomes addicted to sense enjoyment, thinking this as the true enjoyment. Real enjoyment is senses engaged in the service of the Lord, transcendental service of the Lord, but when intelligence becomes rusty, the lusty intelligence forces the spirit soul to acquire false ego, identify with the senses and the mind, thinking that the goal of life, my happiness, is this happiness, and becomes addicted to this happiness.

---
#### Krishna Consciousness, False Ego, Maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CxvZZv_G-DE?t=626
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "False ego makes one think that sense gratification is true happiness, and because we want to become happy, anandamayo bhiyasat, let me gratify, let me maximize."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Krishna consciousness as a means to tackle the enemy of lust and false ego, highlighting how false ego leads to the illusion that sense gratification is true happiness, driving the desire for maximization.

Because of false ego, I am the body, I am the enjoyer. So what we are trying to do here in Krishna consciousness is to tackle this enemy, which Krishna is saying, our senses and mind are contaminated with lust, our intelligence has become lusty, and because of lusty intelligence, untamed mind, untamed senses, we have false ego. I am the body, anything in connection with the body, aham ameti, I and mine, family, relatives, friends, children, house, property, assets, and makes the soul think it as true enjoyment. So this is the scam. We want to enjoy, nothing wrong about it, that's the constitutional position of the soul. The senses and mind start engaging in sense gratification, and we can come under the grip of maya, acquire false ego, and think that is the true enjoyment. This is some substance of material existence. If you see anybody moving around in this world, it is under the grip of this conception. False ego makes one think that sense gratification is true happiness, and because we want to become happy, anandamayo bhiyasat, let me gratify, let me maximize. So in that sense, our scriptures so wonderfully dissect the whole thing, and the stark realities put in front of us.

So what is spiritual life? Spiritual life is from the very beginning, Krishna says, regulate the senses. Therefore, these rules and regulations are there, Vedic injunctions are there. How we can regulate the senses? How we can regulate the mind? And engage in devotional service. So Prabhupada in one place mentions about what is... Just last Sunday somebody asked me this question, we were talking about lust. The series of verses in the third chapter. That from the very beginning, regulate the senses. So one person asked, if there is happiness there and a man and woman enjoys that and it's consensual, it is, they're not hurting anyone, it is in agreement. It's not like molestation or a rape where against the wish of a person you try to enjoy. What's wrong with it? After all, Lord has made this for enjoyment. Sometime it is bewildering. What's wrong? If man and woman comes together and they decide that let's enjoy. They're not hurting each other. They are aiding each other in agreement. They enjoy. What's wrong?

So Bhagavatam gives a very nice explanation. He says, this sense gratification which we think will make us happy... Anandamayo abhyasa. That happiness what we are seeking. Bhagavatam is saying, Tripyanti Neha. It will never satisfy the soul. Tripyanti Neha. What does it mean? What is this? It is God. Gautama controle halves. Just like on Earth, what is God? $480 million annually. $480 million. $1 million is $6 crores. $480 million through different kinds of endorsements, different tournaments, prize moneys, advertisements. And we know he got different scams. He got involved in different scams. He was uncontrolled senses. Because of that, he got into many, many affairs, illicit affairs. And one by one, one by one, one by one, he started getting exposed. And more than 48 women came and gave testimony that how they were handled wrongly by him. And then suits were filed on him. He lost a lot of money in, you know, so to say, closing those cases. Now imagine a celebrity who has all the kind of happiness in the world. #### Sense Enjoyment, Soul Satisfaction, Miserly Pursuit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CxvZZv_G-DE?t=988
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the insatiable nature of sense enjoyment, stating that it never satisfies the soul and describing those who seek happiness in it as misers, settling for something abominable.

Because, you know, limited name, limited fame, limited gratification, feast once a week. So imagine somebody who can eat anything you want. Drink whatever you want. Sometimes you want to drink something, you know, it's costly. Maybe we can take half a glass. Eat whatever you want, drink whatever you want, stay wherever you want, travel in whichever aircraft you want. Whenever you want. And wherever you go, people are noticing. The whole people get alerted. People want selfies. Imagine that kind of a life. Tripiyanti Neha, not satisfied, want more, want more, and got into affair after affair, affair after affair. Sometimes there's an element of curiosity. Okay, you have not done something. So you're curious to know about it. Okay. This is not, been there, done it, so it's over. Slept with one woman, another, another, another, tripiyanti neha. So the nature of the sense enjoyment is it will never satisfy the soul, never ever it will reach a point of saturation. It will always, some permutation combination, which is, which you felt is missing, a person will try for that. Tripyanti neha kripana, a miser, a person who actually seeks this happiness is a miser. He is entitled for much higher form of happiness, much greater happiness, much better quality of happiness. But he is a miser. He is getting satisfied. He is seeking something which is tucham, it's abominable.

#### Sense Enjoyment, Hope, Misery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CxvZZv_G-DE?t=1108
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So the nature of this sense enjoyment is, there is always hope. Maybe this combination, maybe this way, maybe in this particular situation, in this particular circumstances, in this particular environment, it will give me satisfaction."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about Lakshahira and a prostitute to illustrate the illusory hope associated with sense enjoyment, emphasizing that it ultimately leads to misery (bahudukha bhaja).

Prabhupada mentions, I think we were hearing yesterday, that Lakshahira's story. A man goes to that prostitute and he gives the food in a golden cup and also in a mud pot. So he was curious to ask why, it's after all the same thing. Food is the same, it tastes the same, whether it's from a mud pot or from a golden pot. So he says, then why have you come here? So the nature of this sense enjoyment is, there is always hope. Maybe this combination, maybe this way, maybe in this particular situation, in this particular circumstances, in this particular environment, it will give me satisfaction. So tripyanti neha kripana, bahudukha bhaja. Another quality of this happiness, sensual happiness is bahudukha. Yes, Prabhupada mentions, there is some feeling of happiness. After all people, why are they chasing? There is feeling of happiness. There is no doubt about it. People are intelligent in that sense. Bahudukha bhaja. This very happiness, this very happiness. This very happiness which we get, momentary happiness, temporary pleasure, another quality of this pleasure is, it doesn't last, it is temporary. So there is a sensation of pleasure. Prabhupada says, in the genitals, the Lord by his arrangement, he has quoted some very special kind of happiness, which makes a person go for this, even though it's tuchham. How do we know it is here? Bahudukha bhaja. This will be the cause of misery.
