#### tamo guna, divya gyan, subordination, temporary world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fu5uIKUt9AM?t=1806
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord wants to teach us that this is a temporary place. It's not a place where we can permanently settle down peacefully."
**Summary:** This segment explains that without transcendental knowledge (divya gyan), even after being battered by material energy, one is unwilling to accept subordination to the Lord, and emphasizes that the material world is temporary, urging us to seek a permanent, eternal world.

After having got smashed also, he is not willing to give up the idea that he is not the king of the jungle. So, if we are in tamo guna, if we are not blessed with divya gyan, if we don't have transmittal knowledge, when the material energy batters a living entity, he is not willing to give up that I am subordinate to the Supreme Lord. The Lord is supreme. I am amsa. I am a small amsa of the Lord. That's why Krishna says, veviesha gunamai mamamaya dhurateya And he also says, bhau naam janma naam ante gyanvaan mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti samayam. bhau naam janma naam ante gyanvaan mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti samayam. bhau naam janma naam ante gyanvaan mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti samayam. bhau naam janma naam ante gyanvaan mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti samayam. but that living entity, after many many... after getting battered, we learn some things. A person who has some sense will learn from something. Although we are 3rd class intelligent we dont even learn sometimes. This material energy is designed in such a way so that Even the difficulties, even the clashes it is all sanctioned by the Lord for our benefit. Just like, sometime doctor gives an injection. Sometime doctor may prescribe a diet which is bitter. That's why Krishna says, I am the well-wisher of all living entities. So if we are blessed with divya jnana, if we have a mercy of a pure devotee of the Lord, then even when we go through some kleshas, it's a very big learning for us. Miseries can teach us many lessons which otherwise we don't learn. That's why in this material world, we many times are complacent. We have relationship. We think these relationships are permanent, but they come to an end. The Lord wants to teach us that this is a temporary place. It's not a place where we can permanently settle down peacefully. There's another world and he's inviting us to that world, which is permanent, which is eternal. He is supreme. Sita is a material world. There's nothing more that can teach us. There's nothing there. Sita is a material world. There's nothing there. Sita is a material world. There's nothing there.

#### material energy, prison house, Durga Devi, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fu5uIKUt9AM?t=1950
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are in the clutches of this material energy."
**Summary:** This segment compares the material world to a prison house overseen by Durga Devi, emphasizing that we are in the clutches of material energy and can only be relieved by surrendering to the Lord.

So this modes of material nature, which is binding the living entity, which is like the ropes, it is said that in this material world, just like in a prison house, this material world is also compared to a prison house. In a prison house, you have a jail superintendent. Likewise, the Lord's representative, Durga Devi, Durga Devi is in charge of this material world, which is also called Durga. Durga means fort. Just like a person who is imprisoned in a fort, he cannot cross the walls, he cannot come out of the prison easily. Durga Devi, it is said that she has ten hands. The ten hands represent the ten directions. North, east, south, west, north-east, south-east, south-west, north-west, top and bottom. Imagine we are in a room which has ten CCTV cameras. Top all four directions. Top, down, left, right. Can we do something which is, which a person who is monitoring the CCTV cameras, we can avoid him being aware of? No. If it is one CCTV camera, maybe you can take a particular position and you can still not be visible in that camera. So likewise, that's why Krishna says, devī eṣa guṇamāyī mamamāyā dhuratyā. We are in the clutches of this material energy. And the Lord, who is the creator of this material world, He is the supreme controller. He is the cause of all causes. He is the supreme cause, the super-soul of everything. When we surrender unto Him, then He relieves us from the influence of this modes, this material energy.

#### energy management, spiritual energy, transcendence, Sat Chit Ananda
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fu5uIKUt9AM?t=2085
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we chant, when we engage in spiritual activities, we invite the spiritual energy to act on us."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of understanding the context of our spiritual practices, particularly chanting, as a means of inviting spiritual energy to act on us, leading to transcendence and the realization of our true nature as Sat Chit Ananda.

So we should have a context of what we are doing. Many times when we chant, when we do our sadhana, when we do our reading, we should know what are we doing. Like yesterday, we heard in a class, it's energy management. There is material energy, there is spiritual energy. And we are called the marginal energy. Sometimes we are predominantly under the influence of material energy. Sometimes under the influence of spiritual energy. So when we chant, when we engage in spiritual activities, we invite the spiritual energy to act on us. We come under the influence of spiritual energy. And under the influence of spiritual energy, we elevate in the modes and transcend the modes. And when we transcend the modes, try to transcend the modes. And when we transcend the modes, then the living entity who is Sat Chit Ananda, who is eternal, Chit, knowledgeable, Ananda, blissful, he realizes his true constitutional position. He achieves what he is hanging on to. What he is anchoring, what he is desiring, what he is longing to achieve. And that is pure, transcendental, eternal happiness in association with the Lord. We stop here.

---

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2017-08-02 | SB 3.26.19 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### material nature, spiritual nature, impregnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=30
**Verse References:** BG 14.3, BG 14.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living force comes directly from the supreme personality of Godhead and is completely spiritual. Therefore, no material scientific advancement can produce life."
**Summary:** This segment explains the process of impregnation of material nature by the Supreme Lord, emphasizing the spiritual nature of living entities and refuting the materialist view of life's origin.

This impregnation of material nature is described in the Bhagavad Gita, 14th chapter, verse 3. Material nature's primal factor is the mahatattva, or breeding source of all varieties. This part of material nature, which is called pradhāna, as well as brahman, is impregnated by the supreme personative godhead and delivers varieties of living entities. Material nature in this connection is called brahman because it is a perverted reflection of the spiritual nature. It is described in the Vishnu Purāṇa that the living entities belong to the spiritual nature. The potency of the supreme lord is spiritual and the living entities, although they are called marginal potency, they are also spiritual. If the living entities were not spiritual, this description of impregnation by the supreme lord would not be applicable. The supreme lord does not put a semen into that which is not spiritual, but it is stated here that the supreme lord puts a semen into material nature. This means that the living entities are spiritual by nature. After impregnation, material nature delivers all kinds of living entities, beginning from the greatest living creature, Lord Brahma, down to the insignificant ant, in all varieties of form. In the Bhagavad Gita, 14.4, material nature is clearly mentioned as sarvayoni. This means that all varieties of species, demigods, human beings, animals, birds and beasts, whatever is manifested, material nature is the mother, and the supreme personality of Godhead is the seed-giving father. Generally it is experienced that the father gives life to the child, but the mother gives its body. Although the seed of life is given by the father, the body develops within the womb of the mother. Similarly, the spiritual living entities are impregnated into the womb of material nature, but the body being supplied by the material nature takes on many different species and forms of life. The theory that the symptoms of life are manifested by the interaction of the 24 material elements is not supported here. The living force comes directly from the supreme personality of Godhead and is completely spiritual. Therefore, no material scientific advancement can produce life. The living force comes from the spiritual world and this has nothing to do with the interaction of material elements.

---
#### material nature, Krishna's direction, transcendental knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "With transcendental knowledge which Krishna shares with us in the Gita, we can see beyond what meets our eyes."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how Krishna explains material nature in the Gita, emphasizing that it works under His direction, and how transcendental knowledge allows us to see beyond superficial cause-and-effect relationships.

So in the Gita, Krishna talks about material nature. Generally people understand this whole creation, what we see around us: beautiful landscapes, beautiful flowers, mountains, lakes. They appreciate nature's beauty, gifts of nature, nature which means prakriti, nature's bounty. Krishna in the Gita says this material nature, which is producing so many moving and non-moving beings, is working under the direction of Krishna. Mayadhyakshena prakriti suyate sa chara acharam. Chara means moving, acharam means non-moving. This world is designed in such a way that if someone wants to defy God, deny God, don't want to see the hand of the Lord, he can still somehow explain how we came into being because of our parents, how a flower and fruit is coming into being because of the seed, how the seed came into being because of some fruit earlier. So what, if we don't have shastra chakshu, to some extent we can see cause and effect relationship between various phenomena what we see in this material world. But with shastra chakshu, with transcendental knowledge which Krishna shares with us in the Gita, we can see beyond what meets our eyes. That's why it's called transcendental knowledge which transcends the knowledge which we can assimilate through our material senses.

---
#### gratitude, instrumentality, cause and effect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=330
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although he is giving the money physically, he is just being an instrument. It is his duty to deliver that money."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a student in a hostel receiving money via money order, this segment illustrates how superficially we might thank the postman, but deeper analysis reveals the father as the source, highlighting the concept of instrumentality.

I'm sure all of you have heard about this example. Some of us have stayed in the hostel during our college days. And earlier times, unlike present times where you can do online transfer, you have credit cards, common way of transferring the money was through money order. Even in my time when I was in college, either we'll receive money through a check or through a money order. So in money order system, you deposit the money in a particular post office, you fill a form and the post office, wherever you have to send money, receives the money and then the postman goes and delivers the money to the person whosoever is mentioned in the form. So if we see why a person who is staying in the hostel, he is getting the money, the money with which he can pay his mess bills, sustain himself, buy various other items, what he needs for his sustenance, he can see a direct correlation that it's the postman who is coming and delivering the money. And using that money, he is paying the mess bill, he is able to buy things, he is able to buy some fruits. So in other words, there's a direct correlation between the postman and my ability to pay the bills. So superficially speaking that we have to be grateful to that postman because he is the one, unless he gives me the money, how will I pay the bills? How will I survive? So I have to be grateful to him. Someone who cannot see beyond this will say that the postman is maintaining me because the direct correlation, unless he gives you the money every month, you cannot sustain. But with little intelligence, we know the postman, although he is giving the money physically, he is just being an instrument. It is his duty to deliver that money. But there is someone else who is depositing the money in the post office because of which the postman is getting the instruction to go and deliver this money. So if we take a step back and introspect, what's the cause of the postman giving the money? It's the father who has deposited that money in the post office.

---
#### gratitude, Krishna's role, ultimate cause
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=510
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every one of us have different abilities, skills... Krishna says the ability is me."
**Summary:** Continuing the money order analogy, this segment traces the source of the money back to the father's employer, then to the father's skills, and ultimately to Krishna as the source of all abilities, establishing Krishna as the ultimate cause.

So now let's take a look at why the father is depositing the money. Actually, we have to be grateful to the father. The father is getting the money from the employer, the place where he is working, because of which he is able to deposit the money, send the money. So next question is, why the employer is paying my father the money or his salary? He is not doing charity. He is paying the money because the father has some ability, some skills, and there is a trade-off. There is some relationship of give and take. The father contributes his abilities, his skills, does some job, and because of which the employer is willing to pay the salary. So now let's look at, okay, the employer is paying the salary to the father because of which he is able to deposit the money. The reason why the employer is paying the money is because of the abilities and skills which Krishna in the Gita says is coming from me. I am the ability in man. Every one of us have different abilities, skills. Some are good learners. Some have good intelligence. Some are buddhu. Some are able to remember things quite well. So Krishna says the ability is me. So in other words, because of that ability, the employer is employing a person because of which he is getting the salary. Because of the salary, he is able to pay the, deposit the money because of which the postman comes and delivers the money because of which I am able to pay the mess bill. So if you see the root cause of that money which is coming to me is not the postman, is not the father, is not the employer, is the Lord.

---
#### Krishna's involvement, ultimate cause, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=654
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In all aspects of my existence, right from what I am getting, what I am eating, how I am sustaining myself, how the body is running, the Lord is very thickly involved."
**Summary:** This segment extends the analogy to include the mess committee, the market, the farmers, and the body's digestive system, all ultimately sourced in Krishna, emphasizing His pervasive involvement in every aspect of our existence and how Krishna consciousness reveals this ultimate cause.

Now the mess committee who receives the money will take that money and go to the market and buy different ingredients. That ingredients, although the person can buy from the market, they are different traders, wholesalers, but they are again just instrumental. Grains are not produced in factories. Again the nature which is working under the direction of Krishna. The source of grains, the source of all ingredients is also Krishna. Now the ingredients are brought, it's cooked, and finally I eat. There is a very intricate mechanism in the human body to digest and transform what I have eaten into energy. That intricate mechanism is also designed by the Lord. So in other words, in all aspects of my existence, right from what I am getting, what I am eating, how I am sustaining myself, how the body is running, the Lord is very thickly involved. Now that's a different thing that we want to recognize that, we want to accept that, or we can just pass it off and give some material reason. Why I am getting grains? Because I have paid for it. Why the grains are there in the market? Because the farmer has grown it. Why the mess is supplying me food? Because I have paid him money. Why I have the money? Because my father is sending money to me. So you can stop at a particular level, but Krishna consciousness takes us to the ultimate cause.

---
#### human body, complexity, divine design
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "One brain cell is more complex than the best of computers in the world today."
**Summary:** This segment details the intricate complexity of the human body, citing statistics about cells, neurons, sensory receptors, and retinal cells, to illustrate the impossibility of random creation and the necessity of a divine designer.

I just received a message which talks about how intricate this human body is. Although many times we think that we are in control of the body, but actually so many things which are happening in the body, the moment I put something in my mouth and eat, so many reactions, so many hormones, so many enzymes, so many digestive juices, so many actions and reactions happen in the body for that food to get transformed, for that food to get transformed into the energy which sustains me. So human body has 372 trillion cells, out of which 100 billion are skin cells. Constantly the cells are dying and are being replaced by new cells. If for some reason we get a scratch or some kind of a wound, automatically the cells, some clotting happens, blood clotting happens, new cells get formed, and in 7-10 days you see the wound is healed. Our brain has 100 billion neurons. It said that one brain cell is more complex than the best of computers in the world today. One neuron. And our brain has 100 billion neurons. Now I am speaking to you, actually they can measure some electrical impulses passed from one neuron to another. They can measure it actually. So trillions of brain cells are working in tandem with all these neurons firing and electrical impulses being passed. Are we in conscious control? We have 60 million sensory receptors which we call our senses, which receive signals. 127 million retinal cells we are able to discriminate between different colors, light green, blue, dark green, yellow. Like that, we can discriminate between 100 million different colors. If I show you a yellow color and a little bit light yellow color, you can have hundreds of shades of yellow and you can discriminate. Yes, this color is different from this color. Like that, our retina, which has 127 million cells, can discriminate between 100 million different colors. The point is that it's such a complex system.

#### divine design, seed-giving father, existence of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=970
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There has to be somebody who has put this whole system in place."
**Summary:** This segment reiterates the question of who designed the complex human body and the universe, referencing the concept of the Lord as the seed-giving father. It then transitions into a story about a debate on the existence of God with bright students.

Who has done this? Yes, immediate causes, this body here in the pulpit, it's mentioned that the Lord is a seed-giving father. The body is given by the mother. Our body has 30 trillion RBCs, red blood cells. These days, diagnostics have advanced quite a lot. If you go to a typical hospital for a health checkup, they will take blood from you and they can give you a very thick report going into some 25 pages. I know some of you have gone through or not. 20 pages, different parameters in that drop of blood. How much calcium, how much phosphorus, how much B12, how much vitamin D, how much this, how much homoglobin, how much RBCs, how much white blood cells. Are we consciously producing RBCs? Are we consciously in control of WBCs? In Mumbai, we had one session where we invited some students. It's a very prestigious college. And they're very bright students. Admission happens through CAT, which is that IM's exam. So the students came. In fact, one of the professors invited the students to his house. We were also invited. Professor is Krishna conscious. So then the students, we thought that let's have a debate. And we divided the students into two groups. And the topic was, we discussed a bit what could be the topic where we can debate. So it organically emerged. Let's debate on existence of God. So initially, before we started the debate, I asked how many of us strongly believe in God? All but one raised the hands. Just one person, he was not wanting to raise his hands. Later on, when we had Kirtan, he was the one who was jumping and dancing the most. I really didn't know why he didn't raise his hand. So, and then we divided into two groups. And then it's okay. One group is strongly believes that God exists. The other group, you know, strongly opposes that idea. And we'll hold on to the idea that there is no God in this world. Everything is big mess. It's all chaos. We see good things happening to bad people, bad things happening to good people. It's all anarchy here. So the debate started.

#### atheism, scientific advancement, existence of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=1155
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "A day will come when we don't require God, we would know everything."
**Summary:** This segment recounts the arguments presented by the students who opposed the existence of God, focusing on the idea that scientific advancement will eventually explain everything, rendering God unnecessary. It also describes how the speaker intervened to present stronger arguments for God's existence.

And these people who were supporting the idea that God does not exist, they came up with very wonderful ideas. In fact, very convincing ideas. So one of them said, God does not exist because somebody said there's a, you know, there's some ultimate creator, somebody who has eventually created everything. He says today, this person is saying that today we are able to explain a lot of things which we were not able to explain earlier. So our frontiers of understanding of this world is expanding. And whatever we are not able to explain, we say it is God. So he's saying since we are advancing and the frontiers of our understanding of this creation is increasing, a day will come when we don't require God, we would know everything. Just because we don't know anything, some things, we say it is God. Like for example, why this body has 320 brain cells, how it got created, how it, he says today we have no knowledge about it. A day will come and we'll come to know how it was created. And interestingly, I noted in the debate that although they all believed in existence of God, this party which was supposed to be saying that God really exists was a completely weak party. Although they were very good speakers, but they didn't have solid arguments. They would give some argument and this party will punch them back. Finally I saw that it was going in a wrong direction. And then after about 10 or 15 minutes I said okay let's stop it. And then I spoke for about half an hour. Typically we have a lot of wonderful arguments. So everyone was quite wonderstruck when they heard a lot of things about just like, you know the earth moving around the sun in a fixed orbit, at a fixed distance, day after day, day after day, with such an accuracy. There has to be somebody who has put this whole system in place. This whole wonderful gigantic creation has not come from a big bang. So many of them actually for the first time, you know I could make out after the session, many of them came and spoke to me. For the first time they saw yes. In today's world, talking about these things and being introspective, nobody has time. Nobody has time to even think how this world came into being. What's the purpose of our life? Who is the creator?

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment explains that Maya covers the real intelligence and knowledge of the living entity. It then contrasts the concept of the Lord as the seed-giving father with Darwin's theory of evolution, emphasizing that creation is not merely a result of material interactions.

And that's one of the functions of Maya which covers the real intelligence, real knowledge of the living entity. So the point here is, as it's mentioned, the supreme personality of Godhead, impregnates material nature with his internal potency. The Lord is the seed-giving father. This whole creation has not come about, as Prabhupada says, not by some material interactions. A lot of people think that, like Darwin's theory of evolution, initially there was unicellular animals and then survival of the fittest. And as the organisms were adapted, they became more complex. They became more intelligent. And today what you see is evolved organisms which have survived over the years, over millions of years. In other words, what Darwin is saying initially, there was a time when it was all apes were there. Our ancestors were monkeys. So here, from Bhagavatam, from Bhagavad Gita, we get a clear understanding that how there are 84 lakh species, there are 84 lakh species of life.

In 84 lakh species of life, we know that about 4 lakhs are human beings. If you were to put in percentage, human beings are just 5%. Aquatics are about 11%. Trees, 24% of 84 lakh species. Birds, 12%. And bees, 35%. So this article talks about that of this 5% human beings, 5% of the total species, which is 4 lakh species, about 50% of them, who are having human body, are completely uncultured. They have nothing to do with God. Either they are skeptics or they are atheists, or don't care, don't bother. This includes all the uncultured, barbaric, cannibals, aborigines, tribals, who are just in a survival mode. Almost like animals. Have got a human body, but almost like animals. In other words, if you were to analyze their activities, it's all eating, sleeping, mating, and defending.

#### lip service, contradictions, religious hypocrisy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=1531
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A person is saying service to mankind... The same person is chopping a chicken, or whatever animal, and eating daily."
**Summary:** This segment describes the next 25% of human beings as those who offer lip service to religion, but whose actions contradict their words. It illustrates this with a story of a man who claims to serve humanity but eats non-vegetarian food daily.

So 50% of the human species, that is about 2 lakh species, of these 4 lakh are uncultured. When I say uncultured, they could be staying in palatial houses, but no evolved consciousness. They're almost like animals. Then there are 25% of these 5%, which is about 1 lakh of these 4 lakh species, which is 1.25% of the entire 84 lakh species, they do lip service. Lip service means, they say that I believe in God, but have nothing to do with God. Their actions contradict what they say. I met a person again in Bombay. He was following a particular religion, but now he is completely, he doesn't follow any religion, doesn't go to any temple. So he was having an argument with his grandfather. So grandfather one day told him, how is it that you become like this? So then he very proudly told his grandfather, that I don't believe in any religion. I believe in only one religion. And he boldly told his father, my religion is serving the humanity. And then very proudly he was telling me that, when I told this, my grandfather was also completely moved. And till that he says, you have taught me what is real religion. He says, I don't believe in all, my religion is service to mankind. He says, if you give, so I was talking to him, he says, if you give food to a hungry person, and you see a smile on his face, that is religion. If you can help a needy person, and he is grateful to you, that is religion. So he was going on and on, and I was listening. And then in course of the discussion, he said, came to his family, and then his wife is now thickly involved in some sect. And he said, initially there was lot of arguments between us. I said, why? He said, because I am a pure non-vegetarian. Every day I eat non-veg. Without non-veg, I cannot have my meal. Morning and evening, I have non-veg. And she is not ready to cook in the house. There was a big fight. Finally, we have reconciled. So now imagine, it's lip service. A person is saying service to mankind. You give food to a person, there is a smile on his face. The same person is chopping a chicken, or whatever animal, and eating daily. You can see smile on the face of a child when he eats a food. Don't you see a cry on the face of an animal when he is chopped off and shaking in pain? So a lot of people who talk like this, very intelligent person, brilliant person. Most of the time he was talking. Very good speaker. Very convincing. He could convince his grandfather also. Changed his religion also. About to change my religion. So lip service. 25% of so-called religious people doing lip service. They say they are religious. They believe they have a philosophy of life, which could be very convincing, but it's all full of contradictions.

We need some help. We need some blessings for my sense gratification. They go beyond the ritualistic understanding of religion and try to understand who is God, what's the goal of life, who I am. They're a little wise. To some extent, we can call them jnanis. 10% of those who are wise are actually liberated, which means again, .00125%, which is only 1,000 of the 4 lakh species are liberated. And .01% of those who are liberated, Krishna also in the Gita says, Manushya naam sahasreshu kashchidhyatati siddhay yatatam api siddha naam kashchin maam veti tatvatah. .01% of those who are liberated, which is .000125%, in other words, which is around 10 species out of this 4 lakh species actually have some understanding about Krishna.

Sometimes we wonder, we have been chanting for years together, still I don't have taste, still I am Bhagavatam class, I sleep, still I have, when I go in front of the deities, we may wonder sometimes, is this process working, am I really advancing? #### knowledge, Prabhupada's mercy, material nature, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=2040
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are very very fortunate, we should be grateful to Prabhupada that we have got this knowledge."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the fortune of having clear understanding of life, soul, and the Supreme Lord through Prabhupada's mercy. It reiterates that material nature is not independent and concludes with a call to follow the process of Krishna consciousness, share the message, and appreciate Prabhupada's compassion for suffering beings.

And you can make out when they start speaking, when you discuss with them, you have very, very shallow, although they may be talking very intricate subject matter, but as far as understanding of life, you can see they are very, very shallow. So today, if we have a clear understanding, yes, there is life after death, none of us have any doubt about it. Yes, we are spirit-soul. Before this life, we were there. Based on our karma, we are getting different bodies. There are 84 lakh species. Soul is transmigrating from one body to another. How much we are convinced about it, that's a different thing. We may not be able to follow all the principles of Krishna consciousness, but that conviction, that realization, is not a small thing. And that's because of the mercy of Prabhupada, because of, we are following a process, it has become pratyaksha to us. So what is pratyaksha? This material nature, it's not working independently. It's the Lord who is impregnating the material nature. Because the Lord has impregnated the material nature, this nature is producing both moving and non-moving beings. So ultimately, the supreme controller is the Lord, Krishna. The supreme controller is Krishna, who works in the same way. He is everything. Everything is emanating from me. Everything is emanating from me. So we are very very fortunate, we should be grateful to Prabhupada that we have got this knowledge. Now that we have this knowledge, we should very sincerely follow this process and also try our best, janmasartak kari karo bekar. Reach out to more and more people, and also appreciate, more and more people. Prabhupada when he went to America, he genuinely felt that they are, in spite of being technologically advanced, in spite of being rich, in spite of being prosperous, they are suffering. A person who is in knowledge, who is Krishna conscious, actually can see the living entities are suffering. When we are under the influence of Maya, we are not able to see that. We will get attracted by the glitter of material energy. So let's do our bit to follow this process and also share this message with other living entities. Stop here.

#### mind, desire, false ego
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=105
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From this evolves the mind, whose thoughts and reflections give rise to desire."
**Summary:** The speaker recites and translates the verse, explaining how the mind evolves from the false ego of goodness and how thoughts and reflections give rise to desire, emphasizing the mind's instability on the material platform and the importance of fixing it in Krishna consciousness.

Vaikaritad Vikurvanam. Manas Tatvam Ajayatah. Yad Sankalpah Vikalpabhyam. Vartate. Vartate Kama Sambhavahah. Vaikaritad Vikurvanam. Manas Tatvam Ajayatah. Yad Sankalpah Vikalpabhyam. Vartate Kama Sambhavahah. Vaikaritad Vikurvanam. Vaikaritad Vikurvanam. Manas Tatvam Ajayatah. Yad Sankalpah Vikalpabhyam. Vartate Kama Sambhavahah. Vaikaritad Vikurvanam. Vaikarikāt, from the false ego of goodness, Vikurvanāt, undergoing transformation, Manahā, the mind, Tathvam, principle, Ajāyatā, evolved, Yātā, whose, Sankalpā, thoughts, Vikalpābhyā, and by reflections, Vartate, happens, Kāmasambhavā, the rise of desire, Translation, and purport by His Divine Grace, A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Ślaprabhupāda, Translation, from the false ego of goodness, Another transformation takes place. From this evolves the mind, Whose thoughts and reflections give rise to desire. Please repeat. From the false ego of goodness, Another transformation takes place. From this evolves the mind, Whose thoughts and reflections give rise to desire. Translation from the form of trance, through this art bhaj bending the body and Frederica, All these creating creating in thisutation, Which are due to different kinds of desires. We desire that what is favorable to our sense gratification, And rejects that which is not favorable to our sense gratification. The material mind is not fixed, But the very same mind can be fixed when engaged in the activities of Kṛṣṇa consciousness. Otherwise, as long as the mind is on the material platform, It is hovering and all these rejections exist, while browsing the world. Then if you have a good начина, and acceptance is asat, temporary. It is stated that whose mind is not fixed in Krishna consciousness must hover between acceptance and rejection. However advanced a man is in academic qualification, as long as he is not fixed in Krishna consciousness, he will simply accept and reject, and will never be able to fix his mind on a particular subject matter. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta.

#### mind, consciousness, material vs spiritual
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=592
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The source of such thoughts, good or bad, is definitely the mind, but mind is an instrument."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while the mind produces thoughts, the source of those thoughts is consciousness, distinguishing between material and spiritual consciousness and their respective influences on desires and thoughts.

Yes, we all know mind is producing thoughts many times we know that we should not be thinking like this we know that we should not be envious we know that we should not be mindful of things. We should not act like this. We know that we should not be contemplating women, but many times we helplessly end up doing things, speak certain words which later on we regret, do certain actions which later on we feel that I shouldn't have done that, get into some habits which we become slave of. It's difficult to come out of those habits, break those habits. So now the Acharyas have analyzed, gone a step back and figured out what is generating those thoughts. Sometimes the thoughts are empowering thoughts, good thoughts, sometimes the thoughts may be disempowering, maybe bad thoughts. Abominable thoughts. The source of such thoughts, good or bad, is definitely the mind, but mind is an instrument. We have a gross body and a subtle body. Mind is part of the subtle body. Ultimately it is the soul. Even mind is a material element. It's the consciousness. And if we are in material consciousness or material conception of life, mind will generate one kind of thoughts, one kind of desires. Here it is said, from this evolves the mind whose thoughts and reflections give rise to desire. So if our consciousness is impure, if we are in material consciousness, if we have material conception of life, if we are in bodily concept of life, then we will have, the mind will generate a particular kind of thoughts and desires. We all know thoughts are very powerful thing, but what is the cause, what generates a particular kind of thoughts? It's our consciousness. Ultimately mind is also a matter, material element, although it is subtle. And if we are in a spiritual conception of life, if we are, have spiritual consciousness or Krishna consciousness, then naturally our desires will be spiritual in nature, empowering in nature, our thoughts will be pure.

#### consciousness, purification, aspirations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=785
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What is Bhagavatam doing? It is changing our perception of world, perception of who we are, our aspirations, our goals."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how Acharyas advise working on our consciousness rather than battling with determination and rejection at the mental level. He explains that hearing Bhagavatam daily cleanses our consciousness, transforming our perception of the world and our aspirations, shifting them from material desires to spiritual goals like becoming better devotees and servants of Krishna.

Just by determination, the symptom of the mind are determination and rejection. Many times we determine, I will not do like this, I will not speak like this, I will get up every day in the morning. And sometimes we make resolutions. Sometimes we do certain things we want to reject, we want to come out of it, we want to give up. So therefore Acharyas have given, rather than battling at that level, rejection, determination, we work on our consciousness. What happens when we hear Bhagavatam daily? Our consciousness which is polluted, our consciousness which is material. The material conceptions of life get slowly transformed, gets cleansed and we come to spiritual conception of life. And when we come to spiritual conception of life, What is Bhagavatam doing? It is changing our perception of world, perception of who we are, our aspirations, our goals. What is really important in life? If you see an average person outside, in his mind, aspiration, he is full of aspirations. Even devotees have aspirations. But when our consciousness gets purified, aspiration changes. For many of us, aspiration will be how to chant attentively, how to become a better devotee, how to become a better servant, how to read attentively, how to read more and more of Prabhupada books. These are our aspirations. But if you ask a person outside, his aspiration is something. How can I have a better woman? How can I live in a better house, bigger car, more money, more control?

---
#### consciousness, purification, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=972
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we purify our consciousness, automatically our desires, our thoughts, our mind, our intelligence becomes purified."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the difference in aspirations and desires stems from consciousness, with material consciousness leading to material desires and spiritual consciousness leading to spiritual aspirations. He emphasizes that the process of Krishna consciousness aims to purify our consciousness, leading to purified desires, thoughts, mind, and intelligence, and uses the analogy of a fan slowing down to illustrate the gradual nature of this purification.

So why this difference is there in aspirations, in desires, in thoughts? Because of consciousness. If we are in material consciousness, we will desire material things. We will desire name, fame, adoration, sense gratification. In material consciousness, goal of life becomes how I can have more and more enjoyment, more and more sense gratification. When we are in spiritual concept of life, in spiritual consciousness, then our aspirations and desires are, how can I purify myself? How can I become a better devotee? How can I become a better servant of Vaishnavas? When that consciousness is there, automatically, frustrations, anger, pride, greed, lust, envy, jealousy automatically dissolves. So our process of Krishna consciousness, the very name of the organization, International Society for Krishna Consciousness, it is not at the mental level. It is at the level of consciousness. When we purify our consciousness, automatically our desires, our thoughts, our mind, our intelligence becomes purified. Now, as we know, when we follow a process, it takes time. If we switch off the fan, it does not come to halt immediately. In fact, the time it takes to stop depends on many factors. First factor is, the speed it was rotating. As we know, fan can be at different speeds, 5, 4, 3, 2, and then off. So if the fan is at speed number 5, when you switch off the fan, it takes more time to come to halt. This is if the fan was moving at speed 1.

#### conditioning, purification, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1074
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone of us are diseased to different degree."
**Summary:** Continuing the fan analogy, the speaker compares conditioned souls to computers affected by viruses, highlighting the varying degrees of conditioning and the need for patience in the purification process. He introduces the concept of switching the fan back on, representing the influence of association and material desires, which can hinder progress.

So likewise, all of us are conditioned souls. It's like in a computer, we say, affected by virus. Sometimes the whole heart can be corrupted, can be infected with different kinds of viruses. So everyone of us are conditioned souls. Because of ignorance, many, many lives we have, so to say, cultivated different desires, associated with wrong things, acted in ways which have, so to say, solidified our bodily concept of life. We are thoroughly convinced that I am the body. And everyone of us are diseased to different degree. That's why even in devotee community, we see sometimes some devotees are egoistic, sometimes some are short-tempered. But the good part is we are in a hospital and we are being administered a genuine medicine. We are taking that and it will take time for the medicine to act and for us to get cured. So just like this fan example, somebody may be moving at a speed of 5, somebody moving at a speed of 3. So we should be patient. It may take time. Another factor which determines how fast the fan will come to a halt, how fast our desires will get purified, how fast we will become purified of all anarthas. Just like in the case of fan, you can switch off. But if the switch is in your hand, you can again switch on. Now imagine somebody wants the fan to stop. He switches off. The fan starts slowly revolving lesser and lesser and finally it's coming to halt. Again we switch on. And if this is happening on a regular basis and a continual basis, we switch off. It slowly starts reducing the speed and then again we switch on. Again it starts moving. Again we switch off. Again we switch on. This can go on endlessly and the fan will never come to a halt.

#### association, desire, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1226
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Practicing genuine spiritual life is declaring a war against māyā."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of 'saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaha', emphasizing the importance of associating with pure devotees to develop a desire to switch off material desires and serve Krishna. He warns against associating with the wrong people and cultivating material desires, which can hinder spiritual progress, and emphasizes that genuine spiritual life is a war against maya, requiring introspection and awareness of one's aspirations and attractions.

So this is the meaning of saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaha. When we are in association of devotees, pure devotees, then we develop a desire that yes, I should switch off. I should not desire material things. Yes, I should serve Kṛṣṇa. I should serve Vaiṣṇavas. Yes, I should give up all this pride, envy and all those things. What is the use of being envious of devotees? But if you are not careful, if you associate with wrong people, associate with wrong things, cultivate material desires or fan material desires, have material aspirations, Oh, he got such a big service and I should also aspire for that. Then again it's like putting the switch on. Again the fan starts moving. Again that material desires, comparison, enviousness, jealousy, anger, hatred, all that starts brewing in the system. That's why Prabhupāda says practicing spiritual life, genuine spiritual life is declaring a war against māyā. A devotee has to be very, very introspective. What's my aspiration? What is capturing my attention? What am I getting attracted to? What kind of desires are manifesting? What's my aspirations? These are all symptoms of what's our consciousness. If the consciousness is polluted material, naturally we'll have all this name, fame, aspirations. Desire for sense gratification, gross or subtle. Sometimes we may give up gross sense gratification but it will be very subtle, very fine. It could be for recognition, may not be gross. As a life membership preacher, may not be gross, nothing goes in the pocket, but then he's a better collector, he's a bigger devotee. So in that sense, we have to be very careful, very cautious, the goal of we join this mission, this Kṛṣṇa consciousness, following these processes to purify our desires, anartha nivṛtti happens, so that we become a nice devotee of the Lord.

#### spiritual master, devotion, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1376
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To be a devotee of Lord is not an easy thing."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about Śrīla Prabhupāda being asked about the qualities of a genuine spiritual master and whether he himself was a devotee, highlighting the importance of attachment to Krishna and the humility of a pure devotee who never claims to be one.

Śrīla Prabhupāda was once asked, what is the quality of a genuine spiritual master? Śrīla Prabhupāda replied, a genuine spiritual master is a person who is very attached to Kṛṣṇa. So then this person asked, does that mean a person who is a genuine spiritual master has to be a devotee of the Lord, devotee of Lord Kṛṣṇa? Prabhupāda says, yes, he has to be a pure devotee of Lord Kṛṣṇa. So the next question was, are you a devotee of Lord? So Prabhupāda says, I am trying to be a devotee of the Lord. To be a devotee of Lord is not an easy thing. In fact, Prabhupāda is a pure devotee of the Lord, but the quality of a pure devotee of the Lord is, never says that he is a devotee of the Lord. Sometimes we get into conception, I am a senior devotee, I am an advanced devotee. Okay, at the vyābhārik level, yes, some gradations are required. But heart of heart, if someone really believes that he is an advanced devotee or senior devotee or something, then it is an anartha.

#### consciousness, purification, material energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1479
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Original consciousness, or Kṛṣṇa consciousness, that's our original consciousness in which we are completely convinced, we have a realization that I am amsa of Kṛṣṇa, I am part and parcel of Kṛṣṇa."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that spiritual progress is not a binary state but a gradual process of purification. He uses the analogy of rain becoming muddy upon touching the ground to illustrate how our original Krishna consciousness gets polluted by contact with the modes of material nature, leading to a material consciousness characterized by identifying with the body and seeking sense gratification.

And when such anarthas are there, these are all symptoms of material consciousness, material conception. Again, it is not binary logic. It is not that we toggle between pure spiritual consciousness and pure material consciousness. It is not zero or one. We associate with wrong person, our consciousness gets polluted a little bit. We hear one good class, our consciousness gets purified a little bit. That is why the Āchārya says, every moment we should strive for kīrtaniyā sada hari, always we are in touch with Kṛṣṇa, always we are in touch with the words of Kṛṣṇa, so that our consciousness remains pure. Naturally, we have attraction to hear material things, being conditioned souls. So somebody is gossiping, talking about current affairs, we get attracted, we get pulled into it, and then we start discussing. Naturally, it brings our consciousness down. Oh, is it? We start discussing. So, just like example is given, rain, when it falls, the water is pure, but when it touches the ground, it becomes muddy. So likewise, original consciousness, or Kṛṣṇa consciousness, that's our original consciousness in which we are completely convinced, we have a realization that I am amsa of Kṛṣṇa, I am part and parcel of Kṛṣṇa. My duty is to serve Kṛṣṇa, to give pleasure to Kṛṣṇa. But when we come in touch with modes of material nature, material energy, then our consciousness gets polluted. And the symptom of polluted consciousness is, material consciousness is, I am the body, I am the enjoyer, I am the controller, I am the Lord. Sense gratification, is the goal of life. I should maximize my sense gratification.

#### consciousness, liberation, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1607
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are here to purify our consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the purpose of Krishna consciousness is to purify our consciousness and awaken our original, pure state. He contrasts material and spiritual consciousness, explaining how they lead to different desires and outcomes, with material consciousness resulting in entanglement and spiritual consciousness leading to liberation. He also contrasts this with the Buddhist concept of eliminating desire.

So, we should be very clear, what are we into? What are we here for? Material consciousness results in material desires, material thinking, feeling and willing, which will result in entanglement and bondage. Whereas spiritual consciousness results in spiritual thinking, feeling and willing, will result in spiritual desires, and will eventually liberate us from all this material energy, material consciousness, material entanglements. So what are we here for? We are here to purify our consciousness. We are here to awaken our original, pure Kṛṣṇa consciousness. All what we are doing morning to night, the services we are doing, the sādhanā we are doing, the reading we do, chanting we do, the kīrtans we do, the darshan of the Lord, actually is for this only. How we can purify our material consciousness and come to our original Kṛṣṇa consciousness, be attached to Kṛṣṇa. When we are Kṛṣṇa conscious, when we are in our pure consciousness, naturally we will have pure desires. We cannot kill desires. We cannot stop desiring. There are type of spiritualists who see the root cause of misery. In fact, Buddha said so. Buddha made this statement, the root cause of misery in this material world is desires. And his formula for moksha, so-called moksha was nirvana, was kill those desires, become śunyā, desireless.

#### desire, mind, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1733
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goal is not to kill desires, the goal is to purify the desires, purify the mind."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vaishnava perspective with the Buddhist concept of eliminating desire, stating that to become desireless is also a desire. He emphasizes that the goal is not to become inert but to purify desires and the mind, recognizing that a controlled mind is our best friend while an uncontrolled mind is our worst enemy. He uses the example of people surviving an earthquake in Japan to illustrate how extreme hunger can drive people to do anything to survive.

As we all know, Vaiṣṇava philosophers, Vaiṣṇava Ācāryas say that to become desireless also is a desire. And the symptom of a soul is consciousness, is desire, just like here it is said. From this evolves a mind whose thoughts and reflection give rise to desire. The goal is not to be a stone. The goal is not to be inert. The goal is not to have, kill desires. The goal is to purify the desires, purify the mind. The mind, if it is controlled, is our best friend. The same mind, if it is uncontrolled, is our worst enemy. Right now we have false ego. Right now we have a material mind, which is untamed, unchecked. And then we have all kinds of material desires. So the goal of a devotee is not to kill desires, but to purify the desires. And that happens when we purify our consciousness. How does the consciousness get purified? By chanting, by hearing, by associating with devotees. Our conceptions get changed. Our perceptions get changed. Our bodily conception gets transformed into spiritual conception of life. If we are not having spiritual conception of life, if we are not having, if we are not relishing spiritual pleasure, if we are not having higher taste, if we are not relishing higher pleasure, we will be forced to taste lower things. In one class it was mentioned that when a person is very hungry, he has not eaten for a day, he is very, very hungry. You give him anything, he will eat. You give him anything, he will eat. You give him anything, he will eat. There was an earthquake in Japan and many people died. So the rescue teams were trying to, you know, the buildings had collapsed. So people had got, so to say, when the building collapses, then the rescue teams try to, you know, figure out if there is somebody still, the debris, anybody is, so to say, covered by the debris and still alive. So they are trying for that. So it was found that after six days the building had collapsed, after six days they found two brothers alive. And everyone was curious, how did they survive for six days?

---
#### hunger, material vs spiritual, taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=1861
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When a person is very, very hungry, he has nothing to eat. He'll eat anything."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of the earthquake survivors, highlighting their extreme measures to survive, and then shares a personal anecdote about eating poorly cooked rice during a curfew to illustrate how desperation can drive people to consume anything when they are hungry. This is used as an analogy for seeking material pleasure when spiritual pleasure is lacking.

Six days without food, completely, you know, covered with all the debris of the building which collapsed. How did they survive? So they were interviewed finally when they recovered, regained their consciousness. So they were interviewed, how did you survive? What were they eating? What were they drinking? They were drinking each other's urine. Somehow they were together. The building collapsed. Probably they were in some cavity. They could be alive. They drank each other's urine. So where will they get water for? Where will they get food? So it's like recycling. So when a person is very, very hungry, he has nothing to eat. He'll eat anything. I remember when we were in hostel one time, there was a curfew in Haryana, the place where we were staying. And all the shops were closed. In fact, because of the curfew, just before the curfew, the hostels, a lot of students who were from nearby places, they had already left and gone. So practically nobody was in the hostel. The hostel mess, the canteen was off. The employees had gone away. So just some 15, 20, 30 of us were there in the hostel. And we were hungry. The messes closed. What to eat? Morning, we were looking here, there. In the hostel room, sometimes you have some biscuits, some things. So that also got over. And for the last six hours, seven hours, nothing to eat. Messes closed. Everything is closed. So one of them, one of my friends, it was a curfew. Somehow we sneaked in and went to a nearby place. There was one small shop. With great difficulty we got rice. And also it was not a good quality because it was small, village kind of a setting. Typically what villagers eat? Rice which is broken and which is not of good quality, cheap quality rice. So they somehow managed to get rice. None of us know cooking. And in the hostel, you know, you have some pans for, aluminium pans, very, and then you have makeshift heater to heat some water or do something. So one of them said, let's make rice. First time we are making rice. And in a hostel, in a pan. Not knowing how the rice is to make. Believe me or not, finally, we started off with brown rice. Ultimately when it was cooked, it became black rice. It was unpolished rice, brown rice. And finally when it was cooked, it was black rice because even the pan in which it was being cooked, it had some black, black, you know, sticking below it. Pan is also not clean. Imagine what kind of pans you'll have in a hostel room. And we didn't know how much to cook and we are stirring it and finally it became. And after it was cooked, we have an option to eat it or not eat it. All of us ate it. And relished it.

---
#### material vs spiritual, pleasure, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sRz4osFTV98?t=2128
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we don't get spiritual pleasure, we will look for material pleasure."
**Summary:** The speaker reflects on the experience of extreme hunger and relates it to the human tendency to seek pleasure. He explains that when we lack spiritual pleasure, we inevitably turn to material pleasures, even if we know they are not good for us. He points out that many are caught between material and spiritual aspirations, making it difficult to fully commit to spiritual practices.

So, when you are, when you have not eaten, in fact, we haven't, many of us are, so to say, fortunate enough not to, we have no idea of, no doubt, when we fast for a day, in the general fasting, we get some clue of what is, at least we have a hope that so and so time, after dusk, after sunset, I have to break the fast or next day morning, I have to break the fast. And usually fast is followed by feast. So, so hope is there. But imagine somebody is hungry and he has no idea when food will go in his mouth. We have a lot of people like that. 20 million people die of malnutrition. They are not sure of their next meal. So when a person is hungry, he'll eat anything. They say in Hindi, when a person is drowning, even if a straw is floating, he'll try to catch hold of straw. Straw cannot save him. So, when we are in material consciousness, when we are not relishing spiritual pleasure, when we have no transcendental pleasure, we will look for material pleasure. We will go for sense gratification. Even though we may have knowledge, that's why many times we wonder, I know that I should not be doing this. I know that I should not be looking at women. I know, but helplessly I am trying. Because right now our state is, we have given up material pleasure, but we have not started relishing the pleasure which Haridas Thakur was relishing. In one sense, we are right now one leg in both boats. We have some material aspiration also, some hopes also, and we are also aspiring for spiritual pleasure. That's why many times, that resolutions don't help. Because we are pleasure-seeking beings. Anandamayo Bhyasa. We want pleasure. If we don't get spiritual pleasure, we will look for material pleasure.

So we have to be very very serious in our chanting, in our reading, in our hearing Bhagavatam class, so that, we are in spiritual consciousness. So that we relish spiritual pleasure. We experience higher taste. And if we do that, then we will not be battling at, at lower levels. Should I eat this? Should I not eat this

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2018 - 04 - 03 | SB. 3. 27. 19 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### material nature, maya, rules and regulations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/tOilMWErJqw?t=360
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The reason why we have fallen into the clutches of maya is because of, we have rebelled against the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how we are bound by material nature, the stringent rules of nature, and how violating these rules leads to being caught in the clutches of maya.

We regret as if there is some kind of a force. Prabhupada says, his acts are influenced by the qualities of material nature and he becomes passive. So in that sense we all are bound. Although we may appear free, we may appear independent, we celebrate independence day, we are bound by the material nature. And Krishna in the Gita says, this material nature is not easy to surmount. These days in the marketplace you have many books, you know, how to be happy, how by saying some affirmations, how by imagining things. But the laws of nature are very different, very, very stringent. And Krishna in the Gita gives us a clue, in fact, why clue? Directly he says, if we have to come out of the influence of material nature, maam eva yeh prapadyante maya metam taranti te. The reason why we have fallen into the clutches of maya is because of, we have rebelled against the Lord. Just like if someone violates the state laws, we are punished. He is punished. In the recent, we have been hearing about the ball tampering scandal. So there are some rules of fair play. And if you violate the rules, you want to take undue advantage, then you get penalized. So we see in this world how rules apply. Likewise, the material nature has its own rules, stringent rules and knowingly, unknowingly, we violate those rules, we come under the clutches of maya.

---
#### bondage, senses, moth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/tOilMWErJqw?t=485
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Forget about finding out what is the solution."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the question of how a soul can be freed from material nature and uses the analogy of a moth being drawn to a flame to illustrate how our senses can lead us to destruction.

So now, in this verse, we are getting a hint, Prabhupada is giving us, what is the solution? Devahuti is asking. In fact, the beauty of Bhagavatam is, forget about finding a solution, we don't even know the problem. So here it is said, how can there be freedom for a soul? As long as material nature acts on him and binds him. We don't even know that we are bound. Forget about finding out what is the solution. So Devahuti is asking this wonderful question. How can there be freedom for a soul who, as long as the material nature acts on him, we know that material nature acts on us, all of us are in the influence of maya, and binds him. What is the solution? How can the soul extricate itself from this bondage? There is a very nice verse which I came across, I thought it is relevant in this particular context. This verse says, patanga matanga kurunga brunga, meena hata panchabhi reva pancha. It is a very nice verse, you have heard this example earlier. Patanga, patanga means moth. During the rainy season, if there is some bright light, all the moths, they get helplessly drawn towards that fire or that bright light. They cannot resist.

---
#### senses, bondage, elephant
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/tOilMWErJqw?t=684
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So it is said that by the touch skin, you know, we have five senses."
**Summary:** This segment continues the analogy of the five senses, focusing on the elephant and how it is captured through the sense of touch.

The eye cannot resist the temptation of being drawn to that fire. Then, Matanga. Matanga means elephant, especially in wild forests, you know, they are wild, dense forest. They are wild elephants. Now, if they have to be captured, if they have to be tamed, it's not easy. We know elephants are so huge that they cannot resist the temptation. A man, relatively speaking, is much smaller and huge elephants, if you have to catch them and more so if they are not tamed, when we go to Guruvayur or places, they are all tamed animals, they have been trained. And sometimes also we hear that they go astray and you know, sometimes casualties happen. But imagine wild elephants and man is relatively small. So how do you catch a wild elephant? How do you tame a wild elephant? So this wild elephant, it says that they have one weakness, especially in their back, behind their head, there is a spot which is, which is, you know, just like the genitals, you can agitate the elephant when you, when you touch that spot. A female elephant is trained specifically to pass through that wild elephant by the side of the tree. So she can go through this wild elephant and touch that particular spot. It arouses that elephant and then it becomes almost like intoxicated, mad. It cannot resist but to follow that she-elephant. And that she-elephant is trained. She guides this elephant and then finally it leads to a place where there's a pit which is covered by green grass. That elephant who is following madly, that she-elephant cannot even fathom that there is some pit, something which is a trap for him. And the she-elephant passes by the pit and this she-elephant passes on that particular green spot and falls in that pit. So it is said that by the touch skin, you know, we have five senses.

---
#### senses, bondage, deer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/tOilMWErJqw?t=846
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The music is so intoxicating for the deer, it cannot sense danger and gets killed."
**Summary:** This segment continues the analogy of the five senses, focusing on the deer and how it is captured through its sense of hearing.

Then kuranga. Kuranga refers to deer. Deer is an animal which many times, I don't know whether some of you have watched National Geographic. It shows that. It shows that animals, you know, they behave, how they run, how they are chased, how they are killed. So this deer, it runs very, very fast. At times there are chases where even the tiger and deer, the deer to save its life runs faster than the tiger. And sometimes it gets trapped. So imagine a deer has to be shot by a hunter. It's not easy. It runs very, very fast. When it spots there is some danger, it can just flee, it can just run. But this deer has one weakness and that is melodious sound. So especially those who are hunting for deers for various reasons, now it is banned, but otherwise in the earlier days. So they knew this weakness of the deer and what they would do is when there's a deer around or in a particular place, when the deers are there, they would start playing some melodious music, some sound. And when this deers would hear that melodious sound, they stop running, they close their eyes and get completely absorbed in that sound and almost like, you know, and you know it cannot just understand what's happening to it. Just absorbed in hearing that music, and it's like a painting in the wall stationary, standing in one particular place. It's like a duck and you just shoot that person. So, to give an example of Kuranga, how by the weakness of its ears, sense of hearing, it cannot resist hearing the melodious music, at that time it just forgets any danger. The music is so intoxicating for the deer, it cannot sense danger and gets killed.

The lotus platter, they wound up, they wind up. If we follow this, chant around attentively, then it is guaranteed. Follow all the rules and regulations. Try to control our senses, control the mind, all those things. Then we are very fortunate in that sense. There is a light at the end of the tunnel. For all others it is complete darkness, Andhakar. At least we may not be relieved right now. Our senses may be still bothering us, our mind may still be agitated, chanting of rounds may not be, at least we can see the light at the end of the tunnel. We know where we have to head. We know what is the destination. We know what's the goal. ## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2018 - 06 - 13 | SB 3. 27. 27| HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### pure devotion, material desire, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They are not interested in understanding Krishna, but only in loving Him."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the difference between a pure devotee and a mixed devotee, highlighting the pure devotee's selfless love for Krishna in contrast to the mixed devotee's desire for material benefits or spiritual advancement.

In material existence, when a devotee is not completely purified, he expects material benefit from the Lord in the form of relief from material miseries, or he wants material gain, advancement in knowledge of the relationship between the Supreme Personality of Godhead and the living entity, or knowledge as to the real nature of the Supreme Lord. When a person is transcendental to these conditions, he is called a pure devotee. He does not engage himself in the service of the Lord for any material benefit or for understanding of the Supreme Lord. His only interest is that he loves that Supreme Personality of Godhead and he spontaneously engages in satisfying Him. The highest example of pure devotional service is that of the Gopis in Vrindavan. They are not interested in understanding Krishna, but only in loving Him. That platform of love is the pure state of devotional service. Unless one is advanced to this pure state of devotional service, there is a tendency to desire elevation to a higher material position. A mixed devotee may desire to be a pure devotee, or he may desire to enjoy a comfortable life on another planet with a greater span of life, such as on Ramaloka. These are material desires, but because a mixed devotee engages in the service of the Lord, ultimately, after many, many lives of material enjoyment, he undoubtedly develops Krishna consciousness, and the symptom of this Krishna consciousness is that he is no longer interested in any part or any sort of material elevated life. He does not even aspire to be a pure devotee. He does not even aspire to become a personality like Lord Brahma. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Bhava.

---
#### living entity, qualitative oneness, quantitative difference
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=185
**Verse References:** BG 15.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, being Amsa of the Lord, qualitatively we are one with the Lord and we also are Sat, Chit, and Ananda."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of the living entity (Atma) being an Amsa (part) of Krishna, qualitatively the same as the Lord (Sat, Chit, Ananda) but quantitatively different, using the analogy of a drop of water and the ocean.

In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna mentions that the living entities, the spirit soul, the Atma, is his Amsa. mam eva amsa jiva loke jiva bhuta sanatana. And Prabhupada explains that how, as Amsa of the Lord, we are qualitatively the same as that of the Lord. Prabhupada gives an example of how a drop of water in the sea has the same characteristics as that of the entire sea, or the entire ocean. But quantitatively, we are different. A drop of water is very minuscule compared to the entire ocean. So, being Amsa of the Lord, qualitatively we are one with the Lord and we also are Sat, Chit, and Ananda. Sat means eternal, Chit means full of knowledge, and Ananda, blissful. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord. the Lord.
