#### free will, desire for control, rebellion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=277
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When that Jeeva develops that desire, Ichcha, what Ichcha? To be the center, to be the point where everyone is glorifying, to be served, which is not possible in the spiritual world because all the souls are engaged in services of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how the living entity, possessing free will, develops the desire to be the center of attraction and be served, leading to rebellion against the Lord and the creation of the material world as a place for reformation.

In the spiritual world, the Lord is the center, there is no aham, there is pure love and everyone is engaged in serving others. A devotee may engage in serving other devotees but keeping Lord in the center. So there the mentality is pehle aap, your service, your seva, enabling others to serve Krishna. Directly or indirectly, everyone is engaged in serving the Supreme Lord, in glorifying the Supreme Lord. But that amsa of the Lord, when he develops an iccha, we have a free will. We have a power to choose. Krishna has given us the free will. So when that amsa desires iccha, what is that iccha? Desires to be glorified, to be served, to be the center of attraction, to be famous. In other words, take the position of the Lord. Because what is the Lord to serve position? To be in the center, to be glorified, to be served, to be the center of attraction. When that Jeeva develops that desire, Ichcha, what Ichcha? To be the center, to be the point where everyone is glorifying, to be served, which is not possible in the spiritual world because all the souls are engaged in services of the Lord. Now to fulfill that desire, some souls cannot be forced to serve that Jeeva who has developed that desire. Just like in the material world when some souls rebel against the established law, when they don't achieve the material standards of the material world, they become overwhelmed by challenges and they don't want to abide by the law, when they don't want to go by the overall scheme of things, the way things have been planned and if he violates that law, violates that arrangement, if he rebels against the law or against the government then the government is forced to make a prison house. Why prison house? Government can always isolate such people who have violated the laws, who are criminals, who have committed some crime, who have rebelled against the government, who are not willing to abide by the laws. They can put them in a line and shoot them but the government wants to give them an opportunity to reform. Yes that mentality has come, criminal mentality has arisen. A person who is a law-abiding citizen has committed a crime, now rather than just shooting him let's give an opportunity to reform him of his criminal mentality.

#### selfish desire, Adam and Eve, forbidden fruit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=778
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Everyone wants to look attractive so that others get attracted to me."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the desire to be the center of attraction manifests in various ways, from personal appearance to extended selfishness towards family or country, and then relates this to the story of Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden.

The entire garment industry, the beauty saloons, everything, everyone wants to look attractive so that others get attracted to me, others like me and sometimes it is very selfish desire and sometime it is little extended selfish desire may not be me it could be my family it could be my society it could be my country but ultimately it is me self, aham, aham ameti even bible talks about story of Adam and Eve so it is said that Adam and Eve they were in the gardens of Eden and they were allowed that you can enjoy as much as you want and they were full of trees full of trees with fruits and flowers and they were told you can eat as many fruits you want you can enjoy as much as you want in abundance but one condition was given avoid eating apple from that one tree just avoid eating apple from that one tree they were shown that tree and why that tree? because that tree has a snake and we know snake is generally envious so that tree has a snake so just avoid that tree.

The details may vary somebody may try to achieve sat chit ananda through drinking somebody may try through you know drugs somebody may try to different means but ultimately everyone is trying to be happy secure himself be permanent not die seek knowledge come out of ignorance in Mumbai we had the one one day one we have program on Sunday morning so I was preparing for the class and then I peep through a window in that place where we conduct the class some construction work was going on and so we had Mangal Aarti there and we had a chant and it was class time around 8 o'clock when I peeped out of the window I saw those labourers, they were sleeping just outside in one of those rooms so one of the devotee remarked what an amazing life no Mangal Aarti, no chanting 8 o'clock wake up so I said great so getting up at 4 o'clock in the morning especially when it's raining there now so I was a little curious one of them got up at 8 o'clock and I was a little curious now what he will do so I thought let's see how he starts his day typically you know when we get up start with little bit prayers and we go to the bathroom brush our teeth and all that so let me see how does a typical person like him who is enjoying his life how does he start his day so he got up moment he got up he did like he did like this and then put his hand in the pocket and then took out one box and from the box now if I do an action all of you will understand he took out little bit and put his hand why did he do that why did he do that first thing in the morning he is looking for Satchitananda he is looking for Satchitananda he is looking for Satchitananda he is looking for Satchitananda that is his understanding he is looking for Ananda he wants to start his day everyone wants to start his day well that gives him a kick that makes him feel good so even before going brushing the teeth or anything like that he took that put in his mouth and then started off.

Bahunam Janmanam Ante and we are getting frustrated after many many births a person in knowledge a person who is little bit wise he will say why am I suffering why this happiness which I want to achieve is elusive why there is so much of struggle in this world actually animals cannot figure out neither they can ask these questions somebody when we ask this question why am I suffering he is a pessimist there is no strength he got scared of life he is a pessimist actually it is not real knowledge means to understand why am I suffering who am I so when a person starts Bahunam Janmanam Ante Gyanwan a person who is in knowledge who is sufficiently experienced Krishna says will come to me will surrender to me now this is not a binary logic 0 or 1 surrender to means suddenly become a pure devotee no we pass through a stage where we become little awakened we realize that yes the Lord is there Lord has to be glorified Lord has to be served I am not the Lord I am the servant of the Lord I am contributing to happiness God for the sake of.

#### mixed devotee, pure devotee, selfless service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=1559
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pure devotee does not engage in devotional service to the Lord because he wants knowledge, because he wants to get enlightened, because he wants pleasure."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between mixed and pure devotees, explaining that mixed devotees engage in devotional service for personal gain (spiritual benefit, peace of mind), while pure devotees serve out of selfless love for the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

So that's what Prabhupada is mentioned mixed devotee engages in devotional service we are all mixed devotees Prabhupada great Devotees there are pure devotees so mixed devotee does what we do Putra Engage in devotional service for contestation of Sri Mahatmasriz spiritual benefit There is some Aham in that for my spiritual progress, my advancement, for the spiritual benefit of being eternally engaged in transcendental abode of the Lord in full bliss and knowledge. In the material existence, when a devotee is not completely purified, he expects material benefit from the Lord. When we are serving, we are expecting some benefit, peace of mind, satisfaction, feeling good. In the form of relief from material miseries or he wants material gain, lot of people are kamabhaktas. They are serving the Lord because they have some desires to be fulfilled. Advancement in knowledge of relationship between the Supreme Personality of Godhead and living and some are serving the Lord or engaged in devotional service to get enlightened, to know their jigyasu, they want to know. When a person is now, this is all mixed devotee, he wants knowledge, he wants relief from miseries, he wants some happiness, he wants pleasure for the self. Lord is a means. Service to the Lord is a mean. End is what? My pleasure, my satisfaction, My enlightenment, My liberation, My going back to Godhead. That is mixed devotee. So, Prabhupada says, when a person is transcendental to these conditions, he is called a pure devotee. Pure devotee does not engage in devotional service to the Lord because he wants knowledge, because he wants to get enlightened, because he wants pleasure. he wants Satchitananda.

#### pure devotion, selfless service, rebellion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=1709
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We have come here in the material world, we have rebelled against the Lord, we have an impure desire to be the Lord, to be the center of attraction, to be glorified, to be famous."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that pure devotees serve the Lord out of love and are already satisfied, even without going to the spiritual world, while those in the material world have rebelled against the Lord with impure desires.

Why does he serve the Lord? His one interest is that he loves the Supreme Personality of Godhead and he spontaneously engages in satisfying him. A devotee, Prahlad Maharaj says, for me, I do not, I am already satisfied. They are already in a position of Satchitananda. They already reached that stage because they are engaged in the service of the Lord. What more they have to achieve? For them, even going to spiritual world, because what we do in spiritual world, engage in service of the Lord, glorify the Lord. A pure devotee is engaged doing the same thing here in the material world. So he doesn't even have the desire to go back to the spiritual world. So highest example of pure devotional service is that Gopis in Vrindavan, they are not interested in even understanding Krishna, but only in loving him. That platform of love is a pure state of devotional service. So what are we trying to engage in? We have come here in the material world, we have rebelled against the Lord, we have an impure desire to be the Lord, to be the center of attraction, to be glorified, to be famous.

#### devotional service, purification, sincerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=1800
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are taking the medicine, we are chanting the holy names of the Lord, we are engaged in devotional service, and slowly our disease is getting cured."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that through Prabhupada's mercy, we understand our mistake and engage in devotional service to purify our desires, comparing it to taking medicine to cure a disease, and that the speed of purification depends on our sincerity.

And that's the point. That is the root cause why we are in this material world. And by Prabhupada's mercy, by the mercy of pure devotee, we've got some understanding what mistake we have done. And we are now engaged in devotional service. And right now because the desires cannot be purified suddenly, when a person is diseased, you cannot suddenly get cured. It takes time. You have to take the medicine. A fan is rotating. It suddenly cannot stop immediately. So what we are doing is we are taking the medicine, we are chanting the holy names of the Lord, we are engaged in devotional service, and slowly our disease is getting cured. Ichcha, dvesha is getting cured. A mixed devotee may desire to enjoy a comfortable life on another planet with a greater span of life, such as Brahma Loka. These are material desires, but because a mixed devotee engages in the service of the Lord, ultimately after many, many births of material enjoyment, he undoubtedly develops Krishna consciousness. And the symptom of this Krishna consciousness is that he is no longer interested in any sort of materially elevated life. Right now we have desire to be comfortable, to be peaceful, to be properly situated, to be happy. So slowly all those anarthas, all those impure desires, all the material desires, slowly starts getting wiped out. And pure desires to love Krishna, to serve Krishna, to blissfully engage in the service of Krishna, manifest. And that is what we are trying to achieve. It may happen in one life, it may happen many, many lifetimes. Depends on how sincere we follow the process. We'll stop here.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2018 - 08 - 09 | SB 3. 32. 5 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### consciousness, sense gratification, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CgWp53sVJfs?t=451
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The highest level which Prabhupada, great Acharyas are teaching us, which is very, very rare, where a devotee is interested not in his pleasure, but giving pleasure to God."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the different levels of consciousness, contrasting sense gratification with devotional service and highlighting the rarity of acting purely for the pleasure of God.

Next level, a person when he elevates, he becomes somewhat God-conscious. He understands that, yes, I am the enjoyer, but I need the blessings of the Lord to enjoy, or to maximize my enjoyment or sense gratification, I need to satisfy some higher being, may not be understanding of the Supreme Lord. So the Vedas are full of Karmakanda portions which are meant for people in that consciousness, which is like the second level of consciousness, where a person is interested in his own sense gratification, but may engage in some religious activity, even worshipping God, but the end objective is, yes, my sense gratification. Sometimes it is difficult to decipher the difference between a devotee worshipping the Supreme Lord and a person of that consciousness worshipping the Supreme Lord. That's why many times when we take up Krishna consciousness seriously, parents also say, why you have to go to temple every Sunday, every Saturday? We also believe in God, we also worship, we also do puja. We also, five minutes, ten minutes, you do that, what is the use of two hours of puja every day? Because they understand that, yes, God is there. We need some blessings of the Lord, and for that, ritualistically, some puja, some worship. And that's how there are many, you know, when people get into a house, there is some kind of ceremonies done, ritualistic done. They may not be for the pleasure of the God, so that, you know, we live peacefully, prosperity ushers in, and things like that. So second level is where a person is even worshipping God, even going to temples, even going for pilgrimages, but is interested in his own sense gratification. The highest of which could be to get mukti. God is a means, and is me. So, in the base, in the bottom-most level, the sense gratification is very gross. Eat, drink, and be merry. Whereas in the second stage, it becomes a little finer, one may even start meditating. There are a lot of people doing meditation, different kinds of pranayama, different kinds of spiritual practices, awakening of chakras, awakening of kundalini, and all those things, but the end objective is my happiness. It's also one form of spirituality, and that's why it is mentioned in the Vedas, because people cannot jump from the lowest more level to the highest level. So, there are a lot of people in that second stage of consciousness, who are spiritual, who are religious, who have a conception of God, even worshipping God, even praying to God, Our Father in heaven, hallowed be thy name, give us our daily bread. The prayers, what generally the so-called Hindus chant, Om Jai Jagadisha Hare, that prayer has a lot of Sukh Sampati Gharave, you know, all these things. It's all about Sukh Sampati and, you know, give me this, give me that. Worship of God is there, and the highest level which Prabhupada, great Acharyas are teaching us, which is very, very rare, where a devotee is interested not in his pleasure, but giving pleasure to God. The end is not me, the end is God. Now, in the battle of Kurukshetra, here Prabhupada mentions, the whole thing started off with Arjuna mentioning about my pleasure, my family, my dynasty. What is the use of fighting? How will I enjoy a kingdom which is tainted with the blood of my own relatives? How will I enjoy? What is the use of that kingdom? So, he was, in that sense, God conscious, but the end was I. Now, what Krishna is speaking in the Gita is to move a soul, a person who is even so-called God conscious, but end is sense gratification, to a much higher level, or the topmost level, where a person acts for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord. Where a person acts for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord. so here he says he did not want to fight for with them but when he was ordered by krishna and convinced by the teaching of bhagavad-gita that his duty was to satisfy krishna now what is krishna consciousness krishna consciousness is understanding that we are part and parcel of krishna meant to act for krishna satisfy krishna prabhupada gives very nice example of how the hands are part of this body and meant to serve the body and when the hands serve the body the hands also gets its due quota of nourishment so the happiness what we all are seeking the genuine happiness the true happiness the lasting happiness the real happiness is when we serve the lord and the lord blesses us with madhuban uses prasadam pleasure so that pleasure is the real pleasure which everyone actually is seeking for because we are not getting we are trying different means you know working very hard working very passionately chasing different goals having different aspirations trying to fulfill various desires hoping against hope that i'll be happy so prabhupada here says then he becomes working for him one actually becomes completely satisfied and happy the actual happiness and satisfaction which we are looking for everyone is looking for sometimes we feel that this is philosophy and you know so-called outside world is a real life actually everyone this fellow it's not philosophy because it's also talking about the real thing everyone is looking for happiness this is also saying how we can achieve real happiness and satisfaction so here then he fought for happiness not for his sense gratification after hearing gita what happened the transformation of consciousness elevation of consciousness then he fought not for his sense gratification but for the satisfaction of the supreme personality of godhead krishna also in the gita says the four kinds of people who will surrender to me one who is in distress one who is in need of money one who is inquisitive one who is in knowledge actually they all surrender to krishna but the consciousness is at the second level how my desire to be happy or peaceful becomes fulfilled god is a means that's the beginning stages it's okay but finally it we have to elevate to the next stage or the highest stage which here is being talked about in purified consciousness those who are intelligent and are of purified consciousness are the ones who are intelligent and are of purified consciousness what do they do they act for satisfaction and pleasure of krishna even many a times it may not be pleasing to our mind and senses but because it gives pleasure to krishna and guru we act in fact the sadhana bhakti process is like that morning getting up may not be very pleasing but if we keep doing that because it is is ordered by the spiritual master because it is pleasing to Krishna, we keep doing, we gradually shift from these lower consciousness to higher consciousness. That is nothing but purification of consciousness or Anarthanivritti. Everyone is actually looking for happiness but unfortunately like we all have some equations in our mind. We have been hearing in the Bhagavatam class, equations in the mind about what will make me happy. Everyone has equations.

#### happiness, illusion, sense control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CgWp53sVJfs?t=1002
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maya's job is to convince us that yes this will give me pleasure, this very pleasure which we all are trying to maximize to increase to gratify the senses more and more in a much better and better ways more elaborate ways more sophisticated ways Krishna says that very pleasure is a cause of distress."
**Summary:** This segment explores the illusory nature of material happiness, highlighting how our minds create equations for happiness that often lead to distress, and emphasizing the importance of regulated sense control.

Those who are eating meat when they see a chicken or a fish, ah, it's so nice. Equation is there in the mind. That taste which they will get when they see a chicken or a fish, ah, it's so nice. The equation is there in the mind. They eat that, oh, it will make me happy. We may not have that equation. We may have some other equation. When we see a fish or some non-vegetarian, we may, you know, because the equation is different. Equation may vary from person to person. What will make one person happy may not make another person happy. That's why it is called Manasijam. For a man, there is nothing called as man's soul or a woman's soul. When a soul is in a woman's body, man is pleasurable. That equation is there. That's a different, the way the body is constituted, biological urges are, so to say, ingrained in that system, mind-body system. Or for that man, woman is attractive. It's an equation of happiness. That's why Bhagavatam says it's Manasijam. It's mental concoction. Actually, there is no mental concoction. These all are, we have concocted. And some is, you know, the Lord, the way the yantra is designed, some urges are part of the yantra. So what Krishna consciousness involves is through knowledge, form the right equations. Krishna, in the Gita says, Yehi samsparse ja abhoga dukhyona hi evate. Many times or other, most of the times in ignorance, the equations of happiness that we have formed in our mind-body system, we have formed and equation that, if I do this, if I act like this, if I think like this, I'll be happy. In fact, Krishna says that very happiness is the cause of distress. But what is happiness? That very happiness is the cause of distress. this is what Maya's job is to convince us that yes this will give me pleasure this very pleasure which we all are trying to maximize to increase to gratify the senses more and more in a much better and better ways more elaborate ways more sophisticated ways Krishna says that very pleasure is a cause of distress Krishna says just like a tortoise so what should a devotee do yes we cannot completely switch off the senses senses are meant for proper use gives example of a tortoise he uses it as and when required otherwise withdraws it so that's why regulated sense one who is regulated in his eating, sleeping, recreation and work so there are many rules and regulations it's not that you can eat anything and everything you can see anything and everything there are rules there are boundaries you can eat this you can see this other thing is not good for the self otherwise the definition of happiness is whatever is pleasing for my sight I should maximize seeing it and as you are aware many people become addicts adult content it's very pleasing but they become addicts talking about addiction I am sure many of you would have heard about that sometimes people say what is wrong if I drink in limits itna sa thoda sa daily what's wrong? we don't uh you know We are occasional drinkers, when we go to a party or when we go for something, we drink. What's wrong?

#### addiction, desire, higher taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CgWp53sVJfs?t=1280
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There is an illusion, there is something, the living entity wants real happiness, real satisfaction. But this pleasure being illusory pleasure, being a temporary pleasure, that pleasure which will not satisfy the self, what happens is the living entity...because he's Anandamayobi, he wants to increase his pleasure, his pleasure seeking being, he tries to maximize that."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the progression of addiction, explaining how the pursuit of illusory pleasure leads to a cycle of increasing doses and stronger substances, as the soul seeks real happiness.

So there is sometimes, you know, when we see some addicts, it is easy to say that, you know, it's an addict. Actually all addicts started off with the statement, itna kya galti hai? All addicts, they all thought that, yes, itna lene mein kya kharabi hai? But what happens is, this pleasure which we get through sense gratification, because it does not satisfy the self, here Prabhupada says, one actually becomes completely satisfied. There is an illusion, there is something, the living entity wants real happiness, real satisfaction. But this pleasure being illusory pleasure, being a temporary pleasure, that pleasure which will not satisfy the self, what happens is the living entity...because he's Anandamayobi, he wants to increase his pleasure, his pleasure seeking being, he tries to maximize that. So if a person starts with let's say smoking one cigarette, he would want for more pleasure to smoke two cigarettes. And then the kick or the happiness, whatever that thing a person gets by smoking one cigarette because body gets used to it, then to get the same kick a person needs two cigarettes. And that's how, it's a fact, it's not this thing, that's how a person starts with cigarettes, then to get a much higher that thing he starts drinking. And then if this also is not giving sufficient pleasure, a person starts taking drugs. In the drugs also there are so-called drugs which are very commonly available in colleges these days, it's very prevalent. And then people even start taking drugs which are, you know, banned. In many countries people can go behind bars or even face death penalty if you are carrying those drugs. People start taking drugs. I read an article I'll share with you, how this pleasure, how we have wrong definitions of pleasure and how a living entity, because he wants to increase his pleasure, how he moves from one substance to another. Very rarely people are there who remain or maintain that balance, very rarely. And if a person does not have a definition of a higher pleasure, when a person goes through some depression or some tough times or some loss of near and dear ones because he has no understanding of higher pleasure and because he wants to seek pleasure, he will jump more on those substances with much higher intensity. That's why sometime when a person loses job he starts drinking more or starts smoking more. Or if he is in depression he starts taking some kinds of substances or drugs.

#### addiction, sense gratification, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/CgWp53sVJfs?t=1469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Living entity, soul wants higher pleasure. He's a pleasure seeking being."
**Summary:** This segment continues the discussion on addiction, using the extreme example of seeking snake bites for euphoric effects to illustrate the relentless pursuit of pleasure and the dangers of materialism.

Now, you see a person may, as I said, he may want to increase his pleasure moves from higher doses of the same substance or from a higher grade substance. There was an article, you won't believe it, people have reached the stage where even drugs don't give them a kick, which they want. The so called the brown sugar or that, you know, cocaine or all those drugs what we hear of, prohibited substances. So the latest is, a team of researchers from Ranchi, India, recently released a report on how some men occasionally seek snake bites for their euphoric effect. It seems that a snake bite from certain types of snake can produce a toxic effect on nerve tissue and can cause symptoms that appeal to drug addicts and alcoholics. It started with Thodasa. For the most part, it's not just a snake bite. It's a very specific, euphoric effect, happiness, Anandamayo Bhyasat, snake bite, which appeal to drug addicts and alcoholics. In fact, people who are excited by the chance of receiving a potentially lethal snake bite share a few things in common, which includes a tendency to have already used a variety of substances. So substance A, reached the limit of happiness. That's what I'm trying to say. I'm trying to say, if you want to explore that happiness, move to substance B. Explore that happiness, doesn't satisfy, move to substance C. And then, a tendency to have already used a wide variety of substances and a tendency to seek high sensation experiences. So living entity, soul wants higher pleasure. He's a pleasure seeking being. To be frank, I think that the hundred percent risk of a person's happiness is that they're in a very high state of stress, they're in a very high state of desensitization. You know, like you all know, the Mensch hat. So, the patient's happiness is what you call being in a state of stress, and being in a state of emotion. So most people, whatever it is that they're in, well, we know, once they get a little палang, they're an animal, and so, you know, sometimes they want to be bitten by a snake to get high. The euphoric effect or heightened arousal and sense of well-being drive addicts to do many things that are considered antisocial. Even for their happiness, many times, people engage in some sports or some activities which are otherwise dangerous, even for life. You have, you know, people go 35,000 feet and then they jump and all that it takes to be successful in life. all kinds of you know river rafting different kinds of adventure sports are also there where a person may even risk his life for that euphoria for that happiness. Snake bites walk the edge of what is known and this type of uncertainty can appeal to a person who is looking for a novel effect and experience for that novel effect and experience people explore different kinds of things addicts seek snake bite to get high the behavior may seem extreme but is really not too different from other substance use or seeking for or seeking behavior taking risk to get high is a benchmark of addiction so it comes as no surprise the true addicts will seek more and more extreme ways for psychoactive experiences how are we we are so blessed because the world over people are looking for innovative ways of getting more and more happiness trying different means trying different ways going to different places eating different things even eating all kinds of animal is basically a distinct how to explore should not leave anything in China they're saying anything which moves you can eat because you eat and lizard didn't give you happiness look at try anything else so explored everything no animal is left

Freed from the modes of material nature, now when a person acts in that mode he transcends the mode. They do not act for sense gratification, rather since they are situated in their own occupational duties they act as one is expected to act. ## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2018 - 09 - 20 | SB 3. 32. 9 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### material nature, soul, modes of nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living entity, the soul, is actually sanatana and untouched by these material qualities in its true pure form."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the soul as eternal and full of knowledge and bliss, but when it comes into contact with material nature, it loses its original qualities and becomes influenced by the modes.

So this material world which we all are right now the part of, this material nature, we all are under the influence of the modes of material nature which are influencing our senses, our mind, our intelligence, our ego. The living entity, the soul, is actually sanatana and untouched by these material qualities in its true pure form. Being amsa of the Lord, it is also sat, chit and ananda. It is eternal, full of knowledge and blissful. But when the soul comes in contact with the material nature and gets a material body, which we all have now, under the influence of three modes of material nature, it loses its original quality of sat, chit and ananda. This material body which is comprised of the senses, the mind, the intelligence, the ego, they all get influenced by the material nature or material qualities or the modes of material nature under the influence of inhabitable time. The inhabitable time of the three modes of material nature known as prabhda, so this world is created, this material existence is created by the Lord for the living entities who want to enjoy independent of him, and the living entity is given a material body with the senses, with the mind to enjoy. But this material nature as part of the material existence, the soul is not independent. It is completely so to say under the influence of the modes.

---
#### creation, purpose of life, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So this material world which is created by the Lord is not sanatana, unlike the spiritual world which is sanatana, which is comprised of spiritual nature, which is part of his internal energy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Lord Brahma is the secondary creator of the material world, which is temporary, unlike the eternal spiritual world.

So here in this verse it says Lord Brahma, now he has, he is the secondary creator. Aham sarvasya prabhavo matta sarvam pravartate. Krishna says everything is emanating from me. But this material world, we also come to understand that how Brahma creates. So he is the secondary creator. And there is a time when the purpose for which this creation has been created, everything which is created has a purpose. So here it says when the purpose is achieved, even Brahma has a time span, a life span, and he desires to dissolve it and enter into the kingdom of God, others then enter with him. So this material world which is created by the Lord is not sanatana, Prabhupada mentions in the purport, unlike the spiritual world which is sanatana, which is comprised of spiritual nature, which is part of his internal energy. Whereas this material world comes into being, stays for a while, and then goes away. So now we should all understand what are we up to.

---
#### devotional service, purpose of life, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=356
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the goal is, to extricate ourselves from the influence of the modes, to extricate ourselves from this material nature, to extricate ourselves from this cycle of birth and death, and go back to our original home, back to Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding the purpose of life, which is to extricate ourselves from the material world and return to Godhead through devotional service.

We are part of this wonderful mission of Srila Prabhupada, Lord's mission. What are we trying to achieve? What are we trying to do? What is the purpose of our life? We are doing devotional service. Why are we doing? Sometimes we can lose the context or lose the bearings. And at times we wonder, what am I doing? So we should understand that we are in this material world, the spirit soul which is eternal has got encased in a material body. And life after life, because we have so many different kinds of desires, to fulfill those desires, the living entity engages in different kinds of activities and gets different kinds of material bodies. Punarapi janma, punarapi marana. To fulfill the unfulfilled desires, Krishna who is accompanying the soul, gives him different kinds of material bodies. So when we come to this human form of life and brahmanda brahmite kono bhagyavan jiv, when we get the mercy of a bona fide spiritual master, we get to understand who we are. What is the purpose of this creation? We understand, what is the goal of human form of life? As I mentioned, everything has a purpose. So the goal is, to extricate ourselves from the influence of the modes, to extricate ourselves from this material nature, to extricate ourselves from this cycle of birth and death, and go back to our original home, back to Godhead. That's what Brahma is doing. That's the goal, that's the sadhya, that is the ultimate purpose. And that purpose can be achieved in human form of life. And to achieve that purpose, we have sadhana. Sadhya is the goal and sadhana is the means or the process, by which we can achieve the goal. So we'll go one by one.

---
#### sadhana, deity worship, sense purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=529
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So let's understand, why different processes of devotional service, which Prabhupada has so wonderfully incorporated, in our daily schedule, as a morning program, or for that matter, the whole day, the activities which we engage in, what is it trying to do?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of sadhana and explains how the processes of devotional service, particularly deity worship, help purify the senses.

As to what great Acharyas, Prabhupada has given us, as part of our sadhana. And why it has been given? What is it doing? Sometimes we can also do sadhana very mechanically. So let's understand, why different processes of devotional service, which Prabhupada has so wonderfully incorporated, in our daily schedule, as a morning program, or for that matter, the whole day, the activities which we engage in, what is it trying to do? How will it help us achieve the ultimate goal? So we have this material body, in which we are engaged, the spirit soul. The material body has senses, and senses are right now untamed, unchecked, unruly, like a spoiled child. So Acharyas have given us the process of deity worship. There are temples, and then we have the good fortune of worshipping the deities, serving the deities. How does that help? Sometimes people wonder, why are we worshipping statues, or idol worship? So the senses which have come under the influence of the modes of material nature, which are now unchecked, untamed, unruly, impure, the deity worship is a very good way to purify the senses. The eyes which are always getting tempted, allured to see material beauty, the same eyes can see the wonderful, beautiful form of the Lord. And by doing that, the eyes gets purified.

#### sense control, deity worship, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=650
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Right now we have got into material existence because we have got conditioned, we have got habituated, we like seeing things which are not in our self-interest, which will degrade us further, which will further entangle us in this material existence."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain how deity worship purifies the senses, specifically the eyes, by redirecting their focus from material beauty to the beautiful form of the Lord. He then transitions to the sense of smell and how it can be purified through offering flowers and incense to the Lord.

Eyes cannot be made inert. We cannot close our eyes and remain by not seeing anything. That's not the purpose of life. Right now we have got into material existence because we have got conditioned, we have got habituated, we like seeing things which are not in our self-interest, which will degrade us further, which will further entangle us in this material existence. But by culturing, by cultivating, seeing the beautiful forms of the Lord, the beautiful deities, our eyes gets purified. In fact, that's the, the engagement which eyes are meant for. In the spiritual world, everyone is using their eyes, spiritual eyes, spiritual body, for seeing the beautiful form of the Lord. In the material world, we come, we forget the Lord, and we get conditioned because we want to see, and we seek pleasure, so we start seeking pleasure seeing material forms, forms of opposite sex, rupam. Then we have our nose. The purpose is to smell. When we serve the deities, we have an opportunity to smell the flowers which are offered to the Lord, the gandha, the incense which is offered to the Lord. That way our nose which has inclination, these days you see, go to the market, people, it's a very big market for perfumes, deodorants and all kinds of, you know, I met a gentleman in Mumbai, so he says, my wife is very fond of shopping. You go to a mall three hours, she'll be shopping. I said, what is she shopping?

#### sense gratification, deity worship, prasadam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yE33ZXCYHYw?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One food which is very very tasty for our taste buds can entangle us, has karmic reactions, and the same food when offered to the Lord, honoured as Prasadam, can disentangle us, can purify us, can purify our tongue."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of a woman who excessively buys perfumes to illustrate the tendency for sense gratification. He then explains how offering food to the deities and honoring it as Prasadam purifies the tongue and frees one from karmic reactions.

Every time, what she is purchasing? And I was little curious, because there's a limit to everything. He says, my house is full of perfumes. Every time she goes to the market, she gets a perfume bottle. So the living entity wants to smell good, wants to, you know, smell good things. Now, we have a good opportunity, like Ambarish Maharaj demonstrated, smell the flowers offered to the Lord. By the way, by that process, our senses get purified. So Deity worship offers us the opportunity to serve the Deities. Each and every sense, eyes, ears, nose, tongue, we can offer nice foodstuffs to the Deities, to the Lord, and honour the same foodstuff as Prasadam. Now Krishna says, Bhunjate tete agham paapam, yeh bachanti aatmakran, that very so-called tasty food, which is very elusive, alluring, very tempting for our taste buds, that very food, when offered to the Lord, has an opposite effect. One food which is very very tasty for our taste buds can entangle us, has karmic reactions, and the same food when offered to the Lord, honoured as Prasadam, can disentangle us, can purify us, can purify our tongue.

As a peacekeeping messenger, he actually went and told Duryodhana, give them five villages. After all, you know, they are Kshatriyas. Kshatriyas have to administer rule. Krishna, you have given us so much. Everything is coming from you. I can simply offer this much. On one side, Krishna says, I will accept even if you offer just a leaf, but we should try our best to make whatever best we can offer to Krishna. And in the process of offering, we will get purified. Our desire to enjoy independent of Krishna will get purified. So, the Deity worship gives us a wonderful opportunity to serve Krishna, to offer to Krishna, to garland Krishna, to dress Krishna. And in the process, all our senses get purified. We honor the Prasadam. That sanctified food. The devotees are released from all kinds of sinful reactions when they partake the remnants of food which is offered to Krishna. So, that's why Acharyas have given Deity worship. So, we have the senses which are unruly influenced by the modes of material nature.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2018 -12 -14 | SB 3. 32. 25 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### detachment, devotion, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=68
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee has no personal attachment to anything; therefore, for him, there is no question of agreeable or disagreeable."
**Summary:** This segment explains how a devotee, free from personal interest, accepts anything agreeable to the Lord, exemplified by Arjuna's willingness to fight for Krishna.

Perception of the disagreeable arises from attachment. A devotee has no personal attachment to anything. Therefore, for him, there is no question of agreeable or disagreeable. For the service of the Lord, he can accept anything, even though it may be disagreeable to his personal interest. In fact, he is completely free from personal interest, and thus anything agreeable to the Lord is agreeable to him. For example, for Arjuna, at first, fighting was not agreeable. To the Lord, he accepted the fighting as agreeable. That is a position of a pure devotee. For his personal interest, there is nothing which is agreeable or disagreeable. Everything is done for the Lord, and therefore he is free from attachment and detachment. That is a transcendental stage of neutrality. A pure devotee enjoys life in the pleasure of the Supreme Lord. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta because of his transcendental intelligence. A pure devotee is equipoised in his vision and sees himself to be uncontaminated by matter.

---
#### spiritual identity, desire, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=144
**Verse References:** BG 15.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In our pure stage as spirit-soul, we are the same in quality with the Lord, but because we have ichcha and dvesha, we have selfish desires."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the nature of the spirit soul as an amsha of Krishna, equal in quality to the Lord, but conditioned by selfish desires (ichcha) that lead to entanglement in the material world.

We all spirit souls are amsha of Krishna. Krishna in the Gita says, mamaivamshu jeevaloke jeevabhoota sanatanahan. In our pure form, being amsa of the Lord, we are the same in quality with the Lord. Here it is said, he feels himself elevated to the transcendental platform of being equal in qualities with the Supreme Lord. So in our pure stage as spirit-soul, we are the same in quality with the Lord. But because we have ichcha and dvesha, Ichcha, dvesha, samutthena, dvandamohena, bharata. Because we have selfish desires to be the bhokta, to be the enjoyer, to be the Lord, to be the master, to be the well-wisher of other living entities. We have selfish desires.

---
#### material world, suffering, free will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=230
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The reason why we are in this material world is that we have Ichcha, we have Dvesha, we are envious of the Lord, we have a lot of material desires."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of city planning, including a prison, to explain why a kind and loving God would create a world of suffering, suggesting it's a place for souls to reform after rebelling against divine law.

And the Lord has facilitated this material world where we get an opportunity to fulfill these desires. So we are not only equal in qualities, we are also equal in qualities. Somebody asked me this question: If the Lord is all kind, the Lord is all merciful, then why has he created this world which he himself declares as dukh alayam ashashvatam. He is all kind, he is all loving, he is our father. Which father would like his son, child to suffer? So why do we have a place where there is a lot of misery, a lot of pain, a lot of suffering, which Krishna Himself declares, it's dukhālayam aśāśvatam. Just like when city planning happens, planning for a city happens, or a town happens, town planning committee happens. They plan the town, okay, this is the place reserved for garden, reserved for school, reserved for hospital, various amenities, this is a place reserved for market. Invariably in the planning, they reserve a place for a police station, and also some kind of a prison house, jail. Because when the planning is done, it is understandable that some people will take the law into their own hands. Now when someone takes the law in his own hands, in other words, becomes a criminal, rebels against the laws, does not want to follow the laws, creates a nuisance in the society.

#### free will, suffering, material desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=345
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The reason why we are in this material world is that we have Ichcha, we have Dvesha, we are envious of the Lord, we have a lot of material desires."
**Summary:** Continuing the prison analogy, the speaker explains that the material world exists because of our envy of the Lord and our material desires, allowing us to rebel and seek sense gratification independently.

Predominantly speaking, the government has two broad choices. One is that they can make them stand in a line and with the AK-47, shoot them all down. But what the government does is, no, being merciful, let's give them an opportunity to reform. And while they are giving, some, they take them to a path where some reformation happens through punishment, through various kinds of other interventions, they are so to say, put in an area, which is called the prison house. So, that they don't create a nuisance for other law-abiding citizens. And every state, every country, be it a developed country, undeveloped country, there is a concept of a prison house, a jail. Because it is expected, it is understandable that some people will rebel or will not follow the laws. So the reason why we are in this material world is that we have Ichcha, we have Dvesha, we are envious of the Lord, we have a lot of material desires. And because of that, because we have various material desires, we want to gratify our senses, seek pleasure independent of the Lord through our mind and senses.

#### material nature, suffering, battle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are waging a fierce battle with this material nature."
**Summary:** This segment describes our struggle against material nature, which imposes suffering like birth, death, old age, and disease, and how we ultimately lose this battle.

Krishna says, we are waging a fierce battle with this material nature. We are trying to push back. We are trying to push back material nature, material nature which is imposing a lot of restrictions, a lot of misery, which we don't want. We are trying by various ways to push back the material nature, to defeat the material nature, to win over material nature, to win over the material laws. But Prakriti, Sthani, Karshati, we are waging a battle. Battle means there are two forces. So here we have material nature, which is imposing restrictions, birth, death, old age, disease. And the living entity wants to push back, wants to not get defeated. But ultimately we know, in that battle, the living entity loses.

#### desire, material desire, spiritual desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=530
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The root cause of our suffering is material desire."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Buddhist concept of desirelessness with the Vaishnava understanding, explaining that the goal is not to eliminate desire but to redirect it from material gratification to pleasing the Supreme Lord.

Even if somebody is very shrewd, very this thing, you know, ultimately he has to lose the battle, he has to die. So purity. So what happens, oh no, if in this morning there is no... And so eventually because of his the beloved and devoted devotee of the Lord who is in knowledge who is full of wisdom, understands the root cause of all misery. In fact, Buddha also made this statement. The root cause of our suffering is desires. And vanquish all desires, which is not the correct understanding what Bhagavatam is giving. We cannot be desireless. The goal of human form of life is not to be like a stone, inert, have no desires, just sit in one place doing nothing. That is not something which is interesting, which is not possible also. Not to have a desire is also a kind of a desire. To be desireless is a desire. So pure devotee who is in knowledge, understands that the root cause of our suffering is material desire. What is material desire? Material desire is we want to satisfy our own mind and the senses. I and mine, rooted in I and mine. My country, my family, my society, my body, my interest, my happiness, my pleasure. That is material desire. And spiritual desire is desire to satisfy or please the Supreme Lord. So understanding that the root cause of our misery, our entanglement in this material world is Iccha. Iccha is not, Iccha means material desires. Desire to enjoy independent of the Lord, desire to enjoy our own senses.

#### service, knowledge, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1000
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the name of serving, you can make the problem worse."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of a friend in the ICU to illustrate how good intentions without proper knowledge can be detrimental, emphasizing that a devotee serves with the right understanding and humility, recognizing Krishna as the true well-wisher.

Humanity but may not be in the way which people understand as service. Sometimes in the name of serving you can make the problem worse. For example, let's say a person goes to visit a friend in ICU and the friend shares that you know it's so painful you know he's going through a lot of trauma and on top of this doctors are giving him a bland diet only boiled vegetables two times in a day and when the person speaks all his you know difficulties now you feel compassion. Wait a minute, wait a minute I'll get something for you and then go out and get some some very very exotic items. Now intent is right to help the person who is suffering, who is in agony, who is not having the right food to eat but then by helping him if not backed with proper knowledge we can do disservice to him and aggravate his problem. This is a crude example. So in that sense a devotee is genuinely serving the society. He is a great humanitarian because he serves humanity with the right understanding why the humanity is suffering. It is not that God is things are gone out of his control. He understands why people are suffering what is the root cause of the suffering and with that understanding what is the best solution to elevate the suffering humanity. With that, with that knowledge he serves the humanity. And when he serves also he understands that he is not the well wisher of the humanity. He is not Paropakaar. He is not the one who is Suridam Sarva Bhutanam. He understands that actual Suridam Sarva Bhutanam is Krishna and he is just a mere instrument. When he thinks like that, when he serves the Lord, when he serves humanity with that understanding, pride doesn't touch him. He is uncontaminated by matter because he is selflessly serving the Supreme Lord with the right understanding.

**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker recounts a conversation between Mr. Malhotra and Srila Prabhupada, where Malhotra questions the justification of building a costly temple when people are suffering from poverty, highlighting the apparent contradiction between material opulence and spiritual devotion.

Prabhupada when he went to Mumbai, he used to travel to Pune also. There was a gentleman by the name Mr. Malhotra. Okay. And whenever Mr. Malhotra, whenever Prabhupada would travel to Pune, so Mr. Malhotra would always be with Prabhupada and take him in his car wherever Prabhupada had to go. So in that sense, he was very fortunate to spend a lot of time with Srila Prabhupada. So one time, while he was with Prabhupada, traveling in the car, going to some particular place, so Malhotraji very reluctantly asked Prabhupada, Prabhupada, can I ask you one question? I have this doubt for a long time. I was reluctant. Can I ask you? So Prabhupada said, yes, yes, sure. So he said, I have heard that you are building a huge, big marble temple, costly temple in Juhu, Bombay, those days, 70s, Prabhupada was building the temple in Juhu. I have heard this, you are building a huge temple, marble temple for Krishna in Juhu, Bombay. Is there anywhere written in Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says, build a marble temple? Is there anywhere Krishna is demanding, build a costly temple for me? On one side, we see there is a lot of poverty, people are dying of hunger, starvation, no proper food, no proper clothing, no proper shelter, no proper housing. People don't have water to drink. Other side, you are building. How is it justified? Okay, it may be justified if Krishna demands, build a costly temple for me. He is also not asking. He is saying, He is saying, give me one water and leaf and why build a costly temple?

#### devotion, service, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1292
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Option number one, it is his karma, nothing to do with me, just walk away."
**Summary:** Prabhupada answers Malhotra's question with a story about encountering the son of a rich man in poverty, presenting three options: ignoring him, providing temporary relief, or reconnecting him with his father.

So Prabhupada explained, just like if you go to a rich man's house and maybe in some other country, and he is a host and you stay in his house, and 7-10 days during your stay, the whole family was very excellent in their hospitality, you know, took great efforts to make you comfortable, cook the best of things to serve you, and take great care. In other words, it was a very, very pleasant stay. And during that stay, you interact with the family, interact with the family members, get to know the child, son, daughter, everyone. And then you come back. Let's say after a year, you happen to see the son of that rich man in whose house you have stayed in the street. Almost like a beggar. Tattered clothes, no food to eat, dirty, like an urchin. And somehow you are very sure that this is the same person whom, son of the same rich man whom you had stayed in their house for 7-10 days. You had interacted with this boy. And somehow you are very sure. So broadly speaking, there are three options which a person can choose from. Option number one, it is his karma, nothing to do with me, just walk away. Maybe some misunderstanding would have happened. What have I to do with it? Okay. Life will take its own course. Just walk away. Do nothing about it. Do not even see in that direction. What if he recognizes and calls you? Why get into trouble? Sometime it happens, accident happens, just walk away. Otherwise, unnecessarily you will get involved. So option number one, just do nothing, look the other side and walk away.

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Give him some food, give him some clothes, and have the satisfaction that some element of obligation is fulfilled."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the second option: providing the impoverished son with food and clothing as a way to fulfill a sense of obligation for the hospitality received from his father.

Option number two, he doesn't have proper clothes, he doesn't have proper food, he is in the street. So because you are indebted for the good hospitality they have extended during the stay, you are obligated. So what we do is, go to the store, buy the best of food, couple of pairs of dress, and go and give him all that. At least 2-3 days, so that there is no shortage. Give him some food, give him some clothes. And have the satisfaction that, some element of obligation is fulfilled. Good work, what they have done, the service they have done, I have done my bit, small bit, humble self, I have done. So this is option two.

---
#### reconnection, understanding, permanent solution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1500
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Looks like there has been some misunderstanding."
**Summary:** The speaker details the third and best option: reconnecting the son with his wealthy father by understanding the root cause of their separation and facilitating a reconciliation.

Option three is, you go to the person, you introduce yourself, he recognizes you and you recognize, ascertain that he is son of so and so person. Double check. After it is done, you invite him and somehow convince him to come with you to your home. And in your home, after making him comfortable, after he takes his bath, changes to fresh clothes, making him comfortable, strike a conversation and try to understand as to why did he leave his house. And the moment you stop talking, he will start, no, no, my father is so this thing, this thing, that thing, I don't want to stay with him, he is so rash, he scolds for small, small things, he is very lording tendency, I just left my house. All that will come up, all that anger and hatred against the father. So hear him out, everything, and then he says, looks like there has been some misunderstanding. Because remember when I stayed there, your father was so kind, the whole family is so good. I have first hand experience of that. Maybe what you are saying is true, but then looks like there is some communication gap, some misunderstanding has crept in. And then, you know, after having won his confidence, after having heard him out, whatever he had to share, try to, so to say, the good sense in him, prevail the good sense in him, that how his father actually is a good father, actually is a good person. He is a nice person. Just that maybe you have misunderstood, maybe even if he was harsh, it was for your good. Even if he was scolding, maybe it is for your good. Maybe, could possible that he starts looking from another perspective. And when he is in a mood where he is listening and he is able to, you know, see things differently, and somehow convince him, it is not good that you have left home. I am sure your father also will be very disturbed. I am sure he must be in anxiety. He must be very pained when he heard that you are leaving the home. So somehow, when the good sense prevails in him, call the father and tell him that your son is here. Now you don't have to say that I am bringing your son there. The father will say, wait, wait, wait. He is so eager to meet his son. He says, wait, keep him there. I am coming. I am coming. Next flight he will catch and come.

#### reconnection, permanent solution, temple
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If he is connected with his rich father, if he goes back home, then the problem of feeding him and giving him clothes does not even arise."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the analogy by explaining that reconnecting the son with his father provides a permanent solution to his problems, just as connecting people to Krishna through temples addresses the root cause of their suffering.

So of the three options, if you analyze, the option number three is best because once you connect him with his rich father against whom he has rebelled and left the house and because of which he is in the streets not having food to eat, not having clothes to wear. If he is connected with his rich father, if he goes back home, then the problem of feeding him, feeding him and giving him clothes does not even arise. Not for tomorrow, not for day after tomorrow. Permanently we have solved this problem of food, clothing, shelter, everything. So what a devotee is doing is when it comes to serving the humanity, a lot of people misunderstand. You are building temples, costly temples. What are you doing for humanity? Even sometimes we may get a feeling we are building huge projects, 600 crores, 700 crores. On one side, people are dying of hunger. People are not having proper clothing. How is it justified?

---
#### suffering, divine attributes, atheism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1740
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "It is not possible that all three statements are true simultaneously."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the story of a Christian pastor who questions the fundamentals of Christianity (God's omnipotence, omniscience, and kindness) after his son is killed in a street fight.

So we have to understand that the Lord is all kind. The Lord is all powerful. I was hearing yesterday, in one lecture, there was one Christian pastor. He was going out for a walk, maybe for buying something for the church with his small little son, two year old baby in a pram in that. Unfortunately, the place they were passing through, there was some street fight. Two gangs sometimes, there is a rivalry and they start firing. Unfortunately, in that street fight between the two gangs, notorious gangs, they found themselves in the wrong place at the wrong time. And while that shooting was on, a bullet came and hit that small little child, two year old, and he was killed on the spot. So this man, after seeing this, he was a pastor, preaching, giving sermons in the church. Two year old son, dying right in front of his eyes, for no fault of his, after seeing the whole thing in front of his eyes, he started questioning the very foundation of fundamentals of Christianity. The fundamentals of Christianity is based on three principles that God is omnipotent. He is all powerful. There is no power in this world which is more powerful than God. He is all powerful. There is no force, there is no power, there is nothing which is more powerful than God. There is nothing which is more powerful than the Lord. This is omnipotent. The Lord is omniscient. He is all knowing. He knows the past. He knows the present. He knows the future. He knows who is going to fight with whom. Who is going to shoot the bullet when. He knows everything. And the third principle is He is all kind. He is very loving. Because he was going through trauma, he started thinking with what he had experienced. His son dying. Two year old baby. For no fault of his. Innocent child dying. He started thinking and questioning the very fundamentals of Christianity. And he says, it is not possible that all three statements are true simultaneously. One of them has to be wrong.

---
#### suffering, divine attributes, atheism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=1922
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If He is all powerful, then He is not all kind."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the pastor's reasoning, suggesting that if God is all-powerful and all-knowing, then his kindness must be questioned, or if he is all-kind, then his power or knowledge must be limited.

So he started rationalizing. If God is all powerful, if we accept this statement He is all powerful and He is all knowing. He knows everything. Then maybe this statement that He is all kind, all loving is not true. Because if He is all powerful, everything was within His control. There was no power. Even if you call there is Satan and there is you know, Shaitaan and all evil forces are there. They somehow, sometimes rise up. Adharma rises, all that. But He is all powerful. Then He is not all kind. Therefore you see this innocent child got killed. For no fault of His. Or if you think that He is all kind. He is all merciful. He is very loving. Then maybe He is not omniscient. He is all powerful. But maybe He is not omniscient. He is busy in the spiritual world. Lot of matters. He is God. Somehow this is God neglected. Things went out of His control. Or the third statement. If He is all kind. He is all knowing. He knows past, present, future. Maybe He is not all powerful. Maybe things have gone out of His control. He created this world with all good intentions. Now different forces have become more powerful. Just like now Congress has become powerful. Suddenly some forces, some evil forces have become more powerful. And now He has resigned.

---
#### suffering, knowledge, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=2037
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The problem is that we have no proper understanding."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the pastor's bewilderment with the understanding of a pure devotee, who possesses complete knowledge and enjoys the pleasure of the Supreme Lord, emphasizing the importance of scriptures like Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam.

And things have gone out of His control. So He started very deeply questioning. On one side we say God is all kind. All powerful. Omniscient. All knowing. All loving. All loving. All intelligent. All knowing. Then why this has happened? Whereas Shastras, they very emphatically say that yes, God is all knowing. Yes, God is all kind. Yes, God is all powerful. So Bhagavad Gita, that's why if we don't have complete knowledge of Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, which gives in complete detail of why this world is created. What is the intent? On our own, with our own intelligence, using our own analytical skills, we cannot comprehend, we cannot fathom what is the intent? What is the purpose? Why this world is created? Why good things are happening to bad people? Bad things are happening to good people? That's why seeing all this happening in this world, many people actually become atheist. They say, where is your God? There is no God. Actually, the problem is not with God. The problem is not with His creation. The problem is that we have no proper understanding. Why this world is created? Why the God who is all kind, all powerful, all knowing, why there is suffering in this world? Is He a sadist that people are suffering and ha ha ha ha, He is enjoying? Chalo, maro. Accha hai. There are people who are sadists, they get kicked when they see others suffering. There are people like that called sadists. When others suffer, when others are in pain, when others wriggle in pain, it gives them enjoyment. So is God a sadist that He has created this world where old age is there, diseases are, death is there, and then people are suffering, and then He is there in the spiritual world, having a good time. So it's very bewildering. But for a pure devotee, it's not bewildering. He is in complete knowledge. And because He is in knowledge, a pure devotee enjoys life and pleasure of the Supreme Lord.

#### polluted consciousness, devotional service, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=55
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is the duty of every conditioned soul to engage his polluted consciousness, that is our present predicament, we are polluted, our consciousness is polluted, which is now attached to material enjoyment, in very serious devotional service with detachment."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the polluted consciousness of the conditioned soul and the need to engage in devotional service with detachment to purify it.

It is the duty of every conditioned soul to engage his polluted consciousness, that is our present predicament. We are polluted, our consciousness is polluted, which is now attached to material enjoyment, in very serious devotional service with detachment. So that's what we are trying to do, to engage in devotional service with detachment. Thus his mind and consciousness will be under full control, and that's our goal. Right now our consciousness is polluted, it is dragged towards material enjoyment, towards material beauty. Our goal is by practicing the process of devotional service. Our senses, our mind, our consciousness will get purified, and thereby our consciousness and mind will be in our control. So here it is said polluted consciousness, among the 24 material elements, the 24th element is polluted consciousness. We have the 5 working senses, 5 knowledge acquiring senses, 5 sense objects, 5 subtle cross elements, 3 subtle elements, and the 24th is polluted consciousness. So now this polluted consciousness, how this polluted, how the consciousness which is originally pure, how it has become polluted.

---
#### sense enjoyment, false ego, charity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=170
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This false thinking of false conditioned polluted consciousness is a result of contemplation on sense enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment explains that polluted consciousness arises from contemplating sense enjoyment, leading to the false belief of being the enjoyer and proprietor, and discusses the limitations of material enjoyment and the motivations behind charitable acts.

So Prabhupada gives very nice, clear understanding in the purport. This false thinking of false conditioned polluted consciousness is a result of contemplation on sense enjoyment. As a result of false contemplation on sense enjoyment, one starts believing that I am the enjoyer, I am the proprietor. This is the symptom of polluted consciousness. I am the well-wisher of other living entities. We see a lot of these days people, especially the rich people, it's now a very common trend, people committing 50% of their wealth. It started with the joy of giving, Bill Gates pledging billions of dollars in charity. And then many, many rich people followed, including Warren Buffet and so many rich people. And when we look at them, it appears that, you know, they're so charitably disposed. They're such wonderful human beings. They're apparently not attached to their wealth. They want to share their wealth with others, share their wealth to do good to the society, to uplift mankind, to help others, which is a very good sign. It's a very good thing. It is better than being very selfish and trying to hoard wealth and, you know, enjoy more and more. Actually, as living entities, we have limitation as to how much we can enjoy.

---
#### material enjoyment, satisfaction, limitation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=306
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Just like the typical example, if you see, if you want to eat rasgulla, you like rasgulla, you can eat two, three, four, up to four, you can really still enjoy. And then sets in the law of diminishing returns."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of eating rasgullas, this segment illustrates the limitations of material enjoyment and how even the wealthiest individuals eventually reach a point of saturation and dissatisfaction.

Just like the typical example, if you see, if you want to eat rasgulla, you like rasgulla, you can eat two, three, four, up to four, you can really still enjoy. And then sets in the law of diminishing returns. The fifth rasgulla, you will not like as much as you like the first one. And then as you keep eating, sometimes, you know, you have those competitions, especially in hostels. In mango mania, how many mangoes you can eat. So, a time comes when one gets fed up. So, many rich people, they have, you know, how much can you enjoy? Okay, at least we have hope, you know, I don't have a branded shirt, I don't have this thing, you know, common people, I don't have these shoes. You know, if you reach a point, you have everything, you can buy anything, but still, you're not happy. So then, just like here it says, polluted consciousness is, I am the enjoyer, that's the first thing, I want to enjoy matter. Now, there's a limitation how much you can enjoy. Just like we see, there's a saturation, even if you want to eat something, at one point of time, you cannot eat more. That's how there's all this buffet, they are making money. You know, they spread a lot, and finally a man can eat only 500, 600 grams. That's the logic behind all this so-called buffet spread. So one wants to enjoy, but there's a limitation.
