#### simplicity, satisfaction, conditioned soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=426
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And he ate that simple food, rice, dal. He especially requested, please make it simple, please make it simple. I am longing to eat that simple dal and simple rice."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about an expat staying in a five-star hotel who longed for simple home-cooked food, illustrating that material opulence does not guarantee satisfaction.

When I was in Bangalore many, many years back, I was staying with one of my relatives. And there was a person, an expat from the US, who was staying in a five-star hotel here, Windsor Manor. And he was staying in Windsor Manor for over six months, he was an Indian. And, you know, heart of hearts, staying in Windsor Manor, six months, rich man. Because typically when you go to, you know, five-star, it's all flashy, it's all decked up, it's all so comfortable. So you really, I'm not enjoying, these people are enjoying so much. You know, that's a conditioned soul's response. And that person was invited one day for dinner at our place. And he ate that simple food, rice, dal. He especially requested, please make it simple, please make it simple. I am longing to eat that simple dal and simple rice. So he had that, and then he was, I mean, I was there at the dining table with him. He said, it's so good, it's so tasty. If you don't mind, he's practically, I could see, pleading. Can I, you know, reluctantly, because how can you ask? Can I come maybe once in 10, 15 days? And then a cousin of mine says, yes, you can come, provided you invite us to Windsor Manor.

And we have heard about it, there are hopes of enjoyment, people who have done everything. Can buy anything, can eat anything, can stay anywhere. And still they find that something. Nothing is lacking."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how even those who have achieved material success and fulfilled their desires still experience a sense of lack and dissatisfaction, leading them to seek other avenues for fulfillment.

We can't afford anything, many times we go to a hotel, we have limitations based on the budget, we have to decide, yeh nahi kha sakta, yeh bahut mehenga hai. And imagine a person, the company is paying, he is one of the top CEOs, can eat anything. Sometimes when feast is there, some limits are there, only two rasgullas per person, no limits. The person is saying, can I eat simple food? So there are rich people who have done everything in life. We still have hopes, we still have not gone on cruise, we still have not gone on X, still have not done this, still have not visited this country. And we have heard about it, there are hopes of enjoyment, people who have done everything. Can buy anything, can eat anything, can stay anywhere. And still they find that something. Nothing is lacking. So such people, what do they do next? Bhoktaram, they have tried bhokta, there is a limitation, it is not satisfying intrinsically the soul. Then they also try to lord over. You know, you have become chairman and founder of a big multinational company. It is the biggest company in the world. Fortune 500. You are the chairman. If you are a manager in company, you want to be a CEO of that company, okay, that's done. Then CEO of a bigger company. Okay, then what? So you have become one of the best companies in the world. So then what? You have seen that also.

#### philanthropy, sense gratification, pure consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=686
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That doing good, that doing charity, that philanthropy with the mindset that I am supporting this cause. I am feeding this hungry children. When they get pictures of so many schools, children being fed, that inherent, Prabhupada says that is sense gratification only."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that even philanthropic acts can be motivated by a desire for sense gratification and that true well-being comes from understanding Krishna as the real enjoyer, proprietor, and well-wisher.

People are at your beck and call, people are at your command. You are very famous. The next comes. Suhridam sarva bhutanam. Although it looks very nice. Yes, it's nice in one sense. We cannot ridicule. It's a good sentiment to help others. Compassion, daya. When you see a person suffering, that's a natural human emotion to help others. That's good. But that doing good, that doing charity, that philanthropy with the mindset that I am supporting this cause. I am feeding this hungry children. When they get pictures of so many schools, children being fed, that inherent, Prabhupada says that is sense gratification only. They get a sense of satisfaction. So many children are getting fed because of me. That's very fine enjoyment. It's more than rasgulla enjoyment. So many villages, there were no drinking water. Now the drinking water has come. So many villages have benefited because of me. It's a finer sense gratification. At the face value, it looks very good. It is good. We are not denying that. But as a conditioned soul, there is a pollution in the consciousness. I am the enjoyer. I am the proprietor. I am the well-wisher of all living entities. Whereas Prabhupada here mentions, Krishna also mentions in the Gita, bhokta rāmyajña-suprasāṁ sarva-loka-maheśvaram suhṛdam sarva-bhūtānā. One who understands, one who is convinced, one whose consciousness is pure, he understands that Krishna is the real enjoyer. He is the bhokta. He is the real enjoyer. He is the real proprietor. He is the one who is sarva-loka-maheśvaram. And he is suhṛdam sarva-bhūtānā. He is the well-wisher of all living entities. Even when we want to help others, sometimes even so-called service borders between social service and this thing.

---
#### consciousness, devotional service, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=812
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Problem is what is the consciousness behind that act. With what consciousness we are doing that act."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the consciousness behind an action is more important than the action itself, explaining that actions performed with Krishna consciousness purify the doer.

We also want to do good to society. Paropakār. Janma sārtak kari paropakār. Paropakār. Sometimes even Akshāpātra people say, you know, it's a social service, feeding to hungry child. The problem is not in the act per se. Problem is what is the consciousness behind that act. With what consciousness we are doing that act. If we are doing that act with the consciousness that, you know, I am supporting, I am feeding, I am doing this thing. Then that is material consciousness. Then that's a material activity. Then that's a material service. But if we are doing that with the understanding, with the consciousness that everyone is part and parcel of Krishna. Krishna is the real maintainer. Krishna is the real bhokta. Krishna is the real proprietor. And I am just an insignificant speck. A part, an amsa, a servant. And this is being done by Krishna through me. Krishna is so kind that he is using me as an instrument. Provided that service is authorized. Then such service purifies us.

---
#### purification, senses, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=905
**Verse References:** BG 2.59
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The process which Prabhupāda is so mercifully given to all of us is to engage our mind and senses in the service of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains how to purify consciousness by engaging the mind and senses in the service of the Lord, rather than simply restraining them.

So Prabhupāda gives a very good thing. Now our consciousness is polluted. Our senses are contaminated. They are dabbling in sense objects. They have got attached to sense enjoyment. And mind is also impure. Now one thing is you restrict yourself. Restrain yourself. Not engage. Not act in sinful ways. So Prabhupāda says it's very difficult. Viṣyāvinivartante nirahārasya dehinā. So there are some so-called spiritualists or spiritualists who have tried to restrain their senses through yoga processes by abstaining the senses from sense objects. Example is given of Viṣyamitra Muni. But then the senses are even one of the roaming senses on which the mind focuses can carry away the person's intelligence. So the process which Prabhupāda is doing, the process which Prabhupāda is so mercifully given to all of us is to engage our mind and senses in the service of the Lord. We all have natural drives, natural inclinations. We are all rādhsik in that sense. A lot of passion. We want to do so many things. We want to achieve so many things. Okay, do it for Kṛṣṇa. We want to create so many things. Okay, create for Kṛṣṇa.

---
#### purification, devotional service, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1002
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Gradually what happens is when we do that, the mind becomes pure, the senses become pure."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that engaging in devotional service gradually purifies the mind and senses, teaching fundamental principles about Krishna's role as the real doer, enjoyer, and proprietor.

We engage the senses and the mind in the service of the Lord. Gradually what happens is when we do that, the mind becomes pure, the senses become pure. The consciousness, polluted consciousness, bhūkta, rāmas, and this service also is so wonderful when we do services. Sometimes we, you know, when we go for collection, somebody will not give, somebody will chastise us, somebody will, you know, harass us. In the process, or when we go for book distribution, sometimes a lot of books get sold, sometimes no book gets sold. In the process, we are learning the fundamental principles. What are the fundamental principles? That Kṛṣṇa is a real doer. Kṛṣṇa is a real enjoyer. Kṛṣṇa is a real proprietor. Apart from the results what we are trying to achieve, Kṛṣṇa is working, Prabhupāda is working to teach us some very fundamental principles which will purify our consciousness. Sometimes if we are not properly situated, we are overly focused on the results. And success or failure is completely dependent on whether I have got the results or not. But in devotional service, the beauty is even if so-called results are not there, the greatest benefit what we are getting by engaging in devotional service is purification of consciousness, irrespective of external results. So in that sense, there is no loss, there is no diminution.

---
#### kūṭam, karma, reactions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1099
**Verse References:** BG 3.36
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When there is a drive force enjoying the senses, that our ācāryas say is called kūṭam, that drive to enjoy."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the concepts of kūṭam, bījam, and falonmukham, explaining how the drive for sense enjoyment leads to actions, reactions, and entanglement in the material world.

So what happens when we are dabbling in sense enjoyment? When there is a drive force enjoying the senses, that our ācāryas say is called kūṭam, that drive to enjoy. Arjuna also asked Kṛṣṇa, why is it, Kṛṣṇa, that sometimes we are forced to act in sinful ways much against our wish? Even we realize in our day-to-day life, on one side we want to get up early in the morning, the other side, when we get up in the morning, when the alarm rings, there is another force which says, no, let me sleep. So Kṛṣṇa was asked, what is that force? Why is it that many times we act in sinful ways much against our wish, as if there is some kind of a push? So that push is called kūṭam, and that kūṭam results in actions, we act based on that force, and that actions deposit the reaction in our system, and that reactions are deposited in the form of bījam, the seed, bījam. And just like in a seed, we see different seed sprouts at different times, some seeds sprout in one day, some seed sprouts in four days. So likewise, this bījam fructifies into reaction, which manifests in the life as enjoyment or suffering, which is called a stage of falon mukham, falā, falā of the actions which we have done, and that stage is called falon mukham.

---
#### karma, avidyā, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1228
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So ācāryas have analyzed kūtam. Kūtam is that which forces us to act in sinful ways."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the root cause of kūṭam is kāma (lust), which stems from avidyā (ignorance) of one's true identity as a servant of Krishna.

So what we are seeing, different people enjoying and suffering differently, is what has started manifesting in their life. Sometimes people get bewildered when they see good people suffering or bad people enjoying life. That's because, even though at present they are doing good, but there are a lot of bījams which are stored from the actions done in the past, which are now fructifying. A person may have done good in the past, as a result there are good bījams stored, but currently he may be doing bad things, but still he may be enjoying the reactions of the past deeds. And vice versa, for a person who is doing good now, could be result of, still could be suffering because of the actions done in the past. Now ācāryas have even gone further, just like in any other field of activity, they do root cause analysis. What is the cause? What is the cause? What is the cause? So ācāryas have analyzed kūtam. Kūtam is that which forces us to act in sinful ways. Sinful ways or whatever, forces us to act. So they have analyzed what is a step behind kūtam. That force is called kāma, lust. Here it says desire to enjoy the senses. What is lust? Desire to enjoy independent of Kṛṣṇa. And they have gone one step ahead and realized what is the cause of that lust, desire, which results in kūtam, which results in bījam, which results in falonmukham. And they found out that the result of that lust, or kāma, is avidyā. In Bhāgavatam it is mentioned, avidyā kāma karma bhī.

---
#### avidyā, karma, entanglement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1371
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because of avidyā, this knowledge, the true knowledge, who I am, I am amsa of Kṛṣṇa, I am not the bhoktā, I am a small insignificant servant, dāsu asmi."
**Summary:** This segment explains how avidyā (ignorance) leads to lust, which in turn leads to karma and entanglement in the cycle of enjoyment and suffering in the material world.

Because of avidyā, this knowledge, the true knowledge, who I am, I am amsa of Kṛṣṇa, I am not the bhoktā, I am a small insignificant servant, dāsu asmi. Because of this avidyā, Prabhupāda also says, why this contaminated consciousness comes about? Because of contemplation on sense enjoyment. Sense enjoyment, independent of Kṛṣṇa, is lust. That results in avidyā, false understanding about ourself. I am the enjoyer, I am bhoktā, I am the proprietor. So avidyā, kāma, results in kāma, and karma bhī. We act, that deposits bījam, and then chain of actions and reactions, and then the living entity gets entangled in enjoyment and suffering in this material world. To relieve ourselves of that suffering, we further act. Those actions further create reactions. While we are enjoying or suffering the reactions of the past, to counteract those enjoyment and suffering, we further act, and like that, the action and reaction, one gets completely entangled.

---
#### devotional service, detachment, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1467
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So devotional service, one thing what it does is, it not only burns to ashes the bījams, the reactions of the deeds what we have done in the past, it creates no further reactions, no further bījam, and, it destroys kāma, and also destroys avidhyā."
**Summary:** This segment explains that devotional service not only eliminates past karmic reactions but also prevents future ones by destroying lust and ignorance, thus purifying consciousness.

So what devotional service does, here it says, one should very seriously engage in devotional service with detachment. So devotional service, one thing what it does is, it not only burns to ashes the bījams, the reactions of the deeds what we have done in the past, it creates no further reactions, no further bījam, and, it destroys kāma, and also destroys avidhyā. There are so many things which we can do, prāyaścitta, whereby we can take care of the reactions. By doing some yajñas, by doing some homas, we can nullify those reactions. But, that force which, you know, helplessly makes us act in sinful ways, that avidhyā, the ignorance of the self, ignorance of our true constitutional position, still is not addressed. So devotional service, bhakti yoga, by taking shelter of Kṛṣṇa, shelter of a pure devotee, we are not only burning all the sinful reactions of the past, creating no further reactions, because bhakti is akarma. It doesn't create any good or bad reactions. It destroys avidhyā, destroys kāma, cleans up our consciousness.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, goal, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So when we are engaging in devotional service, when we are, when we are practising the process of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, we should have this knowledge, what we are doing. What is the goal? What are we trying to achieve?"
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding the goal and purpose of engaging in devotional service, particularly in the context of polluted consciousness and the need for detachment.

So that is, so when we are engaging in devotional service, when we are, when we are practising the process of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, we should have this knowledge, what we are doing. What is the goal? What are we trying to achieve? Sometimes we can become very mechanical in our service, simply morning to evening doing something, carrying a bag, going here and there. We are engaging in devotional service, service of the Lord. The Lord is having a mission in this world. We are part of that mission. And in the process, we are getting purified. Let's stop here. Anyone has any question, anything like to ask? You can ask. All of us, we are engaged in devotional service, but particularly, if consciousness is polluted, and with that consciousness, how to bring detachment in that service? Because consciousness is, again, fruitive mentality. If I am doing also devotional service, I am expecting some result, some fruitive, my mind is acting in that direction only, some honour, something I want out of it.

---
#### purification, devotional service, patience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So we engage in devotional service, just like, you know, diamonds get rubbed, so this rubbing will go on here also."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that purification of consciousness is an inherent part of devotional service, achieved through a process of gradual refinement and the practice of hearing and chanting.

So with polluted consciousness, how to practice this thing, how to bring this element of detachment? So this purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system. One thing is, the world has been designed so beautifully by the Lord, when you act in wrong way, it will backfire. When you want to act as an enjoyer, act as a proprietor, it will hit you back, even as devotees. That's why Krishna said, bhau naam janma naam ante, jnan maante. After many times getting hit, why not? And now I realise, I am not the enjoyer. So we will start doing devotional service, with that same consciousness, I am the enjoyer. I want to enjoy peace of mind. I want to enjoy lording over bhaktas. We start with that. You cannot help it. Theoretically it will not help. From today onwards, I will not enjoy. From today onwards, I will not think I am the proprietor. It doesn't happen. It will be at a mental level. So we engage in devotional service, just like, you know, diamonds get rubbed, so this rubbing will go on here also. So we will collect, we will think that I am a big collector, finally you will get a hit. No, I am not the collector. You go for book distribution, one day you will distribute a lot of books, wow, you get a feeling, I am a good, I am very expert, very nice. See, I convinced him. Next day you do all your convincing, and nobody will buy a book. Then you realise, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincer. This happens over a period of time. And while we are engaged in devotional service, slowly, dhire dhire, dhire dhire, that purification happens. One comes to our senses. Unfortunately, to some extent, you can fast track the process, otherwise it will take time. That's why it's a dhairiya, patience. You start with polluted consciousness, as we engage, the purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system. For that, what we have to do, we have to, we have to, the process, shravanam, hearing, shunvata, swakataha, krishna, punya, shravana, kirtana, nashta, praesho, badreshu, nityam, bhagavata, sevaya, when we do that, all this abhadras, dirty things, which is clinging to us, clinging to our consciousness, which has overpowered our consciousness, completely gets subsided. So, we have to, just like diamond, it's actually a very precious stone, but when you see original, this thing, it is unpolished, uncut, it is not shiny, but because of that polish, because of rubbing, because of different treatment, it goes through, it starts shining. Same is the case, what happens with us. Okay? Stop here.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2019 - 01- 17 | SB 3. 32. 28|  HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### supreme absolute truth, energy of the Lord, Vaishnava philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=50
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vaishnava philosophers do not accept the different manifestations as false; they accept them as non-different from the Supreme Personality of Godhead because they are a display of his diverse energies."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the Vaishnava philosophical perspective that the diverse manifestations of the world are not false but are non-different from the Supreme Personality of Godhead, being displays of His energies, contrasting this with the Brahma Satyam Jagam Idhya philosophy.

The supreme absolute truth, the Personality of Godhead, is one, and He is spread everywhere by His impersonal feature. This is clearly expressed in the Bhagavad-Gita. Lord Krishna says everything that is experienced is but an expansion of my energy. Everything is sustained by Him, but that does not mean that He is in everything. Sense perceptions, such as oral perception of the sound of a drum, visual perception of a beautiful woman, or perception of a delicious taste of a milk preparation by the tongue, all come through different senses and are therefore differently understood. Therefore, sensory knowledge is divided in different categories, although actually everything is one as a manifestation of the energy of the Supreme Lord. Similarly, the energies of fire are heat and illumination, and by these two energies, fire can display itself in many varieties or in diversified sense perception. Mayavadi philosophers declared the diversity felt that it was false, but Vaishnava philosophers do not accept the different manifestations as false. They accept them as non-different from the Supreme Personality of Godhead because they are a display of his diverse energies. The philosophy that the absolute is true and this creation is false, Brahma Satyam Jagam Idhya, is not accepted by Vaishnava philosophers. The example is given that although all that glitters is not gold, this does not mean that a glittering object is false. For example, an oyster shell appears to be golden. This appearance of golden hue is due to the perception of the eyes, but that does not mean that the oyster shell is false. Similarly, by seeing the form of Lord Krishna, one cannot understand what He actually is.

---
#### knowledge, form of Krishna, sense perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The form of Krishna has to be understood as it is described in the books of knowledge such as Brahma Samhita."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the true form of Lord Krishna can only be understood through authorized scriptures like Brahma Samhita, not through imperfect sense perception or mental speculation, and warns against considering Krishna a common man.

This does not mean that He is false. The form of Krishna has to be understood as it is described in the books of knowledge such as Brahma Samhita. Ishwara Parama Krishna Satchitananda Vigraha. Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, has an eternal, blissful, spiritual body. By our imperfect sense perception, we cannot understand the form of the Lord. We have to acquire knowledge about Him. Therefore it is said here, Gyanam Ekam. Bhagavad Gita confirms that there are fools who, simply upon seeing Krishna, consider Him a common man. They do not know the unlimited knowledge, power, and opulence of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Material sense speculation leads to the conclusion that the Supreme is formless. It is because of such mental speculation that the conditioned soul remains in ignorance under the spell of illusory energy. The Supreme Person has to be understood by the transcendental sound vibrated by Him in the Bhagavad Gita, wherein He says that He is nothing superior to Himself. The impersonal Brahman effulgence is resting on His personality. The purified absolute vision of the Bhagavad Gita is compared to the true and absolute consciousness of the Supreme, to the river Ganges. Ganges water is so pure that it can purify even the asses and cows. But anyone who, disregarding the pure Ganges, wishes to be purified instead by the filthy water flowing in a drain cannot be successful. Similarly, one can successfully attain pure knowledge of the Absolute only by hearing from the pure Absolute Himself. In this verse, it is clearly said.

---
#### knowledge, consciousness, scripture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=275
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One has to acquire pure knowledge from the authorized scriptures."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that those averse to the Supreme Personality of Godhead speculate with imperfect senses, emphasizing that true knowledge is acquired through oral reception from authorized scriptures, leading to purified consciousness and liberation from repeated birth and death.

That those who are averse to the Supreme Personality of Godhead speculate with their imperfect senses about the nature of the Absolute Truth. The formless Brahman conception, however, can be received only by oral reception and not by personal experience. Knowledge is therefore acquired by oral reception. It is confirmed in the Vedanta Sutra, Shastra Yonit Tvaat. One has to acquire pure knowledge from the authorized scriptures. So-called speculative arguments about the Absolute Truth are therefore useless. The actual identity of the living entity is his consciousness, which is always present while the living entity is awake, dreaming, or in deep sleep. Even in deep sleep, he can perceive by consciousness whether he is happy or distressed. Thus, when consciousness is displayed through the medium of subtle and gross material bodies, it is covered. But when the consciousness is purified in Krishna consciousness, one becomes free from the entanglement of repeated birth and death. When uncontaminated pure knowledge is unencumbered from the modes of material nature, the actual identity of the living entity is discovered. He is eternally a servitor of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. The process of uncovering is like this. The rays of sunshine are luminous, and the sun itself is also luminous. In the presence of the sun, the rays illuminate just like the sun. But when the sunshine is covered by the spell of a cloud, or maya, then darkness, the imperfection, or of perception, begins. Therefore, to get out of the entanglement of the spell of nascence, one has to awaken his spiritual consciousness or Krishna consciousness in terms of authorized scriptures. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Papad.

---
#### supreme lord, maya, energy of the lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=386
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything, including Maya, is energy of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that everything in creation, both material and spiritual, is Krishna and His energies, including Maya, which is not an inimical force but an energy of the Lord that covers the knowledge and pure consciousness of the living entity.

So here in this verse, the understanding of the Supreme Lord, the right and the wrong process, how we can go about understanding the Supreme Lord, is mentioned. So Prabhupada in the purport explains, the Supreme Absolute Truth, the Personality of Godhead, is one, and He is spread everywhere by His impersonal feature. So whatever we see in this creation, both material and spiritual, this gigantic universe, is not the same. It is not the same. This gigantic universe, with so many planets, so many stars, huge creation, the essence is that it is all but Krishna and His energies. There is nothing else. Everything we see in the creation is nothing but the Lord and His energies, manifestation of His energies. Now there is a very powerful energy of the Lord, which is called Maya Shakti, which is also called Avidya Shakti. It is the energy of the Lord. Even Maya is the energy of the Lord. Sometimes we get a feeling that Maya is an inimical force like the demons. In the Christian world, they are called Saturn, a demoniac force. And then there is the Lord and His forces, and they are, so to say, battling. The right understanding is everything, including Maya, is energy of the Lord. Including Avidya, Avidya Shakti, which covers the knowledge of the living entity, which covers the pure consciousness of the living entity, which is the Lord's energy.

---
#### maya, avidya shakti, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=519
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Clouds don't cover the sun, clouds cover our vision."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of why Maya appears so powerful if the Lord is all-powerful, explaining through the analogy of clouds and the sun that Maya, or Avidya Shakti, doesn't cover the Lord but covers our vision, facilitating the fulfillment of our desires to be enjoyers and the center of attraction in the material world.

Sometimes people wonder if the Lord is all powerful, He is all kind, He is all loving, then why is Maya so powerful? So it appears that Maya is more powerful, and even though we are chanting, even though we are trying to practice the principles of Krishna consciousness, it overpowers us. The example is given that just like the clouds are created by the sun, in that sense, the sun is the cause of the cloud. And when there are a lot of clouds in the sky, it appears that the sun is covered. Sometimes when it is very cloudy, it is about to rain, you can go out in the sky and say that today there is no sun, because it is all cloudy. Actually, if you see, clouds have no power to cover the sun. Although it is created by the sun, it is said that the sun is 14,000 times bigger than earth. And the clouds may be few kilometers, few miles, may be 20 miles, 10 miles, 15 miles. But when the sun and the size of sun and the size of earth, 15 kilometers, 15 miles is insignificant. Forget about 15 miles, just a small cloud can cover sometimes when we are in the sun, we want sunlight. Just a small cloud comes and for 3 minutes or 5 minutes the sun is so called hidden. There is no sunlight. So sun can never be covered by clouds. It is 14,000 times bigger than earth. But it appears to us that there is no sun in the sky. So what clouds do is clouds don't cover the sun, clouds cover our vision. So this Shakti of the Lord, Avidya Shakti, it is coming from the Lord, it has a purpose. We have come in this world to become enjoyers. We have come in this world to become the Lord, to be the center of attraction, to enjoy name, fame. We want to be glorified. So then our true constitutional position has to be so to say covered.

Then only I can think that I am the enjoyer. To facilitate fulfillment of our desires this Avidya Shakti is there which acts on the living entity. If you see there are lot of books in the market how to be happy, how to enjoy life, how to live. And then Krishna also says that many times when we are also so to say bashed by Maya are many of us when we come to Krishna conscious, in fact not many of us, Krishna in the Gita says there are four kinds of living entities who will surrender to the Lord, who will come to the Lord. Chaturvida, Bhajante, Imam. So one of them is who is in distress, who is inquisitive, who is in knowledge, who is desirer of wealth.

So initially the living entities may come to the Lord but not with the pure desire to be conscious of Him, to serve Him. It could be just because a person wants relief from attacks of Maya. So different people are trying different means to come out of Maya or illusory energy. But Krishna is giving us that unless we surrender to Him, unless that root disease for which we have come in this material world, unless that is, unless we are purified of the root disease that we have Ichcha means desire to enjoy independent of Lord and Dvesha, envy. So unless we become devotees of the Lord, surrender to Him, this Maya Shakti will continue to do its designed work. It is designed for a particular objective. Many times we want to come out of Maya but without being conscious of Lord, without being Krishna, through some material jugglery or through material adjustments which will never be possible. Stopgap, quick fix solutions, some peace of mind, all that is possible. But a real solution to the problem, real happiness which we are hankering to achieve can only come when we become conscious of the Lord. That is Krishna Consciousness which Prabhupada here in the purport mentions is Krishna Consciousness.

#### religion, spirituality, understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=1139
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All religions with different instructions, with different details, with different prescriptions, with different dos and don'ts are trying to take living entities who have got covered with avidya shakti to different degrees, whose consciousness has been covered to take them from one level of their consciousness to the next level."
**Summary:** The speaker explains why people conclude that religion is problematic, attributing it to the seemingly contradictory instructions across different religions, and uses the analogy of a school system to illustrate that various religions offer different levels of instruction to elevate individuals with varying degrees of spiritual understanding.

So why do people come to this conclusion? Because people come to this conclusion when they see that all religions are having instructions which apparently are contradictory. If you go to Christianity they will say Jesus is the only way. If you come to us we will say Krishna is the only way. And some fanatic people would say you know my God is better than your God. Or my religion is better than your religion. And that's how many people in the name of God are trying to save other souls by converting them. But the true understanding is just like in our colleges, in our schools we have primary school, we have secondary school, we have senior secondary school, we have pre-university, then we have university, graduation, and then we have post-grad. All religions with different instructions, with different details, with different prescriptions, with different dos and don'ts are trying to take living entities who have got covered with avidya shakti to different degrees, whose consciousness has been covered to take them from one level of their consciousness to the next level. So that way no religion is bad. Even the so called religion where we see there is a prescription of eating meat. Again we have to understand in context why they are prescribing. But it has a place. Just like we cannot take a two year old or a child who is supposed to be attending second standard and make him sit with a tenth standard student. The same book cannot be taught. Because there are different levels. So different religions are giving different instructions according to time, place and circumstances to take the living entity who has got covered with this avidya shakti whose consciousness has got polluted to the next higher level of purification. Just like we have maths being taught in primary school and then we have secondary school and then we have higher secondary school. All of them are doing their respective duties.

#### yoga, connection, bhakti yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=1372
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The connection between the Lord and the living entity is still there."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the connection between the Lord and the living entity always exists, even when forgotten, and introduces the four yogas discussed in the Gita—karma yoga, jnana yoga, ashtanga yoga, and bhakti yoga—as paths for living entities at different levels of spiritual growth.

Who has forgotten Lord now the living entity can forget the connection with the Lord but that does not mean that connection does not exist a child can forget his father but the connection is still there he is born to a father he can deny that he is not my father he can deny that I don't care of you but then the connection is there so likewise Krishna broadly speaking although we hear of so many yogas these days power yoga and fire yoga you see that the connection between the Lord and the living entity is still there and the connection between the Lord and the living entity is still there billboards you know different yoga is getting because the word yoga has become so international yoga day broadly speaking Krishna talks about four yogas and again it is meant for living entities who are at different levels of their spiritual growth in the third chapter Krishna talks about karma yoga and then we have jnana yoga ashtanga yoga and finally Krishna talks about bhakti yoga

#### karma yoga, detachment, supreme
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=1631
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Karma yoga is art of doing our work in such a way that we do not get entangled more and more in this material world."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on karma yoga, emphasizing that it involves performing one's duty without attachment to results, offering the results to the Supreme, and avoiding entanglement in the material world, distinguishing it from simply considering work as worship.

Yoga it is prescribed that simultaneously you should not be attached to not doing your duty many times we are attached to doing the duty because we aspire for results so karma yoga is where you do not be attached to the results do as a matter of duty without desire for sense gratification and offering the results of the work to the supreme but frankly speaking if we realize the karma yoga part the clear understanding of who that supreme is is not there many people say I am a karma yogi what they actually mean is that I for me work is worship that is not karma yoga. Karma yoga involves one can do some work which one likes doing but offer the results of that work to supreme may not be with a clear understanding of who that supreme is. So that's the stepping stone towards higher levels of consciousness because when we are attached to the results and when we desire to achieve it desire to enjoy the results which is the root Ichcha Dvesha. Ichcha means desire to enjoy, lust. Krishna says Iha Vairinam, it's the greatest enemy of the living, it entangles. So Karma Yoga is art of doing our work in such a way that we do not get entangled more and more in this material world. Sometimes we get this understanding that morning till 9 o'clock we do Bhakti Yoga and after 9.30 when we go out for different services we do Karma Yoga and then when we come back in the evening, Sheya Naarti onwards we do again Bhakti Yoga. For some time I had that understanding. It was not very clear because even Karma Yoga, Krishna talks offering the results of work to me, to the Supreme. Yajnat Karmano Anyatra Lokoyam Karma Bandhana. Yajnat Karmano Anyatra Lokoyam Karma Bandhana.

#### jnana yoga, speculation, impersonalism

**Summary:** The speaker transitions to Jnana Yoga, where one inquires about the Supreme, but cautions against speculation without scriptural understanding, which can lead to impersonal conclusions about the Supreme being a formless energy due to limitations of time, space, and imperfections.

So Jnana Yoga is a place where a person starts inquiring who is that Supreme and that inquiry can be backed by speculation, not necessarily through scriptural understanding, understanding from authorized scriptures. When can start using speculation to understand who that Supreme is, then it becomes very difficult to understand who that Supreme is. This or that Supreme, I simply doesn't know how to interpret it. And I would try to make us understand in the same way as we try to understand Supreme and that Supreme. And often we come across, you know, Prabhupada mentions about mayavadis, impersonals. Using logic, using inference one can conclude that the Supreme is impersonal. He has no form, he is not a person, he is some powerful force, some energy. Butorigine, curiosity, mayavadis, impersonalism, Just like we have an understanding that a person is limited by time and space. If you are here today, it's very clear for me that you are not in the ashram. Because you cannot simultaneously be there in two places. Knowing that Lord is omnipresent, He is present everywhere, using logic one can conclude that the Supreme Absolute Truth is impersonal. Because person means limited by time and space. And also we see person in our experience undergoing old age, disease, death. But the Lord who is Supreme, who is beyond all these imperfections, Janma, Mrityu, Jara, Vyadi, so He has to be impersonal.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2019 - 02- 08 | SB 3. 32. 33 |  HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### bhakti yoga, supreme person, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ng51E26XRgU?t=138
**Verse References:** BG 9.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimately, the supreme person is the destination of all different processes."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while different paths exist to find the supreme godhead, bhakti yoga allows one to see the supreme person face to face, and devotional service is the only path to appreciate the supreme truth perfectly.

When we see milk, we see that it is white. When we taste it, it appears that the milk is very palatable. When we touch milk, it appears very cold. When we smell milk, it appears to be very good flavor. And when we hear, we understand it is called milk. Perceiving milk with different senses, we say that it is something white, something very delicious, something very aromatic, and so on. Actually, it is milk. Similarly, those who are trying to find the supreme godhead by mental speculation may approach the bodily effulgence, or the impersonal Brahman. And those who are trying to find the supreme godhead by yoga practice may find him as localized super soul. But those who are directly trying to approach the supreme truth by practice of bhakti yoga can see him face to face as the supreme person. Ultimately, the supreme person is the destination of all different processes. The fortunate person who, by following the principles of scriptures, becomes completely purified of all material contamination, surrenders unto the supreme lord as everything. Just as one can appreciate the real taste of milk with the tongue, and not with eyes, nostrils, or ears, one can similarly appreciate the supreme truth perfectly and with all relishable pleasure, only through one path, devotional service. This is also confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita, bhakti avam abhijanati. If one wants to understand the absolute truth in perfection, he must take to devotional service. Of course, no one can understand the truth in perfection, that is not possible for the infinitesimal living entities, but the highest point of understanding by the living entity is reached by discharge of devotional service, not otherwise.

---
#### brahmananda, transcendence, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ng51E26XRgU?t=240
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One should try, therefore, to transcend even the Brahman pleasure in order to approach the position of dealing with Krishna face to face."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the pleasure derived from impersonal Brahman with the higher pleasure of devotional service to Krishna, stating that the latter surpasses even the unlimited Brahmananda.

By following various scriptural paths, one may come to impersonal effulgence of the supreme personality of Godhead. The transmittal pleasure derived from merging with or understanding the impersonal Brahman is very extensive because Brahman is ananta, tad brahma, nishkalam anantam. Brahmananda is unlimited, but the unlimited pleasure can also be surpassed. That is the nature of the transcendence. The unlimited can be surpassed also, and that higher platform is Krishna. When one deals directly with Krishna, the mellow and the humor relished by reciprocation of devotional service is incomparable, even with the pleasure derived from transcendental Brahman. Prabodhananda Saraswati therefore says that kaivalya, the Brahman pleasure, is undoubtedly very great and is appreciated by many philosophers. But to a devotee who has understood how to derive pleasure from exchanging devotional service with the Supreme Lord, this unlimited Brahman appears to be hellish. One should try, therefore, to transcend even the Brahman pleasure in order to approach the position of dealing with Krishna face to face.

---
#### senses, Krishna, perception

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As the mind is the center of all the activities of the senses, Krishna is called the master of the senses, Rishikesha."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna is the master of the senses, Rishikesha, and that bhakti is the basic principle of all processes, without which Jnana Yoga and Ashtanga Yoga cannot be successful.

As the mind is the center of all the activities of the senses, Krishna is called the master of the senses, Rishikesha. The process is to fix the mind on Rishikesha, or Krishna, as Maharaj Amritsar did. Bhakti is the basic principle of all process. Without Bhakti, neither Jnana Yoga nor Ashtanga Yoga can be successful. And unless one approaches Krishna, the principles of self-realization has no ultimate destination. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport. So Prabhupada in the Purport mentions that how milk, when perceived by devotees, through the different senses, appears differently. Actually, we perceive holistically through all the senses. If we were to explain to somebody what a milk is, then all the inputs which we have gathered through various senses will be different. And if we were to explain to somebody what a milk is, then all the inputs which we have gathered through various senses, will be different.

#### perception, senses, milk analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ng51E26XRgU?t=376
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "For him, milk is only a liquid which is white in color."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of perceiving milk through different senses to illustrate how different people perceive the Supreme Lord based on their spiritual development and the processes they follow.

That perception which we have formed through various senses will be articulated and explained. So we will say it is white in color, it has so and so aroma, it is very tasty, it smells like this, it is liquid. But take a scenario, a person who has only eyes, just for the discussion sake, who does not have other senses, there are sometimes people who are deaf and dumb, some people who cannot taste. So hypothetical case, assuming that a person has only one sense, eyes, for him, milk is only a liquid which is white in color. He has no idea of aroma, he has no idea of how it tastes. Or for that matter, a person who is blind, he may not have the perception that milk is white in color. But a person whose all senses are in order, are working, he can perceive the taste, the aroma, how it looks, the color, the characteristics. We can say it is something white, something very delicious, something very aromatic, something very sweet, something very sweet, something very sweet, something very sweet, something very aromatic, and so on. So likewise, the Supreme Lord can also be perceived. Just like Prabhupada is giving the example of milk.

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

Just like Prabhupada is giving the example of milk. Not everyone's transcendental senses are awakened. And as a result, different people following, different spiritualists following different processes can perceive the same Supreme Lord. Which is the same end destination. Everyone is in this case talking about milk. But a blind man may not perceive it as white. Or a person who is deaf and dumb will perceive it something differently. So likewise, different processes are there for God realization. And different so-called spiritualists are practicing different processes and perceiving Supreme Lord differently. Although they all are about the same person. In the ultimate sense.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### 2019- 01- 04 | SB 3. 32. 26 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### knowledge, bhakti, realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=46
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimately, to understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead, one has to accept Bhakti Yoga."
**Summary:** This segment explains that while the Supreme Lord can be understood as impersonal Brahman or Paramatma through Gyana Yoga or Dhyana Yoga, the complete understanding comes through Bhakti Yoga.

The word Drishi Adi Bhi is significant. According to Jiva Goswami, Drishi means Gyana, philosophical research. By different processes of philosophical research under different concepts, such as the process of Gyana Yoga, the same Bhagavan or the Supreme Personality of Godhead is understood as impersonal Brahman. Similarly, by the eightfold yoga system, he appears as the Paramatma. But in pure Krishna consciousness or knowledge in purity, when one tries to understand the supreme absolute truth, one realizes him as the supreme person. The transcendence is realized simply on the basis of knowledge. The words used here, Paramatma, Eshwara, Puman are all transcendental and they refer to Supersoul. Supersoul is also described as Purusha, but the word Bhagavan directly refers to the Supreme Personality of Godhead who is full of opulence, six opulence, wealth, fame, strength, beauty, knowledge, and renunciation. He is the personality of Godhead in different spiritual skies. The various descriptions of Paramatma, Eshwara, and Puman indicate that the expansions of the Supreme Personality of Godhead are unlimited. Ultimately, to understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead, one has to accept Bhakti Yoga. By executing Gyana Yoga or Dhyana Yoga, one has to eventually approach the Bhakti Yoga platform, and then, Paramatma, Eshwara, Puman, etc. are all clearly understood.

---
#### devotional service, liberation, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=165
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only for the devotees is the Lord visible."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of devotional service, stating that even those seeking liberation must engage in it, and that only devotees can see the Supreme Lord face to face, unlike the Jnani or Yogi.

It is recommended in the Second Chant of Srimad Bhagavatam that whether one is a devotee or a fruitive actor or a liberationist, if he is intelligent enough, he should engage himself with all seriousness in the process of devotional service. It is also explained that whatever one desires, which is obtainable by fruitive activities, even if one wants to be elevated to higher planets, can be achieved simply by execution of Dhyana Yoga, or devotional service. Since the Supreme Lord is full in six opulences, he can bestow any one of them upon the worshipper. The one Supreme Personality of Godhead reveals himself to different thinkers as the Supreme Person or Impersonal Brahman or Paramatma. Impersonalists merge into the Impersonal Brahman, but that is not achieved by worshipping the Impersonal Brahman. If one takes to devotional service, and at the same time desires to merge into the existence of Supreme Lord, he can achieve that. If someone desires at all to merge into the existence of Supreme, he has to execute devotional service. The devotee can see the Supreme Lord face to face, but the Jnani, the Empiric Philosopher or Yogi cannot. They cannot be elevated to the position of associates of the Lord. There is no evidence in the scriptures stating that by cultivating knowledge or by worshipping the Impersonal Brahman, one can become a personal associate of the Supreme Personality. Nor by executing the yogic principles can one become an associate of the Supreme Godhead. Only for the devotees is the Lord visible.

---
#### Supreme Personality of Godhead, impersonal Brahman, Paramatma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=277
**Verse References:** BG 8.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Paramatma feature and the Brahman feature are invisible, but Bhagavan feature is visible."
**Summary:** This segment explains the different features of the Lord, emphasizing that the Paramatma and Brahman features are invisible, while the Bhagavan feature is visible, and that the supreme abode of the Lord is the ultimate destination.

Here the statement, Drishi Adi B is significant. Since the Supreme Personality of Godhead is both invisible and visible, there are different features of the Lord. The Paramatma feature and the Brahman feature are invisible, but Bhagavan feature is visible. In the Vishnu Purana this fact is very nicely explained. The universal form of the Lord and the formless Brahman effulgence of the Lord being invisible are inferior features. The concept of the universal form is material, and the concept of impersonal Brahman is spiritual. But the highest spiritual understanding is the personality of Godhead. The Vishnu Purana states, vishnu brahma svarupena svayam eva vyastitihi. Brahman's real feature is Vishnu or the Supreme Brahman is Vishnu, svayam eva that is his personal feature. The supreme spiritual conception is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. It is confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita, yad gatva na nivartante tad dhamam paramam mama. That specific abode called paramam mama is the place from which once one attains it, one does not return to the miserable conditional life. Every place, every space and everything belongs to Vishnu. But when he personally lives in tad dhamam paramam, his supreme abode, one has to make one's destination the supreme abode of the Lord. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport.

#### understanding, Supreme Lord, impersonal Brahman
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=369
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "It is just a matter of who knows the best."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of three villagers observing a train to illustrate how the Supreme Lord appears differently to different people based on their level of understanding.

The Supreme Personality of Godhead alone is complete transcendental knowledge. But according to different processes of understanding, he appears differently. Either as impersonal Brahman, as Paramatma, as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, or as the Purushavatara. We've all heard this example of three villagers. This was in those days when trains were very newly introduced. Not many had seen the trains. Not many had travelled by the trains. Unlike today where every one of us, practically everyone has some time or other travelled in train. So these villagers, they were quite, they'd heard about it. Somebody had told them that trains have been introduced. Like sometimes when something is newly introduced, people are newly, just like now the big statue of Sardar Patel. So everyone is inquisitive to go and have a look, to be there. So these three villagers, they set out so that they can get a better understanding of what a train looks like. So one of them had some faint idea, some idea about what the train is. And when he saw, when he heard the train approaching on the track from a distance and making the sound, that engine sound, and a sharp beam of light falling on the tracks, it was something similar to what he had heard about the train. And he also wanted to be the first one to go back to the village and, you know, it's always good to be the first one, right? There is some rasai in that. I did it for the first time. So he went back, rushed back to the village and naturally the villagers were quite inquisitive. How does the train look like? So he described sharp beam of light and makes a sound like this and moves on a track. So the villagers were quite happy with the answer. And the second one was relatively more patient. He kept on moving the train. He kept on moving towards the track and actually he saw the train not only making a sound like this, not only throwing a sharp beam of light on the tracks, actually there were engines, there were bogies, and they were all connected, they were moving. Between the bogies there was some connection. And each bogies were lit with some light. There were some passengers inside. So he was also quite clear that he has a better understanding of train and he rushed back. And he gave his understanding of train to the villagers. And the third one kept moving and actually reached the station where the train came and halted. And there he could see further more details. He saw actually in the engine there is a driver and he was busy putting the coal in the engine. And he saw the passengers. They are coming down the bogies with their luggage and some of them getting inside, some of them putting the luggage. There is a place to keep the luggage. There is a place like that. Whole lot of details. Now is the person who was the first to rush back to the village wrong in his understanding of train? The answer is no. He is right. He also has some understanding of train. It actually makes a sound like this. It also throws a sharp beam of light on the tracks. It moves on the track. But it is not a complete understanding. The second one, was he wrong in his description of train? The answer is no. He was right. Whatever he observed, with all integrity he explained. But again it is a partial understanding of what the train is. So likewise here this verse is mentioning the Supreme Lord appears differently. Just like the train appears differently. The three villagers, it appears differently. It is just a matter of who knows the best.

Believe in God but using predominantly Jnana process. They come to the understanding that the Lord is impersonal. That the Lord is impersonal Brahman. And there are some Yogis who predominantly use the Yogic process. Ashtanga Yoga process. Everything is Yoga. But Ashtanga Yoga which is Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama. Sit in rigid posture, meditate on the Lord within the heart. They come to the understanding that Lord is present as Supersoul or Paramatma. The Paramatma feature of the Supreme Lord. They all are not wrong but not necessarily have the complete understanding of the Supreme Absolute Truth. And Prabhupada mentions here in the purport that only by devotional service. Bhakti Yoga. #### Vedic culture, Charvak Muni, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=756
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Final goal is enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the Vedic understanding of life with the philosophy of Charvak Muni, who advocated for enjoyment as the ultimate goal, even through unethical means.

In Vedic times, everyone had a very clear understanding about, predominantly, that the Lord is there and we have to serve Him. The goal of life is not to eat, drink, be merry, enjoy life. Who has seen the next life. That was not the philosophy by and large which people were practicing. Then we have heard about. Prabhupada mentions in his books about Charvak Muni. He had a philosophy. Charvak Muni means you know. They speculate and come up with an understanding of what the world is. So he had a philosophy which mentioned that ultimately the goal of life is to enjoy. And he says, because we have to enjoy, eat lot of Ghee. Gritam Privet. Rinam Kritva Gritam Privet. Now where you get Ghee. Ghee is very expensive. Many of us know Ghee is good cooking medium. We should have Ghee. But then, pure Ghee. It's very expensive. Not everyone can afford it. So he says, Rinam Kritva. You beg, borrow or steal. You get Ghee and enjoy life. Eat nicely. Then comes the question. If beg, borrow and steal. Won't you get caught. Won't you be accountable. In case something goes wrong. He says that you don't worry. Because ultimately the body has to become ash. There is no concept of soul. After life. God. Somebody watching. Pious. Punya. Papa. What if you get caught. Just eat. Drink. Be merry. Enjoy life. Final goal is enjoyment. Cook or crook. Enjoy. That is Charvak philosophy.

#### karma, universal law, subjectivity vs. objectivity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JM8-wrLDjXw?t=215
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The law of karma, what reaction you get, is it dependent on what you think as right or wrong? Or irrespective of what you think is right or wrong, it is a well defined law."
**Summary:** The speaker questions whether the law of karma is subjective, dependent on individual understanding, or an objective, well-defined law, using the example of driving on the wrong side of the road in the US.

We say wrong action means there will be reaction, papa, and as a result, you will have to suffer. And good action means, punya, means the reaction will be prosperity, happiness, good reaction. Now law of karma varies from country to country, varies from or it depends on what our understanding is of right and wrong. Yes, no. The law of karma, what reaction you get, is it dependent on what you think as right or wrong? Or irrespective of what you think is right or wrong, it is a well defined law. How many of you have said it's a well defined law? For example, I go to the US. I'm quite comfortable driving on the left side. I think, I think in my opinion, it is much better, it's much more comfortable, it's much more safer to drive on the left side. I think so. In my opinion, that is better. Am I allowed? If I drive on the left side, will I be penalized? So very fundamental question to you all, before we get into actions. Right or wrong, is it left to my judgment, my opinion, my thinking or irrespective what I think right or wrong, there is some actions which are right, some actions which are wrong. How many of us say irrespective what I think right or wrong, there are some actions are wrong, some actions are right, basis which will get reaction.

Know it, there will be a bad reaction. And just like Prabhupada gives an example, a child puts his hand in fire, there is a law that it will burn irrespective of whether the child knows it or does not know it. Ignorance of law is not an excuse. So therefore, in human form of life, we have got developed intelligence. Why are we held responsible when a dog crosses the street, when there is red light on, will the dog be given a chalan? Why? Have you seen a policeman stopping a dog and giving a chalan, drunk driving? No, because he does not have the intelligence to discriminate, to understand, this is right this is wrong, red light means I will have to stop. It is not expected out of him. He is not having the required discrimination, intelligence to find out. Hence, it's let go.

#### human responsibility, karma, universal law
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JM8-wrLDjXw?t=531
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Right and wrong is not subject to our understanding or our opinion. There are higher laws."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that human beings are held responsible for their actions due to their intelligence and that right and wrong are not subject to personal understanding but are governed by higher, universal laws.

As human beings, if you are driving and red light, you don't stop. You can't say, I didn't see. Last time I said, you know, I was first time when I went to Bangalore, I was on a two-wheeler driving on the road. I was stopped. I was surprised. I had helmet, I had license, everything was there. And in one point, I just started questioning the person who stopped, why are you penalizing me? He said, without even asking me, he took out his that chalaan book and started cutting a chalaan. Then he told me, dekha nahi aapne? What was the reason? It was one-way traffic. Then I literally pleaded, no, I'm sorry, I didn't see it, I'm new to it, nahi aage se nahi karunga. Nothing doing. Nothing. Chalaan kada. So, in human form of life, we are leading our lives with our own understanding of what is right, what is wrong. That's why sometimes people think, maine toh kush bhi gulp nahi kiya, ek aadmi ko maine tang nahi kiya, sab ka bhala hi socha hai maine, lekin mujhe kyun saffar karne ko mil raha hai. That's a different thing. Karmic reactions are there of the previous life, previous actions also. But even in this life, we may be thinking that I am doing everything right. For example, some people who think that I am a vegetarian, I'm great. Done. But Krishna says, bunja te te te agham papam ye pachantiya. Even it's better than eating non-veg, but even if we do not offer the food to the Lord, even that incurs some amount of sin. So right and wrong is not subject to our understanding or our opinion. There are higher laws.

#### five factors of action, material causes, supreme cause

You see the place of action, many times you have heard about people saying, this place is not good. Have you heard of this? And now these are Vastu compliant. Place also matters. Although, we as devotees many times do not give undue value to those laws knowing that the law maker is ultimately the supreme soul. So when we go to a place, yes you can do all the rituals, go all the things, we generally do Kirtan. Because you see these four are material causes. The place of action, the performer, the senses, endeavour and this is the ultimate cause, Daivam, the supreme lord. Even all these four causes are against you, if this cause is in favor of you, you will be victorious.

#### material causes, supreme cause, self-help, law of attraction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JM8-wrLDjXw?t=1118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In all the books the Daivam is missing."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Krishna and Arjuna to illustrate the importance of the ultimate cause (Krishna), contrasting it with material causes, and critiques self-help books and the law of attraction for neglecting the divine element.

Give me an example. Krishna and Arjun. Arjun, the Pandavas were weak. Army of Kauravas were much much superior. They had people like, you know, Karna, Bhishma, Dronacharya, performer, the senses, endeavour. The army is much bigger. But Krishna, the ultimate cause was with Arjun. So these four are material causes. And in this world, if you see the interview of people who crack IIT or even when they win the world cup. So you will see, they will be talking about these four causes. You ask Virat Kohli. So he will be talking about, I thought like this. Some person who cracks IIT says, all credit goes to my parents. They gave me a very conducive environment in the home to study. They encouraged me a lot. And somebody will say, when I was in 11th class, I determined that I have to crack the IIT. So every day without fail for the last two years, 16 hours, 14 hours, I was studying. Endeavour, performer, credit to self. And many times people try to copy these four causes, material causes. There are many self-help books on how to win friends and influence people. How to become a millionaire in one year. Give me examples of more books. How to, how to make more money, success, win happiness. So all that writer is writing, I did like this, I did like this, I did like this. And therefore I am happy. People will be reading. You know there is a book called The Secret. What is, anyone has heard or read about the book Secret? So in that it is there, you know, you visualize the end result, it has happened. Feel the feeling it has happened. So lot of people sit and feel the feeling. But lot of people are not finding that secret working. So there are books written on why the secret doesn't work. Or there are books written on how to make the law of attraction work. Because in all the books the Daivam is missing.

#### duty, detachment, equanimity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JM8-wrLDjXw?t=1391
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One of the symptoms of a devotee is equipoised."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that a devotee performs actions as a matter of duty, remaining peaceful and equipoised, understanding the role of higher laws and giving credit to the Lord.

So devotee that way, any action he does as a matter of duty. And he's peaceful because he doesn't say, I worked so hard. Why is that? Because then the higher laws work. He sincerely works. There are two extremes. One is everything is dependent on the Lord. So why I should work? That is wrong understanding. Another extreme is I have worked hard. Therefore, I should get the result. That is also wrong. Is that clear to everyone? So devotee, he works sincerely. He's not lazy. He doesn't say Daivam is there. Everything will be done. Arjuna can say, I will go home. You fight Krishna. I am dependent on you. You fight. And at the end, I'll come back next day morning. Tell me the result. We won or we lost. No, you fight. Do your duty. You are Kshatriya. That's why people sometimes get very discouraged, very demoralized, very dejected when they don't get results, when they meet failure, when something untoward happens in their life. Or when something good happens, very elated, very joyful. One of the symptoms of a devotee is equipoised. Samadarshi. He's equipoised. Neither too happy, nor too sad. When it is happy, the credit goes to the Lord. When he's sad, Lord has a higher plan. I have done my best.

Certainly not very intelligent and cannot see things as they are. See, these days, not these days, over many, many centuries for that matter, there have been self-help books. #### happiness, materialism, introspection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JM8-wrLDjXw?t=1683
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "I could see he's miserable. I could see that he could not squeeze out happiness."
**Summary:** The speaker continues his anecdote, describing his observations of a friend who sought happiness through partying and other material pursuits, but ultimately appeared miserable, leading the speaker to question the true source of happiness.

I used to see my friends drinking, I used to see my friends smoking, are they happy? And one of my friends who was from Delhi, every weekend, you know, Delhi and Kurukshetra where I studied, it's quite close by, it's just three hours drive, so every weekend, Saturday night, he would go to Delhi, come back on Monday morning and when friends would gather, he would say, what did you do, I had a lot of fun, what did you do, I had a lot of fun, went with the girls, saw the rock show, you know, all kinds of typical young boys talking to each other. So and you know, he would be so thrilled and excited and happy, so one time it also took me, rock show. And I could see, you know, forcefully trying to squeeze out the energy, trying to squeeze out happiness, and I really could not enjoy. And he would come and say, I did this, I did that, I did this, I said, my God, so lucky, so fortunate, he's enjoying life. And then I could see he's miserable. I could see that he could not squeeze out happiness. So was I. And when we came back, the friends asked, how was it? This guy is again, shut up. So nice, it was so nice, I said, it's farce, he's cheating. So I was a little inquisitive as to what will make a person happy, will drinking make a person happy, will smoking make a person happy? If not, that maybe higher drugs would make a person happy. Like you're curious, exploring life, understanding things and the world is like that, you drink, you'll be happy, you smoke, you'll be happy, you eat this, you'll be happy. And I was like, what?

### 6 PRINCIPLES FAVOURABLE FOR BHAKTI | HG Gaurmandal Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### bhakti, spiritual life, guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yH4Gs2MuYYY?t=125
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we follow them nicely, our success in spiritual life is guaranteed, it is assured."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the six principles that enhance bhakti, emphasizing their power to guarantee success in spiritual life if followed correctly.

A very warm welcome to all the devotees. His Grace Amitāsana Prabhu very kindly explained to us six things which destroy our bhakti. The class was based on the second verse of the book Nectar of Instruction. I hope you would have come across that book. If not, kindly try to read that book. It is very, very important. It is a very important book for those who wish to attain success in spiritual life. The practical rules and regulations that we have to follow are mentioned over there. So there are six things which destroy bhakti. And then in the next verse which we are going to discuss today, Srila Rupa Goswami explains six principles which enhance bhakti, which are favorable for bhakti. And these six principles are so powerful that if we follow them nicely, our success in spiritual life is guaranteed, it is assured.

#### spiritual master, guidance, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yH4Gs2MuYYY?t=179
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you have to have the success in the most difficult field of activity, getting freedom from all the laws of nature, you need very very expert guidance."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the necessity of a spiritual master's guidance for success in spiritual life, comparing it to needing a professional coach for mundane achievements, and addresses newcomers who may be unfamiliar with the concept of bhakti.

So spiritual life has to be followed under the strict guidance of a spiritual master. Just like people take help of professional coaches for attaining even some mundane success of having a high or long jump, running few meters very, very fast. So if you have to have the success in the most difficult field of activity, then you have to be very careful. If you have to have the success in the most difficult field of activity, getting freedom from all the laws of nature, you need very very expert guidance.
