#### devotion, offering, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xuVstCHcGJE?t=1863
**Verse References:** BG 9.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna in the Gita, is requesting us... He is saying, Patram, Pushpam, Phalam, Toyam, Yome Bhaktia Prayatchati. If you offer me, with love, and devotion... I will accept it."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Krishna from the Gita, emphasizing that the Lord is requesting us to offer Him something with love and devotion, not for His own sake, but for our benefit.

You have to give. You have to give your time, for that relationship to crystallize. You have to give your energy. You have to give your emotions. It doesn't happen automatically. Krishna in the Gita, is requesting us. He is asking us. In one sense, He is pleading us. He is saying, Patram, Pushpam, Phalam, Toyam, Yome Bhaktia Prayatchati. If you offer me, with love, and devotion, Patram, a leaf, Pushpam, a flower, Phalam, fruit, Toyam, water, with love and devotion, I will accept it. Now do you think that, he does not have flowers? He does not have fruits? He does not have water? He is asking us, so that he can quench his thirst, so that he can eat the fruits, which we offer and, you know, satiate his hunger. Why is he asking? Not for himself, but for us.

So that, when we learn to give, when we learn to recognize, that this belongs to the Lord, it is coming from the Lord, and voluntarily, are willing to partake, to give, that as an offering to the Lord, we advance in our spiritual consciousness. It is for our good. Krishna is said to be Bhavagrahi Janardana. Everything is his. All the wealth in this world is his. But still he is asking, Sarvadharman Parityajya Maam Ekam Sharanam Raja. Surrender to me. Give to me. Offer to me. Offer as sacrifice to me.

#### offering, sacrifice, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xuVstCHcGJE?t=2009
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we cannot offer our intelligence, if we cannot offer our money... Krishna says, you offer me flower, a fruit, a leaf, water."
**Summary:** The speaker explains different ways to offer to the Lord: Prana (life), Artha (wealth), Dhiya (intelligence), and Vacha (words), emphasizing that even the poorest can offer something.

Why? We can offer Prana, Artha, Dhiya, and Vacha. Prana means life. Some of us had the good fortune to give our life to the Lord. Engage 24 bar 7 in the service of the Lord. Not everyone can do that. Then Prana. If we cannot offer Prana, Dhiya, we can offer our intelligence. Using intelligence, we can serve the Lord. We can offer our intelligence in the service of the Lord. There are lot of people now, you know, writing for the Lord, making videos for the Lord, to propagate his message, singing melodious Kirtans to glorify the Lord. Using their intelligence. Artha. Artha means the wealth. We can offer the Lord our wealth. Our earnings. A portion of it. Prana, Artha, Dhiya, and Vacha.

#### offering, intention, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xuVstCHcGJE?t=2097
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Not necessarily it will make a difference to us, but it will make a huge difference to that person."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that even if one cannot offer much, the intention to give is what matters to the Lord, and shares a personal experience of asking for donations from financially challenged devotees.

Words. If we cannot offer our intelligence, if we cannot offer our money, assuming a person is you know, very poverty strict, and does not have anything to offer, Krishna says, you offer me flower, a fruit, a leaf, water. Tell me, who cannot offer a leaf? Who cannot in this world offer a flower? And then, even if a person says, I cannot offer even that, Krishna says, Vacha. Just offer a word. Chant the names of Krishna. Many times when we ask donations, I have specifically during Janmashtami, written to few devotees. I know that, believe me I am telling you the truth, I know that they are financially challenged. I know that they are earning quite less. But still I have asked them. Knowing that they can even contribute just 500 rupees a month, or 1000 rupees a month, but still I have asked them, believe me, that amount for a person, for an organization which is operating in Mumbai, it's too small an amount to run the operations. But still I have asked. Not necessarily it will make a difference to us, but it will make a huge difference to that person.

Tell me, for an organization which is operating in Mumbai, with a base in Powai, the rentals are so high, how much will it cost? The rentals are so high, will 500 rupees a month make a difference? But it will definitely make a difference to the person who has contributed that 500 rupees. Even though he may be earning 15000 rupees, 20000 rupees. At times I have even requested that person. So, we have to learn, in Christianity, many of you may be knowing, there is a principle called principle of Taithing. Every devout Christian, who is associated with a particular church,

#### spiritual growth, levels of consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So likewise, there are 84 lakh species of life."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the lecture, verses 11-15 of the 12th chapter of the Gita, and explains that Krishna is addressing individuals at different levels of spiritual growth, using the analogy of weight lifting.

So today we are going to cover from the 12th chapter of the Gita, verse numbers 11 to 15. So these are series of verses where Krishna is trying to tell, understanding that all of us are at different levels of our spiritual growth. Like somebody is trying to practice weight lifting. Somebody can lift 80 kgs, somebody can lift 60 kgs, somebody can lift 40 kgs, somebody can lift 20 kgs, somebody cannot lift at all. Am I right?

#### spiritual evolution, human life, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=46
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is basis what? How evolved a person or a soul is spiritually?"
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of 8.4 million species of life, with 400,000 being human, and emphasizes that the gradation of human beings is based on spiritual evolution, not external factors like skin color or income.

So likewise, there are 84 lakh species of life. Out of this 84 lakh species, 84 lakh yoniyas, we have been discussing how many are human beings? 4 lakhs. 4 lakhs are human beings. And this gradation of human beings is not basis the color of skin, not basis nationality, not basis gender, not basis your income strata. It is basis what? How evolved a person or a soul is spiritually? So if you were to categorize the evolution of the soul spiritually, awakened, how much he is awakened, then you can broadly classify in 4 lakh categories.

We have, you know, sometimes people completely barbaric, cannibals, man-eaters, no conception of Dharma. So that is one end of the spectrum. The other end of the spectrum is complete pristine pure, self-realized, completely aligned to the interest of the Lord, completely God conscious, fully pure, self-realized. So in between there are many many many many many many many levels. All of us in that sense could be of different species. We think that, you know, human beings is one species, plants are another species. No, four lakh species. This is the evolution of.

#### body analogy, nourishment, interdependence

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of the hand and the body to illustrate how the soul, as part of Krishna, finds nourishment and happiness by serving the whole, just as the hand gets nourished when it feeds the body.

Now this hand is is being is being being part of the body it serves the interest of this whole body like it helps in writing, it helps in picking up things, it takes the food and put in the mouth and it is designed that when the hand or the respective organs play their respective roles being part and parcel of the body, body functions properly and that part gets its view quota of happiness and nourishment. Suppose the person is very very hungry, is very hungry is not eaten for a day or so. May be he had fasted Nirjal and after that fast he is given you know a nice plate with lot of rice, dal, Sabji and whole lot of variety of food stuffs, the person is hungry, if actually this hand which is part and parcel of the body is also which is tired because of the fasting, if this hand wants to get nourished, it just hovers over that plate and tries to, it feels all the eatables, will a person hunger gets satiated, yes, no.

#### service, happiness, constitutional position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are designed in such a way that when we serve the Lord, when we are completely aligned to the interest of the Lord, when we follow the instructions of the Lord, when we serve the whole, instead of serving us, we get our quota of happiness, our quota of enjoyment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the hand cannot act independently to nourish itself, but must perform its designated duty of feeding the body, and similarly, we are designed to find happiness by serving the Lord and the whole, rather than seeking independent enjoyment.

If it wants its nourishment, it cannot act that way. It can act independently and not be part of the whole. When it does its designated duty, it takes that food, puts in the mouth, the tongue actually enjoys, tongue enjoys and the belly gets that food. When this process is followed, what happens to this hand? It gets its due quota of nourishment. Morning I was speaking to someone, so that person said, so does that mean that we cannot enjoy ourselves? Is the Lord saying that we should not enjoy? No, we are designed in such a way that when we serve the Lord, when we are completely aligned to the interest of the Lord, when we follow the instructions of the Lord, when we serve the whole, instead of serving us, yes, the false masters, we get our quota of happiness, our quota of enjoyment. Just like this hand is a good example. So now, if somebody is told, you don't try to enjoy independently, try to do your function, put the hand, it looks like the mouth is very egoistic, the stomach is very egoistic. It's saying, put in me, work for me, looks like the body is very egoistic. Looks like, am I right? Why the hand cannot enjoy independently? What's wrong? It cannot because that's not how it is designed, it's not the constitutional position.

#### natural habitat, happiness, company of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=876
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "You put the fish back in water, you have, you don't have a velvet, you don't have any of those things, it will be happy."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a fish out of water to illustrate that we, as amsa of the Lord, are designed to be happy in the company of the Lord, just as a fish is happy in its natural habitat, water.

Once in California, there are fishes, you know, people are on the beach and then, you know, one of the fish saw all the people enjoying, some of them are drinking, some of them are drinking, some of them are drinking, and singing and dancing and doing all kinds of, somebody is reading, somebody is bathing, somebody is lying on the beach. So one of the fish saw that, let me also enjoy. They are enjoying, let me also enjoy. And that fish jumped out of water. To? To? Enjoy. And then seeing that the fish has jumped out of the water, there were people trying to help that fish so that, you know, they could enjoy. And then they were trying to help that fish so that, you know, that fish can also get a glimpse of what we are doing, what we are, how we are enjoying. So some of them, you know, brought a velvet cushion where that fish can come and sit comfortably. And then somebody brought a beer, beer can. You also drink, have fun. And, you know, somebody brought a magazine, okay, you also can see and see what's happening in the world. And all along the fishes, why? Because that's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not its natural habitat. It's not in its elements. You put the fish back in water, you have, you don't have a velvet, you don't have any of those things, it will be happy. So we being amsa of the Lord Krishna says mamaeva amsa jiva loke... we are designed to be happy in company of the lord.

So, that's the highest thing. Next, Krishna says, knowing that we cannot do that, my dear Arjuna, O winner of wealth, if you cannot fix your mind upon me, it's coming closer to us. Isn't it? Krishna is now coming closer to us. If you cannot fix the mind, then what can you do? Tell me. If you just fix your mind, this is the first instruction. Fix your mind upon me. Because you are my Amsha. You serve me, I serve you, both of us in reciprocal relationship, just like Arjuna and Krishna. By the way, when we serve the Lord, it is not that the Lord is like a master and we are like the slave and chalo, seva karo mera. When we serve the Lord, the Lord in turn serves His devotees. That's why the Lord has said, bhakta vatsala.

#### levels of practice, chanting, fortunate
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=1271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, if you have started chanting and doing some this thing, we are actually very fortunate. We have reached the second level."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that many people will not even practice the regulative principles of bhakti yoga, and that those who have started chanting are fortunate to have reached the second level of practice.

Now, next stage is, there are lot of people out of this four lakh species who will not even practice the regulative principles of bhakti yoga. Then what? Then Krishna says, if you cannot practice the regulations of bhakti yoga, we all are in which level? We all are in which level? Actually, if you have started chanting and doing some this thing, we are actually very fortunate. We have reached the second level. We are trying to practice. That's a different thing that in that also there are many species. Sixteen round is one species. Twelve rounds is another species. But at least, we are broadly speaking in that second level. Try to practice the regulative principles of bhakti yoga and try to develop a desire to attain me. You all agree with this? First is, completely absorbed in me without deviation, meditating on me, fully devoted to me. If you cannot do this, then try to practice the principles of bhakti yoga in a regulated way and develop a desire to attain me.

#### charity, blessings, sanctification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=1453
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "More than we needing your money, you need the blessings of the Lord which comes by parting away our hard-earned money for the service of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that giving charity to a qualified brahmana engaged in propagating the mission of the Lord is the highest form of charity, and that it is more for the giver's benefit, to receive the Lord's blessings and sanctify their existence, than for the receiver's need.

When someone gives to a qualified brahmana who is engaged in propagating the mission of the Lord, is the highest charity. There is charity in the mode of ignorance, tamo guna, charity in the mode of passion, charity in the mode of sattva guna, and shuddha sattva, transcendental charity. A request to all of you. Make it a point. Whatever you earn, some portion of it, give it to Krishna. Because, I will explain to you why. More than we needing your money, you need the blessings of the Lord which comes by parting away our hard-earned money for the service of the Lord. Both ways. We also need for propagating the mercy of the Lord. When this program is organized, it doesn't come freely. But more than we needing, Lord will somehow manage his mission. Yes or no? Lord will manage his mission. More than we needing, in that scenario where we need your money and you have to give money, more than we needing money, it is your need to give money to sanctify your existence.

#### sacrifice, attachment, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=1521
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The idea of sacrificing something which is dear to us, makes us dear to Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that real devotion involves sacrificing something dear to us, using the example of Bakrid and the sacrifice of a goat to illustrate the principle of kurbani, or sacrifice for the Lord's pleasure.

If you say, who is more in need? Actually, you are more in need. If you cannot practice the regulations of bhakti yoga, then try to work for me. Because, working for me, you will come. Now, sometimes what happens is, we do lip service. Yes, yes, we are very devoted. But real devotion is, are you willing to sacrifice what is dear to you? Something which we are attached to? This whole idea of even bakrit, you know, sacrificing of goat. Actually, this whole idea is kurbani, sacrifice for Lord. And I don't know the full details, but the overall principle is, you have a goat, which you, which grows and stays with the person in his house and a person develops attachment for that animal. And when the person develops attachment, you are attached to someone and you sacrifice it for the Lord. Kurbani. Sacrifice for the pleasure of the Lord. We will not get into details, whether it is pleasing to the Lord, why goat and why not, you know, all these things. Let's not get into details. The idea of sacrificing something which is dear to us, makes us dear to Lord.

#### donation, message of the Lord, propulsion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2Nc1yR31YuI?t=1600
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That act of his propels him exponentially towards the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that donating money for spreading the message of the Lord, even without full awareness, propels one exponentially towards the Lord, and that working for the Lord can involve sacrificing prana (life), artha (money), diya (intelligence), or vacha (words).

So, somebody who may donate money for spreading the message of the Lord, he may not even know that he has, but then, that act of his propels him exponentially towards the Lord. Does it make sense? He may not even know what he has done, but that action of his propels him towards the Lord. If you cannot practice the regulations of Bhakti Yoga, then try to work for me, because by working for me, now working for me, pranair, artha, diya, vacha. There are four things in which we can work for the Lord. Prana means? Prana means life. We cannot give our life. We cannot dedicate our life, because we have other commitments in this life. Prana. The next is? Artha. Yes, we cannot give prana, but we can give? Artha means? Money. We can sacrifice money, which is dear to us. Which is dear to us. That money, we can transact and do lot of things which will give me enjoyment, but I sacrifice it for the Lord. Pranair, artha, artha. Now somebody cannot give artha also, then diya. Diya means? Diya does not mean ghiya. Ghiya means? Ghiya means intelligence. One can use his intelligence for the Lord. And if you cannot use even your intelligence, vacha, words. Use your words in the service of the Lord. Tell someone about the Lord. Tell someone to chant the names of the Lord. Pranair, artha, diya, vacha.

#### abode of the Lord, liberation, material lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The lecture begins with a discussion of Bhagavad Gita text 5, outlining the qualifications necessary to attain the eternal kingdom, including freedom from illusion, false prestige, false association, and material lust.

So, the title is Abode of the Supreme Lord, dedicated to His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Shilpa Prabhupada. This is text 5. One who is free from illusion, false prestige and false association, who understands the eternal, who is done with material lust. How many of us are done with material lust? Done with material lust. Finished. Chapter closed. So, one who is done with material lust and is free. Freed from duality of happiness and distress. Equipoised in happiness and distress. And who knows how to surrender unto the Supreme Person, attains to the eternal kingdom.

#### eternal world, material world, Satt-Chit-Ananda
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=69
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas the world which Krishna is talking about is the world where the world full of Satt, Chit and Ananda: permanence, full of knowledge and bliss."
**Summary:** Krishna speaks about His eternal abode, contrasting it with the temporary and ever-changing material world, and highlighting the aspiration for happiness, peace, and contentment that drives human endeavors.

So, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna talks about His abode. And He says, Parastasma tu. Bhavanyo. Vyakta. Avyakta. Sanatana. That world is eternal. Unlike this material world, which is Vyakta and Avyakta. Vyakta means, it manifests. At one point, it got created. It came into existence. It stays for some time. Shrishti, Stiti, Pralaya. It is created. Stiti, it is maintained. And Pralaya, at time, it is annihilated. Krishna in the Gita says, There is yet another world. Parastasma tu. Bhavanyo. Vyakta. Avyakta. Sanatana. Beyond this world, which is Vyakta and Avyakta. Which is Sanatana, which is eternal. That is my abode. Krishna says. And we have discussed this many times. We all have aspiration to live a life of happiness. We all have aspiration to live a life of happiness. We all have aspiration to live a life of peace, contentment, and we all are striving for that. Each one of us are striving for a life. Therefore, some miseries come. We immediately get into how to solve that, so that I can restore back my peace, my happiness. Then I am happy for a while. Another something lands up in my life. I sort the things. I sort the things out, hoping that now I will be happy. Whereas the world which Krishna is talking about is the world where the world full of Satt, Chit and Ananda. Permanence, full of knowledge and Ananda, bliss.

#### surrender, prison house, qualifications
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=190
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This material world in that sense is compared to a prison house and Krishna is inviting us...that how by developing the right attitude, right qualities, right understanding we can go back to our original home and be eternally happy."
**Summary:** Krishna is giving the qualifications required to go back to the eternal abode, comparing the material world to a prison house and inviting us to develop the right attitude and qualities to return to our original home.

So Krishna is giving in this verse, the qualification required to go back to the world. To that eternal abode of ours. To that eternal abode of ours. our eternal home. Just like for any person prison house is not the place to be. He has got into prison house for some crime which he has committed for a reformation and ultimately the goal is to come out of the prison house, be a law abiding citizen and enjoy a comfortable life with all the amenities, facilities which government wants to give. So this material world in that sense is compared to a prison house and Krishna is inviting us, we are waging a battle, we are struggling here. There is a struggle for existence. So Krishna is inviting us that how by developing the right attitude, right qualities, right understanding we can go back to our original home and be eternally happy. So what are the qualifications required? We will go one by one. The surrendering process is described here in this particular verse. When we surrender to the Supreme Lord with all these qualifications being the requisite. Then we qualify to go back to that spiritual abode.

#### pride, illusion, self-denial
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=289
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pride stems from the understanding that I am the controller. I am the Lord."
**Summary:** The first qualification is to not be deluded by pride, which stems from the illusion that one is the controller and the Lord, leading to frustration when things don't go as planned.

The first qualification is that one should not be deluded by pride. Pride not in the sense which we have an understanding this world that you have a big car so I am proud of a big car. It has a much deeper connotation. When one is freed from delusion caused by pride one can start the process of self-denial. What is the process? When you are free from delusion caused by pride, one can start the process of surrender. Pride stems from the understanding that I am the controller. I am the Lord. Maybe in a small way of the things which are, that's how we are trying to always have things in our control. Have things arranged as per my will and wish. Frustration comes when things don't transpire the way I want it to happen. When kids don't behave the way I want them to behave. When things in life don't manifest the way I want it to manifest. Frustration comes. So pride is when one thinks that I am the Lord. I am the controller.

#### free will, suffering, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Because we misuse that independence, act in ignorance, in illusion, out of pride, we mess up our lives."
**Summary:** Suffering is not God's desire, but a result of misusing free will and acting in ignorance and illusion, emphasizing that Krishna's call to surrender and become a devotee is for our own benefit.

In ignorance we act and those who are wise can see that. But those in ignorance cannot see that. We mess up our lives. Krishna has not created this world where he feels very glad and happy that you are suffering. He is our father. Which father would like his son to suffer? Which father would like his son to starve to death? Which father would like his son to die? Which father would like his son, you know, all the kinds of anomalies what we see in the societies, tsunamis, earthquakes, natural calamities, disasters. Which father would like his son to do? That's why sometimes people get bewildered and say there is no God. Very simple. So he has given us a free will, choice. We all have minute independence. And because we misuse that independence, act in ignorance, in illusion, out of pride, we mess up our lives. So whole Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is, when he is saying surrender to me or he is asking to become his devotee, manmana, think of me. It is not that, it is for him. Just like, you know, somebody is there. He gets lot of cake by somebody. Obeying him or serving him, yes sir, or giving him respect. He is not a lot where he is thirsty of our respect. When he is asking us to do that, it is for our benefit. For our self-interest.

#### Atma, temporary body, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aQJ9iad0NM0?t=876
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When one is freed from such illusion, realizes that I am Atma, this body is a temporary dress which I am wearing."
**Summary:** Realizing that we are Atma and the body is a temporary dress helps us break free from false associations and dual conceptions, leading to equipoise and the possibility of surrender to the Lord.

From quote unquote normal perspective, this is brainwashing. But, strictly speaking from the perspective of atma, every life, atma has different bodies. different body. Based on that body has different sets of relatives, different sets of associations which are false and temporary. So what happens when one is freed from such illusion, realizes that I am Atma, this body is a temporary dress which I am wearing. Like you might associate with the car which you are driving, you might associate with the dress which you are wearing but you know that you are not the dress, you know that you are not the car. Today something happens to your dress, you change the dress and wear a new dress. Beyond a point you will not fret over some dress which goes bad. You might feel bad but you know that you are not the dress, you change the dress. These fake associations bind one to this material world because of these false conceptions. False understanding, illusion, one is entangled. Then he becomes freed from all dual conceptions such as happiness and distress, pleasure and pain. When one is with knowledge, destroys this illusion and gets freed of that pride, he comes to a state where he is equipoised. There will be a state where he is equi-poised. There will be a state where he is equi-poised. There will be a state where he is equi-poised. There will be a state where he is equi-poised. There will be happiness in life, he is not overly elated and when there is distress, he is not depressed, demoralized. He is samadarshik. He is equi-poised. He becomes full in knowledge. Then it is possible for him to surrender. What is keeping us away from surrender, keeping us away from the Lord, keeping us away from the knowledge because of pride, because of illusion? Because of illusion I think, why should I I am the Lord I will control things. Anyone has any question, anything based on what we discussed so far.

#### modes of nature, duty, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zjqGbzcs3gg?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Action in accordance with duty which is performed without attachment, without love or hate by one who has renounced fructive results is called action in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** The lecture begins by introducing the concept of action in the three modes of nature, focusing on action in goodness as described in Bhagavad Gita 18.23-27, emphasizing detachment from results.

Action in three modes, goodness, passion and ignorance, based on the teachings of His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. So this is text 23, we will be covering five verses today, 23 to 27. As for actions, that action in accordance with duty which is performed without attachment, without love or hate by one who has renounced fructive results is called action in the mode of goodness.

**Summary:** This segment discusses the concept of fruitive action, emphasizing the need to avoid attachment to results and highlighting the negative consequences of expectations, leading to happiness or sadness based on outcomes.

Before we get into the discussion, what is actions with fructive results? Because action has an expectation of an outcome, so why do we have to avoid the actions? An attempt is possible if the mind is able to process the acceptance and translate into that acceptance, by contemplating them and interacting with them. So we can communicate with sure, we can work with the mind to know all of those experiences in practice of action. And if we do not unfortunate truly attach to the results, then the result will be suppressed. This is an exiled inspiration, hope. So if we do not attach to the results, that is here it is right, we cannot order. Now what happens is like this, we will be away from our life. For this reason, don't obstinate yourself. When you are attaching to the results, it's not that we would be there for an accident. We are not going to on you on such a matter, that is why we are in this of the action by observing in these four parts. and if the results are positive, we are happy, we get enthused. If results are negative or we don't get the results or it's not as per our expectations, we feel sad. That's why you would see many quotes floating on WhatsApp that the root cross of problems are expectation, have zero expectation. Even that is not the right understanding. When we perform actions attached to the results of that activity with the intent of enjoying the results, taking the position of bhokta.

**Summary:** The speaker explains Krishna's teachings on achieving peace by understanding that Krishna is the enjoyer (bhokta), contrasting this with the fruitive mentality that leads to a roller coaster of emotions.

That's why Krishna in the Gita says there are three things if one follows, three important principles if one understands and keeps in his mind, he will become peaceful. The first principle is, Krishna says, bhoktaram yajnataprasam, that I am the enjoyer, I am the bhokta. When we do actions taking the position of a bhokta, that's the first principle. I will work hard so that I can enjoy the results of my activities, that action is called frutive action. Fruits are driving that action and that mentality is called frutive mentality. We are driven by the results and that's why in our life, we always on a roller coaster ride. Sometimes happy, sometimes sad. Seldom we are equipoised. Arjuna asked this question to Krishna, what are the symptoms of a person who is Sita Pragna? Sita Pragna means who is self-realized, who is God conscious. **Summary:** The speaker contrasts attachment to results with performing actions in Krishna consciousness for the satisfaction of the soul, differentiating between lust (satisfying personal senses) and love (satisfying the Lord's pleasure).

So as I mentioned earlier our things which drive us, the motive behind our actions is the results. Attachment to the results. Krishna says a person in goodness that attachment to result is not the motive, should not be the motive. What should be the motive? Should be performed in Krishna consciousness for the satisfaction of the soul. Atmaendriya, Preeti, Vansha. Atmaendriya means for our own mind and senses. Preeti, satisfaction, Vansha, Tave, Naam, Kaam. The name of that action is called Kaam. It's lust. Krishnendriya Preeti, for the satisfaction, for the pleasure of the Lord, Tave, Naam, Preeti. That is love.

The pluses, the minuses, the strengths, weaknesses, nature, habits, everything they know. Still, 60 to 70% marriages in America end up in divorce. He also wants love of his mother. And many times, or many, many children in America are single parents, where the father and mother are separate. #### caste system, Varnas, birth

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that the Varna system is not based on birth, unlike the caste system in India, which has been criticized by social reformers. The problem arose when individuals claimed the rights of a Varna based on birth rather than qualities and actions.

Now in this varna ashrama, brahmanas, Krishna says these four varnas and ashramas are not based on birth. The caste system what we see in India which many social reformers came, they criticized. What happened was the evil of caste system what we hear of is because the son of a brahmana having taken birth in a brahmana family would claim the rights, the powers, the prestige, the fame of a brahmana without having a caste system.

#### Varnas, qualification, guna and karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zjqGbzcs3gg?t=196
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The problem began when people started claiming to be brahmanas, claiming to be kshatriyas based on their birth rather than their guna and karma."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a judge's son not automatically being a judge to illustrate that Varna should be determined by qualities and actions (guna and karma) rather than birth.

In crude sense, just to give you an example, it's like son of a judge or son of a chief minister claiming to be chief minister. Son of a judge claiming to be a judge although he may not have the required qualification. We may have good doctors, heart surgeons but son of a heart surgeon is not necessarily a heart surgeon. If he also studies like his father, does the required courses, acquires the required qualification, he can be a heart surgeon. But not by birth. So the problem began when people started claiming to be brahmanas, claiming to be kshatriyas based on their birth rather than their guna and karma.

#### Varnas, gunas, goodness, passion, ignorance

So the problem began when people started claiming to be brahmanas, claiming to be kshatriyas based on their birth rather than their guna and karma. Now brahmanas are situated in sattva guna. Guna, karma, vibhagasha. According to guna, we are reading about gunas, goodness, passion, ignorance. So who are brahmanas? Not by birth. Who by gunas are situated in sattva guna? Who are kshatriyas? Kshatriyas. Passion. Predominantly passion. Rajoguna. Who are vaishyas? Is mixture of passion and ignorance. And shudras are?

**Key Quote:** "Whichever varna and ashram a person is sitting in, and ashram a person is situated in based on his guna and karma one performs his prescribed duties and by performing his prescribed duties by situated in his varna and ashrama he will gradually progress."

So the Vedic system was, whichever varna and ashram a person is sitting in, and ashram a person is situated in based on his guna and karma one performs his prescribed duties and by performing his prescribed duties by situated in his varna and ashrama he will gradually progress I tell you how he will progress the sutras are expected to obey whom all the three categories the Vaisyas Kshatriyas and the Brahmadas the Vaisyas were expected to obey the Kshatriyas and sorry the Kshatriyas and the Brahmadas the Kshatriyas the Kings were obeying Brahmadas and Brahmadas were obeying see how the society was divided based on guna and karma activities for the activities the sutras situated in influence they were obeying the Kshatriyas were enacting laws they were ruling that's why you have Chanakya Pandit emperors they had brahmanas advising them as advisors and they were quite well versed with the understanding of scriptures right and wrong in last class we discussed what is right and wrong it is not dependent on what I feel is right or wrong according to someone me repeating is right according to someone it is very wrong so the brahmanas whose main duty is to obey the Kshatriyas and Brahmadas their main duty was to give and decline their qualities of a Brahmana so brahmanas were quite enlightened they were situated in sattva guna they had knowledge of scriptures they were self-realized they had understanding about God they had understanding a purpose of life such brahmanas who are followers or obedient to God would guide the Kshatriyas the Kshatriyas would enact laws and administer the Kshatriyas and Brahmadas the followers could be obedient to God and would guide the Kshatriyas the Kshatriyas would enact laws and administer the kingdom based on which the Vaishyas will carry on their activities, trading, occupational duties and the Shudras who are working were under the direction of Vaishyas, Kshatriyas and Brahmanas. So who obeys whom? Ultimately it's the Brahmanas.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Adam and Eve: A Vedic Perspective | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### envy, disobedience, fall from grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rNo6TVJ6Usc?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We want to be God, eat the forbidden fruit, rebel against the Lord, be the center of attraction, be the enjoyer, and fell from the Garden of Eden, which is the spiritual world, into the material world."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Adam and Eve from a Vedic perspective, highlighting the themes of envy, disobedience, and the desire to be God as the cause of their fall from the Garden of Eden, which is analogous to the soul's fall from the spiritual world into the material world.

Even the Bible talks about the story of Adam and Eve. It is said that Adam and Eve were in the Garden of Eden, and they were allowed to enjoy as much as they wanted. They were surrounded by trees full of fruits and flowers, and they were told they could eat as many fruits as they wanted, enjoying in abundance. But one condition was given: avoid eating the apple from that one tree. They were shown that tree, and the reason was that tree had a snake. We know a snake is generally envious, so that tree had a snake. Just avoid that tree. You can take fruits as much as you want from any number of trees in the entire garden. So one day, they were living happily and enjoying. Whenever they were hungry, they could eat as many fruits as they wanted from as many trees as they wanted. But one day, when they were passing by, somehow the snake was like, 'This snake will make him eat the apple from that tree.' All the enemies of that tree came. They killed the snake. My friends, I confused. They killed the snake from that tree, but it did not happen. They killed the snake like I was doing. Anyway, it came back. It did not happen. They are deep into it, then describe it now. So it gets to that place, that one boom, there is something about it, and it is a story of estan tic van tan, not to eat. What is the necessity? The whole garden is full of trees. We can eat from wherever trees. You know, the real reason has not been told to you. The real reason is if you eat the apple from this tree, you'll become as powerful as God. You will become God, and God does not want you to be as powerful as him. He wants to keep secure his position. Therefore, he has prohibited, 'Don't eat the apple from this tree.' So it says sanjots, you know, when you associate with envious persons, you also develop. So Adam became envious. 'This is not fair. Why does God not want us to be God? He wants to secure his position and be God. Therefore, he has very intelligently prohibited us from eating the apple from this tree and said you can eat anything you want.' So she told Adam, and they plucked the apple from that tree, and filled with that enviousness, they ate the apple. It says the moment they ate the apple, it says Adam became he, and he became she, and they fell from the Garden of Eden, jokingly in the Eden Gardens Calcutta to play around. So the fellow, so it's something similar what even Bhagavatam is saying. We want to be God, eat the forbidden fruit, rebel against the Lord, be the center of attraction, be the enjoyer, and fell from the Garden of Eden, which is the spiritual world, into the material world.

#### spiritual identity, material bondage, envy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rNo6TVJ6Usc?t=229
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Likewise, living entities are pure, but because of this nirma Tera, this enviousness, the December, the Iccha, Dvesha, that Sat Chit Ananda has come in this material world, just like in a prison house a prisoner is shackled using chain, imprisoned, likewise that Sat Chit Ananda has been imprisoned in this material world, has put in this material world."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the living entity, originally sat chit ananda, falls into the material world due to envy and the desire to be God, becoming imprisoned in a material body characterized by Asat, Achit, and Nirananda.

So ultimately, what are we trying to do? We, the living entity, amsha, who is sat chit ananda, we are trying because that's our original position. That's what we are as amsha of the Lord. That's what we are. Just like air here is pure. When it passes through a rose garden, it becomes very fragrant. When it passes through a place Gutter, it has a foul smell. The air is pure, but it takes the stench or the aroma from the place where it passes through. So likewise, living entities are pure, but because of this nirma Tera, this enviousness, the December, the Iccha, Dvesha, that Sat Chit Ananda has come in this material world, just like in a prison house a prisoner is shackled using chain, imprisoned, likewise that Sat Chit Ananda has been imprisoned in this material world, has put in this material world, and what are the chains by which we have got imprisoned? That living entity has got a material body, which is Asat, Achit, and Nirananda.

So, the topic is All Devouring Death, based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, our founder. We will chant this verse together, Pradadas Chasmi Daityanam, Kala Kalayatam Aham, Mriganam Chamrigendro Aham, Vainateyas Cha Pakshinam. To give you a background about the verses, what we are going to discuss today, Arjuna asked Krishna to explain more about his energies, his opulences, his creation, his powers. Then in reply to that, as we all know, the powers of the Lord are infinite. And then he said that now I am going to describe to you some principle manifestations, for my energies, my powers are limitless. So then in the last few verses we discussed that he says how of the mantras, I am Om, of the animals, of the mountains, I am Meru, of the immovable things, I am all the great and magnificent, glorious things we see in the manifestation, I am the sun. So Krishna is explaining that.

#### interpretation, Bhagavad Gita, intended message
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7FF1llHdrg0?t=101
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unfortunately, the message sometimes gets coloured, twisted, interpreted and the original intent of the message which Lord had while giving this instruction to Arjuna gets lost."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses various interpretations of the Bhagavad Gita, including Mahatma Gandhi's, and emphasizes the importance of understanding the original intent of Krishna's message to Arjuna.

So now in today's verse, he says, among the daitya demons, I am devoted Prahalad, Prahalad is a very exalted devotee. We all see many times people interpreting scriptures. We have many interpretations. Even Bhagavad Gita, if you carefully read, you know people have given different interpretations. Like the Gita written by Mahatma Gandhi, he's actually trying to conclude the message of Gita is non-violence because during those times when he was imprisoned, he was civil disobedience movement and non-violence, this thing that was his path of fighting freedom, fighting for freedom. So he gave a twist, he said actually the battle of Kurukshetra is a fiction. There is nothing called as you know the real war which happened between Pandavas. This actually is the war between the virtuous and vices. The five Pandavas represent the five senses. So this is a twist. So like that there are many many interpretations. Philosophers have written, sometimes big you know scholars have written commentaries on Gita or on the Vedic literatures and actually it appears that I am reading Gita just like this wire when it is connected to the source, it is connected to the source. You know the amplifier works, my voice is getting amplified but assuming that the power switch is turned off, the same wire, the same amplifier, the same microphone but voice will not get amplified. What this mic is supposed to do will not do. The objective of me holding the mic that you are able to hear me will not happen. Why I am holding the mic? It is not very comfortable. I rather if my hands are free, it is much more better. But I am holding the mic because I am able to reach my voice to you. That's the objective. So likewise when Krishna spoke the message of Gita to Arjuna, there was a very very clear, well defined message to be transmitted and a desired transformation which was supposed to happen in the heart of Arjuna which actually happened. Finally Arjuna says, shishyasteham, now I am a soul, surrendered unto you, please instruct me. And then after hearing the Gita, Krishna says whatever you have said, I fully accept it. And then when finally Arjuna was asked, now I have told you what you should be doing, now do what you want. Krishna says karishye vachanam tava, I will do what you have said me to do, what you have asked me to do. So, there was an intended message.

#### Mahajanas, scriptural understanding, contradictions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7FF1llHdrg0?t=356
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you have to understand the true meaning of Shastras, you have to follow in the footsteps of Mahajanas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that to understand the true meaning of scriptures, especially when faced with apparent contradictions, one must follow in the footsteps of Mahajanas (great devotees).

So, Prahlad Maharaj is one of the Mahajanas. How do we know what is the actual meaning of what our Shastras are saying? What Bhagavad Gita is saying? Sometimes in one scripture we see, the scriptures are saying that we should be like, what is that person who took care of his parents very nicely, Shravan Kumar. We have examples like that. We have also examples of Prahlad Maharaj who disobeyed his father, who was his father of Hiranyakashipu. He said, do not worship Vishnu. Or we have example of Bali Maharaj, his guru, Shukracharya. He says, do not worship Vishnu. Do not surrender. Vishnu has come as a Brahmana. He will take everything. Do not listen to him. Do not give him three steps of land. And he, so to say, discarded his guru. So, in that sense, we have contradictory examples. On one side, we have examples of Shravan Kumar who very obediently follow his father. And we have examples of Prahlad Maharaj who disobeyed his father. So, somebody may read, he will get confused. So, how do we understand Tatvata? Tatvata means in truth, the actual meaning. So, it says that if you have to understand the true meaning of Shastras, you have to follow in the footsteps of Mahajanas. Mahajanas means great devotees. Mahajano ye na gata sa pantaha. Panta means footsteps. Mahajano, the footsteps, the way they have led their lives, the way they have acted, follow in their footsteps. Don't imitate them, follow in their footsteps. Then we will get the actual understanding of scriptures and then apparent contradictions will get resolved.

What Krishna is interested in is, are we doing the best we can in the given circumstances? Just now I was speaking to some people, some devotees. If you have a hundred rupee, you give ten rupees for Krishna, it is much more better, much more, that sacrifice is much more dearer to him than a person who is a lakh of rupees and give thousand rupees. So whatever circumstances we may find ourselves in, there is no point in, you know, why did I end up in this state? We have ended up in a state, it's because of the deeds we have done in the past, the choices we have made in the past. Doesn't matter. Happened, happened, over, over. From now on, let's make the best use of the bad bargain.

So more we make the choices, which the life throws up every moment, which are aligned with the instructions of Guru, Sadhu and Shastra, we disentangle. We disentangle. ### Are We Entangled? | HG Amitasana Prabhu

#### Maya, Free Will, Material Nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lC_bw-8iUkw?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although active, we are passive."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how Maya influences our actions, making us think we are in control when material nature is actually goading us.

When we are under the influence of Maya, we think that we are the doer of activities. We think that we are active, we think that we are making the choices. But here Prabhupada mentions that being under the grip of material nature or Maya, we are in one sense passive. Although active, we are passive. That's why there is a verse in the Gita, one who sees action in inaction and inaction in action as they are. Passive in the sense because material nature is goading us, is forcing us to act in a particular way. We see a plethora of activities in this world.

#### Material Nature, Sinful Activities, Modes of Nature

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

In this world people are acting in different ways, some are very very short tempered, some are good natured, some are kind, loving, wicked, cruel, all kinds of people. Actually many times we decide I should not get angry, I should not behave like this, I should be more gentle. But our nature overpowers us. So that's why here Prabhupada mentions that under the influence of modes of material nature which is forcing us to act at times in sinful ways, this is the same question which Arjuna also asked. What is it that we end up acting in ways which later on we regret, as if there is some kind of a force. So here he says, Prabhupada says, his acts are influenced by the quality of nature. He says that the nature of nature overpowers us. So that's why here Prabhupada mentions that under the influence of modes of material nature, he says that us are already bound.

Although we may appear free, we may appear independent, saying some affirmations how by imagining things but the laws of nature are very very stringent and Krishna in the Gita gives us a clue in fact why clue directly he says if we have to come out of the influence of material nature maam eva ye prapadyante maya metam tarantite the reason why we have fallen in the clutches of Maya is because of we've rebelled against the Lord just like if someone violates the state laws he's punished in the recent we have been hearing about the ball tampering scandal so there are some rules of fair play and if you violate the rules you want to take undue advantage then you get penalized so we see in this world how rules apply likewise the material nature has stringent rules and knowingly unknowingly we violate those rules we come out of the influence of the law and we are punished and there is no punishment under the clutches of Maya.

So here it is said, how can there be freedom for a soul as long as material nature acts on him and binds him? We don't even know that we are bound, forget about finding out what is the solution. **Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

This verse says, patanga matanga kurunga brunga, meena hata pancha bhi reva pancha. It's a very nice verse, we have heard this example earlier. Patanga, patanga means moth. Many times we have seen, during the rainy season if there is some bright light, the sun is shining. All the moths, they get helplessly drawn towards that fire or that bright light. They cannot resist. Morning time when we see these moths are all lying dead. So do they not, every animal, every insect has a natural instinct not to die, to protect. It's a natural instinct. Prabhupada. Prabhupada says four things are common between human beings and animals, eating, sleeping, mating and defending, to protect. You take an ant, ant is going, you just put a finger in front of it, it will turn. It senses a danger and tries to go away. The moth, I am sure they have that much intelligence to protect themselves, but when there is some fire or there is some light, they are helplessly drawn. And in fact the body starts getting heated. They can feel the sense of burning. But that fire is so intoxicating, the sight of that fire, the eyes, it cannot resist the dancing flames of fire. And then it hovers around it and drops dead.

And even in India, there are some remote parts, there are some places where there are some parts where still that untouchability is practiced where in a particular village, there is separate village for the higher caste, there is separate village for the lower castes. And people call their Dalits Achut or they cannot come to the temple. And that's how the different social reformers came and they tried to dismantle this so-called caste system in India. But Krishna in the Gita says, Krishna is saying, I have created this caste system. #### caste system, guna, karma

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is not basis birth. Chatur varna maya shrishta. Maya shrishta means I have created. How? On what basis? Guna, karma, vibhagasha. Basis on the gunas."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that Krishna's caste system is based on guna (qualities) and karma (work), not birth, and that the societal evil arose when people claimed superiority based solely on their birth in a brahmana family.

So Krishna says in the Gita, anyone can tell me what is the right understanding of caste system? It is not basis birth. Chatur varna maya shrishta. Maya shrishta means I have created. How? On what basis? Guna, karma, vibhagasha. Basis on the gunas. Whether a person is predominant. Prominently in sattva guna, in raja guna, in tamo guna and what kind of work he is engaged in. Now the so-called evil came in the society when people who were born in brahmana family started claiming that I am a brahmana, so you should respect me. It is like, it is like judge of a high court judge, his son claiming that I am also a judge. Or it is also like a doctor who is a big surgeon and his son saying I am also a surgeon. No, you can't be a surgeon by birth. If you qualify, if you go to the required qualification courses, yes, you can be also a surgeon. Now this whole evil came in the society when people started claiming that I am a brahmana, you are a shudra.

So, a person who is engaged in these activities, irrespective of where he has taken birth, is considered a Ramana. Now, our consideration while deciding a doctor is not the caste, it's not the religion. If suppose one, Mr. Joseph Murray is a good cardiologist, we don't mind getting operated from, because he has the required qualification. So, such Brahmanas, that's why in Vedic times, even in our customs, it's a custom that Brahmanas are called, to the house, they are invited, their feet is washed. Or Brahman Bhojan, you know, sometimes when there is ceremony in the family, the Brahmanas are invited and they are fed.

**Summary:** The speaker gives another example of Bali Maharaj, who disobeyed his guru Shukracharya and agreed to give three pieces of land to Vamanadev (Lord Vishnu), thus being respected as a great devotee.

Another example, we are talking about spiritual master. Any example? Another example? Have you heard of Shukracharya? Who is Shukracharya? Shukracharya, anyone? He was the guru of Bali Maharaj. He was the guru of Bali Maharaj. He was the guru of Bali Maharaj. Bali Maharaj have you heard of? Who is Bali Maharaj? Don't tell me, disciple of Shukracharya. Yeah. So Bali Maharaj, you know after Diwali we have Bali Paadmi or we have Vaman Jayanti, that is the festival observed for this particular pastime. So Bali Maharaj was a great king. But. But he was born in Asura family, but still he was a great devotee of the Lord. So one time, Lord Vishnu came at his doorstep dressed as a Brahmana. What is the name of the Brahmana? Vamanadev. And just like, you know when Brahmans come or something, we give something in charity. They ask something and we say this thing. So this Brahman boy said that I don't want anything, I just want three pieces of land. Three pieces means the land on which I can step, three steps of land. So the Shukracharya who was so-called very intelligent, he understood that this is Vishnu has come in disguise. He is calling for three steps, but actually he will take away everything. And if he takes away everything, my thing also is gone. Because all his source of income, whatever it is, this thing is coming from Bali Maharaj. So everyone is a little selfish in that aspect. So he said don't give. He is Vishnu himself who has come in disguise. Don't give, don't give. He is asking for three pieces. Don't give. So Bali Maharaj said you only have told, no, that Vishnu is supreme. And goal of life is to surrender to Vishnu. If Vishnu himself has come and he is asking, what's wrong in it if I give? So much against the wish of Shukracharya, Bali Maharaj agreed to give three pieces of land to Bhamanadev.

#### parents, discretion, scripture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Qgz1f2m93Io?t=1057
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, yes, we have to respect our parents. Yes, we have to respect our elders. But sometimes they can be, because everyone has individual soul. And even today, the so-called parents could be spoiled children of yesterday."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the stories by saying that while respecting parents and elders is important, one must use intelligence and discretion, especially if their instructions contradict scriptural injunctions, citing examples of great devotees who disobeyed their parents.

And that's why Bali Maharaj is also regarded, respected as a great devotee. So to cut the story short, he took one step, he covered the entire material universe. The second step, The outer universe, the entire so-called spiritual, this thing. And he said, where do I keep the third step? He says, keep on my head. I am yours. So, yes, we have to respect our parents. Yes, we have to respect our elders. But sometimes they can be, because everyone has individual soul. And even today, the so-called parents could be spoiled children of yesterday. You know, slowly the culture is eroding. These days we see a lot of so-called young people having a lot of bad habits. They're smoking, they're drinking, they're womanizing, they're doing all kinds of things. They're going to be parents for another ten years, five years. So we cannot just blindly take a statement that you have to respect our parents. We have to use our intelligence. Yes, the culture is we have to respect our parents. But if you are very sure what they are saying is against scriptural injunctions, we have examples of great devotees that they have disobeyed their parents also.

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the need to discern genuine brahmanas from those who merely claim the title, and shares an anecdote about brahmanas performing a havan for material gain, illustrating the deteriorated state of affairs in Kali Yuga.

Yes, so such brahmanas can be just officially given respect. But the kind of worship, what is respected, we have to use our discretion. So-called brahmanas, they're all, you know, dressed as brahmanas, claimed as brahmanas, but may not be. I told you the qualification of a brahmana. So that as a matter of ritual, you want to give, they're asking something. 1000 rupees, 2000 rupees, at your discretion, whatever their market rate is, you can give. Here we are talking about a different category. Qualified brahmanas who are genuine. And after we understand this science, we will be able to at least have some discern ability. Most of the so-called brahmanas, yesterday we went to Axis Bank, we had some work. So the bank manager, I said, some havan and all was happening. So then I said, what is this? So she said, for positive energy in the bank, havan is happening. So the two brahmanas were there. And they did all that yajna and whatever, that ceremony and all that. And then one of them had that laptop bag. He put all the things, item back and all that. And then he was standing, waiting for his remuneration. The bank had to pay something for having conducted this. So it is happening at a very, very... the person who is doing it also is doing with material motive. The person who is actually getting into this occupation also is purely because it is a source of revenue for him. The whole original intent of thing is lost. It is a profession. It fetches their money. They do a yajna and gets 4000 rupees, 2000 rupees. And the person who does it also, He has no intent of pleasing the Lord. Some positive energy. Yajna is not done for that. Dramanas are not meant to be used for this. So the whole, in Kali Yuga, the whole equation is that way very deteriorated.

#### cleanliness, internal, external
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Qgz1f2m93Io?t=1279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the austerities of the body, including respecting superiors, and emphasizes the importance of cleanliness, both external (physical) and internal (through chanting and devotional activities), to cleanse the heart.

Worship of spiritual masters, superiors like father and mother. Yes, we have to respect them. But there have been occasions where great devotees have even disobeyed their fathers and it is considered to be okay. So then austerity of the body also includes practicing, practice cleansing the body externally and internally. How do we clean externally? By taking a bath regularly. It is mentioned, Dramanas take minimum of how many baths in a day? Three times. In today's world, one time is also enough. So it brings about external cleanliness. Internal cleanliness, how does it come about? Internal cleanliness. So internal cleanliness happens by chanting, by remembering the Lord. So practice cleansing, cheto, darpanam, marjanam, bhava maha, davagni, nirvapanam. Cheto, our heart which is now impure, which is covered with the dust. We are not able to see ourself. This chanting process cleanses the mirror of our heart. Just like a mirror.

#### heart, simplicity, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Qgz1f2m93Io?t=1377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Right now, our state is like that. A heart, the mirror of a heart is covered with a layer of dust."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a dusty mirror to illustrate the state of the heart covered with ignorance and materialism, and explains that chanting cleanses the heart, relieving us from the blazing fire of material existence, and that austerity of the body also involves simplicity in behavior and avoiding duplicity.

Assuming you have a mirror in your this thing and you open the house after three years, it was locked. The mirror would have been coated with a layer of dust. And you will not be able to see properly. And you wipe that mirror, you cleanse the mirror, you will be able to see properly. Right now, our state is like that. A heart, the mirror of a heart is covered with a layer of dust. What is the dust? Dust of? Dust of ignorance, dust of materialism. And cheto, darpanam, when we cleanse the dirt, cheto, darpanam, darpanam means mirror, marjanam, marjanam means cleansing. bhava maha davagni, because of this dust, because of this material thing which has, so to say, glued on to us, bhava maha davagni, we are the blazing fire of material existence. It's called davagni. Just like in a forest, it's a forest fire. Everything is burning. Likewise, we are also burning in the fire of material existence. So when chanting happens, it cleanses the heart and we get relief. We get relief from the blazing fire of material existence. Austerity of body also involves to be very simple in behaviour. Further, it will be said, no duplicity. Many times, people will say something in the front, something in the back, something in the front. They will say something else in the back. So no duplicity, simple. Simple living, high thinking.

Internally. We don't mind cheating someone. We don't mind stabbing someone. We don't mind pulling someone down. We don't mind speaking behind the back of a person. #### simplicity, devotion, material nature

**Summary:** The speaker explains that simplicity in behavior is a natural consequence of purification, and that good qualities manifest in a devotee's behavior without extraneous effort, as opposed to artificial commitments that are easily broken due to the influence of material nature.

Simplicity in behaviour is a natural consequence of a person becoming pure. When a person becomes a devotee in the real sense, all the good qualities manifest in his behaviour. In one sense, we don't have to make an extraneous effort. No, I won't be selfish. A lot of people make a commitment like that. Somebody makes a comment, you are very selfish. You have a lot of ego. No, I won't have ego from tomorrow. I will be very honest from tomorrow. But we are forced by material nature. Because we are overpowered by greed, lust. It's goading us. When we see someone making progress, we become envious. So when a person reaches a state where he has conquered his lower selves, his mind, his senses, he is experiencing happiness from within, then he doesn't have to indulge in all this. Pulling someone down, harming someone. Does it answer your question? It's natural. It cannot be artificially imitated. That will be artificial. If you say, I won't harm anyone after tomorrow. I won't do this after tomorrow. I won't get angry after tomorrow. It will not work.

#### soul, symptoms, rajoguna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Qgz1f2m93Io?t=1708
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is a conditioned soul. The soul is Sat Chit Ananda. It's blissful."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the soul is blissful (Sat Chit Ananda), and that trying to suppress anger symptomatically is superficial, as the root cause lies in rajoguna (passion), which gives rise to kama (desire), and unsatisfied kama leads to anger.

Yes. That is a conditioned soul. The soul is Sat Chit Ananda. It's blissful. Yeah. It can be a stepping stone. But the foundation is weak. Foundation on which that quality is resting is weak. It is just like for example, if I have fever, I go and take a paracetamol tablet, the fever has subsided. But it's a symptomatic relief. The real cause has not been at rest. There are many books, when you are getting angry, do deep breathing. Yes, it helps. I will not say it doesn't help. But, that's very superficial. The root cause, why you are getting angry, is not at rest. Why we get angry. kama esha, krodha esha, rajoguna samudbhava.

#### rajoguna, purification, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Qgz1f2m93Io?t=1774
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we come under the influence of rajoguna, kama is born. And when kama is not satisfied, anger is born."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the influence of rajoguna, leading to kama and krodha, and emphasizes that a lasting solution requires addressing the root cause rather than just the symptoms, and that purification of the heart through devotion leads to natural good qualities.

When we come under the influence of rajoguna, kama is born. And when kama is not satisfied, anger is born. kama esha, krodha esha, rajoguna samudbhava, mahashano mahapaapma ihavairina. Now, we are not wanting to address, rajoguna. If symptomatically we try to address the symptoms of rajoguna, which is anger, which is frustration, which is anxiety, it will not be a long lasting solution. There are lot of people who don't get angry, but the foundation is shaky. Lot of people who are very peaceful, lot of people who are tolerant, but something happens in the family, they are eventually shattered, rattled for life. Death happens in the family, rattled for life. Otherwise they are very bold people, rattled for life. Because the foundation on which this quality is built or practiced is shaky. That's why I am saying purification of the heart when it happens, when a person becomes a devotee of the Lord, when he controls his lower self, his base qualities, kama, krodha, then all these good qualities are a natural consequence. They come on their own. They are not stuffed on your personality.

#### spiritual life, backbone, Brahma Murta
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/QTy8CMriXqU?t=270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we have to have a very nice, healthy, genuine spiritual growth, what is the most important thing which we should lay emphasis on, which we should prioritize, which we should not compromise?"
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the four main topics of the discussion: defining spiritual life, identifying the backbone of spirituality, understanding the importance of Brahma Murta, and discussing practical do's and don'ts.

So, today's discussion, I have broadly divided in four broad categories or four broad subtopics. The first one would be, what is spiritual life? #### Brahma Murta, creator's hour, sunrise
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/QTy8CMriXqU?t=1527
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahmamurta is also called Brahma. Brahma is Lord Brahma, who is the creator. It's also called the creator's hour. It's a very, very important time of the day."
**Summary:** The speaker defines Brahma Murta as the creator's hour, a very important time of day, occurring 96 minutes before sunrise and lasting for 48 minutes.

What is Brahmamurta? All of us have heard about Brahmamurta. Brahmamurta is also called Brahma. Brahma is Lord Brahma, who is the creator. It's also called the creator's hour. It's a very, very important time of the day. And Muhurta, each Muhurta is for 48 minutes. And Brahmamurta is the penultimate Muhurta before sunrise. In other words, it starts 96 minutes before sunrise. Now, mind you, Brahmamurta starts 96 minutes before sunrise. So, what time can vary from place to place depending on what time the sun is rising in that particular place. So, it starts 96 minutes before sunrise and it lasts for 48 minutes, which is 48 minutes before sunrise. The end time is 48 minutes before sunrise. So, that 48 minutes period is called the Brahmamurta. 48 minutes, it lasts, it ends 48 minutes before sunrise and starts 96 minutes before sunrise. The total duration is 48 minutes.

---
#### Brahma Murta, prime time, Satwaguna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/QTy8CMriXqU?t=1614
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This morning time one and a half hours before sunrise is very, very prime time."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the morning time, especially 1.5 hours before sunrise, is prime time for spiritual growth, similar to prime time in television, due to the predominance of Satwaguna.

Again, we don't have to be very precise that I have to get up at that time and I have to necessarily stop all activities, spiritual activities as soon as the Brahmamurta time ends. No. Just for our understanding that this morning time... One and a half hours before sunrise is very, very prime time. Just like in television also, there's a prime time where a lot of people are watching the television. So, all the, you know, the ad companies, they're looking for prime time. Because that's when they get the maximum mileage if they put an ad. So, even for a person who wants to grow spiritually, practice spiritual life, this is the prime time. Why Brahmamurta is the prime time? Because this is a time where there's a lot of Satwaguna. They say as day passes by, the day starts, morning time before sunrise or even after sunrise, Satwaguna is predominant. There are many reasons why Brahmamurta is very special. One of the reasons is Satwaguna is very prominent. As day passes by, the Rajaguna becomes prominent. A lot of action, a lot of activities gets triggered. We have seen how as the day starts, a lot of activities gets triggered. A lot of action. And then, towards the end of the day, it's mode of ignorance of Tamoguna is prominent. That's how you'll see a lot of bars, pubs, brothels. All that become active during the night time. The temples open in the morning time, day time, early morning. For that matter, all places of, irrespective of religion, all places of worship, they start the activities morning time.

We just want to watch some television mindlessly, watch some news channels. Whatever is possible, we should aspire for sleeping on time, irrespective of day of the week. Not on weekends. We can relax a little bit, get up a little late. Ideally, we should sleep at the same time and get up at the same time. Then what happens is the body clock gets set. Then there is no struggle to get up in the morning. For many of us, if you want to get up in the morning, it can become a very big struggle. #### material duties, spiritual duties, balance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9qz9Xp1h9WA?t=180
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So today the discussion is all about that, how we can strike a healthy balance."
**Summary:** The lecture begins by introducing the topic of balancing spiritual and material duties, acknowledging the difficulty many people face in reconciling these two aspects of life.

The topic we have selected for discussion today is a very interesting topic, very relevant for all the people who are trying to practice spiritual life while engaged in their so-called worldly duties. We often find it difficult to balance the two because our material obligations and duties also expect quality time from us to do justice to those duties. On the other side, we also understand that our spiritual pursuits also need a lot of energy, quality time. And at times we might find ourselves riding on two boats, with one leg on a boat heading towards a material direction, and one leg on a boat heading towards an opposite direction, and it may not be a very, very pleasant experience. So today the discussion is all about that, how we can strike a healthy balance.

#### prayer, Krishna consciousness, spiritual master

Before we start the discussion, let's start the session with prayers. I would request all of you, depending on the circumstances at your end, you can also join in offering these prayers. Either you can repeat after me if it permits. Otherwise, you can just hold your hands and be with me while I chant these prayers. Let's start with the prayers now.
