That's most appropriate. Okay, anybody else? How many of us feel that gradual tapering down is a good idea? How many of us say, if you have decided to give it up, give it up? Others? Some people have not raised their hands, still thinking, still not made up their mind, should we give up or not give up? Life continues, status quo, maintain status quo. Okay, so anybody else? Yes. Okay. You see, we have to deal with a very, very important aspect in our mind-body system, and that is our mind. And mind, Krishna says, is a great enemy if it is not tamed. Can be friend also if it is tamed.

---
#### mind, temptation, commitment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RD9Gq3ImnME?t=781
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So I subscribe to the idea, if one person is convinced that this is not the right thing to do, better not leave it to the mind to cheat."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about friends who started smoking and repeatedly failed to quit, illustrating the dangers of gradual tapering and the mind's tendency to deceive, advocating for a firm commitment to giving up harmful habits.

So many times... So, if we tread this path of gradual, this thing, we can maybe go on for a long time. And this time it works, let's take it once. I remember when I was in hostel, so some of my friends, you know, because of social pressure started smoking. And you know, it started off once in a while, one cigarette a day, maybe on some occasions, on some parties or something like that. And then it started, the numbers started increasing, two cigarettes, three cigarettes a day, four cigarettes a day. A time came, you won't believe, friends coming from good families. I did mentioning from Kurukshetra, Faridabad, Ghaziabad, big affluent families, industrialists. But parents, to keep a check on their children, would send a limited amount. So they would reach a stage towards the fag end of the month where all the money would have got exhausted. These friends coming from affluent families got so hooked on to smoking, they started smoking BDs. Those days, I remember used to get a BD bundle, 50 BDs for about two rupees or one rupee or something. They could not afford cigarettes, but they started smoking BDs. And they were friends, I still remember, they would make a promise. This is the last cigarette of my life. Hold on with great conviction, this is the last cigarette, after this I will not smoke. There was a friend of mine, very close, we used to share the room. And I have heard him saying this, I think 30-40 times, this is the last cigarette of my life. And I met him recently, he is in Singapore, in a very fairly good position. And I met him, he is still smoking. After 25 years, still smoking. I don't know how many times he would have resolved. This is the last cigarette of my life. So I subscribe to the idea, if one person is convinced that this is not the right thing to do, better not leave it to the mind to cheat. Because if we say we will tread that middle path, thin line, once in a while take it, we can always get cheated and trip over and fall for the temptations.

Now, definitely when it comes to managing the fire. Now, definitely when it comes to managing the fire. When it comes to managing the friend circles and all that, there is a way of doing it. In fact, these days people respect, they give space to others, there are people who say I am a vegan, I don't take tea or maybe milk or this thing. People respect person for his what you call choices. So I think we should be a little firm and not play around with these kind of things where the mind can cheat us. Provided the person is convinced. If the person is convinced that yes, there is a craving, you should try to. No doubt, occasionally some fall downs will happen. That's a different thing rather than making it a plan that once in a month is okay. Slips can happen. It should not be premeditated slips. Once in a month slip. That is not advisable. Anyone else would like to? Vikas, would you like to contribute something? Yes. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. When we come to this path, we will lose some friends. Let's be ready for it. We will lose some friends. But we will also in the process make some more friends with like-minded people. Birds of same feather flock together. So when we have people with different interests who don't resonate with you with your choices. I'm sure they would not like to very thickly associate with you. You may lose some friends. and in due course of time if you are quite fixed about this you may also end up making new friends

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### DEALING WITH ATTACHMENTS | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, attachments, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So overall it's going to be a very informative session with some important insights based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of dealing with attachments, outlining the session's focus on the root cause, types of attachments, and insights from the Bhagavad Gita and Srila Prabhupada's teachings.

Hare Krishna! Warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. We have chosen a very, very interesting topic. The topic is dealing with attachments. All of us have attachments. Attachments to people, attachment to things, attachment to our own life, attachment to situations, circumstances. So this session will be focusing on what's the root cause of attachments? What are the various kinds of attachments? We'll talk about that. And towards the end of the session we'll be sharing some wonderful insights which we find in Bhagavad Gita. What is the right way of dealing with attachments? So overall it's going to be a very informative session with some important insights based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada. We'll start the session with prayers seeking their blessings. Blessings of Prabhupada, blessings of Lord Chaitanya, blessings of Lord Shri Krishna. So that we can have a very, very meaningful session with lot of practical insights. So please join me in offering the prayers.

---
#### detachment, materialism, story
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Tere baap ki seat hai."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa shares a personal anecdote about a train journey in India to illustrate the futility of attachment to temporary material possessions.

Many years back, when I was studying, I remember having travelled from Kurukshetra to Delhi. I was doing my B.Tech from Kurukshetra REC. And I happened to travel by train and it was an unreserved bhogi. We could not get the reservation. In fact, it was a short distance. And I got into the unreserved bhogi. And typically, we all have some idea how the systems work in India. I was fortunate enough to get a seat in the unreserved bhogi. And I somehow sat very tightly so that I should not lose the seat. And in no time, the entire bhogi was jam-packed. There was not even space for people to move. Practically, the whole bhogi was like people on top of each other. And I remember halfway through, I had some bit of urge to go to the restroom, to the toilet. But somehow I resisted. A time came when it was inevitable. I had to go. But I am sitting in an unreserved bhogi. And the moment I get up, not sure if I will get the seat back. So I requested the person sitting next to me that for two minutes, I very specifically mentioned, for two minutes I am going, kindly help me with this seat. And I made sure that I have kept one handkerchief. That's a typical Indian system of booking a seat. So I kept the handkerchief, requested him kindly, please keep a watch on the seat. I'll just come back in two minutes. Somehow with great difficulty, I rushed to the toilet and I came back in two minutes. And to my surprise, there was a person sitting on that seat. A very hefty, well-built person. And the moment I requested that person, very politely, very gently I requested, Sir, I was sitting here, my seat. The moment he heard me, he looked at me with a stern face and in Hindi, he mentioned to me, not mentioned, I would say he remarked, Tere baap ki seat hai. And I was quite surprised. I said, just two minutes back I was sitting here, I am coming from Kurukshetra, all along I was sitting in this seat, I requested this gentleman also, I pointed to that person, don't you remember I had told you for two minutes, I am going, I am coming back. So this person remarked, this hefty person who was sitting on my seat, quote unquote my seat, said that this is an unreserved bogie. There is no reservation here. Do you have a reservation? Show me your reservation. So for a moment I was getting very upset, very angry, very irritated at that person. I was about to shout at him, get angry at him, but seeing his size, seeing my size, I somehow decided not to get angry, not to get into a brawl with him, a fight with him. Then, I started thinking, on one side, yes, I can keep quiet, maybe because I am weak, maybe because I am much smaller in size, maybe I cannot fight it out. And then my mind saying, but this is injustice. Actually, you were all along sitting in this seat, just went for two minutes, and how can he now claim that he will not get up?

---
#### detachment, life's journey, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=645
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We are here in this world for a temporary period."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that life is a temporary journey, like a train ride, and emphasizes the importance of not getting overly attached to material possessions or achievements, as everything is temporary.

In life, we will encounter many situations where we are hell-bent on having things which we want to have. At times, we are attached to many things. Now Bhagavad Gita gives us a much larger perspective of life. Many times in life, we want to achieve things by hook or crook. We see many scandals, many scams, where people have amassed wealth, thinking that, believing that, assuming that rest of the life, for eternity, they will be enjoying their riches. But we know the harsh reality of this world. We are here in this world for a temporary period. In that sense, this life is like a journey. At one point, we boarded this train called life, so called birthday, the day we took birth. And a day will come when we will deboard this train. We will so called reach the destination which is the end of the life. And we leave this body. The soul leaves the body. In that sense, this journey, be it 50 years, 60 years, 80 years, 100 years, is temporary. Just like in a train journey, there is no point getting attached to the bogie, attached to the seat. No doubt, while we are travelling, there may be some comfort. But in a scenario what I have mentioned, there is no point in overly getting attached to something, getting overwhelmed, getting bewildered, if we have the understanding that this life is temporary. One day, I have to leave everything and go.

---
#### detachment, soul, eternality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=798
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna in the Gita says, one who has taken birth is sure to die. And for one who has died, is sure to take birth again."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the importance of understanding the soul's eternality and the body's temporary nature, which can help in reducing attachment to material things, people, and situations.

Krishna in the Gita says, one who has taken birth is sure to die. And for one who has died, is sure to take birth again. So this perspective of life where we understand that this life is temporary, this body is temporary, this is a temporary journey, in our eternal time scale, this small phase of our existence, the way we look at things, the way we chase things, changes. The way we are attached to things, attached to people, changes.

---
#### detachment, karma, results of actions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=877
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You have the right to do your duty, but you are not attached, do not be attached to the results of your activities."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa discusses attachment to results, quoting the Bhagavad Gita on performing duty without attachment to outcomes, and emphasizing that results depend on more than just our efforts.

There is another kind of attachment. That attachment is, attachment to results. Results of our actions. Many many places in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna talks about, you have the right to do your duty, but you are not attached, do not be attached to the results of your activities. When we get overly attached to the results, when we want things to transpire in a particular way only, we are setting the game for misery or problems or difficulties in our life. Just like in chess, the game is set, the board is set. Likewise, if we are overly attached to outcomes, overly attached, things have to happen the way I want it to happen. Then only, I will be happy. We are setting the game of life, which will inevitably lead to difficulties or misery in our life. As I mentioned in a previous session, for the results of our activities, it is not just our endeavor. There are so many parameters which are involved. Krishna in the Gita says, ultimately it is Daivam, the super soul. So, this understanding that results of my actions, is not solely dependent on my endeavors alone, is again a very very important aspect which we have to keep in mind, so that we are not overly attached to the outcome of our actions.

---
#### detachment, offering to God, story
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=985
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Everything will slip out from our hands."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa shares a story about a man carrying puffed rice to illustrate that everything is temporary and will eventually slip away, emphasizing the importance of offering the results of our actions to God.

One time, there was a person who was carrying in his hand puffed rice. All of us know about puffed rice. He was carrying in his hand and he was passing through a forest. And, with both hands clasped, having the puffed rice, he was trying to hold on that puffed rice, ensuring that nothing slips out from his hand. And on that particular day, it was quite windy. Suddenly the wind started blowing and became very very so called something like a hurricane. So, this person clasped his hand even more tighter and tried holding on to that puffed rice, kept on holding, holding till little by little, because of that wind pressure, the rice started slipping out one by one, one by one from his hand till practically, practically nothing was left. And, while he was trying to hold very tightly, ensuring, realizing that he will not be able to hold, everything is slipping off, he held his hand up high in the air and said, Govindabhog, Govindabhog, Govindabhog, indicating to Govinda that I am offering this puffed rice to you. The lesson we can learn from this story is, everything will slip out from our hands. Either we will leave the money we have or the money will leave us. So, we are not going to waste our time on this. Therefore, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna many, many places talks about do not be attached to the results of your activities.

---
#### detachment, charity, wealth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=1118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we are overly attached to the wealth and we want to enjoy the results of our activities without keeping lord in picture, without realizing the results of all my work is coming from the lord our mentality is that of ravana although we may be so called religious and pious people"
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa discusses the importance of giving away the results of our activities and cultivating detachment, using the example of Ravana and Hanuman to illustrate the difference between selfish enjoyment and offering to the Lord.

That's why, in many places, Krishna talks about to give away the results of your activities, learn to give away, cultivate that detachment. Again, we require results. For example, a person is working, the result of his action is his salary, the money what he gets in return. No doubt it is required for our sustenance. But many times we are overly attached to the wealth. It is said that Ravana was a great devotee of Lord Shiva. But he wanted to enjoy Sita Devi, who was property of Lord Ramachandra. Hanuman also wanted Sita Devi, when Ravana kidnapped Sita Devi, Hanuman also wanted Sita Devi. But he wanted Sita Devi for pleasure of Lord Ramachandra. So when we are overly attached to the wealth. So when we are overly attached to the wealth. to the wealth and we want to enjoy the results of our activities without keeping lord in picture, without realizing the results of all my work is coming from the lord our mentality is that of ravana although we may be so called religious and pious people

#### attachment, relationships, transcendental knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=1415
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A sober person, a person in knowledge, a wise person, is not bewildered by such a change."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa discusses the entanglement caused by excessive attachment to the material world, particularly to people, and emphasizes the importance of transcendental knowledge to navigate relationships with understanding and detachment.

When we get overly attached to anything in this material world, we get entangled. Next, I was talking about various kinds of attachment. We talked about attachments to comforts, attachment to results of our work. We can be attached to people, attached to our near and dear ones, our family members, our kith and kin. And, at times, that attachment can be very, very strong. Jagjit Singh, Chitra Singh, I am sure all of you have heard about. They had a son who met with an accident and he left the body. He expired. It was such a shock. It was such a shock to the whole family. It seems from that day onwards, Chitra Singh stopped singing. When we are overly attached to people, overly attached to our family members, no doubt, somebody may say, what's wrong? What's wrong with attachment? Is it not right to be attached to our near and dear ones? Yes, some amount of attachment is understandable. But Krishna in the Gita says, while we live this world, we should have the understanding that Dehinosminyatha Dehe Kaumaram Yavanam Jara Tatha Dehantra Praptir A time will come when we have to leave this world. All relationships will break. Dheeras Tatranamuyati A sober person, a person in knowledge, a wise person, is not bewildered by such things.

---
#### relationships, detachment, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=1555
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If we are overly attached to someone, more will be the pain, more will be the agony, more will be the distress, which we will have to undergo."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa uses analogies of straws floating on a river and strangers in a hotel to illustrate the temporary nature of relationships in the material world, emphasizing that excessive attachment leads to pain and distress.

In the Bhagavatam, we get very nice example of what actually these relationships are in this material world. Bhagavatam gives example of the straws floating on the river. You know in a river, there are waves and the straws are floating. They are practically like blown with the waves. So for a while, the straws come together. And the next wave comes, they all get segregated. So we all, as souls, are individuals. In this life, in this particular life, we have come together as friends, brothers, relatives, father, mother. And then the wave of Kala will come, which is inevitable. We all will be again separated. Sometimes people say that, you know, why do you talk about things which are painful to hear, which are not very pleasant. Why do we talk about, yes, a time will come, relationships will break. Why don't we talk about some pleasant things of life? Yes, we can talk about pleasant things. There are many pleasant things in the Bhagavad Gita also Krishna mentions about. But let's not turn a blind eye to the realities of life. This is a reality. We are not talking about philosophy. We are talking about realities of life. A day will come when relationships will break. If we are overly attached to someone, more will be the pain, more will be the agony, more will be the distress, which we will have to undergo.

---
#### detachment, life as a dream, story
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=1773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Should I moan for those eight children which I had in the dream when I was a king or should I moan for Haru who died yesterday?"
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa shares a story of a farmer who dreamt he was a king with eight children to illustrate that life is like a prolonged dream, and excessive attachment to it is futile.

Once there was a person, a farmer, who got married and for many years he could not get a child. He tried different things and eventually by the blessings of the Lord, he was blessed with one child, one son, and his name was Haru. So every day he would go to plough his fields, work quite hard to earn his livelihood and come back and it was a small family, he, his wife and Haru. Haru grew up and one day while he was tilling the fields, he was in the farm, suddenly a person rushed to him and said, Haru is not feeling well, something has happened to him. And he rushed home and then he was not in a good condition and then some days passed by, his condition worsened, he was diagnosed with cholera and eventually he died. So everyone in the family were completely devastated, the wife could not be consoled, she was crying pathetically. And this person, this farmer, was very calm and composed. In fact next day he again went to his fields for working. So when he came back home, he saw his wife was even weeping very bitterly. So family members had assembled, the relatives had come home and one of them asked, why are you so calm? Why are you not crying? So he told, last night I remember I had a very very vivid dream. In the dream, I was a king, I was living in a palace and I had eight children. I vividly remember the details of the dream. I was in a palace, I was a king and I had eight children. And now, today, after knowing that Haru has passed away, I am actually bewildered. I am actually in a dilemma. I am actually confused. Should I moan for those eight children which I had in the dream when I was a king or should I moan for Haru who died yesterday?

---
#### detachment, life as a dream, soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=1963
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This life is nothing but an extended dream from the perspective of the soul."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that life is like a prolonged dream from the perspective of the eternal soul, and understanding this can help us avoid excessive attachment to the happenings in this life.

The understanding we get from this story is that this life in one sense is like a prolonged dream. In the night, we have experience of dreams. We get dreams. Although the dream may be unreal, but the experience of the dream is real. That's why in the dream, if we have a dream where we are being chased by a tiger, at times we perspire. The experience is real, although the content may not be true. We go into a dream world, but the experience is real. Likewise, from the perspective of eternal soul, this life is like a dream. Experience is real, but from the perspective of the soul, this is my brother, this is my sister, this is my father, is like a dream. In that sense, it's like a prolonged dream. In the night, we have a dream for 2 hours, 3 hours, 4 hours, and we wake up. And even if we have a pleasant dream or unpleasant dream, we somehow reconcile. It was after all a dream. This life is nothing but an extended dream from the perspective of the soul. So that way, if we have this understanding that this life just like a dream, we don't get overly attached. Suppose in a dream we had, we got lot of riches, lot of money, we were living in a palatial house, big bungalow, we had many many cars, and suddenly we wake up, we don't start crying. We just reconcile with the fact that after all it was a dream. We don't get overly attached to the contents of the dream knowing that it's a dream.

---
#### attachment, material world, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=2153
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Although Krishna in the Gita talks about the nature of this world as Dukkhalayam Ashashvatam we get overly attached to this place."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa shares the story of Indra becoming a pig to illustrate how attachment to situations and circumstances in the material world can lead to ignorance and reluctance to return to the spiritual world.

Once, Indra Indra was cursed by his spiritual master, Brahaspati to become a pig. So Indra took birth as a pig and he was living with his family. The wife was there and piglets were there, children were there. And you know typically pig lives in very filthy places, stools strewn all around. And he got attached to his family, to the piglets, to his children, to his wife. And a time came when Brahma realized that Indraloka, there is a necessity for him to come back now because things were not going properly in his absence. So Brahma came down to take Indra back to Indraloka so that he can now start taking care of his responsibilities as Indra. So when Brahma came, he saw Indra in the form of a pig and you know dabbling with day to day affairs with his children, overly attached to his wife. And Brahma came and requested now is the time for you to come back. Please come back and take charge of Indraloka. So Indra was reluctant. He said no, no, no, how can I go? I have so many responsibilities. I am attached to my children, my wife, everyone. How will they manage without me? I cannot go. And Brahma to shake Indra out of that so called attachment actually killed the wife of the pig and the children. And this Indra in the form of a pig became very perturbed. How can you do that? Then Brahma pointed out anyway you see there this person you know pigs generally are slaughtered for meat. So he pointed out the person who was sharpening his knife they were supposed to be slaughtered. So please wake up. Realize that you are Indra and come back to Indraloka. So what happens is many times we have come to this material world for a short while, for few years and although Krishna in the Gita talks about the nature of this world as Dukkhalayam Ashashvatam we get overly attached to this place. Although we encounter many many miseries many many difficulties but because of ignorance we get attached. We don't want to leave.

So far we have covered in the session that attachment if it is not properly regulated attachment to situations, attachment to people attachment to circumstances attachment to the results of our activities attachment to success or failure can call for trouble in our lives. If you are overly attached to things when those things move away from us we lose those things it brings a lot of pain and misery in our lives so therefore it is very very important that we no doubt attachment is natural but we regulate that attachment with the right understanding with right knowledge there's another kind of attachment which is attachment to success we want the outcome of our activities to be in a particular way we want to succeed in our lives as we are not exactly responsible for our decisions we want the outcome of our activities we want to be grateful and grateful and so in the Gita it says be steadfast in your vice and be honest in your actions and be humble in your actions and be honest in your actions so this is what Gita says about attachment that the attachment is complete and that as long as we are not attached to things that are in our lives we have no trouble in any way

#### detachment, yoga, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=3255
**Verse References:** BG 2.47, BG 2.57
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He who is without attachment, who does not rejoice when he obtains good, nor laments when obtains evil, is firmly fixed in perfect knowledge."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa quotes verses from the Bhagavad Gita emphasizing the importance of performing duty without attachment to success or failure and maintaining equanimity in happiness and distress to be firmly fixed in knowledge.

In yoga, O Arjuna, perform your duty and abandon all attachment to success or failure. Again, the emphasis on doing as a matter of duty. Such evenness of mind is called yoga. Another verse in the Gita, 2nd chapter, 57th verse, Krishna says, He who is without attachment, who does not rejoice when he obtains good, nor laments when obtains evil, is firmly fixed in perfect knowledge. In other words, Samadarshi. Equipoised in happiness and distress, in pleasure and pain, in gain or loss, he is fixed in knowledge. No doubt, we cannot artificially reach that stage.

#### detachment, spiritual advancement, mode of goodness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37c7ip5pmuw?t=3339
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The worker who is freed from all material attachments and false ego, who is enthusiastic and who is indifferent to success or failure, is a worker in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that detachment and equanimity are natural outcomes of spiritual advancement and growth in Krishna consciousness, and describes the characteristics of a worker in the mode of goodness.

When we tread this path of spirituality, when we advance spiritually, when we grow in our Krishna consciousness, this happens. This stage becomes easily accessible. The worker who is freed from all material attachments and false ego, who is enthusiastic and who is indifferent to success or failure, is a worker in the mode of goodness. So these are few instructions from the Bhagavad Gita as to what should be our mentality, what should be our consciousness, when we approach any work, for any work, the outcome could be success or it could be failure. When it is successful, we become very elated, very happy, very joyous, we celebrate, and when we meet with failure, we become morose, we become depressed. So here, instruction of Gita is one who acts as a matter of duty, who is equipoised, he is firmly fixed in knowledge. Now what is the cause of attachment? Many, many times we know things, there's a gap between knowing and doing. All of us know, Khali Aad Aaye Thee, Khali Aad Jayenge, everyone knows. But still we get overly attached to our wealth, to our property, to our acquisitions, our near and dear ones. Everyone knows about it but still, in the end. But still, in the end.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### DEALING WITH TEMPTATIONS | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### temptation, control, restraint
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All of us invariably get tempted, more so in present day world where there are so many distractions, so many allurements, so many sense objects all around us alluring us with so many channels, with so many opportunities to see, to hear, to touch, to eat."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of dealing with temptations, emphasizing the prevalence of temptations in the modern world and the importance of exercising restraint and control, followed by a prayer offering.

Hare Krishna! A warm welcome to all of you for today's Geeta Live session. We have a very interesting topic today. It is to do with temptations and the topic is dealing with temptations. All of us invariably get tempted, more so in present day world where there are so many distractions, so many allurements, so many sense objects all around us alluring us with so many channels, with so many opportunities to see, to hear, to touch, to eat. We all get tempted day in and day out. So today's session will talk about temptations, why we have to exercise restraint, why we have to do it, why we have to control and what are the different things we can do to effectively resist temptations, more so when we are convinced that it is not in our self-interest. So before we get into the discussions, let us start with prayers. Request all of you to kindly fold your hands. You can join me in offering these prayers together and immediately after the prayers, we will get into the discussions.

---
#### mind control, senses, spirituality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=294
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The first point is beware the mind tricks, I repeat beware the mind tricks."
**Summary:** The first principle is introduced: beware the mind tricks. The speaker uses the analogy of a chariot to explain the relationship between the soul, intelligence, mind, and senses, highlighting the importance of controlling the mind to achieve spiritual progress.

The first point is beware the mind tricks, I repeat beware the mind tricks. In the previous session I had shared with you an analogy of a chariot driven by five horses, in which the five horses represent our five senses, the reins which control the horses represents our mind, the chariot driver represents our intelligence and the passenger who is sitting in the chariot represents the soul. Now we also discussed that how in normal circumstances the flow of command is from the passenger to the driver to the horses or to the steering wheel and then the vehicle drives in the direction in which the passenger wants to go. But in present circumstances with uncontrolled mind and uncontrolled senses the flow of command is in the reverse direction, it is the mind, it is the senses which is dictating us and the bewildered soul is completely lost, not knowing where he is heading. Actually that is a predicament of most of the people in present day circumstances. So today the first principle we are covering is beware the mind cheats. Beware the mind cheats. Beware the mind cheats. Beware the mind cheats. Now as I mentioned the senses are untamed, they are going berserk. Just visualize you are in a chariot driven by horses, all the horses are not tamed, are all unruly, each one trying to pull in different direction. The chariot will wobble, it can skid off the road and can meet with accident and I am sure the passenger who is seated in that chariot will be completely bewildered and panic stricken. So as scriptures say that mind if it is controlled is our best friend but the same mind if it is uncontrolled is our worst enemy. What is spirituality? Spirituality is all about how we control our mind. How we control our mind. How we control our mind. How we control our mind. How we control our senses. How we control our urges so that we can focus our mind on the Supreme, on the Lord.

---
#### temptation, mind, control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So many times we know that I should not be doing something, I should not be eating something, but we create circumstances or rather we get into some circumstances at times intentionally where we will get tempted and we fall for it."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the mind's tendency to lead us astray, even when we know what is right. He shares a story about a child who went swimming in a forbidden canal, illustrating how the mind can trick us into putting ourselves in tempting situations.

So mind is not bad but mind if it is untamed, if it falls prey to all kinds of temptations and allurements, it can completely take us away from the ultimate goal of human form of life. That's why in Vedic times from the very beginning, the first ashrama, the first 25 years was spent on training, controlling the mind, controlling the senses. And many times as I talked about the principle is beware the mind tricks. The mind at times makes plans although yes we know that I should not be doing something, I should not be eating something, I am convinced but still the mind will trick and we may fall prey to such allurements. One time a father told his son, beta do not go to that canal to swim because that canal had huge currents. The current of water was quite high. And the father warned the child do not go to that canal to swim. There was a danger there. The child was quite obedient in the sense that he agreed in principle. He said yes father, I will not go. But one day, the father noticed the son came back home completely drenched in the swimming trunks. The water was dripping from his body, quite obvious he had gone to the canal to swim. So the father asked the child, I told you not to go to the canal for swimming, why did you go? So the child replied apologetically, father I was in the swimming trunks next to the canal, I got tempted. It is quite obvious now you are in a swimming trunk, canal is there, fresh water, obviously you will get tempted. So he said, I was in the swimming trunks next to the canal and I got tempted. So I just took a dip. So father said, but why did you go to the canal with swimming trunks? So child innocently replied. I went to the canal with swimming trunks so that in case, in case, in case I get tempted I could take a swim. See how the mind tricks, although father has told him not to go, but he has gone to canal with the swimming trunks, obviously he will get tempted. So many times we know that I should not be doing something, I should not be eating something, but we create circumstances or rather we get into some circumstances at times intentionally where we will get tempted and we fall for it. So mind is in that sense cheats us. So we have to be beware.

---
#### sense gratification, austerity, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=697
**Verse References:** SB 5.5.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Human form of life is meant for tapo divyam for restrain for control now somebody may say why control if something is pleasurable if something gives me pleasure why should he restrain why should he stay away from it so Rishabhdev says tapo divyam when we restrain our senses when we undergo some austerity when we undergo some penance."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Rishabhdev from the Srimad Bhagavatam, emphasizing that human life should not be spent in unrestricted sense gratification. He explains that human life is meant for austerity and control, leading to purification and real pleasure, contrasting this with the temporary pleasure derived from uncontrolled senses.

Rishabhdev who is the father of Bharat Maharaj. This is in Srimad Bhagavatam. This India is also called Bharat Varsh and the name is also on the king Bharat Maharaj. So Bharat Maharaj has been advised by Rishabhdev. Naayam Deho Deha Bhajan Nirloke. For one who has taken birth as a human being. Kastan Kaaman Arhateyam. For one who has taken birth as a human being. Kastan Kaaman Arhateyam. For one who has taken birth as a human being. We should not unrestrictedly dabble in sense gratification. In other words, the senses are getting allured. We have various temptations and we fall for it and just enjoy our senses. So he says we should not fall for it. In other words, we should restrain, regulate. Kastan Kaaman Arhateyam Vidh Bhujan. And the sense gratification, temptation for some objects this is there in all the species of life, temptations are there even in animal life and they fall for temptations and they enjoy and what is human life meant for so Rishabhdev says tapo divyam shudhyet brahma saukhyam tu anantam human form of life is meant for tapo divyam for restrain for control now somebody may say why control if something is pleasurable if something gives me pleasure let's say a person who is very fond of smoking or is addicted to smoking it gives him pleasure why should he restrain why should he stay away from it so Rishabhdev says tapo divyam when we restrain our senses when we undergo some austerity when we undergo some penance like for example today is Ekadashi many devotees are fasting it is not that there is shortage of food but it is tapasya so what happens when we do tapasya tapo divyam shudhyet we'll get purified, our mind will get purified our consciousness will get purified tapo divyam shudhyet will get purified and then what happens brahma saukhyam tu anantam and then and only then we have pure consciousness will be able to enjoy shudhyet will be able to get purified brahma saukhyam tu anantam will be able to enjoy will be entitled to enjoy real pleasure.

#### spirituality, happiness, temptation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=876
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spirituality is not about just giving up things spirituality is not against enjoyment but one needs to know the art and science of how to enjoy."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that spirituality is not about giving up enjoyment, but about understanding the proper way to enjoy. He uses the analogy of a fish being tempted by bait to illustrate how temporary pleasures can lead to misery, contrasting this with the unending and fulfilling nature of real pleasure.

Spirituality is not about just giving up things spirituality is not against enjoyment but one needs to know the art and science of how to enjoy how to properly enjoy even the fish which gets tempted to the bait which is hanging on the hook it's tempted. Apparently it gets pleasure, it gets momentarily some enjoyment but that very enjoyment becomes the cause of its death. So Maya Sukhaya, there are many sorts of happiness in this world which are very tempting but they entangle us, they bind us and become the cause of our misery. Let's say in today's news we have been hearing that how the NCB has been cracking down on the drug racket. So many famous Bollywood actors and actresses have now been questioned and they have even confessed. They all are rich, they can all enjoy parties, they all have name, fame, enough of sense gratification but why are they going for drugs? Something is missing in their life. That happiness what they are enjoying is not fulfilling, they are not contented. So Rishabhdev is saying, Brahma saukhyam tu anantam, you will enjoy real pleasure and what is the nature of that pleasure? Nature of that pleasure is its anantam, it is unending. It is real pleasure, it satisfies the self and it is anantam. It is not temporary. It completely satisfies the self and it is permanent in nature. So why we should restrain, why we should control, why we should try to resist the temptations?

#### discrimination, intelligence, control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=1010
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Now in human form of life we have got that intelligence to discriminate, spiritual intelligence to understand what is good, what is bad and hence Rishabhdev is saying human form of life is meant for that."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts human and animal behavior, noting that humans have the intelligence to discriminate between good and bad and to control their impulses. He reiterates that human life is meant for controlling the mind and senses, leading to purification and the enjoyment of real, unending pleasure.

In animal form we cannot resist. You throw a bone to a dog and say no today is Ekadashi, you can probably eat it tomorrow, the dog will not understand. Even if you speak in the language which dog understands. I am sure the dogs communicate, let's say dog probably who is quite wise, may be one who is full of wisdom, he cannot instruct a fellow dog today is Ekadashi, better you eat the bone tomorrow. Even if wants to he cannot, they act impulsively, throw a bone, it acts impulsively, they don't have the development. They have developed intelligence to understand, to control, to introspect, it's not good for me, I should not do it. Now in human form of life we have got that intelligence to discriminate, spiritual intelligence to understand what is good, what is bad and hence Rishabhdev is saying human form of life is meant for that. Tapo avatara. When we do this kind of tapasya, when we control our mind, control our senses, lead a principled life based on instructions of Krishna in the Gita, then shudhyet will become purified and when we get purified what happens, we become entitled to enjoy real pleasure, point one and the nature of that pleasure is anantam, it is unending, it is permanent and it completely satisfy the self.

#### illusion, death, worldly pleasures
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=1112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Imagine a person who was quite hungry, had not eaten in a fearful situation and then he has abundant supply of honey. Honey is there. Honey is dripping, pure honey, pure organic honey."
**Summary:** The speaker begins a story about a man chased by a tiger who climbs a tree for safety. While on the tree, he notices rats gnawing at the branch and a honeycomb dripping with honey. Despite the danger, he becomes engrossed in tasting the honey.

Once there was a man who was going through a forest and suddenly he found a tiger chasing him and just visualize the scenario, you are a tiger in a forest and the tiger suddenly comes in and starts chasing, I am sure the tiger runs very fast but the man has no option but to chase. He has no heart to run, although heart of heart he knows that the tiger runs ten times faster than him. So the man started running, trying to save his life, fortunately, obviously he cannot run and save his life, fortunately he came across a tree and he quickly jumped on that tree and started climbing. And by the time the tiger reached the tree, the man was running. He was running. When he reached the tree, he had almost climbed to safe distance. So he went and sat on the branch of a tree, high enough that the tiger cannot leap and catch him. So he felt a sigh of relief. He was happy. But when he looked down, the tiger was watching him. Waiting. When he reached the tree, he had almost climbed to a safe distance. He was happy. But he thought, maybe he will come down so that he can have a nice meal. Maybe the tiger was quite hungry that day. So the tiger is sitting, patiently waiting for the man with his head up, looking at the man, waiting when he will come down, when he will fall from the tree so that he can have a nice feast. And the man, panic-stricken, with fear, with anxiety, is sitting on that branch of the tree. And then this man noticed this branch of the tree, at the corner of that branch, at the end of the branch, the end where the branch is connecting to the main trunk of the tree, he saw that there are two rats, two rats, they are nibbling on the branch. One rat, he noticed carefully, one rat was white in colour and another rat was black in colour. And what they were doing, they were not only nibbling on the branch, trying to eat that branch, maybe some kind of food for that rats, and they were also moving in circle, you know, the branch is circular and they were moving around the branch, nibbling on the branch. So somehow the branch was, you know. Once you eat that, you know, it is becoming little thinner. So this person was again fearful because the rats are going in circle, one white, one black, nibbling on the branch, the branch is becoming thicker and he is sitting on the branch. And below, the tiger is sitting with his head up, upwards, looking at this man. Quite fearful situation. While he was sitting there, trembling in fear, he looked up. He looked up. He looked up. He looked up. And he was quite hungry also because, you know, he was sitting there for quite some time. He was quite hungry. And when he looked up, there was another branch on top of him. Tree was huge. So another branch on top of him, when he looked at that branch, there was a honeycomb, the beehive where, and the honeybees were there and when he looked at that honeycomb, he saw some honey was dripping from that honeycomb. And since he was hungry, he just put his hand in the front, few drops of honey fell on the hand and he started licking. And it was so tasty. Imagine a person who was quite hungry, had not eaten in a fearful situation and then he has abundant supply of honey. Honey is there. Honey is dripping, pure honey, pure organic honey. So he is having his hand outstretched, honey is falling and he is licking. Again hand outstretched, few honey drops fall, again licks. And he is completely mesmerized by the taste of that honey. In fact, becomes so absorbed in licking that honey, enjoying that beautiful taste of that honey that he forgets that the branch on which he is sitting is being nibbled by two rats and any moment that branch can fall off and he becomes completely oblivious of the tiger who is sitting down, waiting for him to come down so that he can have nice meal.

#### illusion, death, worldly pleasures, time
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nheS-kNXJLg?t=1433
**Verse References:** BG 9.3, BG 15.15
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "That honey represents the worldly pleasures. The various temptations, the various pleasurable objects which tempts us. We get so absorbed. We get so tempted. We get so allured."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the symbolism of the story: the two rats represent day and night (time), the honeycomb represents worldly pleasures, and the tiger represents death. He emphasizes how we become absorbed in temporary pleasures and forget the reality of death and the ultimate purpose of life.

So now this story is very instructive. The two rats here represents the day and night. Day time as we know we have light and night time is dark. So the black rat represents night and day time is represented by white rat. So Kala, Mrityu Sarva Haras Chaham, Krishna says in the Gita, Kala, which comes in the form of day and night. Cycle of day and night, cycle of day and night, it eats away our life. If today I am 30 years, 40 years, 50 years, Kala has nibbled 40 years of my life, 50 years of my life. It has nibbled. I am on a branch of a tree where half of the branch is nibbled. By what? Black rat and the white rat, which is represented by Kala. By day and night, which comes in a cyclic order. Today it's day, then comes night, then comes day, then comes night, then comes day and that's how the Kala unfolds. Eats away our life. Now what does that honey comb represent with the honey dripping, which is so wonderful, so tasty. The man is completely enamored and completely absorbed in licking the honey. That honey represents the worldly pleasures. The various temptations, the various pleasurable objects which tempts us. We get so absorbed. We get so tempted. We get so allured. Bhogeshwariya prasaktanam tayaparita cetasam, our intelligence is lost, we are bewildered. We forget that death is there. We forget that we have limited time. We forget that there is an ultimate purpose to life. More than just eating, drinking, sleeping and mating. Eat, drink, be merry and enjoy life. There is some higher purpose. We forget that. We forget that Kala is eating away my time. Death is inevitable. Very soon I have to leave this world. We get tempted with the sense object. We get enamored. We get allured and forget the harsh realities of life. We get allured, tempted with false relationships which looks very pleasurable like honey and forget that day and night Kala is eating away and my life, my precious life with limited time what I have is getting over. So therefore it is very important that especially in human form of life, all of us who are blessed with some spiritual understanding.

**Summary:** The second principle is introduced: that which you resist persists. The speaker explains that suppressing emotions and urges can backfire, and that resisting temptations in the wrong way can be counterproductive.

Now we move to the second principle. While we are on the path of purification, trying to control our mind, control the senses and resist the temptations, we have to remember this principle and the principle is, please hear it carefully, that which you resist persists. I repeat that which you resist persists. So, now we have our senses, we have our mind and we have plethora of sense objects all around us, alluring us, tempting us. Now we try to restrain. At times suppress our feelings, suppress our emotions, suppress our urges and at times it can backfire. Yes we have to control. Scriptures are saying, Tapo divyam shudhye brahma saukhyam to anantam, we have to control. But many times we can control the wrong way. That which we try to resist, persist.

**Summary:** The speaker conducts an exercise where listeners are asked to avoid seeing anything green, illustrating how the act of resisting something makes it more prominent in our perception. He emphasizes that this is not the right way to control temptation.

Let us try one small exercise. I am sure all of us are at our place. I am literally asking each one of you, try, just try, if your situation permits. Just get up from where you are seated right now. Maybe you are sitting right in front of your laptop, in front of your TV screen, you are watching this video. Just get up for a moment. And maybe go to another room of your house. Again everyone's circumstances are unique. Get up from your seat and go to the adjacent room and come back after two minutes. Some of you can literally do this exercise. Before you get into this exercise, let me just complete the instructions. You can get up from your seat, go to some adjacent room, in your house. But while you go to the adjacent room and come back to your seat after two minutes, make sure you do not see anything which is green in color. I repeat the exercises. You have to get up from your seat, go to an adjacent room, if possible get out of your house, and come back in exactly two minutes. You have to get up from your seat, go to an adjacent room, if possible get out of your house, and come back in exactly two minutes. But while you do this exercise, make sure, make sure you do not see anything which is green in color. Some of you, if you want to, you can actually try it out. You can actually get up from your seat and return back to your seat in a minute or so. Maybe go away from the room where you are currently seated and come back in a minute or two. But make sure you do not see anything green in color. So let us wait. Maybe, who knows, somebody would have actually done this exercise, would have actually got up from the seat. So let's wait for a minute or so. Those who are not participating in this exercise, if you have any questions, anything which you would like to clarify based on what we discussed, feel free to ask. We will have enough time today to answer the questions. And in case some questions remain unanswered, we will request you to put them in the comment section and we will reply to those questions. So okay, here we are. I am sure some people who have tried doing this exercise would be back on their seats. If you have any questions, feel free to put them in the comment section and we will reply to those questions. What happens is, when we have an instruction that do not see anything green in color, we invariably end up noticing objects which are green in color, which otherwise we would have not noticed. Some of you will agree with what I am saying. If you have got up from your seat, gone to adjacent room, by the time you come back, although you are trying, I should not see green, I should not see green, all the objects which are green in color will actually stand up and you will notice them more than what you would have noticed even otherwise. So the principle is that which you resist, persist.

#### temptation, control, suppression

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the futility of suppressing emotions and urges, comparing it to a pressure cooker building up steam. He cautions against making unreasonable resolutions, as they can lead to a loss of confidence and self-esteem when broken.

So that is not the right way of controlling temptation. By resisting it, by suppressing those emotions, by suppressing those so-called inclinations and urges, no doubt control of urges has a place. So there is a saying, you cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it. I repeat, you cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it. I should not think about it. I should not think about it. I should not think about it. But you are indirectly thinking about that object. In fact, you are thinking more about that object. For a man who is, let us say, you know, invariably drawn to, I should not think about her, I should not think about her, I should not think about her, I should take her away from my mind, more you are thinking about that person. So that is not the process. This is not the process. This is the process. And what happens is short while, yes, when we suppress, when we restrain artificially from the sense objects for a while, yes, we can restrain, but then the pressure builds up. Just like in a pressure cooker, the steam may not come out but the steam is getting built up. And that is how pressure cooker has safety walls so that at one point when the steam has really built up in the pressure cooker, it can happen, actually ooze out. Imagine if the steam does not ooze out. There have been accidents where the pressure cooker burst. Imagine the thick pressure cooker with such a thick metal body it actually burst glass because the pressure has built up. So many times we make some very unreasonable rules from today onwards I will not eat sweets. From today onwards I will not watch any woman. It's unreasonable. And when we make such resolves or resolutions from today onwards I will not do anything wrong. It's unreasonable to arrest human. So when we make unreasonable resolutions then what happens is we invariably. From today onwards I will always get up at 3.30 a.m. From today onwards I will resist the temptation when the alarm rings to snooze it and again go back to my bed. From today onwards I will not do it.

#### integrity, self-esteem, control

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of a "personal integrity account," where keeping resolves increases self-esteem, while breaking them decreases it. He reiterates the importance of controlling the senses and resisting temptations, but emphasizes that artificial control is unpleasant and that experiencing a higher taste is the key.

Mistakes will happen. Mind will trick. The first principle beware the mind tricks. And what happens is if we have made unreasonable resolves, the mind tricks and then we lose confidence on ourselves. We lose our self esteem. Next time onwards you know we say I'm good for nothing. So many promises I have made, so many goals I have set. I'm not able to achieve anything. I'm an unsuccessful person. I'm a failure. I'm good for nothing. Such kind of thoughts starts coming in our minds. In fact, I've shared with all of you in one of the videos there's something called as personal integrity account. Personal integrity just like we have a bank account whenever we deposit some money there's a credit to our account. The bank balance increases. Whenever we withdraw some money from our bank there is a debit entry in the bank account and the balance reduces. So we have something called as personal integrity account. Every time we make a resolve and we stick to that resolve there's a credit entry in that integrity account. Our self esteem increases. And whenever we make a resolve I will not see this. I will not do this. And if I fail in doing that there's a debit entry and my bank balance personal integrity account will be reduced. So yes we have to control our senses. We have to resist temptations. There is no doubt about it. Our scriptures are saying human form of life is meant for controlling the urges. Human form of life is meant for tapo devyam, is meant for controlling the mind. So that we can focus that mind on Krishna, on the Lord, on self realization. Self realization. So this is not the right way. Krishna also in the Bhagavad Gita says the verse starts that this sense control if we do that the sense control if we try to do artificially it is very very unpleasant for a conditioned soul. And when a person experiences a higher taste automatically taste for lower things pales off.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### DESTINY & FREEWILL | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### destiny, freewill, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Jadi tolong ikut saya dalam memberikan doa ini. Anda bisa berpikir dan anda bisa berdoa dengan saya."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of destiny and freewill, framing it as a discussion about the extent to which our lives are predetermined by karma versus the choices we make, and then leads the audience in prayer.

Hari ini kita memiliki topik yang sangat menarik dan topiknya adalah Destinasi dan kebebasan. Untuk berapa jangka kehidupan kita dikaitkan dengan karma yang dikaitkan dengan predestinasi? Untuk berapa jangka kehidupan kita dikaitkan dengan destinasi? Apakah kita memiliki pilihan untuk memulai kehidupan kita? Jadi kita akan berbicara tentang hal ini hari ini. Sebelum kita mulai, mari kita berdoa kepada Tuhan Sri Krishna, Shiloprabhupada, Tuhan Chaitanya Mahaprabhu dan mencari kebenaran mereka supaya kita bisa memiliki diskusi yang berarti dengan pengetahuan yang menarik. Jadi tolong ikut saya dalam memberikan doa ini. Anda bisa berpikir dan anda bisa berdoa dengan saya. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Prishtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pantai Nithinamini Ashyatya Deshatarine Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadhadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratushe Namaha Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hithaya Cha Jagad Hithaya Krishnaya Govindaya Namo Namaha He Krishna Karunaya Karunasindhu Dinabandhu Jagadpate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostute Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanusute Devi Pranamami Hari Priyate Hrini Hari Priyate Vaan.

---
#### inequality, karma, destiny
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Dan ada seorang laki-laki lain, yang tinggal di rumah yang sama, bekerja selama 3 atau 4 jam, bekerja dari rumah, dan memanfaatkan kereta, memiliki Audi, 2 BMW, hidup dalam kesalahan, kadang-kadang terlihat seperti, tidak adil."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa shares a story about two men living in the same building in Mumbai, one a wealthy businessman with multiple luxury cars who works few hours a day, and the other a security guard who works long hours for very little pay, to illustrate the apparent unfairness and disparity in the world.

Kurang 3 and a half years ago ketika kami pertama-tama berpindah ke Mumbai, kami diberikan sebuah apartemen, tempat di mana kami harus tinggal pada dasarnya oleh salah satu pembantu kami. Sampai kami menemukan tempat yang sesuai untuk 6 bulan ke 1 tahun, kami tinggal di tempat itu. Dan tempat itu sangat luar biasa, di hirandani gardens di pawai. Dan salah satu pembantu kami, dia seorang yang sangat kaya, seorang laki-laki Gujarati. Dia memiliki 2 BMW, 1 Audi, dan kami selalu berinteraksi dengan dia. Dia selalu pergi ke pejabat selama 3-4 jam, kebanyakan dia bekerja dari rumah. Pada pagi dia pergi bermain tenis, hidup dengan sangat nyaman. Dan di bangunan yang sama, ketika saya selalu pergi berjalan-jalan pada pagi, kadang-kadang saya berinteraksi dengan laki-laki keamanan, atau orang-orang yang datang ke bangunan itu untuk bekerja. Jadi salah satu laki-laki keamanan, ketika saya berbicara dengan dia, saya sedikit berpikir-pikir tentang berapa banyak jam dia harus bekerja dalam sehari. Apa pemahaman yang dia miliki dengan pekerja. Jadi dia memberitahu saya, bahwa dia bekerja 7 hari seminggu, tanpa pernikahan, 12 jam dalam sehari. Meskipun kita memiliki kekurangan pekerjaan di negara, tapi saya tidak tahu bagaimana hal-hal itu sebenarnya berlaku di tingkat bawah. Dia bekerja selama 12 jam, 7 hari seminggu, dan salari dia, perbelanjaan mingguan adalah 8000 rupiah. Saya berbicara tentang Mumbai. Dan, kemudian saya sedikit berpikir-pikir, bagaimana dia bisa menjalankan hidup di Mumbai dengan salari 8000 rupiah. Jadi dia memberitahu saya bahwa dia dari Bihar, dan sekitar 5-6 orangnya berbagi ruang, ruang kecil, sekitar 8 km dari Powai. Dan dia berkata, ruangnya sekitar 8000 rupiah, jadi sekitar 5 orangnya berbagi ruang, sekitar 1,500 rupiah per orang, dan mereka memasak sendiri, mereka memasak sendiri. Dan masih lagi, dari 8000 rupiah, sekitar 3000-4000 rupiah, dia bisa menjalankan hidup dan menghantar ke rumahnya. Jadi saya bertanya ke dia, bagaimana kamu melewati, bagaimana kamu berjalan, sekitar 6 km dari Powai, bagaimana kamu berjalan? Jadi dia berkata, saya berjalan sehari-hari, ke atas dan ke bawah. Jadi saya berkata, kenapa kamu tidak mengambil bus? Saya yakin Mumbai memiliki banyak, terkenal untuk kereta, transportasi publik, bus. Jadi dia berkata, di satu sisi, tiket bus sekitar 20 rupiah. Jika saya berjalan sehari-hari dengan bus, apa yang akan saya simpan? Bagaimana saya akan bertahan hidup? Sekarang, ambil contohnya, orang yang bekerja 12 jam sehari, 30 hari sebulan, tanpa beban, bekerja dengan senang hati, mendapatkan 8000 rupiah, tidak mampu mendapatkan transportasi publik. Dan ada seorang laki-laki lain, yang tinggal di rumah yang sama, bekerja selama 3 atau 4 jam, bekerja dari rumah, dan memanfaatkan kereta, memiliki Audi, 2 BMW, hidup dalam kesalahan, kadang-kadang terlihat seperti, tidak adil.

**Key Quote:** "Jadi di sesi hari ini, kita akan berbicara tentang, apa itu destinasi? Jika kerja keras, menyebabkan, kehidupan yang baik, kekayaan, kekayaan, maka, banyak orang di dunia ini, harus kaya."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa outlines the questions to be addressed in the session: What is destiny? What is the role of hard work? What forces are at play when some live in luxury and others in poverty? What is in our hands, and what is not?

Jadi di sesi hari ini, kita akan berbicara tentang, apa itu destinasi? Jika kerja keras, menyebabkan, kehidupan yang baik, kekayaan, kekayaan, maka, banyak orang di dunia ini, harus kaya. Banyak orang yang bekerja dengan senang hati, bekerja dengan keras hati. Jadi, apa itu kekuatan, yang berfungsi di dunia ini, di mana, beberapa orang, hidup dalam kesalahan, semua kebaikan, dan beberapa orang, hidup dalam kesalahan, hidup dalam kesalahan. Apa adalah peran destinasi? Apa yang ada di dalam tangan kita, dan apa yang tidak ada di dalam tangan kita? Tidak ada di dalam lingkungan kita.

---
#### freewill, choice, destiny
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=619
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Titik pertama adalah, kita bukan robot. Tidaklah semuanya disesuaikan, kita tidak memiliki pilihan, kita tidak memiliki kebebasan, kita berkelakuan tanpa bantuan, berdasarkan beberapa program, yang mengandalkan kita. Tidak, itu bukan fakta."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa begins the first of three key points: We are not robots. He explains that humans are not pre-programmed and without choice, as robots are.

Jadi, itulah topik hari ini. Seperti yang kita lakukan, di sesi terakhir, saya akan menjelaskan, hanya tiga aspek yang penting hari ini. Seperti yang kita lakukan minggu lalu, saya akan meminta Anda, meminta Anda, meminta Anda, membuat perhatian tentang tiga aspek penting itu, dan kita akan recap, agar pada akhir sesi ini, kita kembali, dengan tiga aspek utama, tiga aspek penting. Dan, jika kita hanya ingatkan tiga aspek ini, kita akan mengetahui, asasnya apa yang kita bicarakan hari ini. Jadi, kita akan mulai dari titik pertama. Titik pertama adalah, kita bukan robot, atau robots. Saya tidak tahu bagaimana Anda mengatakannya. Kita semua telah mendengar tentang robot. Hari ini, kita hidup di dunia, di mana banyak hal terjadi secara otomatis. Jadi, ada waktu yang akan datang, ketika banyak hal terjadi, seperti penunggang di hotel, di restoran, kita akan memiliki robot, yang akan menyediakan perkhidmatan kepada pelanggan. Jadi, apakah kita seperti robot? Program, semuanya disesuaikan, kita tidak memiliki kebebasan, seseorang di atas itu, mengawal hidup kita, apa yang benar? Jadi, titik pertama adalah, kita bukan robot. Tidaklah semuanya disesuaikan, kita tidak memiliki pilihan, kita tidak memiliki kebebasan, kita berkelakuan tanpa bantuan, berdasarkan beberapa program, yang mengandalkan kita. Tidak, itu bukan fakta.

---
#### freewill, destiny, choice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=745
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Sama seperti itu, kita juga memiliki sesuatu yang dikirimkan oleh destinasi. Mereka adalah kekuatan yang berlaku pada kita. Dan, ada beberapa elemen kebebasan atau pilihan yang bisa kita lakukan."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that we must avoid two extremes: the belief that we are completely free and can achieve anything, and the belief that everything is predetermined. He uses the analogy of a cow tied to a pole with a rope to illustrate the balance between destiny and freewill.

Setelah mengatakan itu, kita harus mengelakkan dua ekstrim. Ekstrim pertama adalah, kita benar-benar terdiri. Kita bisa melakukan apa saja dan semuanya, apa saja yang kita inginkan lakukan, apa saja yang kita inginkan mencapai dalam hidup, kita bisa mencapainya. Jadi, itu ekstrim pertama. Semuanya ada di tangan kita. Kita bisa melakukan apa saja dan semuanya. Hanya itu, kita harus bekerja keras, bekerja dengan jujur, dan kita bisa mencapainya. Jadi, itu adalah satu pemahaman ekstrim. Pemahaman ekstrim lain adalah, semuanya disesuaikan. Jika ada di kisah saya, saya akan mendapatkannya. Jika tidak, saya tidak akan mendapatkannya. Itu ekstrim lainnya. Jika Anda ingat, saya telah berbagi dengan Anda, minggu lalu, mengenai contoh cowok yang ditutup ke pol dengan garis. Mari kita ambil contoh yang sama. Jika jarak garisnya 10 meter, cowok itu bebas untuk bergerak di radius 10 meter. Jika cowok itu bebas, cowok itu memiliki kebebasan. Tetapi, ketika cowok itu berusaha bergerak lebih jauh dari 10 meter, garis itu menutupnya kembali atau memastikan gerakannya. Sama seperti itu, kita juga memiliki sesuatu yang dikirimkan oleh destinasi. Mereka adalah kekuatan yang berlaku pada kita. Dan, ada beberapa elemen kebebasan atau pilihan yang bisa kita lakukan. Jadi, ini adalah perkahwinan antara dua kekuatan ini. Kita memiliki pilihan di dalam diri kita. Sama seperti cowok, bisa bergerak secara bebas di radius 10 meter. Tetapi, lebih jauh dari 10 meter, garis itu akan mematikan cowok. Jadi, ada beberapa kekuatan yang mematikan cowok. Kami memiliki pilihan yang dikirimkan oleh destinasi. Tetapi, kita juga memiliki kekuatan bebas. Banyak orang bertanya, mengapa Tuhan memberi kita kekuatan bebas? Mengapa Tuhan memberi kita keputusan? Jika Tuhan ingin menguasai kehidupan kita, Tuhan adalah semuanya baik. Semuanya akan baik. Kita tidak akan memiliki orang-orang yang melakukan hal yang salah, membuat keputusan yang salah. Itu akan sangat baik. Dunia akan menjadi tempat yang luar biasa untuk hidup. Tidak akan ada teroris. Tidak akan ada kriminalitas. Semuanya akan dikirimkan oleh kebenaran Tuhan. Mengapa Tuhan telah memberi kita kekuatan bebas?

#### freewill, love, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=949
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Jika tidak ada kekuatan bebas, jika tidak ada pilihan yang diberikan kepada kita, kita adalah seperti robot."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains why God gives us freewill, arguing that without it, we would be like robots, and any love or service offered would be meaningless. He shares a story about a man who tries to force a woman to love him to illustrate this point.

Jawabannya adalah, jika tidak ada kekuatan bebas, jika tidak ada pilihan yang diberikan kepada kita, kita adalah seperti robot. Robot bisa berfungsi dan melakukan banyak hal yang intelijen. Tetapi, mereka tidak memiliki pilihan. Ada program yang memandang mereka. Jika robot memiliki program untuk mengatakan, Saya mencintai Anda. Dan program itu menjalankan, dan robot itu mengatakan, Saya mencintai Anda. Saya yakin, Anda tidak akan mendapatkan kegembiraan yang orang akan mendapatkan ketika orang hidup yang memiliki kekuatan bebas menggunakan pilihannya, datang ke depan dan mengatakan, Saya mencintai Anda. Pada suatu saat, ada seorang laki-laki yang mencintai perempuan. Dia mencoba melakukan pilihan banyak-banyak kali. Tiba-tiba, dia melihat bahwa ada sentuh dari perempuan. Dia menjadi sangat beres dan suatu hari, dia pergi ke tempat perempuan dengan revolver di tangannya. Dan dengan revolver di belakang tulang perempuan, dia berkata, Anda lebih baik mencintai saya. Beritahu saya bahwa Anda mencintai saya. Jika tidak, saya akan menembak Anda. Saya akan membunuh Anda. Jadi perempuan, tanpa khawatir, tanpa panik, menangkap kedua-dua tangannya. Anda tahu, kita menyerah pada suatu saat. Jadi perempuan menangkap kedua-dua tangannya tanpa khawatir, dengan revolver di belakang tulang perempuan. Dia berkata, Saya mencintai Anda. Saya mencintai Anda. Saya mencintai Anda. Tetapi cinta itu yang ditutup tidak berarti. Tuhan bisa menutup kita. Dia berkuasa. Dia bisa menutup kita untuk melakukan hal yang benar. Dia bisa menutup kita untuk mengikuti-Nya. Dia bisa menutup kita untuk mencintai-Nya. Dia bisa menutup kita untuk melayani-Nya. Tetapi perkhidmatan itu, cinta itu tidak akan menyenangkan bagi Tuhan. Saya memberikan contoh, seperti robot, jika itu disesuaikan untuk mengatakan bahwa Saya mencintai Anda, cinta itu tidak berarti. Karena itu tidak keluar dari pilihan. Jadi, Tuhan telah memberikan kita kebenaran untuk setiap dari kita.

The second point is human life is very special I repeat human life is very very special although we have free will the living entity atma being amsha of the lord lord has given us free will choice but that free will varies from species to species person to person like I gave example of a person who is not a person I gave example of a person who is not a person I gave example of cow the cow can be tied to a pole with a rope the length could be 2 meters could be 5 meters could be 10 meters could be 20 meters there are 84 lakh species of life 84 lakh yonia in the tree form in the world of trees and plants in the world of trees and plants they cannot move they don't have a choice if it is raining winds are blowing they don't have a choice that they can move and take shelter of like human being when it rains we take shelter of a place where we can save ourselves from the rain they don't have a choice they cannot move Animals like carnivores, they don't have a choice. So this research was all about why it is like this as we know we have studied in our textbooks about the bell curve or which is also called the normal distribution curve which is also called when it comes to understanding about height weight if we were to map it if we were to tabulate it and make a graph we will invariably the curve will be like a bell curve where we have an average average height of individuals and there are some people who are above average and some people below average just like in a bell curve and on either side it tapers off very few people will be there where height is quite abnormal and there are very very few people where they are abnormal in the sense they are 6 feet 10 inches 7 feet 2 inches very few people by and large there is an average and a lot of people above average a lot of people below average and it follows a normal distribution curve even in terms of height weight in terms of number of hours a person is working if you were to tabulate it and make a graph it will be a bell curve on an average let's say a person works 8 hours in a day so there will be some people working 12 hours 14 hours and some people working 3 hours 4 hours so it will again form a bell curve so when this research was happening they found something very interesting when it came to the results of the work on an average everyone on an average everyone is working and as i said 8 hours 9 hours in a day but when it comes to result they found that if they were to map that results it does not follow the normal distribution curve or the bell curve there are people.
