#### wealth distribution, luck, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jtjIwlw2BBs?t=1862
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Randomness is an intrinsic principle in this world. In other words many things are happening randomly you cannot explain."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that the research found that wealth distribution does not follow the bell curve. He describes how the researchers introduced a parameter called "luck" into a computer simulation, which then resulted in a distribution that matched the real-world 80-20 principle, leading to the conclusion that randomness is an intrinsic principle in this world.

There are people working 8 hours and earning billions of dollars and there are people working 12 hours 14 hours and getting 10,000 rupees the normal understanding is if a person works hard if he uses his intelligence if he works sincerely he should earn a lot of money become successful and be rich but the researchers saw that there are people who are working hard working sincerely putting in 10 hours 12 hours a day but when it comes to how much they are earning it is not following the bell curve the normal distribution pattern so in a computer simulation they introduced a parameter called luck generally before exams or even before some events best of luck so they introduced a parameter called luck and when they introduced this parameter luck in that equation in that computer simulation model and introduced it randomly the outcome of that program or that simulation was following the normal distribution and was following the 80-20 principle 20% people owning 80% of wealth so the conclusion of that research was randomness is an intrinsic principle in this world in other words many things are happening randomly you cannot explain otherwise how do you explain a person working for 4 hours 5 hours earning a lot of money and a person putting in 12 hours a day is not able to earn even 10,000 rupees a month if you were to talk about sincere work hard work yes he is also working very sincerely if you were to talk about no no that person is not as intelligent as sharp he was blessed with that intelligence in fact the person who was earning a lot of money by working 3 hours 4 hours he did not consciously choose his intelligence maybe he would have attended some training shop workshops or maybe attended some college or some course which would have improved his analytical skills but basic ingredient he has been blessed with.

#### destiny, karma, detachment

**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the third point: Material sukha and dukha is destined. He quotes Srila Prabhupada, explaining that destiny is strong but can be changed through Krishna consciousness. He uses the cow and rope analogy again to illustrate this point.

Advantages of material happiness, we shall obtain them automatically. Thus there is no need to waste time and energy fighting against distress or working hard for happiness. Our only business in human form of life, I mentioned the second point, human life is very very rare, is very very special. We have got free will, we have an ability to make choices, we have got intelligence to understand higher things. In human form of life, what are we supposed to do? Our only business in human form of life should be to revive our relationship. With the supreme personality of Godhead and thus become qualified to return home back to Godhead. So we should endeavor and work hard for that. This is what our scriptures is saying. In due course of time, just like dukkha is served to us based on our karma, based on our karma, the sukha will also be served even though we do not work hard to achieve that sukha. So this is a very very important point. For many of us, it will be difficult to digest. As I mentioned earlier, it may look very controversial but if we contemplate and introspect deeply, we will say that yes, it makes sense. So then Shila Prabhupada says, destiny is extremely strong. The forces of kala, the forces of karma, we cannot easily undermine. Destiny is extremely strong, but one can change destiny if one comes to the platform of Krishna consciousness, by Lord's grace, by Lord's mercy. Just like the cow is tied to a pole with a rope, the cow on its own cannot change the length of the rope. The cow can move freely in the 10 meters radius, but the cow man who has tied the cow can change the length of the rope, can make that rope 6 meters, can also let the rope a little more loose and make it 12 meters. So that's why in human form of life, Athato brahma jagyasa, asato ma satgamaya. So that is what our scriptures are talking about. So this is the third point, which is, material sukha and dukha is destined.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that Maya is Krishna's energy, addressing the apparent contradiction of Maya being both a trap and a divine energy.

So today we will understand how maya traps a living entity and why it does so. Why we fall into the traps of maya. Maya is an energy of the Lord. Devi Esha Gunamayi Mamma Maya Dhurateya. Krishna says Mamma Maya. It is my energy, my divine energy. On one side we say that we are trapped in the trap of maya. On the other side we say it is the energy of Krishna. It looks a little bit of contradiction. On one side we see maya as a villain. On the other side we say it is an energy of the Lord.

#### maya, law, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3Eq6dt2L5mk?t=290
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we forget the Lord and desire to enjoy, keeping Lord aside, make our independent plans of enjoyment, completely forgetful of Lord, krishna bhulia jiva bhoga vancha kare."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a policeman to explain Maya's role, stating that Maya acts as an enforcer of divine law, troubling those who deviate from serving Krishna and seek independent enjoyment.

So how do we understand this? The right understanding is just like a policeman, a person who is maintaining law and order, is a government servant. But for a criminal, for a person who has transgressed the law, that person can be very notorious, can be very painful to deal with, can be very troublesome. So likewise it is said, jivira krishna bhulia jiva bhoga vancha kare. When we forget the Lord and desire to enjoy, keeping Lord aside, make our independent plans of enjoyment, completely forgetful of Lord, krishna bhulia jiva bhoga vancha kare. When we try to, nikatasta maya tare jhapatiya dare, maya immediately pounces on him and traps him.

#### maya, law, crime
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3Eq6dt2L5mk?t=365
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Exactly the same role maya is playing."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the analogy of law enforcement, comparing Maya to the police who punish those who violate divine laws, and suggesting that entering the material world implies a kind of 'crime'.

A very crude analogy. Just like you have traffic laws, you have traffic lights, moment you violate that law, you will have the police chasing you and penalizing you. So long as you are abiding by the laws, the police will not disturb you. In fact, the police is there to maintain law and order. But moment somebody transgresses that law, violates that law, the police department will come and nab that person and punish that person. Exactly the same role maya is playing. And in one sense, all of us have come in this material world. Now, some of us may ask this question. Do you mean to say we have committed some crime? Are we criminals?

#### body, enjoyment, rebellion

But we have been designed just like this limbs of the body. When they work for the interest of the body, they get their due court of enjoyment. Hand cannot enjoy rasgulla independently on its own by squeezing the rasgulla and trying to extract the juice out of it. The hand has to play its role. It is designed to put the food in the mouth, the stomach digest it, and the hand gets its due court of nourishment. We don't call it unfair. This is how it is designed. Each limb is playing a particular role. The legs walk, the hands write, the eyes see, and body as a whole is designed. Each limb working in synergistically. And that's how the whole body functions nicely and does wonderful things. So when a particular limb rebels and says, no, no, I will independently enjoy. That is the case.

#### maya, devotees, role
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3Eq6dt2L5mk?t=617
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The devotees of the Lord and maya are actually playing the same role, but little differently."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that devotees and Maya play similar roles, with Maya punishing those who violate God's laws until they surrender to Krishna, while devotees preach and encourage people to follow Krishna's instructions.

In fact, it is said that the devotees of the Lord and maya are actually playing the same role, but little differently. The mode of operation is little different. Police department is meant to nab the person who has violated the law and penalize him. Beat that person up. Until he comes to his senses and becomes a law-abiding citizen. So what maya does is, moment you violate some law, God's law, nature's law, maya will punish that person, torment that person, make that person suffer, beat that person, till he comes to the senses and eventually comes to the point of surrender to Krishna and becomes a law-abiding citizen. In this case, a devotee of the Lord. Devotee also does the same thing, but he preaches and tells the person, better be a law-abiding citizen. Better follow the laws of the land. Better follow Krishna's instructions. Be a devotee of Krishna and your life will be sublime. In that sense, there is a common role being played by maya and the devotees of the Lord.

It is said that in the animal kingdom, for each of the senses there is an example given of a particular animal which eventually even loses its life just because it is weak as far as that particular sense is concerned. It gets easily tempted because of that particular sense. For example, the taste, the tongue, the example is given of a fish. Even though it is not hungry, but whenever a fisherman goes and throws a hook with a nice bait dangling, the fish helplessly goes for that bait and eventually gets caught, trapped and gets killed.

We have seen wild elephants, especially the untamed ones. If you have to capture that elephant, wild elephant and tame it, they have realized it's very difficult because elephants are huge. For a man who is 70 kg, 80 kg, it's not easy to capture that wild elephant. But they have learned that when the she-elephant who is trained passes by that male elephant and touches the body at a particular spot, the male elephant gets aroused and madly starts chasing the elephant. She-elephant. And the she-elephant is trained, it passes in a particular way and passes across by the side of a pit which is deep pit covered with green grass. And as soon as this male elephant madly is chasing that she-elephant, he falls on that pit and eventually gets trapped and then beaten till it becomes tamed. Till it gets controlled.

The sense of smell, the nose, the deer runs looking out for that smell, the source of that smell and runs right from the mountains to the mountains. mountains to deep forests, deep deserts, looking where is that smell coming from, what is the source of that smell, not knowing that it's within its body itself. Eventually it burns that moth and next day morning if you see it, all the moths would be lying dead. The same light which was attracting that moth. The moth helplessly becomes the cause of its death. And this is how the Maya acts, instead of acting as per the original design of being servant of the Lord, Krishna Das, we have become slaves of our senses, slaves of our eyes, ears, tongue. We have become slaves of our senses, slaves of our eyes, ears, tongue. Each sense trying to goad us, to pull us, see this, eat this, drink this, you'll be happy, touch this, you'll be happy, eat this, you'll be happy. And if we analyze all the lifestyle diseases, all the challenges we face, all the problems we have encountered, all the suffering, it's all because of uncontrolled mind, uncontrolled senses.

Another thing about the sensual pleasure is, although there is a sensation of pleasure, yehi samsvarshya bhoga, dukhyo nahi evate, Krishna says. It is not only like a drop of water. It will not satisfy. But this very happiness which we get by bringing the senses in contact with the sense objects, this very happiness becomes the cause of our distress, our misery, our entanglement. Therefore, all the scriptures of the world talk about regulating the senses, controlling the mind, taming the mind. Mind if it is controlled is a best friend. The same mind if it is uncontrolled is a worst enemy.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### DIFFERENTIATE NEED AND GREED | HG Amitasana Das | Gita Life Live | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, prayer, Vaishnava tradition
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The topic for the day is, Differentiate Need and Greed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of differentiating between need and greed and leads the audience in a series of Vaishnava prayers and chants.

Thank you. Hare Krishna. A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is, Differentiate Need and Greed. We all have some desires. We all have some bona fide needs. What is that fine line, which if it crosses, it gets translated into greed? So today we are going to discuss more on this. There are a lot of insights we get from Bhagavad Gita, where Krishna has directly spoken about greed. So before we plunge into this discussion, let us all pray together. Please fold your hands. Join me in offering the prayers. We will chant a few prayers together, which will be displayed on the screen. And after that, we will get into this discussion. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swaminitinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishna Ya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratvishetana Namaha. Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hitaya Cha Jagadhitaya Krishnaya Go Vindaya Namo Namaha. He Krishna Karuna Sindhu Dina Bandhu Jagatpate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostu Te. Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vansha Kalpatroobhyascha Kripa Sindhubhyaevacha Atitanam Pavnebhyo Vaishnave Bhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

#### greed, lust, anger, spiritual progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=229
**Verse References:** BG 16.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna in the Gita says there are three gates leading to hell: lust, anger and greed."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the three main aspects of the lecture: the root cause of greed, its consequences, and the cure, referencing Krishna's teachings on the three gates leading to hell: lust, anger, and greed.

So today we are going to talk about three aspects. First we are going to talk about what is the root cause of greed. Krishna in the Gita says there are three gates leading to hell: lust, anger and greed. So what is the root cause? Why are we greedy? Why greed overpowers a person? Although many of us know it is not good to be greedy. So that is the first thing we are going to cover. Second aspect is, what are the consequences? Why greed is dangerous? Why Krishna says there are three gates leading to hell and one of them is greed? What are the consequences of a person who gets trapped by greed? And third, which is the most important one, what is the cure? What can we do? Yes, we understand it is not okay to be greedy. But presently we have fallen trap to these base enemies: lust, anger, greed, pride, envy. What can we do to come out of it? What can we do so that we, so to say, neutralize these enemies?

---
#### greed, scarcity, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=322
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Earth provides enough to satisfy a man's need, but not every man's greed."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to explain the root cause of greed, stating that the world's shortages are not due to a lack of resources or overpopulation, but rather due to man's greed.

First let us understand, what is the root cause of greed? We see lot of shortages in the world around us. Lot of people dying of malnutrition. People not having enough roti, kapda and makaan. There is, there seems to be a deficit. Sometimes we wonder why Lord has made this creation where there is a deficit. Why so many people are dying of hunger? So they are saying earth provides enough to satisfy a man's need, but not every man's greed. So in other words, if we see shortages in this world, it is not because earth has not provided enough. It is not because of overpopulation. It is because of man's greed.

#### gunas, greed, passion, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=387
**Verse References:** BG 14.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From the mode of passion, if we are too much Rajasic, if we are influenced by Rajaguna, then greed develops."
**Summary:** The speaker shares quotes from Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam, explaining that the root cause of greed is the influence of Rajaguna (mode of passion), while ignorance leads to foolishness and illusion.

So I will just share few quotes from Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, which gives us a clue what is the root cause of greed. From the mode of goodness, real knowledge develops. Krishna in the Gita talks about, three gunas: Satwaguna, Rajaguna and Tamoguna. And depending on the modes which influence us, we develop some characteristics. From the mode of goodness, if mode of goodness is predominantly influencing us, we are in Satwaguna, then real knowledge develops. From the mode of passion, if we are too much Rajasic, if we are influenced by Rajaguna, then greed develops. So we are talking about the root cause. The root cause is Rajaguna. Kaam, Esha, Krodh, Esha. Rajaguna, Samudbhavaha. This mode of passion is the root cause, is the breeding ground for greed. And from the mode of ignorance develops, foolishness, madness and illusion. This is in the 14th chapter, 17th verse.

---
#### gunas, spiritual elevation, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna extensively talks about the three gunas."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Krishna's teachings about the three gunas and how one can gradually elevate from Tamoguna to Rajoguna to Satwaguna, and even transcend Satwaguna to Shuddhasattva.

Krishna extensively talks about the three gunas. Food in the mode of goodness, passion and ignorance. Sacrifices, work in the mode of goodness, passion and ignorance. And many instructions Lord has given how we can gradually elevate from Tamoguna to Rajaguna to Satwaguna and even transcend Satwaguna to Shuddhasattva. So the root cause of greed is the influence of Rajaguna. Another reason why a man becomes overpowered by greed. A man becomes too greedy for wealth and power when he has no higher objective in life. And when he thinks that his earthly life of a few years is all in all.

---
#### eternal existence, ignorance, materialism, greed
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=530
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When a person forgets that this earthly life is a small phase in his eternal existence and considers this life as all in all and there is no higher objective in life, the objective of life is eat, drink and be merry, then a person invariably will fall under the clutches of greed."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of remembering our eternal nature as spirit souls and warns that forgetting this, and believing that earthly life is all there is, leads to greed.

This is very important. We are spirit soul, we are eternal. This life is a small phase in our eternal existence. Just like in a drama. A person acts like a king but he is very much aware that he is acting like a king. He is not the king. He is playing the role of a king. And very soon when the drama gets over, he has to come back to his original identity. So likewise, when a person forgets that this earthly life is a small phase in his eternal existence and considers this life as all in all and there is no higher objective in life. The objective of life is eat, drink and be merry. Then a person invariably will fall under the clutches of greed. Ignorance is the cause of all these anomalies in the human society. And to remove this ignorance, especially in this age of degradation, the powerful sun is there to distribute light in the shape of Srimad Bhagavatam. So because of ignorance, we forget who we really are. We think that this earthly life is all in all. I am this body and there is no higher objective in life. If that is our consciousness, then invariably we will fall under the clutches of greed. So this is what Srimad Bhagavatam is saying. Bhagavad Gita is saying the root cause is Rajoguna. Mode of ignorance. Mode of passion. And the Bhagavatam is saying that when we are ignorant of ourselves, when we think that there is no higher objective in life, the philosophy of life is eat, drink and be merry. And definitely such understanding of life will brew greed in the mind-body system.

---
#### mind control, lust, anger, greed, yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=664
**Verse References:** BG 2.62-63
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If a yogi gives his mind a chance, in other words, does not checks his mind, mind will give facility to enemies like lust, anger and greed, and they will doubtlessly kill the yogi."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of an unchaste woman to illustrate how an uncontrolled mind can lead to lust, anger, and greed, ultimately destroying the yogi.

The third clue which we get is that as an unchaste woman is very easily carried away by paramours, and it sometimes happens that her husband is violently killed by her paramours, if a yogi gives his mind a chance, Krishna in the Gita says mind if it is controlled is a best friend, the same mind if it is uncontrolled is a worst enemy. So here Bhagavad Gita is saying, if a yogi gives his mind a chance, in other words, does not checks his mind. We are different from the mind. Mind is part of sukshma sharira which covers the soul. So when a yogi gives his mind a chance, and does not restrain it, does not tame it, does not control it, does not check it, mind will give facility to enemies like lust, anger and greed, and they will doubtlessly kill the yogi.

#### greed, contentment, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=807
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Earth provides enough to satisfy a man's need, but not for every man's greed."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about a man who lost his umbrella and, even after receiving a new one, remained unhappy because he wished he had two umbrellas, illustrating the insatiable nature of greed.

Once there was a person who was regularly attending satsang, and it was rainy days and he would invariably go to the satsang with his umbrella, and one day unfortunately somebody stole his umbrella while he was attending the satsang. So he was very disturbed and it was quite visible. Next few days although he would come for the satsang, but he was quite disturbed, it was quite visible on his face. He was not at ease. Sometimes people even ask as devotees or somebody who is practicing spirituality, why does he at times become angry or become greedy? The idea is that a person may be taking the medicine, but that does not mean that a person who has started taking the medicine should get cured that very day. It takes time. It takes time for a person to get diseased, and even if a person is taking the right medicine, it takes time for the disease to subside. Therefore in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna also says, One who is practicing the process of Krishna Consciousness, even if at times he engages in some abominable acts, still he has to be considered saintly because he is rightly situated. He is taking the medicine. He is following the process. It may take some time. So here this person also was attending satsang. You may say if he was attending satsang, why is he getting perturbed? Yes, he was disturbed. Many times we cannot help it. Theoretically we may know it is wrong, but there is conditioning, there is habitual response. So this person was quite perturbed, was quite disturbed. He could not focus and the speaker could really make it out because he was in the front row and he was always little jittery and not comfortable. So one day he asked him after the session, Why are you so disturbed, look so agitated? So he clearly told Swamiji, What to tell? Unfortunately I lost my umbrella and I am so disturbed. I wonder why a person has stolen in such an auspicious gathering and somehow I am missing my umbrella. More so it is rainy days. I cannot afford to buy another one. So the Swamiji requested another satsangi, the person there in the satsang, Why don't you buy an umbrella for him? And next day the person got him a new umbrella. So for a moment the person was quite happy, he has got an umbrella much better than what he had earlier, much bigger, much better, much better quality. And that day the person attended satsang. But next few days the speaker noticed that still the person is not happy, still the person is morose. Even though he has been gifted a new umbrella, what could be the cause of his moroseness? Again after the session one day this person, the speaker inquired from him, Why are you morose? I have somehow got a new umbrella for you, now you should be happy. So he said, Yes Swamiji, what to do? Always this thought bothers me. Had I not lost that umbrella, I would today be having two umbrellas. I somehow missed that umbrella, now I have only one. So this is a symptom where a person in spite of having enough, is not contented, is always greedy, is wanting more, is never satisfied. Therefore it is said, Earth provides enough to satisfy a man's need, but not for every man's greed.

#### servitude, false masters, senses, mind
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1080
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Instead of serving the Lord, have become slaves, have become slaves of false masters, and the false master are our senses, our mind."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that we are originally eternal servants of the Lord, but have become slaves to our senses and mind, which have become our false masters.

So we were talking about the root cause. We are originally eternal servant of the Lord. Jeevera Swaroopahoe Nittera Krishna Das. But we have come in this world, forgetting our real position, having rebelled against the Lord, Ichcha Dvesha Samutthena Dvandamohena Bharata. And we have now, instead of serving the Lord, have become slaves, have become slaves of false masters, and the false master are our senses, our mind. Kama, Krodha, Moha, Lobha, these all have become our masters. What is the meaning of a master? And what is the meaning of a slave? Master is the one who dictates, who gives orders. And the slave just follows, at times blindly follows, helplessly follows. So if we analyze, we as Atma, our glorious position is that we are servants of the Lord, Nittera Krishna Das. But unfortunately, in today's world, we have become the servants of false masters in the form of our senses and the mind. That's why it is said, if the mind is given a chance, mind will give rise to lust, anger, greed, and it will kill a yogi.

---
#### senses, animal kingdom, traps
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1192
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the animal kingdom, there are animals which get trapped because one of their senses is quite loose, is quite unchecked."
**Summary:** The speaker uses examples from the animal kingdom to illustrate how unchecked senses can lead to being trapped, such as a fish being lured by bait or a deer being captivated by music.

So the mind and the senses have become our masters. It is said that in the animal kingdom, there are animals which get trapped because one of their senses is quite loose, is quite unchecked. And a hunter or a person who is trying to catch that animal has a clue, has an understanding, and that becomes the trap for that animal. For that matter, a fish, when a fisherman goes, he puts a hook with a bait. Although the fish is not hungry, just because the bait is very, very tempting, it will go for it, not knowing that behind that bait is a hook, and the fish gets trapped. Likewise, there is deer. One of the senses of the deer, which is the power of hearing, becomes the cause of its death. The hunter knows otherwise the deers can run very fast, and it's very difficult to chase and and, you know, hit them, or kill them. So the hunter would arrange for playing very good music, soothing music, and the deer becomes so enchanted with that music, with that beautiful melodious sound, it just stays put in one place, rapt attention, just hearing that melodious sound, and the hunter shoots the deer down. So likewise, for each of the senses, there is an example from Animal Kingdom where the animal gets trapped because that sense is weak. That's a weak point for that animal.

#### senses, temptation, greed, hell
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1306
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the case of man, it is not one sense, it's not two sense, it's not three sense, it's all the five senses by which we are getting tempted, we're getting trapped."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts humans with animals, stating that humans are tempted and trapped by all five senses, becoming slaves to their minds and senses, leading to negative emotions. He then transitions to discussing the consequences of greed, referencing the three gates leading to hell.

But it is said that man is said to be a rational animal, but unfortunately, in the case of man, it is not one sense, it's not two sense, it's not three sense, it's all the five senses by which we are getting tempted, we're getting trapped. Our eyes, are constantly looking for seeing beautiful forms. Ears, always hankering for melodious sounds. Tongue, always wanting to taste palatable foods. So likewise, in present times, we have become slaves of our minds, slaves of our senses, and that has given rise to lust, anger, greed, pride, envy, and so forth. So these are the root cause why we are in this present situation. The next point, the next important aspect which we want to cover is what are the consequences? What happens if we get trapped by the enemy called greed? Here Krishna is saying the three gates leading to hell. The life, our present life can become hellish and the life after death, can also be hellish.

---
#### greed, consequences, revenge, self-destruction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1395
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When we get overpowered by lust, greed, envy, pride, we can act in ways which can be even fatal. Fatal for the body, fatal for the soul."
**Summary:** The speaker shares the story of the two-headed bird Bharunda, where one head's greed led to the other head's envy and ultimately to their mutual destruction, illustrating the fatal consequences of greed.

So, what are the consequences? Once there was a bird, a two headed bird called Bharunda. The name of the bird was Bharunda. It had two heads, but it was one bird with two heads, one belly. And one day it so happened that this bird while it was in the forest found some very nice, pleasant, tasty fruit, a big fruit. And one of the heads started eating it. And it said, Oh, such a wonderful, so delicious it is. I wonder whether it is from the divine Parijatha tree or from some divine sandalwood tree. I wonder what fruit is this? But it is so sweet, so nice, so wonderful, so tasty. It started glorifying and simultaneously eating. The other head, which was on the other side, said, kindly share this also with me. I also would like to taste. I also want to eat that same fruit. You are glorifying so much. It is so delicious, so nice, so tasty. So this head, the one who was tasting said, why you have to eat? Anyway we have a common stomach. Why you have to eat? I am eating it. That's enough. Anyway we have common stomach and we will share the energy. And it deprived an opportunity to the other head to eat or relish that fruit. From that day, the other head developed great enmity or hatred towards the other head. So much so day and night it was also always thinking how to take revenge. We see in present times also, you know, family members, two brothers in the same family can end up being enemies, court cases. So the other head became very envious, developed lot of hatred and looked for an opportunity when it can take revenge. So one day, while they were in a forest, the other head found a poisonous fruit, very poisonous fruit and deliberately started eating it. The other said, wait, wait, don't eat, don't eat, it's poison. Don't you know it's poison? It's poisonous. Don't eat, we'll die. But the other head wanted to teach that head a lesson, kept on eating, ate that entire poisonous fruit and the bird, both the heads including, died. The other head did not mind dying so that it can take revenge from the other head.

#### greed, consequences, loss of good sense, degradation of the soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1592
**Verse References:** BG 16.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "He who is not contended with what he has will not be contended with what he would like to have."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that being overpowered by negative emotions can lead to fatal actions, both physically and spiritually, and that a lack of contentment leads to a never-ending rat race.

So when we get overpowered by lust, greed, envy, pride, we can act in ways which can be even fatal. Fatal for the body, fatal for the soul. So these are the consequences. We lose our good sense. It is said that he who is not contended with what he has will not be contended with what he would like to have. We all have goals, we all have aspirations, we all are looking for happiness, we all are looking for contentment, satisfaction, joy. If we are not contended and happy and joyful with what we have, there is no guarantee that even after we achieve our goals, our aspirations, we will be happy, we will be contented. Life always becomes so to say, a rat race, waiting for something to happen, hoping against hope that I will be fully happy, I will be fully satisfied. The three gates leading to hell, lust, anger and greed. Every sane man should give up these for they lead to degradation of the soul.

---
#### greed, consequences, degradation of the soul, loss of purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "It degrades the soul. It makes us forget our real identity."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to discuss the consequences of greed, stating that it degrades the soul, makes us forget our real identity, and causes us to lose sight of the real purpose of life.

So this is another consequence. It degrades the soul. It makes us forget our real identity. We lose our good sense. The real knowledge, the real purpose of life is forgotten if the soul degrades overpowered by lust, anger and greed. My dear Lord, all living entities within this material world are mad after planning for things and they are always busy with a desire to do this or that. So another consequence is we almost become mad. Wanting to do this, do that, achieve this, achieve that. Always hankering, waiting for something to be happen so that we become happy. This is due to uncontrolled greed. The greed for material enjoyment is always existing in the living entity. But your Lordship is always alert and due course of time you strike him like a snake, seizes a mouse and very easily swallows him. So overpowered by greed we lose our good sense, act in so many abominable ways and in due course of time death comes and overpowers such a person. That valuable human form of life which is very very meaningful, gets lost in false pursuits driven by the enemy called greed.

---
#### greed, consequences, loss of good sense, abominable acts
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=1783
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "People out of greed lose the good sense think that this earthly year years of as all in all and end up doing such abominable acts including taking someone's life."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a shocking real-life story about nurses in an ICU who were betting on patients' survival and even turning off ventilators to win their bets, illustrating the extreme consequences of greed.

Once there was a very big hospital in the US and suddenly they were alarmed. All of a sudden the number of deaths happening in the ICU increased exponentially. And since they were keeping tab of the number of patients admitted in the ICU, number of patients recovered, number of patients who died. So they were quite alarmed with that sudden exponential increase in the number of deaths. And they started doing root cause analysis. What is the cause? Is it that the patients are going through some ailments which we are not able to cure? Or is there some lacuna in our facilities? Is there lacuna in our care? Is there some shortcoming in the training of the nurses? Because, you know, new nurses join and is there something which is going wrong or something which we need to improve because of which, because of willful or willful negligence these deaths are happening. So they started doing root cause analysis and eventually they found out and what they found out was very, very shocking. They found that the nurses in that ICU they were addicted and they were gambling and on each patient in the ICU they used to have betting done. Just like you have betting in sports. You bet which team will win by which, how many and there is always a ratio and basis that there is a risk involved and the gains proportionate gains depending on the risk involved. So you bet on a particular team. Bet on a particular horse. So the nurses started betting and they would bet on a particular patient as to how many days this patient will survive or after how many days the patient will leave the ICU or will die. And it so happened that some of the nurses in order to win the bets even if the patient is recovering will go and turn off the ventilator so that the patient dies. This is a real life story. Actually it has happened. People out of greed lose the good sense think that this earthly year years of as all in all and end up doing such abominable acts including taking someone's life.

---

#### greed, root cause, consequences, review
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/e7m8EolgG4k?t=2712
**Verse References:** BG 14.20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the Bhagavad Gita, 14th chapter it is mentioned, in the mode of passion, people become greedy."
**Summary:** The speaker provides a quick recap of the topics covered so far, including the root causes and consequences of greed, referencing the influence of Rajoguna and the story of the nurses in the ICU.

A quick recap of what we have so far covered. First we covered about the root cause of greed. And in that there were three important sub points. First was when a person is overpowered by... Mozhi and greed. Second... when a person, out of ignorance... forgets the real meaning , he feels that there is no higher objective to life. Just animals are also leading their life. It is all about eating, sleeping, mating and defending. So when there is no higher purpose of life and a person thinks that these earthly years are all in all, then a person will fall trap to greed. And the third sub-point was if the mind is untamed, unchecked, uncontrolled, our senses are unbridled, then also we will fall under the trap of this enemy called greed. So these are the root causes. And then we talked about consequences of greed. What happens if the greed is unchecked, untamed? So we said that how it can kill a yogi. We took an example that how people at times can act very abominably. Gave example of the Barunda bird, two-headed bird and how the nurses in the ICU were even killing patients just because of greed. Just because they were greedy to win the bets. In the Bhagavad Gita, 14th chapter it is mentioned, in the mode of passion, people become greedy.

#### greed, sense gratification, happiness, Krishna Consciousness

**Summary:** The speaker explains that while there is a limit to man's need, there is no limit to his greed, and that even with wealth and sense gratification, happiness and peace of mind are unattainable without elevating oneself to the mode of goodness through Krishna Consciousness.

And their hankering for sense enjoyment has no limit. As we all discussed, there is no limit to man's greed. There is a limit to man's need. But even if one has enough money and adequate arrangement for sense gratification, there is neither happiness nor peace of mind. That is not possible because one is situated in the mode of passion. If one wants happiness at all, his money will not help him. He has to elevate himself to the mode of goodness by practicing Krishna Consciousness. Money has a place, money is required. But when becoming rich, having more

#### Guru, Mission, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O8KJyOI4umg?t=64
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavad Gita is not a sectarian message only for Indians, only for Hindus. It's a universal message."
**Summary:** This segment details Srila Prabhupada's mission to the West, emphasizing the universality of the Bhagavad Gita's message and his initial struggles in the US.

His spiritual master Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur had ordered him that since you are, you can speak English, you have got educated in an English medium, these things, preach this message, spread this message in the Western world. Bhagavad Gita is not a sectarian message only for Indians, only for Hindus. It's a universal message. So he says, preach this message in the Western world. It's a very materialistic society. So to fulfill the order of his Guru, Prabhupada went to the West. And many of us know he was 70 years old. He had 40 rupees in his pocket. He had a lot of money in his pocket. He had a lot of money. With great difficulty, he got a passage in a cargo ship. He went to US and one year he was struggling, no proper place to stay. No idea how long he is going to continue in US.

**Summary:** This segment describes the establishment of ISKCON, the beginning of Gita classes, and the importance of book distribution as a means to spread transcendental knowledge, referring to books as 'Bhrad Mridanga'.

And 1966, he formally registered International Society for Krishna Consciousness. And slowly people started coming. He started conducting Gita classes. And more and more people started, you know, coming in touch with Krishna Consciousness and started becoming devotees. So Prabhupada, from the very beginning, wanted to distribute this message to more and more people. And his Guru Maharaj also had instructed that if you get an opportunity, print and distribute books. They called the books as Bhrad Mridanga. Mridanga means, we know Mridanga, you know, we play the sound during Kirtan. But the sound of that Mridanga, if you play it, just goes in this hall. But Bhrad Mridanga means the sound, the transcendental vibration which is there in the book can spread all over the world through the medium of books.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O8KJyOI4umg?t=205

**Summary:** This segment highlights Srila Prabhupada's emphasis on book distribution, even before establishing ISKCON, and his efforts in printing and distributing 'Back to Godhead' magazine.

So, his Guru Maharaj also ordered, if you get money, print and distribute books. So that was his main, what you call, to distribute this, disseminate this message, Prabhupada emphasised a lot on writing, publishing, printing and distributing books. Before going to the West, Prabhupada, when he was a grahasta, he single handedly, with great difficulty, he would go all the way to Delhi. He was staying in Vrindavan. So that was his main, what you call, distribute this message. So, this is an example where you could say, you know, in Vrindavan and he would print a magazine called Back to Godhead. He wouldn't have even means to have proper clothes. In that sense, he was financially quite challenged. But in spite of that, he would go to the printer, sit with him, get the typeset done, single-handedly print those Back to Godhead magazines and distribute them. So when Prabhupada established ISKCON and the movement started growing, more and more devotees joined, Prabhupada encouraged all of them to also distribute books.

If we have to reach this message to more and more people, one thing is, yes, we can all gather together and we can speak, we can talk, we can give Bhagavad Gita discourse. But another medium of disseminating this knowledge is through printing. That's how Prabhupada encouraged many devotees to go and distribute the books. So that's how in the initial days, 70s, many devotees were there in the airports, many devotees were there in different malls and all that. And during the December month, which happens to be a Christmas month in the Western countries, especially it's unlike India, where you can see people walking around in roads and you know, people are unlike in the West. Most of the people are traveling. In the cars only, you'll find them in malls and supermarkets. And more so on Christmas time, people are having vacations. So everyone would be in a festive mood and everyone would be in that sense accessible. If you go in a mall, you invariably find people, you can access them, you can put a book table, you can talk to them. So book distribution was, you know, very... If you analyze... Good book distribution. Number of books got distributed in the month of December when people were relatively free and accessible.

#### Book Marathon, Competition, Sankirtan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O8KJyOI4umg?t=360
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada, just to ensure that, you know, more number of books gets distributed, so he said, okay, we'll have a competition in the month of December and that he called us Books Marathon."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the 'Books Marathon,' a competition initiated by Prabhupada to increase book distribution during December, involving temples and countries competing, with results published in the World Sankirtan Newsletter.

So Prabhupada started something called as Books Marathon. Can anyone help me? What is Books Marathon? Okay. We have heard of marathon. You run 10 kilometers, five kilometers per hour. What is Books Marathon? What is Books Marathon? Yes. Okay. Okay. Okay. So you have some kind of a competition. You run and then you adjust somebody who is a winner, second and there's some kind of a competition who completed 10 miles in this many hours and some kind of a race, some kind of a competition. So Prabhupada, just to ensure that, you know, more number of books gets distributed. So he said, okay, we'll have a competition in the month of December and that he called us Books Marathon. So what the devotees would do is temple to temple, they would compete continent to continent. Asia versus Pacific and then countries to countries, temple to temple and there would be a world Sankirtan newsletter. Even today it's printed, world Sankirtan newsletter, which compiles the number of books distributed for each temple and they would compute the scores based on temples, small temples, medium temples, big temples, country wise, state wise and all kinds of various results were published. And in marathon, the devotees would go extra mile. There were times when devotees would cut down their sleeping, cut down their other activities, including the temple president would go out and this month they would be focusing their energies, their time on distributing more books with the idea of pleasing Prabhupada.

#### Spiritual Master, Service, Pleasing Prabhupada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O8KJyOI4umg?t=459
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One way of repaying our debt to the spiritual master who has given us the spiritual knowledge, we can't pay him by money because money is too small a thing to pay for what we have got in terms of money."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that the primary motivation for devotees during the Books Marathon was to please Srila Prabhupada, viewing it as a way to serve the spiritual master and repay the debt of spiritual knowledge.

One way of repaying our debt to the spiritual master who has given us the spiritual knowledge, we can't pay him by money because money is too small a thing to pay for what we have got in terms of money. We can't pay the debt, but we can pay the debt to some extent by serving the spiritual master, by fulfilling his desires, we can as an instrument try to fulfill those desires. So devotees, just to please Prabhupada, competition in that sense was secondary, number one, number two, that was secondary. The main thing is, they all knew that it is very pleasing to Prabhupada. Prabhupada would be very pleased if somebody goes and tells Prabhupada we distributed 100 Gitas. He would be very pleased. And Prabhupada, there are many examples where devotees went and Prabhupada was very happy and you know he patted them on the back and encouraged them to distribute more books. So it was very evident, Prabhupada was very pleased. So devotees, to please Prabhupada would, you know go extra mile and distribute the books. And this festival started being called all over the world as Prabhupada's Books Marathon.

#### Charity, Transcendental Knowledge, Atma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O8KJyOI4umg?t=619
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** This segment contrasts temporary material help with the lasting impact of distributing transcendental knowledge, which helps the Atma escape the cycle of birth and death.

They say if you feed a man, you feed him for a day. You teach him how to fish, you feed him for a lifetime. All of us want, charity means what? You are trying to help someone. No matter how much we do charity, we can only help a person temporarily. Suppose he is having some disease, you can pay for his hospital bill. You have helped him temporarily. He will go to the hospital, he will get cured, he will come out. But again, there will be disease, there will be old age, there will be death. So what we have done is, no matter whatever charity we do, we are doing a symptomatic help. But real help is when we can help that Atma to get out of this, this repeated cycle of birth and death. And for that, Divya Gyan, transcendental knowledge, is very important.

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment contrasts the common perception of the West as prosperous with Srila Prabhupada's understanding of the suffering present there, despite material wealth.

His objective is to give this knowledge to as many souls as possible. Prabhupada, when he went to the West, he said, Oh Lord, why are you taking me to a miserable place? When we go to the West, and first time I went, there is always a sense of curiosity. Jaake dekhenge, bhot suna hai. You are going to a new place. You have heard that it is very prosperous. You have heard that it is economically developed. So there is an element of awe, reverence. You know, some kind of appreciation. Prabhupada said, why are you taking me to this place? So he didn't go West enamoured by the wealth there or the prosperity there. He went there understanding that, you know, in spite of all the money they have, prosperity is actually suffering.

**Summary:** This segment explains the Krishna consciousness movement as centering on Krishna, where one offers the results of their work to Krishna without attachment, similar to how the hand feeds the stomach.

Eat, but it cannot enjoy directly. That is not possible. It must give to the stomach. And when it is given to the stomach, the energy produced by eating that foodstuff is distributed not only in these fingers, but other fingers and other parts of the body. This is Krsna consciousness movement. We are making center, Krsna. Krsna says, yad karo si, yad yuho si, yad asne si, tapasya siddhati, tat kuruṣya mad-bhaṁ. You work. Whatever you like, you work. But ultimately you give me the result of the work. That is Krsna consciousness. You do not want to stop anything. People may not misunderstand that we want to stop business, we want to stop industries or our general modes of life. You will

#### free will, surrender, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=290
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the first principle for attracting divine intervention: surrender, explaining that Krishna has given us free will and reciprocates according to our degree of surrender.

So today we are going to discuss three aspects of how we can go about attracting and experiencing the intervention of the Lord. The first factor, the first principle is as you surrender, kindly make a note of it, as you surrender. Krishna has given us a free will. Lot of people ask if God is all powerful, then why bad things are happening. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. He could have forced us to do something. the world could have been a better place to live. So why do we see so much anomalies? On one side we say that God exists and other side we see lot of anomalies and aberrations in what we observe, things happening around us. So, Krishna has given us a free will, a choice and He does not interfere. In certain circumstances, in case of devotees, He does interfere but in general, He does not interfere with our free will. Krishna in the Gita says, As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate. The choice is ours. If we surrender 0%, Krishna's reciprocation is 0%. He is indifferent. Although He is present in our heart as Supersoul, He is witness to our activities and His sanctions, the reactions. Yathamam prapadvayam. As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate.

#### surrender, devotion, Krishna's reciprocation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=454
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The devotees try to serve the Lord and the Lord in turn tries to serve the devotees."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a mother and child, the speaker illustrates how pure devotees are completely dependent on the Lord, leading to the Lord's reciprocation and service to His devotees, as exemplified by Arjuna's choice of Krishna over His army.

To give you a crude example, a toddler, a child who is barely 6 months, 8 months old, the lap of mother is completely dependent on the mother. If by chance mother forgets, although the mother does not forget, by chance the mother forgets to feed the child, the child will simply cry. It has no means to find the food by itself and go and take it. And because the mother knows about this, she is always conscious of the child. She is always concerned, she is always anxious, now my baby must be feeling hungry, I have to feed her, I have to feed him. So likewise, pure devotees are dependent on the mother, completely dependent on the Lord, completely surrendered to the Lord. And as a result, the Lord also reciprocates. The devotees try to serve the Lord and the Lord in turn tries to serve the devotees. That is our constitutional position. Sometimes we become little jealous that why we should serve the Lord. We get a picture as if Lord is some kind of a cruel master, who is demanding, just like in case of slaves. He is demanding some kind of service from us. Better you serve me, otherwise you are doomed. That is not the scenario. In the spiritual world, the devotees are trying their best to serve the Lord, and the Lord in turn is making plans how to serve the devotees, which is exemplified in the case of Arjuna. Arjuna was asked, what do you want? You want me or you want my army? The condition being that I will not lift the weapons, I will not fight. Because Arjuna knew the position of the Lord, it was a no-brainer for Arjuna, he chose, I want you, my Lord. And because Arjuna chose Krishna, he was completely dependent on the Lord, he knew that if the Lord is with me, I don't have to worry about anything. In fact, as we all know, Arjuna had Krishna by his side and he eventually won the battle. Although in terms of army, in terms of number of soldiers, in terms of the generals, Duryodhana and the Kauravas were far more powerful. In fact, Arjuna was given, you want me or you want my army? Arjuna chose Krishna, knowing very well that if I have Krishna, I have everything. But Duryodhana, apparently, materially speaking, was wise, and he said, what's the use of Krishna? Let me have the army. So, because Arjuna chose Krishna, he was completely dependent on the Lord, surrendered to the Lord, Krishna reciprocated, literally in the sense that he served Arjuna in the battle of Kurukshetra as Parthasarathy, as the chariot driver. And we all know at all crucial times, critical times, Krishna intervened

---
#### surrender, divine intervention, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=678
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Without you, I am helpless."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Arjuna's defeat after the Kurukshetra war to emphasize that even with the same abilities and resources, without Krishna's presence, one is helpless, highlighting the importance of complete surrender.

After the battle, when Arjuna was assigned the task of taking the queens of Dwaraka, he was attacked by some dacoits. And these dacoits were not some powerful warriors, armed with all kinds of emanations and weapons. They were having some sticks in their hand, like cowards' men. And they were attacked. And Arjuna did try to protect the queens. But unfortunately, they overpowered them. And at that point, after having got defeated by these so-called dacoits, Arjuna, there's a verse which says that, Arjuna is saying, O Lord, this is the same Arjuna, the same Arjuna who became victorious in the battle of Kurukshetra. This is the same Gandiva bow. This is the same chariot. The same chariot in which Arjuna was seated in the battle of Kurukshetra. This is the same chariot driven by the same horses. In other words, all the circumstances were the same in terms of his abilities, his strength, the chariot, the horses. But he says, without you, I am helpless. I couldn't do much. So that is the position of the devotee. In fact, this is the reason why a devotee is not proud. Even if he accomplishes something big, he knows that it is all because of the mercy of the Lord. He knows that it is not artificially psyching himself or sentimentally believing that, no, no, no, sometimes people try to fake some humility. No, it is not because of me, it is because of X, it could be because of something else. They try to pass on the credit to their fellowmen, to their colleagues, to their teammates. But Arjuna was very clear, being conscious of the Lord. He said, it is because of you. So when a devotee is completely surrendered to the Lord, the Lord also accordingly serves his devotees, intervenes. In case of materialists or people who are not at all surrendered to the Lord, who are not at all conscious of the Lord, Krishna is the witness and sanctioner. This law of karma is acting impersonally, just like law of gravity. If I throw a ball, if anybody else throws a ball, it follows a law, the law of gravity, it acts impersonally. So law of karma also acts impersonally, in case we are not surrendered to the Lord. But when we are surrendered to the Lord, then the Lord is not just the witnesser and sanctioner, He is also the director. He takes charge of the life of devotees.

---
#### free will, surrender, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you are praying, only when you are in problem, you have a problem."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that while we have free will, the Lord does not intervene unless we voluntarily choose to serve and depend on Him, and that our relationship with the Lord should not be limited to seeking help only in times of difficulty.

Just like in case of, you know, when the bridge was being built to Lanka, as per the law, the stone should be sinking in the water. But the stones were floating. So free will, we have the free will, the Lord does not intervene in terms of how we exercise our free will. The choice is ours. If that free will is not given, then there is no difference between us and, let's say, a programmable machine, a robot, or for that matter, a computer. We have a software, we execute that code and this computer gives the output. So there is no difference. But we are not like robots. Krishna has given us a choice. If we voluntarily choose to serve the Lord, to think of the Lord, to depend on the Lord, to come back to our original constitutional position, the Lord reciprocates. So if we have to experience the intervention of the Lord in our lives, it cannot be just by praying to the Lord in the times of difficulty. There is a quote which is often floated around. It says that if you are praying, only when you are in problem, you have a problem. In other words, we cannot have Lord as, just like when we have something broken, we take out quick fix, fevicol, and we apply it, and once the work is done, the joint is made, we put it back in the drawer. So we cannot use Lord as a quick fix to quick fix our problems. At times we are disappointed that the Lord is not intervening. So this relationship with the Lord, the surrender to the Lord, is a process which we have to follow.

#### devotion, consciousness, obstacles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1017
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you become conscious of me by my grace, you will cross over all obstacles of conditional life."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the injunctions in the Gita inspire us to depend on the Lord and become His devotees, emphasizing that by becoming conscious of the Lord, we can overcome all obstacles in conditional life through His grace.

It's a path which we have to undertake. And all the injunctions which is given in the Gita is to inspire us, to motivate us, to tread that path, to depend on the Lord, to think of the Lord, to become his devotee. Krishna in the Gita says, mak chitta sarva durgaani mat prasadat tarishas If you become conscious of me, although he does not force, he says, if you become conscious of me, he could have forced us to become conscious of him, but he doesn't do that. mak chitta sarva durgaani mat prasadat tarishasi If you become conscious of me by my grace, how do we achieve God's grace, Lord's intervention? If we think of the Lord, if we are conscious of the Lord. mak chitta sarva durgaani mat prasadat tarishasi atha chettu ahankaram But if you act out of false ego, not remembering the Lord, in our scenario, most of the time what so happens is we are completely forgetful of the Lord and we remember the Lord only at times of difficulty. Whereas Lord is saying, if you become conscious of me by my grace, you will cross over all obstacles of conditional life.
