#### surrender, divine intervention, Draupadi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1096
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As you surrender so shall I reciprocate."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Draupadi's attempted disrobing to illustrate the principle of surrender, explaining that only when Draupadi completely surrendered to the Lord, letting go of her own efforts, did Krishna intervene and provide unlimited lengths of saree.

So, to give an example of, I think we covered last last session, Draupadi. As we all know, Dushashan and the Kauravas in the gambling match, the Pandavas lost. They even at one point put on bet Draupadi and she was lost. And Dushashan unfortunately in the whole assembly attempted, because now the Draupadi was belonging to them. They had all rights on her. When you lose something, it goes to the other person. So, Dushashan attempted to strip Draupadi in the whole assembly. And Draupadi appealed, begged. Looked for help all around. Just like anybody in danger, we look for help. More so women in this kind of a scenario. She looked at her husbands, the five Pandavas imploringly as if, please come forward, save me, protect me. But since they had lost the match and lost her, their heads were down in shame. They couldn't come forward. Then she looked at Bhishma Pitamah, who is the Grandshire of the Kuru dynasty. She even tried to implore Bhishma, please come forward and step and save me. But Bhishma also was helpless. Then finally she knew that if she has to save, she has to depend on her whole strength. And then she caught hold of the saree with both hands very tightly. And on the other side, there was Dushashan trying to disrobe her. And she was holding on to the saree very very tightly with all her strength, with all her might, with all her ability, determined that she will not let Dushashan do what he is attempting. But compared to her strength, he was very very powerful. Physically well built, very strong, part of Duryodhan's army, one of the generals. So Draupadi very soon realized that her strength compared to Dushashan's strength is limited. So she begged the Lord. She caught the saree with one hand and with other hand she raised her hands. Surrender means you raise the hands. Just like we have seen sometimes in wars, the soldiers surrender when they are defeated. Surrender means to give oneself to the control of another person. So still she had one hand holding the saree, the other hand she raised. And tried to chant the names of Govinda, calling on Govinda to help her. To help her in that situation. And at that point she did not experience any of Lord's intervention. Maybe the Lord was waiting for her to surrender fully. Lord has wonderful ways to make the devotees experience his reciprocation by forcing the devotees into circumstances where they are left with no choice but to surrender. There is a very wonderful interaction, beautiful interaction between devotees and the Lord. So then Draupadi realizing that she is helpless and she cannot protect herself, raised both hands, let loose her saree. Stopped holding on and raised both hands and said Govinda, Govinda. Completely dependent on the Lord. And at that point the Lord intervened and supplied unlimited length of saree. And on one side Duryodhana, Dushashana was trying to disrobe and there was no end to the saree. And he could not do that. So as you surrender so shall I reciprocate.

---
#### surrender, divine intervention, practical application
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1389
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As you surrender so shall I reciprocate."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that surrender is not an all-or-nothing concept, and even a small degree of surrender will result in some reciprocation from the Lord, and addresses the practical implications of surrender in everyday situations, such as taking precautions during COVID-19 or seeking medical treatment.

Now this is not a binary logic, 0 or 1. I have also shared this earlier. It is not that we have to surrender 100% and only then and only then we will experience Lord's intervention. As you surrender so shall I reciprocate. If we have surrendered even 10% there will be 10% reciprocation. Although we can't quantify the reciprocation and surrender like that but we will experience some reciprocation. The very fact that we are continuing in our journey in Krishna Consciousness, the very fact that we are chanting, we are experiencing some reciprocation from the Lord. So the more we surrender the better it is for us. And if we want to experience the Lord's intervention then we have to surrender more and more. We have to depend on the Lord more and more. And in fact towards the end of the Gita Krishna is out of compassion, knowing what is the best self-interest is saying surrender to me. We all have created our own so-called responsibilities, duties. We have weaved our own plans of protection. Krishna is saying, means give up. Surrender to me. Now, does that mean that okay let's take a practical example. Now we are going through COVID times. You know we have lot of announcements being made, lot of guidelines being issued, social distancing, wear a mask, take all the precautions. Can we say that I am completely dependent on the Lord, I am surrendered to the Lord. I don't have to worry, Lord will protect me. I don't have to wear mask, I can go anywhere and everywhere. I can open the temple and you know call all the people and have a mass gathering. I know that Lord will protect. I am surrendered to the Lord. Can we do that? Or for that matter if we become sick, can we say that I don't have to go to the doctor. I don't have to take any medicine. I don't have to take any supplements. I don't have to do any of these things. I am completely dependent on the Lord.

---
#### surrender, divine intervention, intelligence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1530
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What can I do? I sent the rescue boat. I did the forecast. I sent the helicopters. But if you don't accept, what do I do?"
**Summary:** The speaker uses the story of a priest who refused rescue efforts during a flood to illustrate that dependence on the Lord does not negate the need for intelligence and accepting practical help.

On the contrary, if I go to the doctor, does that mean that I am not surrendered to the Lord? I am not dependent on the Lord. Am I dependent on the doctor? Am I dependent on the medicine? Lot of people get confused. I should not take vaccine. I am dependent on the Lord. We have that story, wonderful story of you know a priest. There was a flood. In the village. And the priest was praying to the Lord for protection. And then there was a weather forecast and a warning was given that it is going to be raining very heavily and please evacuate, evacuate. But the priest did not listen. He was there in his church and was praying. And eventually it rained very heavily and the whole village was flooded. And the priest went inside. And even to the rooftop of that church. And then lot of people were fleeing trying to protect themselves. And even there were some rescue boats. Rescue operations were carried out. And the boats also came. The priest said no. And he was praying intensely to Jesus. Please protect him, protect him. The rescue boats came but he said no. I don't want. I am dependent on Lord Jesus. He will protect me. And even the rescue boats went off. And finally the water level kept rising. It reached to a state where the whole church was submerged. And finally there was a cross on the top of the church. And he was somehow holding on to it, praying to Jesus. Oh Lord, please save me. And even then he heard, he saw in the sky there were some helicopters. They were throwing some, you know, rescue operations was being conducted. They even threw some kind of a ladder so that he could hold on and come up. But he was denying even that. Praying intensely to the Lord. Finally, unfortunately, the water level increased and the whole village was submerged including the church. And he also died. He went to the heaven being a very pious soul, devoted soul. But he had a lot of complaint against the Lord. This is the characteristic when our prayers are not answered. We become very angry with the God. So here also he was very angry. He said, I prayed intensely. I dependent on you. I did all the things. But why did you not protect me? So Lord said, what can I do? I sent the rescue boat. I did the forecast. I sent the helicopters. But if you don't accept, what do I do?

---
#### devotion, faith, instrumentality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1710
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee knows a doctor may have saved his life but the doctor is just a mere instrument in the hand of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that devotees should see worldly help, like doctors and medicine, as instruments of the Lord's protection, using their intelligence and fulfilling their duties while maintaining faith in Krishna as the ultimate cause.

So we should not see all these things which are happening as independent of the Lord. A devotee may go to the doctor, will take the medicine. Seeing it as part of the Lord, the protection of the Lord is coming through that. He has to use his intelligence. Prabhupada said, great devotees may not even do that. Because they are completely dependent on the Lord. They have complete faith in the Lord. They are completely pure. We are not in that state. We cannot imitate that state. Prahlad Maharaj was completely dependent on the Lord. He was thrown in a pit of snakes. He was completely chanting the names of Krishna. Knowing very well the Lord will surely protect him. But right now our faith is not that strong. So if we were to do that, it will be an imitation without having that strong faith and dependence and remembrance and consciousness of the Lord in our heart. It will be an imitation. So devotee as a matter of duty goes to the doctor, takes the medicine but having the faith that these are just instruments. Prahlad Maharaj says in the Bhagavatam, you may have the best of doctors, you may have the best of medicines but if Krishna does not protect, they all will be of no use. Same way Arjuna says, the same Gandiva bow, the same Arjuna, the same chariot driven by the same horses but minus Krishna it is all null and void. I could not even protect the queens from such dacoits. Who are wielding sticks made of wood, wooden sticks, bamboo sticks. So although a devotee will, will as a matter of duty go to the doctor, take the medicine but he has, sometimes when person goes to a doctor or medicine, they think that the doctor has saved the life, the medicine. In other words, ultimate cause for their protection is the medicine. The ultimate cause is the doctor. That's why many times people even go and tell the doctor who have, you know, if they have gone through some major surgery and have recovered, they say you have given me the next life, you are God for me. A devotee knows a doctor may have saved his life but the doctor is just a mere instrument in the hand of the Lord. Sarva Karana Karanam is the Lord. So credit goes to the Lord. Due credit goes to the doctor. Due credit goes to the medicine. We are not fanatical. Yes, doctors have a role to play. Parents have a role to play in our upbringing. We give due credit. We give due respect. Knowing very well that our Supreme Lord, He is the cause of all causes.

---
#### detachment, duty, Krishna's protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1895
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You have the right to do your duty, but do not be attached to the results of your activities."
**Summary:** The speaker uses examples of prominent figures who passed away despite having access to the best medical care to illustrate that Krishna's protection is paramount, and emphasizes the importance of performing one's duty without attachment to the results.

So Prahlad says, you may have the best of doctors, you may have the best of medicines, but if Krishna does not protect, all will be of no use. And we are seeing in present times a lot of rich people, lot of affluent people with all the resources at their command, the best of doctors attending to them. We have many celebrities who have lost their lives. Just couple of days back, CBI director, 65 year old Mr. Ranjit Singh, he left the body because of COVID. So many ministers, we have even in the BJP government, not because of COVID, even otherwise, Mr. Arun Jaitley, Sushma Swaraj, very eminent personalities. Having the best of doctors attending to them, the best of resources, very powerful, very eminent. In a ruling party, among the top five leaders ruling the whole country. But the doctors could not save them. So we as a matter of duty, that's why Krishna many many places in the Bhagavad Gita talks about, you have the right to do your duty, but do not be attached to the results of your activities. As a matter of duty, yes, we will go to the doctor. As a matter of duty, we will take the medicine.

#### gratitude, surrender, instrumentality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GccuoDdnBGw?t=1974
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is because of Him, the doctors could make the right kind of medicine."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the first point by reiterating that while we should make arrangements for our protection, we must thank the Lord for successful outcomes and give due credit to instrumental causes, recognizing Krishna as the cause of all causes.

If we get protected, we give due respect to the doctor, give ultimate respect to the Lord, who is the cause of all causes. It is because of Him, the doctors could make the right kind of medicine. It is because of the Lord, the right medicine was available to you at the right time. These days, a lot of people, in fact, hospitals, the beds are not available, ICU, oxygen, things are in shortage. We thank the Lord. So if we have to have intervention of the Lord, we have to be dependent on the Lord. We have to surrender to Him. We cannot imitate that. As a matter of duty, make whatever arrangement needs to be made for our protection. And if those arrangements, the outcome is successful, we thank the Lord and give due credit to whatever instrumental causes are there in the chain of cause of causes.

#### guru, divine mercy, spiritual master

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "By whose mercy, if we get the mercy of a spiritual master, Guru, we will get the mercy of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second principle: approaching the Lord through a Guru, emphasizing that the mercy of Krishna is attained through the mercy of a spiritual master.

So the second principle is, the second principle is, go through Guru. Many times, we approach the Lord and we directly go to the Lord, directly start offering prayers. In the morning prayers which we chant, there is a saying, yasya prasadat bhagwat prasado yasya aprasadan nagati kutopi By whose mercy, yasya prasadat, if we get the mercy of a spiritual master, Guru, yasya prasadat bhagwat prasado, we will get the mercy of Krishna.

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is like the sun, when Krishna is there, there is no darkness."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about Srila Prabhupada's association with a devotee's grandfather, highlighting Prabhupada's dedication to his Guru's order and his encouragement to depend on Krishna, even in times of anxiety.

Once, Srila Prabhupada, this was, you know, a devotee who was recollecting memories of Srila Prabhupada and he said that when Prabhupada was in Calcutta, his grandfather had the good opportunity to have the association of Srila Prabhupada. In fact, few mornings he had the opportunity to walk with Prabhupada. Prabhupada would go for morning walk. So his grandfather noticed that Srila Prabhupada was very brave, he would walk with authority. So he was very impressed with Srila Prabhupada. So one day he introduced himself and requested Srila Prabhupada if he can also join him and walk along with him. So Prabhupada agreed and soon they became friends, they got to know each other. So Srila Prabhupada mentioned to this gentleman who was grandfather of this person, this person was sharing the memory. The Srila Prabhupada said that my Guru Maharaj has given me an order to preach this message in the western world and I have taken his order very very seriously. I am now his servant. So Srila Prabhupada mentioned that and he also said that, he told this person who is the grandfather of this, this person who is narrating this memory, he also told me that you also chant the names of Krishna, depend on Krishna and whenever you have any anxiety, any fear, remember Krishna. And he told me that Krishna Surya Sama, Krishna is like the sun, when Krishna is there, there is no darkness. I have taken up this mission of fulfilling the order of my Guru Maharaj. Although I have no capacity, I have no ability, knowing very well that Krishna will supply me everything. If I am conscious of Krishna, Krishna will provide me everything. I am completely dependent on the Lord.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### DOES SPIRITUALITY KILL AMBITION? | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### spirituality, ambition, fear, attachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=41
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Does spirituality involve killing all ambitions?"
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of whether spirituality kills ambition, addressing common fears and apprehensions people have about pursuing a spiritual path.

The topic for the day is, does spirituality kill ambition? I have come across this question in different forums many, many times when people say, is it wrong to have some material ambitions or goals when we are pursuing or treading the path of spirituality? Or they have some kind of inherent fear. Does spirituality involve killing all ambitions? You should not have any material goals. Or they have that apprehension that if I tread this path, who knows I will lose interest in everything. I lose interest in my family. I lose interest in my job, in my goals, in my aspirations. And sometimes people are fearful because they have attachment and some particular things are very dear to them. Out of fear that they will lose interest, they don't want to pursue spirituality.

---
#### desire, materialism, goals
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=307
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Goal of life is not to be desireless and inert like a stone."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of 'many zeros,' explaining that everyone has aspirations and desires, which are not inherently wrong, as the goal of life is not to be desireless.

The first aspect is, many zeros. Many of us must be wondering what is this heading, many zeros. Okay, let me explain. We all have some aspirations, some desires, something which we want to achieve in life. Some goals, some desires, fulfillment of desires. Otherwise we are like dead stone. The difference between us and a dead stone is that we have desires. We all long to achieve something, to do something in life, to be something in life. And it's not wrong. Goal of life is not to be desireless and inert like a stone. So we all have aspirations, we all have goals. We all want to do something.

---
#### soul, eternal, body, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=376
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The soul is the real person, the soul, the Atma."
**Summary:** The lecture shifts to the perspective of the Atma (soul), emphasizing that we are not the body but the knower of the body, and the soul is eternal, contrasting it with the temporary nature of the physical body.

Now let's see the life from the perspective of Atma, the soul, the real self. We are not the body. We are Shetragna, the knower of the body, the soul. What is the difference between a living body and a dead body? Living body minus the soul is a dead body. Many of us have encountered a situation where we have seen someone near and dear to us lying dead. The body is right in front of our eyes but we see all the relatives gathered around crying, weeping, in grief. And many of the saying is gone. Actually the body is right there in front of our eyes. The soul is the real person, the soul, the Atma. And that body which is minus the soul is cremated, is buried, is burnt. That body which we have so nicely taken care of, so painstakingly taken care of is just burnt to ashes. So the main thing is the Atma.

---
#### soul, eternal, life, perspective
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=463
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This life is a small phase in our eternal existence."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Krishna from the Bhagavad Gita, stating that the Atma is eternal and never dies, and this human life is just a small phase in our eternal existence.

And that Atma, Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, is eternal. It's never dying. It's Nithya. Nithya means eternal. It is eternal, from time immemorial. Never ever was a time when the soul did not exist or it came into being. It is eternal. Ayam Puranu na hanyate hanyamane sharire. Hanyamane means when the body is killed, the soul is not killed. The soul does not die. The soul is eternal. And in this life, we have got this body. Fortunately, a human form of life. So from eternal perspective, from the perspective of soul, this life is a small phase in our eternal existence.

#### detachment, materialism, soul, education
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=567
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We have to leave everything one day."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the concept of detachment, stating that we come into this world empty-handed and will leave the same way, and all material acquisitions, including educational qualifications, are left behind when the Atma leaves the body.

And we all have heard this. We all have, we also repeat sometimes. What did you bring here? We have come empty handed. We will go empty handed. We may not have realization of this fact. But theoretically, yes, we all have heard this statement many many times. We have also said this statement many many times. We have to leave everything one day. So we all have come in this world empty handed. And the harsh reality is no matter how expert we are, how many aspirations we have, how many desires we have, how smart we are in accumulating wealth, in properties, assets, near and dear ones. The harsh reality is one day we have to go empty handed. All what we have painstakingly by hook or crook, by cheating people, by telling lies, by right means, by wrong means, all what we have accumulated will be left behind. And we move on. So from Atma perspective, all our material acquisitions, material accomplishments, I have so painstakingly worked on. You know, I was undergrad, then I worked very hard, became a graduate, and then post graduation, and then I worked very hard. I did MBA from Ivy League college. Then I did PhD. All the educational qualifications, all the degrees from the Atma perspective, when the Atma leaves his body, is left behind. And the same Atma, next life, again starts from LKG.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that wealth is often sought for sense gratification, but human life is meant for Tapasya (austerity), specifically divine austerity, to purify oneself of negative qualities.

Why do people want lot of wealth? If we analyze, if we introspect, wealth can buy us lot of sense gratification, enjoyment for the senses. Then Nayam Deho Deha Bhajangri, Kashtan Kaaman Arhate Vidh Bhujaam Yeh, Tapo Divyam, then what should be our aspiration, what should be our goal? Tapo Divyam, human life is meant for Tapasya. What kind of Tapasya, what kind of austerity? Divyam, Divyam means divine austerity. Even people working in offices, day and night, 16 hours a day slogging, they are also doing Tapasya. But our scripture is saying, human life is an opportunity to engage in Tapo Divyam, divine Tapasya. Shudhyet, what will happen because of this Tapasya? Shudhyet will become purified. Purified of what? Purified of all the Anarthas, all the base qualities, which is clinging on to us. Lust, greed, pride, envy, jealousy, illusion, madness.

Tapo Divyam Shudhyet Brahmasau Khyamto Nantam and once we get purified, we will enjoy true happiness. Right now we believe that when I have lot of wealth, when I have achieved my goals, I will be truly happy, I will be truly satisfied. A day will come when I would have all the accomplishments which I am aspiring for and from that day onwards, I will be so satisfied, I will be so happy and I will really enjoy life. And people actually believe that. This is what is missing in my life. I have to work hard. Once I get this, once I get married, once I get this job, once I pass out from this college, my life is done. And this goes on and on and on. So, Rishabhdev in Srimad Bhagavatam is pointing out that human life is not meant for that. So, does that mean that we should not be working hard for our goals? Our scriptures point out that these goals in itself, without Lord being in the centre, one, in front of those zeros, are actually zeros.

But if you put one in front of the zeros, if you have one zero, the value is ten. If you have two zeros, it is hundred. Three zeros, thousand. Four zeros, ten thousand. So, all the zeros acquire meaning and value if you have one in front of this. So, from soul point of view, the real self point of view, the atma point of view, nothing wrong about having wealth. Nothing wrong about acquiring certain skills. Nothing wrong. But if we are working very, very hard to acquire these zeros and have no time to add one and that one, Lord is not there in our lives, just struggle for existence, then it is It is useless labor only.

Anta Narayana Smriti. So, main thing is not that whether we should have material aspirations or not, the main thing is to have a clear understanding. #### wealth distribution, poverty, suffering, research
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1195
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Visualize no food to eat, no money in the pocket, not eaten for days together, killing a wild rat, roasting it, burning it live, and feeding the children."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of unequal distribution of wealth, sharing a story about people in Burma during the COVID lockdown who were starving and resorting to eating rats from sewage pipes to survive.

As a matter of duty. Once there were group of researchers who were researching into this particular aspect that why there is so much of unequal distribution of wealth in this world. On one side we see billionaires, hundred billion dollars, and other side we have people living in abject poverty, do not even have food to eat. I was reading an article during this lockdown, COVID time in Burma. There were people, because many of them lost jobs, they were hand to mouth, no earnings, starving for days. In the sewage, in the sewage pipes, you know at times there are rats, they were actually searching out, looking for, hunting those rats, the wild rats living in sewage pipes, killing them, roasting them, and eating, feeding their children. Visualize no food to eat, no money in the pocket, not eaten for days together, killing a wild rat, roasting it, burning it live, and feeding the children.

So there are people like that. The Pareto law, 80-20 principle, 80%, 20% of the people in this world own 80% of the world. There was one more article which said that the wealth of top 8 persons in this world, the richest top 8 persons in the world, the total wealth, is more than, is more than 300, 3.8 billion people in this world. 3.8 billion people, their collective wealth, and these 8 people put together, the wealth, their wealth is more than them. So these researchers were trying to figure out why there is so much of unequal distribution of wealth.

#### meritocracy, height, weight, bell curve
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1345
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We are living in a world of meritocracy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that we live in a world of meritocracy where hard work is believed to lead to success, but the researchers found that while height and weight follow a normal distribution (bell curve), wealth distribution does not.

This was the topic of research. In today's world, we are living in a world of meritocracy where we say that, where we believe that if you work hard, if you study hard, if you are, you know, put lot of efforts, then you can get educated and with the right kind of degrees, with the right kind of education, with the right kind of jobs, we can earn right kind of money, and be successful in life, be rich in life. So, the researchers found out that when it comes to height, weight, it follows, if you were to plot it in a graph, then the graph would look like a bell curve, which is also called the normal distribution. You take average height of people in a particular class, and then some students will be above that average height, and as we move, it tapers down, and then some students will, the height will be much lesser than the average height, and it tapers down on the left side. And if you were to plot, you will get a bell curve, be it height, be it weight, very few people, too much underweight, very few people overweight, average weight, and then it's a bell curve.

---
#### time, effort, results, distribution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1432
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Something very unusual is happening, and it does not follow the normal distribution."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while time spent working also follows a bell curve, the results of that work do not, as some people working fewer hours become billionaires while others working long hours struggle to survive.

So, when it comes to time which people spend in working, the time which people invest in their jobs, again, it follows the bell curve. Lot of people on an average work 7 to 8 hours, 9 hours in a day, that's the average. There are few people who work 12 hours, 14 hours, 16 hours, 18 hours a day, there are people like that, workaholics. So, that's one side of the bell curve, and there are people who take it very easy, work for one or two hours in a day. So, as far as the effort being put is also, if you were to plot that, you will get a bell curve. But this research has found out that when it comes to the results, the amount of time what you're spending, and the outcome of, the results of that work, something very unusual is happening, and it does not follow the normal distribution. A person may work for 2 hours, and he may be billionaire, and a person may be working 16 hours, and he may not even have food to eat for his family members.

---
#### child labor, poverty, hard work, inequality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1507
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We get to eat here, we get to live here."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about seeing a 6-year-old boy working 14-16 hours a day, 7 days a week, washing dishes at a roadside dhaba for a meager salary, highlighting the stark contrast between hard work and the resulting outcome.

I remember once, we were travelling, we were on a highway, going from one town to another town, long drive, about 300 odd kilometres, this was in Punjab. This hot sunny day, we stopped in a roadside dhaba, for some refreshments, and then we ate something. I saw one small boy, must be about 5 or 6, although we talk about child labour, still the countryside, a lot of small towns, small young children also work. So I saw a 6 year old boy, 5 to 6 year old boy, he was collecting all the dishes, you know when people would eat, the plates, he would collect all that, go to one small tap, they had made some kind of a sink, and he would take the plates there, put it in water, apply the soap, get it cleaned, again, you know, after washing them, keep it for drying. So that was his job, and I had finished my meal, so I was standing next to him, I had to wash my hands. So I saw him, it was quite obvious that he had not taken bath for few days, because the face was quite black, all dusty, dirty, and, you know, the clothes were quite tattered. And he was working, so I became little inquisitive, and I asked him, how long you work, what time your day starts? So he told me, 7 o'clock in the morning. I was surprised, small boy, 7 o'clock in the morning. I said, till what time you have to work? So he said, till 9.30, 10 in the night. Imagine, 14 or 15 hours. I said, so, you know, again, I said, weekly off, when do you get off? Do you get leave on Sunday? When do you get off? He says, we don't get any leave. He works 7 days a week. And then he interrupted me, saying that, but, we get to eat here, we get to live here.

---
#### child labor, poverty, hard work, inequality, Warren Buffet
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1667
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, revealing the boy's meager monthly salary and contrasting it with the wealth of people like Warren Buffet, who earns billions while spending his days reading.

So some provision is made for him to stay, and, and the person stays there, works from 7 in the morning to 9.30, 10 in the night, 7 days a week. And then, I felt very sorry for him, and asked him, how much are you paid? How much do you get? So he told me, 400 rupees. Those days, this was about 15-20 years back. In today's time, maybe it will be 2000 or 2500 rupees. Imagine, a child working 7 days a week, 14-16 hours a day, sincerely, it's not that he is calm chore, sincerely working, and he is getting 400 rupees. In a month. In a month. And there are people who work for 2 hours, 3 hours. I was reading an article on, Warren Buffet. Every day without fail, he reads for 4-5 hours. He may not be working hard in that sense. He is in his house, reading the books. Billions of dollars.

#### hard work, results, meritocracy, astonishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1731
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "They were astonished."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the disparity between hard work and results, noting that while hard work follows a bell curve, the resulting wealth does not, leading the researchers to be astonished by this deviation from the expected meritocratic outcome.

So when it comes to results, hard work versus result, as far as hard work is concerned, yes, it follows the bell curve. If you plot, the number of hours a person puts in. But when it comes to result, somebody is working 8 hours, is getting billions of dollars, somebody is working 16 hours, is getting few thousands of rupees. So they were astonished. Why is this happening? More so when we, in today's time, it's a world of meritocracy. You work hard, you follow a process, you will get the results. Everything is becoming very process oriented. Six sigma. How we can master the process, so that we can get hold of the results? We can minimize the errors. So when we are living in a world of meritocracy, why is it that some people work hard and get practically nothing and some people by not working get so much of results?

#### randomness, luck, algorithm, simulation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1797
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "They had to introduce in that computer simulation a parameter of randomness or called luck."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that to explain the ground reality of wealth distribution in their algorithm, the researchers had to introduce a parameter of randomness or luck, suggesting that factors beyond hard work play a significant role.

So now, the ground reality, to explain the ground reality in the algorithm and they simulated this whole thing, different people working different hours and getting different results, to get that kind of a graph, they had to introduce in that computer simulation a parameter of randomness or called luck. And when they introduce this factor called randomness, randomness means you cannot explain, which you can also call luck. We say sometimes he was quite lucky. He was very lucky. He got the right break at the right time. So introduce this factor called randomness or luck and then they could get the results what they are observing as far as the ground reality is concerned.

---
#### randomness, luck, laws of nature, villager, airplane
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1844
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "For him, it's magic."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the researchers concluded that randomness is a phenomenon operating in the world, using the analogy of a villager seeing an airplane and considering it magic because they don't understand the laws of aerodynamics.

So the researchers came up with this understanding that randomness is a phenomena which is operating in this world. Randomness. In other words, what is randomness? Random means it cannot be explained. We cannot explain why somebody is working 16 hours and still getting few thousands rupees and somebody is this thing. Now, randomness, yes. What cannot be explained? For example, a villager, when he sees a plane flying, for him, he cannot explain why it is happening. For him, it's magic. It's magic. It's beyond his conception. How it is flying? Such a huge machine. It's flying in the air. When you throw something in the air, by law of gravity, it falls down. But here, a huge machine, jumbo 747, 737, with so many passengers flying in the air, not falling down. For him, it's difficult to explain.

---
#### laws of nature, karma, luck, aerospace engineer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0WMPy9sWEMs?t=1907
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There are stringent laws of material nature operating."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the villager's perspective with that of an aerospace engineer who understands the laws of flight, stating that what seems like luck or randomness is actually governed by stringent laws of material nature and karma.

But for airspace engineer, it is not a random phenomena. He precisely knows the laws which are operating because of which a huge airplane is in the air. So likewise, because we are not aware of the laws which are operating because of which somebody is born rich, somebody is born poor, somebody is earning so much, somebody is living in abject poverty, we can call it luck, we can call it a random phenomena. But the fact is, there are stringent laws of material nature operating. The laws of karma, daivam. Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita talks about five factors of action. Endeavour, our endeavour, hard work is just one of them. Now not knowing these laws, we may say it's a random phenomena or we may say it's luck.

#### soul, body, eternity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XQvsJzWx4qM?t=331
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those with the vision of eternity can see that the soul is transcendental, eternal and beyond the modes of nature."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to discuss Text 32, emphasizing that those with the vision of eternity can see the soul as transcendental and beyond the modes of nature, contrasting this with those who are in the bodily concept of life and whose philosophy becomes "eat, drink, be merry"; he explains that human life begins when we come out of this bodily concept and realize "Aham brahmasmi, I am not this body, I am a soul."

Text 32, those with the vision of eternity can see that the soul is transcendental, eternal and beyond the modes of nature. Despite contact with material body, O Arjuna, the soul neither does anything nor is entangled. Now here it says, those with vision of eternity, which means there are some people who do not have vision of eternity, they are not able to see beyond the body. Not everyone can see beyond the body. Not everyone is blessed with the vision of eternity. Those who are in bodily concept, I am the body, this life is all in all. And the philosophy of life, knowingly or unknowingly becomes, eat, drink, be merry. Now that is the philosophy which even animals have. They may do it in a crude way, we may do it in a sophisticated way. So human life begins when we start coming out of this concept. Athato brahma jignyasa, aham brahmasmi, I am not this body, I am a soul. I am Brahman. Those with the vision of eternity, what happens to them? They are not able to see beyond the body. What happens to them? They see that the soul is transcendental. Soul is different from the body. We are that soul. We are not the body. The body is the dress. The body is kshetra or kshetra jna.

Real ego means I am. I am the Lord. I am a living being. So different spiritual paths, Different religions, Different processes may vary in details. Somebody may recommend fasting. Somebody may recommend austerity of one kind. Somebody may do tapas of one kind. Somebody may recommend chanting. It's all about purifying us. Purifying us of that false ego. And once that false ego gets purified. Or gets dismantled. And get replaced by the real ego. One realizes that I am a pure soul.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Divine & Demoniac Qualities | BG 16.01 - 16.03 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### divine qualities, demoniac qualities, pride, arrogance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sGLjEz_Koag?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Unlike that, a demoniac person thinks that he has made it. He is the cause of his accomplishment."
**Summary:** The lecture begins by defining divine and demoniac qualities, focusing on arrogance and pride as traits of demoniac individuals who believe they are the sole cause of their accomplishments.

The name of the title is divine and demoniac qualities. So today's discussion will be based on the wonderful purpose teachings of His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada. This is text 4 from the 16th chapter: Arrogance, pride, anger, conceit, harshness, and ignorance. These qualities belong to those of demoniac nature, O son of Pritha. So we talked about the chapter name, it is divine and demoniac qualities. So here Krishna is talking about the qualities which are manifest in the behavior of a demoniac person. It is mentioned that they are arrogant or proud in possessing. Suppose somebody has some talent, some skill, some abilities, some wealth, something in possession. There is an element of pride: I have done it. I have made it in life. I have accumulated this asset. And this also brings an element of arrogance. Unlike these qualities which are manifest in a demoniac person, a divine person in his consciousness believes. Although he may have property papers, he may go and sign the thing. In an official level, yes, this is my property. But deep within he believes that everything belongs to the Lord. Unlike that, a demoniac person thinks that he has made it. He is the cause of his accomplishment.

#### respect, anger, spiritual advancement, tolerance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sGLjEz_Koag?t=127
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As we become devotees, the percentage of divine qualities will increase."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts demanding respect (demoniac) with commanding respect (divine), and explains that spiritual advancement is measured by the increase of divine qualities and decrease of demoniac qualities like anger and intolerance.

Now, there is a difference between demanding respect and commanding respect. So, the demoniac demands respect, whereas a divine person, based on his divine qualities, commands respect. He is not asking for respect, but seeing his wonderful qualities, invariably, he demands respect. He draws respect. A demoniac person, invariably, for small, small things, gets angry. And each one of us have a mix of divine and demoniac qualities. As we become devotees, the percentage of divine qualities will increase. And this is one of the measurements that, yes, we have. We are advancing in our spiritual journey. If we look back, now somebody says, how do we know that we are advancing? The qualities what we are discussing today, the demoniac qualities which we may have, will eventually, in due course of time, if we are on the right path and advancing, will diminish. And the divine quality will start manifesting in our behavior. So, one of the qualities of demoniac are, they get angry, small, small things. They get irritated, agitated. Whereas a divine person is very calm, very sober. He is very tolerant.

#### tolerance, forgiveness, divine qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sGLjEz_Koag?t=227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is a great blessing today that I have got an opportunity to take bath in the river 108 times."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about an exalted devotee who demonstrated extreme tolerance by repeatedly bathing in the river after being spat on 108 times, ultimately leading the perpetrator to repent.

This reminds me of a story of a person who was a very exalted devotee. And as a result, his fame. He became quite famous and people from different villages would come to meet him, have his darshan, pay obeisances to him. So, demoniac people, they don't like this. So, one such demoniac person somehow wanted to bring dis-repute to this saintly person. He wanted to show to the people that he is just a farce. He is just putting up a show. Actually, he is not a divine person. So, this person hired a person and gave him an assignment that I will pay you so and so amount if you could somehow make him get angry. Make him lose temper. And then when he loses temper in public, in front of everyone, he will be exposed. People will see that it's all a show. He is also short tempered. He also gets agitated, irritated. Small, small things. So, this person who was hired went to the river, quite close to the place where this person used to do his meditation. And on his way back, passed by that way and spat at him. Spit at him. So, this person, if we were there, what will we do? We will get angry. We will get agitated. How dare you? So, this person looks at him, smiles and then gets up, goes to the river and takes his bath. And again comes back and sits in his place. Again, the second time, the same person comes and again does the same thing. Again, this person goes to the river. In fact, sorry, this person had gone to the river. He was returning back home and this person spit at him. So, this repeated, you won't believe, 108 times. Every time he would spit at him, he would smile, go back to the river, take his bath and then come back. When this happened, the 108th time, this person who was hired to make him angry fell at his feet. He says, I am very, very sorry. I stooped to such low heights. Low heights just for the greed of money. And then he started disclosing that how he was paid so and so amount. And then he says, I am so sorry that I had to make you take bath 108 times. But I am really surprised. Every time, he didn't get angry. He just went. Then this person said, why you have to be sorry? It is a great blessing today that I have got an opportunity to take bath in the river 108 times. And then he says, in fact, I am sorry. Had you told me that you are going to get such big amount, I would have acted as if I am getting angry. I wouldn't have let you wait for so long. Wasted so much of your time. Again, we don't have to do this extreme.

By the way, does anger have a place in life? Should we not get angry at all? What is the answer? Anger has a place? Yes? No? How many of you say anger is important? Sometimes. How many of you say we should not get angry? Anger is an enemy. Anger kills, it burns within. We should never get angry. So, it is a big topic. But there is a place for anger in our life. And there is something called as rightful anger. When a teacher gets angry at a child for right reason, when he is not doing his homework or probably not mending his ways, not improving in his studies or something, there is a place for it. Parents getting angry has a place. In fact, devotees also, we have many examples of exalted devotees getting angry. Example? Hanuman, yes. He got angry, he burned the whole Lanka. Anybody else? Durvasa is not a right example. Who else? Lord Chaitanya himself. Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, when Nithyananda Prabhu, his associate was somebody threw stone at him, hurt him, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu became very angry. It is said that when somebody blasphemes the devotee or the Lord, one should become angry. So, I think we will not get into details. It is a big subject.

#### demoniac qualities, whimsical actions, higher laws, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sGLjEz_Koag?t=549
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Demoniac people do not care that there are higher laws. I should act responsibly. I like doing it, I enjoy it, I will do it. I don't care."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that demoniac people act whimsically, driven by their own opinions and desires, disregarding higher laws, accountability, and the law of karma, focusing only on present enjoyment.

Now, demoniac people do things whimsically. They are driven by their opinions. According to me, this is right. In my opinion, I think so. And suppose you tell a demoniac person, don't do this, it is not right thing. You don't like, you don't do it. If you tell a demoniac person, don't eat non-veg, I like it, I do it. You don't like, you don't do it. Who cares? This is the spirit. Now, what is good, what is bad, what is right, what is wrong, what is papa, what is punya is not subject to our whims. It is not what I think is right. There are well-defined laws operating in this nature. For example, traffic laws. In India, we drive on the left side of the road. In the US, it is on the right side. It is not subject to my opinion, my likes. I cannot be driving on the left side and then say, in my opinion, I think so. If I do that, I will be penalized. So, demoniac people do not care that there are higher laws. I should act responsibly. I like doing it, I enjoy it, I will do it. I don't care. I act whimsically. Do not recognize any higher laws, higher authority. Their actions are being witnessed. Lord is witness to their action. Accountability to their actions. Existence of law of karma. If I do wrong, I will be punished. I will have to suffer the consequences. They don't believe. Eat, drink, be merry, enjoy life. Whatever you like, do it. They are very focused in the present. Somebody tells, in future you will be punished. There will be consequences. Who cares? I am enjoying.

It is not because you have demoniac qualities, therefore you are getting angry and taking up this fight, this war. You are born with divine qualities. #### karma, individual soul, family, divine qualities, demoniac qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sGLjEz_Koag?t=752
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Each one's karma is different."
**Summary:** The speaker poses the question of why siblings in the same family can have vastly different qualities (divine vs. demoniac), and answers that each individual soul has their own baggage of karmic reactions, and that family relations are temporary.

Now, a question to all of you. We will pause for a while. Have you noticed, sometimes in the same house, brothers or sisters, siblings. One of them is relatively very sober, very calm, very tolerant. Apparently divine qualities. And in the same house, another child is having, relatively speaking, demoniac qualities. Anyone noticed? Anywhere, in any circumstances. May not be in your house, in your neighbor's house. Why it happens? Or sometimes, parents have divine qualities. The son has, relatively speaking, demoniac qualities. Or vice versa. Give me an example of vice versa. Prahlad Maharaj. His father was demoniac. Hirnakashyapu. And son was Prahlad. In fact, when mother of Prahlad. What was her name? Kadyu. Kadyu or... She was about to... She was carrying Prahlad. The Devatas wanted to kill her. Because, thinking that she is carrying the son of Hirnakashyapu. Who will be also a big demon. So, why is it that some children are born with demoniac qualities. Some children are born with divine qualities. I know one family. They have two sons. One of them, elder son is... You know, very, very quiet, calm, composed. By chance, he is playing with a friend. Somebody hits him. He will not give back. He will come back and report to his parents. Mujhe Maharaj. And, you know, relatively speaking, quite tolerant. And this younger brother... Is just the opposite. He is three or four years younger to his... This elder brother. But, if elder brother's friends hit him. This young guy will go and hit them. And for small, small things, he will pick up quarrel. And the parents were telling me that... Now, I really wonder... How much difference is there between the two? What could be the reason? But, same family. Each one's karma is different. Yes, this is a very important point. That each one of us, although born in a particular family. We are individual souls having our own baggage of karmic reactions. Bhagavatam gives example that how the straws which are floating in water. With the wave, they come together. And with another wave, they disperse. So, that way, yes. We are not eternally related in a particular family. We have come together in this life. As brothers, sisters, father, mother. And in due course of time. We will segregate, we will separate. Okay, this is one reason. We are all individual souls.

So today we will be discussing from the 10th chapter of the Gita, so the title is Divine Manifestations, text number 36 to 42. So this chapter has 42 verses based on the teachings of our beloved spiritual master, His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. We will chant this verse together. Please repeat after me. I am also the gambling of cheats, and of the splendid I am the splendor. I am victory, I am the adventure, and I am the strength of the strong. So this is in reply to Arjuna's question, can you please reveal some of your glories, some of your glorious manifestations, some of your potencies, your energies. So in reply to that Krishna says that my potencies, the creations are infinite, but he is trying to list out few of those creations just to give us a clue of his glorious manifestation or his greatness.

**Summary:** The lecture narrates the story of how Krishna orchestrated the death of Dronacharya by having Yudhishthira, known for his truthfulness, utter a half-truth about the death of Ashwatthama.

So it so happened that when the Mahabharata war was on, after ten days, the fighting was happening very fiercely and there was no sign of you know the warfare getting to a conclusive end. Both sides were you know fighting fiercely. And on one side there was Fierce, you know, people like Bhishma, Dronacharya, the other side we had Krishna, Arjuna and the Pandavas and their army. And it was very clear that so long as these great warriors like Dronacharya, Bhishma are there fighting on behalf of Kauravas, they are not going to easily get overpowered and let the Pandavas win. So after the tenth day, Krishna went to Yudhishthira Maharaj, Duryodhana, sorry Dronacharya, he was the military teacher, in fact he taught the military art to the Pandavas also. And it was very difficult to defeat him. And unless he gets defeated, you can't defeat the army of the Kauravas. So Krishna on the tenth day, knowing that somehow Dronacharya has to be killed, he went to Yudhishthira Maharaj. He had a weakness that if he hears about the death of his son, Ashwatthama, he will get completely so to say shaken, shattered, rattled. And then in that state he can be killed, not otherwise. So Yudhishthira Maharaj was called Dharmaraj. He would never, never, never break any principle. He was very upright, man of high integrity, would never tell a lie at any cost. So Krishna knowing that if Dronacharya has to be killed, he has to be somehow told that Ashwatthama is dead.

**Summary:** The speaker details how Yudhishthira was convinced to say 'Ashwatthama is dead' while subtly adding 'an elephant named Ashwatthama', leading to Dronacharya's demise and justifying Krishna's actions for a higher purpose.

So he asked Yudhishthira Maharaj, go and tell Dronacharya that Ashwatthama is dead. Now how can Yudhishthira Maharaj tell a lie? He was reluctant. He said, I can't say the lie. But then through a trick, he made a statement to Dronacharya, which means Ashwatthama hata kunjara. Hata means dead. Dronacharya. Kunjara means an elephant. So Ashwatthama, elephant by name Ashwatthama is killed. But this last two words of the statement, hata, kunjara, he very softly mentioned. And blew the conch. When Dronacharya heard this statement from the mouth of Yudhishthira Maharaj, who is Dharmaraj, who is known for his uprightness. He would never ever tell a lie. He had no doubt. Sometimes you know you want to verify the fact. You want to double check. Since he had come from the mouth of Yudhishthira Maharaj, he got completely convinced that this has actually happened. And he was, the death of his son, he was completely shaken, completely shattered. And then he sat down. And at that time he was killed. Now morally speaking, you may say, no. No. Morally speaking, you may say this is wrong, this is unfair. But then Acharyas explain why Krishna did all these acts. There is a context behind all these things. The higher purpose for which the Lord had descended, yada yadahi dharmasya glanir his, ultimate goal was to enthrone the Pandavas who were righteous, righteous kings, and establish dharma. And as I mentioned, there were some so-called breaks in moral principles or laws which Acharyas have explained.

The Kauravas were unrighteous, the Pandavas were the righteous. Now Pandavas have to be put back into the kingdom as the rulers. That means the Kauravas have to be defeated. #### divine intervention, Duryodhana, righteousness, dharma

**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker recounts how Krishna advised Duryodhana to cover his private parts before seeing his mother, Gandhari, thus preventing him from gaining full invincibility and allowing for his eventual defeat.

So when he was going to the room, Krishna came in. And he said, come on, how can you go in front of your mother naked? At least, at least, Kirtsi said, cover your private parts. And said, at least take a banana leaf and cover that part. So he went to the room of the mother. Mother was? What is the name of the mother? Gandhari. Gandhari. Gandhari. Gandhari. So he went to the room and she was, the eyes were covered and she expected as she had requested her son that son would come and he would have opened the eyes and have a glance on him and his whole body becomes like a stone, he becomes immortal. But moment she opened the eyes, he saw Duryodhana standing right in front of him, the private parts covered with a leaf of banana leaves. In one sense she was shocked. What have you done? Because she expected that if he sees him, his whole body becomes stone. Now only that part is now not immortal. In that sense, the body is like a stone. And in the war, when it happened, when Duryodhana was supposed to be killed, Krishna knew this part. He knew that if you hit anywhere else, it's not going to kill him. He cannot be killed. But this part below the waist, which is otherwise not allowed in fighting. You know even the earlier wars were thought on principles. Below the waist you are not supposed to hit. Krishna told Arjuna, Hit there. Sorry, Bheem. I am sorry. Hit there. And that's how he got hit and killed. So, among the cheats, it says Krishna is the greatest cheater. Now this was done for a higher purpose of establishing Yudhishthira Maharaj and the Pardvas on the throne because they would uphold Dharma, uphold the righteousness.

Only two people can say like that, yes or no? If you and me say like that, then it's like, because who can say everything is coming from me? I am the source of everything. All what you see is a small insignificant portion of my manifestation. The Lord is boldly declaring that because it is actually a fact and nobody laughs at the statements. Bhagavad Gita has been widely distributed all over the world. When someone makes a statement, we always say he is so proud, he is so egoistic. He is making the statement. There is no one superior to me. Everything is emanating from me. One with that understanding, that consciousness. They worship me. It is not a question of Krishna being egoistic. This is the absolute truth, the samambonam, the cause of all causes.

On the backside of the toothbrush you will find some instruction vis-a-vis how to use the product, am I right? Do not dip the bristles in hot water. And you don't write the instruction after the product has been used and is ready for disposal. As soon as the product is launched, the product is ready for disposal. The product is ready for disposal. The product is ready for disposal or released in the market along with the product, there is a manual how to optimally, efficiently use the product. Likewise, when this creation came about, entire knowledge about this creation, material and spiritual, the purpose of creation, what we are supposed to do in this creation, the entire body of knowledge is called Veda, Veda is a Sanskrit word, meaning is knowledge. But if there is an aberration in understanding, if that original intent for which that knowledge was spoken gets somehow lost, that's why Krishna says, yada yadahi dharmasya glanirbhavati bharata, whenever there is a decline in religious principle, dharmasansthapnartha, to re-establish the dharmic principles, the true understanding, I advent myself. Now this is called Shruti and 5000 years back when the Kali Yuga is about to begin, knowing that in people of Kali Yuga will be short lived, their memory will be less, they will not be able to easily comprehend. So this is called Shruti. Vedavyasa was the literary incarnation of the Lord, put those understanding of Vedas into writing and subdivided into four parts, where we have Rig Veda, Sam Veda, Yajur Veda and Atharva Veda. So all these Vedic literatures, Ramayana, Mahabharata, all these are compiled, they are not authored, mind you. Vedavyasa is not the author. He compiled all the Vedic literatures. And Krishna says. ## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Doing Justice To Human Life | HG Amitasana Dasa | SB 4. 4 .4 | 2019-07-26 |
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### discipleship, obedience, purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anu Charaha means those who can immediately understand the purpose of their master."
**Summary:** This segment explains the meaning of 'Anu Charaha' in relation to Lord Shiva's disciples and their understanding of his purpose.

Sati was going very fast so that she might not be checked by her husband, but she was immediately followed by many thousands of disciples of Lord Shiva headed by the Yakshas, Maniman and Mada. The word Gata Vyataha used in this connection means without fear. Sati did not care that she was going alone, therefore she was almost fearless. The word Anu Charaha is also significant for it indicates that Lord Shiva's disciples were always ready to sacrifice anything for Lord Shiva. All of them could understand the desire of Shiva who did not want Sati to go alone. Anu Charaha means those who can immediately understand the purpose of their master. Thus ends the Vaktivedanta.

#### materialism, animalistic tendencies, purpose of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=54
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This 15 minutes had all these four things very nicely graphically displayed or you know projected."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts watching a National Geographic episode about a jungle cat and reflects on how its activities are limited to eating, sleeping, mating, and defending, contrasting this with the potential of human life.

Few days back I had visited someone's house, had gone to meet someone and this morning time and I was received well in the house and they asked me to please sit down and it took about 15 minutes for him to come as I was sitting in the drawing room and there was a young son also sitting and watching the television and in the television he was watching something on National Geography that was the channel which was playing and since I was waiting I also happened to see what was coming although I haven't watched any channel for many years now so just curious as to what the son is watching so what about 15 minutes and it was a huge LED big TV so very crystal clear very nice photography I mean technology has advanced I remember when I was young there was initially black and white television small 14 inch and then after that first time color television came so compared to that these days things are quote-unquote advanced we are living in advanced world so this episode was focusing on the jungle cat there was a cat jungle cat wild cat and it was all about 15 minutes I was just you know also keeping what a scripture say in mind from Shastra perspective so 15 minutes it was all about how this cat goes about making arrangements for its eating not only for its eating feeding the young ones how it is making arrangement to defend in the daytime defend in the night time defend at the time when there are dangers from other wild animals then how it is sleeping in the night and while it is sleeping how it is making arrangements for protection and defense because their animals in the night time also can attack and no doubt procreation so this 15 minutes had all these four things very nicely graphically displayed or you know projected.

And if you ask any animal if you take morning to night all what they are doing are Shastra's point or broadly their actions can be classified in these four categories which is to do with eating sleeping procreation and defense and I could see that all though it was very nicely shown there was a lot of shot all the activities of this wildcat was broadly classified in these four categories only there was nothing else which it can do our scriptures point out that human form of life like here it is mentioned in the purpose those who can immediately understand the purpose of their master so this world also has been created by the Lord and there is a purpose the Lord has created anything we see created anything wonderful anything orderly anything which is done systematically it is done for a purpose it is not done just for the heck of doing things or done whimsically with no purpose with no intention with no goal so the Lord has created this world for a purpose and unfortunately especially in Kali Yuga most of the people are ignorant of the purpose of this creation why we are in this material world what is the purpose of human form of life what is the ultimate goal what does the Lord expect us to do and ignorant of the purpose of the creation or the purpose for which the Lord has sent us here the purpose for what we have come here and we have got this human body, we are acting in ways which are detrimental to our self-interest. #### materialism, theft, hypocrisy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=481
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "I am a small thief, you're a big thief."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about Alexander the Great and a thief, where the thief points out that Alexander is also a thief, but on a much larger scale, highlighting the hypocrisy of condemning others for actions one is also committing.

It all boils down to eating, sleeping, mating and defending. One time Alexander was camping with his soldiers. Earlier times they would go to a particular kingdom or a country, plunder it and then move on with all that loot, with all the jewels and all the valuables which they have looted and then move on to another kingdom, attack that kingdom and then move on. And they would have tents, mobile tents which whenever they would camp, they would put those tents. And at that time, Alexander was in his camp and a thief entered that particular tent where Alexander was staying. Unfortunately, or you could call fortunately, he was caught red-handed, and he was brought before Alexander. So Alexander, with all his army, with powerful soldiers and horses and all the kind of weapons he became very angry how dare this person enter our camp and dare to steal something so he became very angry and then he called his minister take him off and chop his head kill him and this thief said you can't do that wait you can't do that he says you are calling me a thief even you are a thief so Alexander became even more furious you know when a person is in a very big position nobody dares to speak against them so here first time he is hearing all the people, ministers, soldiers everyone subservient yes sir, yes sir, yes Alexander yes we will do it all very humbly accepting whatever his orders were here one person although he is doing wrong he is telling that even you are a thief dare to speak against Alexander you can't do that so Alexander became even more violent he said you are calling me a thief I am a small thief you're a big thief scale is different so Alexander became even more how do you say that he says I go in the night to small private properties and I went to loot and fill my bags and tried anything I could find to flee or escape. Your scale is different. Instead of small private homes, you go into big kingdoms and not alone, not in the night time with your armies and attack them, kill them and plunder them and not with some back loads, animal loads, those times you know they had camels and all kinds of animals, loaded with all the jewels and then you flee. Then you move on. So you call me a thief and then Alexander hearing this became even more vey, he says you call me a thief. I am a small thief, you are thief the great, just like Alexander the great, you are thief the great. So Alexander when he heard that, you know he was quite stuck and it seems that he let go that person, because after all what he was saying was true.

#### human intelligence, purpose of life, spiritual growth, miserliness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=692
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Scriptures point out that if we also, use our developed intelligence which can enable us to understand the higher purpose, the purpose of this creation, about God, our relationship with Him. If we do not use that intelligence for that purpose, then we are at best, just like we have small thief and a big thief, we are refined, sophisticated, well dressed, two legged animals."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that if we don't use our intelligence to understand the higher purpose of life and our relationship with God, we are merely refined animals. Such a person is a miser (Kripana) who wastes the opportunity of human life.

So scriptures point out that if we also, use our developed intelligence which can enable us to understand the higher purpose, the purpose of this creation, about God, our relationship with Him. If we do not use that intelligence for that purpose, then we are at best, just like we have small thief and a big thief, we are refined, sophisticated, well dressed, two legged animals. Dvipada pashu. It looks very harsh but it's a fact. If we use only that portion of intelligence which enables us to eat better, sleep better, defend better, mate better, then we are just animals but little more refined, more sophisticated. Prabhupada was very blunt, very straight forward in his talks, he said that animal may have sex in a street. Openly, but a man will have in a very very nice air conditioned multistoried building, very with nice incense sticks burning, very very serene, very very well decorated, this thing. But the act is the same. The pleasure is the same. Animals may defend using claws and dogs may defend barking. But we may defend using intercontinental ballistic missiles. All this thing is about you know uranium enrichment, atom bombs and you know all these kinds of missiles are getting tested and all the nations are becoming very very fearful. It's all about defend. They may do in one way, we are doing in a very very technologically advanced sophisticated ways but the act is the same. So our scriptures point out that if we don't understand the purpose, if we don't seek higher level of understanding, consciousness, purification of consciousness then we are spoiling, we are wasting this wonderful opportunity which we have got in this human birth. And such a person who is spoiling this opportunity, Bhagavad Gita Prabhupada uses the word in the purport Kripana. Kripana means miser.

#### miserliness, materialism, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=837
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Miser is a person who has the wealth but he doesn't have the means to buy the car but the wealth is locked up and he eats like a beggar stays like a beggar he doesn't want to spend the wealth he has the means to live decent life but he's because of his miserly nature he doesn't want to spend."
**Summary:** The speaker defines a miser as someone who has wealth but lives like a beggar, unwilling to spend it, and shares a childhood anecdote to illustrate this point.

Who is a miser? Miser, in Hindi they call conjus. Who is a miser, conjus? I had an experience when I was young of a family staying just two houses away from our house. And I still remember although I was just about five or six years old that time. So this person he had three, two sons and one daughter. otter and when we would go to their home for generally when guests come somebody comes children come you know you give them something to eat so he will sit in a chair in the center and then three children are there and we will be there and he will have that Chali you know Chali Bhutta that corn in his hand he'll remove three pieces and give it to one three pieces and give to another so in other words it was very very I mean there many other reasons to say that he was miser somebody may be thinking that he must be doing out of hospitality or love it was because he didn't wanted to let go what he had in his hand just okay tin dana below and he had a car in those days it was not an antique car but was antique because in spite of he having the means to buy the car he would have a car which actually does not move and once in a while when he would want to move for some reason we all would be called in the neighborhood and you know typically old cars if you remember someone they don't especially in winter season you have to push and then they'll put the gear and suddenly it starts working so we have done that so he was like that so who is a miser miser is a person who has the wealth but he doesn't have the means to buy the car but the wealth is locked up and he eats like a beggar stays like a beggar he doesn't want to spend the wealth he has the means to live decent life but he's because of his miserly nature he doesn't want to spend.

And then we came, we checked, we removed our bag, checked in bag, all that we removed. And suddenly I said my handbag is very light.
