#### attachment, detachment, suffering, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/p-A-VckP77c?t=1410
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "At the point of death, you lose your laptop also, your desktop, your mobile phone, your father, your mother, your relatives, all your degrees. All what you have so painstakingly 30 years, 40 years, 50 years you have worked and accumulated. Tak! Gone."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of losing his laptop, emphasizing the pain of losing something valuable and relating it to the ultimate loss at the time of death, when everything accumulated in life is taken away.

And then we went to that Indigo, the counter where lost baggage is there, immediately. I said, please, I have that laptop. So fortunately, you know, they have walkie-talkies and before the aircraft gets ready for the next flight. So they, the people go in and. They clean up the aircraft and all that. So fortunately, they also had walkie-talkies. So immediately, they know that people leave items like that. So they have some mechanism to sort out these things. So he immediately called on walkie-talkie, the person who is in charge of that aircraft. And he said, this one laptop. And along with me, two, three other also people were also there with similar. So one level of chinta reduced because I am not alone. I am not alone. When you go to a doctor with a disease, he said, allon ko bhi hota hai na? Haan, hota hai. Theek hai. Your pain increases if you know that you are the only one who is suffering. So there were others also. And then he said, he called on walkie-talkie. And then he said, aa mil gaya, ek laptop pada hai. Then he said, aata hai abhi. He will bring it. Then I am anxiously waiting. Kab aata hai ho? It took about ten minutes and fortunately, I got the laptop. It was so painful because I didn't have taken a backup. I had not taken a backup for few months. And lot of things were there. Imagine last two, three months of work which you have done. You know, you worked on excel. You worked on so many documents. You prepared so many things. It's all there. It's almost like losing three, four months of your work. So then I immediately came back and looked for a backup solution. And so it's very painful. Imagine one laptop is taken away from you. It's so painful because you are all what you did three months is going down the drain. One laptop. At the point of death, you lose your laptop also, your desktop, your mobile phone, your father, your mother, your relatives, all your degrees. All what you have so painstakingly 30 years, 40 years, 50 years you have worked and accumulated. Tak! Gone. It's taken away. You don't want it to go but gone. Imagine I could experience a sense of what it is. What's the pain? Three months of work is going down the drain. You can always buy another laptop. It's not the cost of the laptop. So that's the pain. You lose everything. What you have acquired. All the properties. All the things.

#### Vedas, deities, worship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=116
**Verse References:** BG 9.20

**Summary:** The speaker explains the translation of Bhagavad Gita 9.20, which discusses the worship of deities for heavenly pleasures, and then poses the question of why the Vedas recommend the worship of different deities instead of a single God.

So, the translation for the 20th verse is, those who study the Vedas and drink the Soma juice, seeking the heavenly planets, worship me indirectly. They take birth on the planet of Indra, where they enjoy godly delights. So, the understanding we get from this verse, they are higher planetary systems, which have a different nomenclature. Sometimes people address this as heaven, swargaloka, or higher planetary systems. Just like even in this planet earth, we have countries like Somalia, where the standard of living is higher. Likewise, in this universe, there are planets where the standard of happiness, the duration of life is much higher, and they are called higher planetary systems or swargaloka, and they are lower planetary systems, where there is more misery and definitely the standard of living. So, the standard of living is much lower than what we have here in the planetary system. So, now Vedas recommend different kinds of sacrifices, different kinds of worship, different devis and devatas are mentioned in the Vedas. Can anyone help me with an answer? Why the Vedas are recommending worship of different devis and devatas? Indra Yagna Karo, Ad Lakshmi Puja Karo. Or in this festival, Ye Puja Karo. Why it is like that? It could have been much simpler, much easier if it was just one God and everyone was worshipping that same one God and there was utter clarity. There wouldn't be fights in this world, people fighting in the name of religion. Why the Vedas are recommending worshipping different devis and devatas? Anyone?

Different needs. Different needs. Why can not that one supreme God provide all the needs? Why I have to worship Indra for rains? Why I have to worship Saraswati for learning? Why I have to worship Lakshmi for wealth? Why not same supreme Lord I worship and I get everything? He has given different departments of... He could have managed himself. See, in this company, you know, CEO actually is limited. He cannot do all the work. So, he has no option but to delegate. But our definition of supreme Lord is he is all powerful. He can manage everything. But why different devi and devatas are created in the first place, point one. Point two and Vedas which is coming from the Lord himself. Krishna says, Vedaishcha Sarvair Aham Eva Vedya. I am the creator of the Vedas. Vedaantakrit Chaham. And in that Vedas, Krishna's recommendation is do Indra Yagna, do Lakshmi Puja, do Saraswati Puja and other devi and devatas. And thereby, we all have got confused. In Bangalore, the place where we have our temple. In a one kilometre radius, there are nine temples of different devi and devatas. So, for a common man who has to worship someone, it is very confusing. Where do we go? So, any answer? He has deputies. Why he wants deputies? See, the question what I was asking was, in a company, let's say Bill Gate is running Microsoft. He needs deputies. He is a human being. He has limitations. He cannot expand himself. But Lord can do that. So, we will come to that.

#### Vedas, spiritual evolution, humanity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=393
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "First thing is, Vedas is a body of knowledge which has come from the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Vedas are not man-made but come from the Lord, like a manual for creation, and address the needs of a vast spectrum of living entities at different stages of spiritual evolution.

Now, first thing we have to understand. So, we are covering some very fundamental aspects today. First thing is, Vedas is a body of knowledge which has come from the Lord. Tene Brahma Hridaye Adi Kavaye. This Vedas is not a man-made book or a scripture. It is Aparushaya. Aparushaya means it is not man. Parush means man. Aparushaya means it is not man-made. It is coming from the Lord Himself. Just like any creation, any gadget, any product in the market is accompanied by a manual by the same manufacturer which talks about how the product has to be used. Likewise, for this creation, we have the Vedas which was revealed at the dawn of creation. Now, in the Vedas, Vedas are dealing with a huge spectrum of human beings. There are totally how many types of living entities? Different species, plants, birds, aquatics, reptiles. How many of them? 84 lakhs out of which 4 lakhs are human beings. And these divisions is not basis of color, not basis of religion, not basis of caste, not basis of color. All of these are spiritual evolution. So different people are at different stages of their spiritual evolution. Quite possible, we all here are different species. Although we have many things in common. We are human beings. We stay in Mumbai. We stay in Mumbai. Many things in common. There could be different species. So Vedas have to now address the requirement of this huge spectrum. Right on one side of the spectrum is complete aborigines, cannibals, uncultured human beings, more like animals, almost like animals. And the other side of the spectrum is completely exalted, Paramahamsa, pristine, pure. So these are the different personalities.

#### Vedas, spiritual growth, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=548
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So likewise the Vedas, knowing that people are at different levels of their spiritual growth, just like in a school everyone cannot understand trigonometry and algebra."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a school with different classrooms for different levels of students, the speaker explains that the Vedas cater to people at various stages of spiritual growth, offering different paths and recommendations.

Now you cannot have different strokes for different folks. Have you heard of that? Now in the school also you have different classrooms. First standard, second standard, third standard. Because the level of understanding of different subjects is different of different students. According to the age group they divide and they teach a particular level of maths and then graduate to the next level, then to the next level, then to the next level. You don't have a school where students from first standard to tenth standard sit in the same classroom and the same teacher is starting the same subject. No. So likewise the Vedas, knowing that people are at different levels of their spiritual growth, just like in a school everyone cannot understand trigonometry and algebra. There are students who don't even know 2 plus 2 is 4. So for them, the way the maths is taught is different. For a student in the eighth standard, the way the maths is taught, the subject is the same, but the way it is administered and taught is different. So likewise there are people who are not interested in God. The bottom end of the spectrum are people who are completely maybe not interested in God. And yes, the other side of the spectrum are completely God conscious people, pure, self-controlled, free from false ego, free from pride, free from greed, very pure in their character. Now, the Vedas talk about Dharma and by following Dharmic principles, Dharmic principles, we can fulfill our Kama, our desires. We will get Artha. Artha means prosperity, material, whatever our requirements gets fulfilled. And ultimately, Dharma is meant to take us to Moksha.

#### Moksha, Artha, Kama
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=686

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

So these four lakh species are there, each of them at different levels of spiritual evolution. But all in this material world, the ultimate goal of Vedas is to take them to higher realms of understanding. And finally, finally what? Take them out of this material existence, which is called Moksha. In different religions, it's called by different names. And ultimate goal is to go back to the kingdom of God, our Father in heaven. Somebody calls it heaven, somebody calls it Jannat, somebody calls it by different names. So now people are at different levels. So, some are just interested in Artha and fulfilling their Kama. They are not interested in Moksha. They are not interested in God per se. They are just interested in Artha and Kama, fulfilling their desires. So Vedas understanding that there are such people at that level of spiritual evolution, they are just interested in Kama and Artha, they say that, if you have to fulfill this Artha and Kama, worship this. At least the concept of, there are some higher personalities whom I have to approach, whom I have to surrender to, whom I have to worship, is a very very big stepping stone for a person who is completely oblivious of God. At least some fundamental steps he is learning that he is not God. For his requirement, he has to approach some higher power. Who that higher power is, they are not interested. And the rules associated with that higher power, they are not interested. Some do's and don'ts, they are not interested. But yes, are interested in Artha and Kama.

They are, in fact, Shiva Purana, it is recommended that you worship Shiva. Yes, by worshipping Shiva, all their Ashutoshas, Shiva is also called Ashutoshas because he gets easily pleased. He gives benedictions very easily. And that's why people who are more interested only in benedictions, they find it is much better. So to deliver such people, he has taken the responsibility by worshipping him. At least they are taking some steps towards their spiritual growth. Now, ananyas chintayanto maam. Ye jana pariyupasana, without any deviation. Ananyas, anya means others. Ananya, ananyas chintayanto maam. Who is saying this? Krishna. Ananyas chintayanto, those who worship me with undivided focus. Ye jana pariyupasate, who worship me. Tesham nityabhuktanam yogakshemavahamiham. Now, we have those who worship me with devotion, meditate on my transcendental form. To them, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have. Now, we have nine limbs of bhakti, which is also called navavidha bhakti. Which is different from worshipping different devatas. In this navavidha bhakti, the aim, the objective is to please the Supreme Lord, to glorify the Supreme Lord. And it starts with shravanam. Shravanam means hearing about the Lord. Then we have kirtanam, smaranam, padhasevanam, archanam, vandanam, dasyam, sakhyam, atmani vedam. I will not go into details of each. There are nine ways in which we can worship and serve the Supreme Lord.

#### Bhakti, Supreme Lord, Yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=1625
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says, I provide what a person lacks. And preserve what a person has."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that engaging in any form of navavidha bhakti pleases Krishna, who provides what the devotee lacks and preserves what they have, and that chanting Hare Krishna mantra propels one towards the ultimate goal of purifying consciousness and returning to God.

When we start navavidha bhakti, in any one form, could be shravanam, could be kirtanam. Krishna says, I provide what a person lacks. And preserve what a person has. Such activities are all auspicious and full of spiritual potencies. This chanting of Hare Krishna mantra, what we are doing, it is not to fulfill any of our material desires. Although we may start with that, but the main benefit of this is, it is propelling us towards the ultimate goal. To purify our existence, purify our consciousness. And then eventually go back to the kingdom of God. The Lord helps the devotee. If we take one step towards the Lord, the Lord is very very eager to take ten steps towards us. And it says that the Lord's step is much bigger than our step. So here the Lord says, I provide what the devotee lacks and preserve what he has. So now this is the ladder, yoga ladder. Starts with karma kanda. You will not be able to see, but nevertheless I will explain. Karma kanda is where we are more interested in artha and kama. So there are sections in the Vedas which talks about, you do this, you will get this. The centre is us. And then we have sakama karma yoga. I will not get into details now, it is a separate topic by itself. And then we have nishkama karma yoga. Third chapter Krishna talks about karma yoga. Fifth chapter Krishna talks about karma yoga. In sixth chapter Krishna talks about jnana yoga. And then we have understanding of ashtanga yoga. And finally in the ninth chapter Krishna is talking about bhakti yoga. Ananyas cintayanto maam. Which involves not any kama, not any desire for artha. It is mainly to serve the Supreme Lord. Hear about the Lord, chant the glories of the Lord, serve the Lord, worship the Lord. And these activities will free us from this cycle of birth and death.

Because Krishna has further said, aham adir hi deva naam. You are worshipping me only. But it is yajante avidhi purvakam. Vidhi means? Vidhi means process. Avidhi means, which is not the right way of worship. Whatever a man may sacrifice to other gods, O son of Kunti, is really meant for me alone. But it is offered without true understanding. Because all these devatas are my subordinates. But it is avidhi purvakam. Now here we have some, we had covered this presentation long time back. Some slides I have taken, just to recap. We see people worshipping different gods and visiting various places of worship. We have, you know, various devi and devatas being worshipped. So now the question which might come in our mind, why so many gods? Why so many religions? And sometimes people who have interest in spirituality get perplexed with questions like, whom do I follow? Whom do I believe? If they come here, they say Krishna is supreme. If they go to church, they say Jesus is the only way. If they go somewhere else, they say this is not right, that is right. #### God, religion, understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/53Ag6IKI5UQ?t=2003
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All the bona fide religions and scriptures of the world are the word of that one god, which teach the same truth in essence, like, god is one, develop love for god, obey god's laws, sin no more."
**Summary:** The speaker asserts that there is one God, addressed differently in various religions, and that all bona fide religions teach the same essential truths, but differ in details according to time, place, and the capacity of individuals to understand.

How many of us agree with this? Okay, how many of us say, no, there could be multiple gods? There is one god. The definition of god is the supreme person, the cause of all causes, the supreme power, who has created everything, the supreme creator. So there is one god. Then if there is one god, just like, same god is addressed differently in different religions, just like sun, Surya, Bhaskara, Ravi, likewise, Allah, father in heaven, Krishna, the supreme personality of godhead. All the bona fide religions and scriptures of the world are the word of that one god, which teach the same truth in essence, like, god is one, develop love for god, obey god's laws, sin no more. The underlying principles are the same, the details may vary from religion to religion. However, god gives his message directly or through his representatives according to time, place, circumstances and the capacity of individuals to understand the message. Just like in an elementary school we have a teacher teaching maths, 2 plus 2 is 4, 2 minus 1 is 1, 3 minus 2 is 1. If somebody asks the teacher what is 2 minus 5, the teacher will say it is not possible to subtract 5 from 2. The same teacher in high school will say it is minus 3. And the same teacher when he teaches students in the higher levels of college, may be teach some algebra, trigonometry and higher advanced maths. So, teacher instructs according to level of understanding of the student. Likewise, different religions teach the same principles, but according to time, places and circumstances.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### ESCAPE THE CAGE - BEFORE WE AGE | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, entrapment, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So today the session is all about what is this cage in which the Atma is trapped, how it is trapped and how we can escape from this cage, this entrapment."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of escaping the cage of material existence, which traps the soul in a cycle of birth, death, old age, and disease, and outlines the session's focus on understanding this entrapment and how to achieve liberation.

Hare Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Life session. The topic for the day is, escape the cage before you age. Many of you must be wondering, what is the cage we are talking about? As we have been discussing in our previous sessions, Atma, the soul, keep the main thing, the main thing. So the main thing is the Atma, the soul, without which when that Atma leaves the body, we cremate the body or bury the body. Body minus Atma is a dead body. So this Atma is encaged, is entrapped, entrapped in a material body, made of five elements, earth, air, water, fire and ether, and in one sense, trapped in this body. Although the Atma is eternal, never dying, because it is trapped in the body and identified with the body, it experiences death, old age, diseases and rebirth. So the cage what we are talking about is getting trapped in body life after life, punar api janma, punar api marana, much against our wish, in different species of life, as plants, as birds, as aquatics, reptiles, animals, and after many many lifetimes, human beings, which also is not a permanent life form. And this Atma is transmigrating. We all don't want death, but we are forced to experience death, forced to experience old age. Much against our wish, we are forced to experience diseases of various kinds, and nobody is an exception. So today the session is all about what is this cage in which the Atma is trapped, how it is trapped and how we can escape from this cage, this entrapment. So before we get into the details, let's start the session with prayers as usual.

---
#### spiritual practice, age, social pressure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=442
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "People don't say that it is wrong, but they say that this is too early."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the common misconception that spiritual practice is only suitable for old age, after fulfilling worldly responsibilities, and challenges this notion.

So the first principle which we are going to focus on is the right age to start. I am sure many of you might have encountered this once we start practicing Krishna consciousness, when we start wearing a Kanthi Mala or putting a Tilak or start chanting on the beads and especially if a person is young in his twenties, thirties, forties, invariably you will hear comments like this is not the right time. Yes spirituality is important. Yes religion is important, but you will definitely hear this, but this is not the right time. There is time for everything. This is a time when you have to grow in your career, make a lot of money, buy a good house, settle yourself in life, get married, have nice children, take care of their good upbringing. And yes, post retirement, post seventy years, seventy five years, maybe when your grandchildren have got sick, they have got settled comfortably, they've got married and you are relatively free from all so-called family responsibilities, then you can practice spiritual life. This is the common understanding. People don't say that it is wrong, but they say that this is too early.

---
#### spiritual education, childhood, Vedic culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=650
**Verse References:** SB 7.6.1
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Lady, rush, rush, you're already five years late."
**Summary:** Using the example of Socrates and Prahlad Maharaj, the speaker emphasizes the importance of starting spiritual education from a very young age (Kaumaram), highlighting the rarity and value of human life for understanding and escaping material entrapment.

Lady, rush, rush, you're already five years late. Prahlad Maharaj in the Srimad Bhagavatam seventh canto, in his instructions he says, Kaumaram acharedh pragno dharman bhagvatan yah, Kaumaram from the very childhood, acharedh dharman bhagvatan yah, this understanding has to be taught from Kaumara age. Kaumaram is right from the very childhood. This human form of life, he continues saying that this human form of life is very rarely achieved. And other life forms, as I mentioned, there are 84 lakh species, plants, birds, innumerable species of plants, birds, reptiles, aquatics, underwater, 84 lakh species. Although this human form of life is temporary, it is not that eternally we get this human form of life, which is very valuable. Arthadam. It is very, very meaningful. We can achieve something substantial as far as coming out of the cage is concerned. Although this human form of life is temporary, but it is very meaningful because it affords us a wonderful opportunity to understand about who we are, why we are encaged and how to come out of this cage. And when does this learning this understanding starts from the very childhood that's why in vedic times there were gurukul systems where a child including pandavas krishna himself went and learned this science brahmavidya under the guidance of a guru and learn the art of controlling the mind controlling the senses because which is very important for us to understand this great science when our mind is bewildered by boga and aishwarya.

#### mind control, sense control, brahmacharya, Vedic stages of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=798
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the first 25 years was predominantly focused on controlling the mind controlling the senses, learning those principles which was predominantly called brahmacharya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that when our senses are uncontrolled and the mind is rattled with materialistic thoughts, it is not possible to have steady intelligence to understand God. He describes the Vedic system of brahmacharya, vanaprastha, and sannyasa, emphasizing the importance of controlling the mind and senses in the early stages of life to prepare for detachment and focus on spiritual liberation.

When our senses are uncontrolled mind is rattled with so many materialistic thoughts it is not possible steady intelligence whereby we can understand about god think of god remember god will not come about so the first 25 years was predominantly focused on controlling the mind controlling the senses learning those principles which was predominantly called brahmacharya and the next 25 years a person would get into family lives would get so to say exposed to worldly pleasures but in a regulated way more so because of the training which he has undergone in the first and knowing that ultimate goal of life is not to eat drink be merry and enjoy life and settle down comfortably at the age of 50 people would voluntarily start detaching themselves from family life and that stage was called vanaprastha and after 25 years of preparing for that so-called renunciation a person would completely give up all connections with the family and that life was called sannyasa so that with single-mindedness with no distraction whatsoever the person could focus on extricating the soul from this body or escaping the cage.

---
#### human life, purpose of life, eating, sleeping, mating, defending
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=1020
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If advancement is all about making elaborate gorgeous arrangements for eating, sleeping, mating and defending then we are wasting this valuable human form of life."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that if human advancement is limited to making elaborate arrangements for eating, sleeping, mating, and defending, then we are wasting the valuable opportunity of human life, which is meant for understanding our true nature and escaping material entrapment.

In all life forms, maybe in a very crude way maybe in a very unsophisticated way in all life forms we dabble in eating sleeping mating and defending if we also predominantly do these activities in human form of life in a much more refined way much more gorgeous way much more sophisticated way animals may defend using claws we may defend using intercontinental ballistic missiles which have a range of 7500 meters with a precision of plus minus one meter that precision missiles we may defend but the act of defense is the same we may have big big chefs you know nice channels giving us so many recipes so many ways in which we can cook our food make it look so attractive attractive to look at very palatable in taste it's all about eating shila prabhupada was quite blunt when he said and animals may have sex on the street we may have it in an air-conditioned building in a multi-storied apartment in a very cozy bed in an air-conditioned room but the act is the same the pleasure is the same the quality of activity is the same therefore our scriptures say if advancement is all about making elaborate gorgeous arrangements for eating sleeping mating and defending then we are wasting this valuable human form of life.

So this is the first point i wanted to share with all of you the right age to start and what is the right age the right age is koumaram acharit pragna from the very childhood i've shared this example from the very childhood i've shared this example let's say this pen represents our life this represents the day we came in this world the day we were born and let's say this end represents the day we are going to leave this world which is inevitable one was taken birth is sure to die for one who has died is sure to take birth again so I have used this example in many of my talks many of you may be familiar with it if this pen represents our life this is the day we took birth this is the day we're going to leave this world so if I ask somebody and I've done this practically where are you currently placed as far as your life is concerned so people quickly calculate if they are 40 years so you know if I call someone they'll come and point it here no assuming that our life is for hundred years so or 80 years so if I call someone they'll come and point it here assuming that our life is for 100 years so or 80 years average lifespan so 40 years mean somewhere in between and then I ask the audience is that answer correct can we be very confident of it people start laughing some of them have understood where I am heading we start laughing because we cannot be sure we cannot be sure that today if I am 40 years I am halfway through it could be could be the last year of my life it could be the last day of my life.

#### recap, right age, rarely achieved, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=3050
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** summary
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Sarva Dharmaan Paritya Jaya Maam Ekam Sharanam Raja."
**Summary:** The speaker recaps the first two points: the right age to start spiritual practice (Kaumaram Acharedh Pragno) and the rarity of human life. He emphasizes that out of many Jnanis, Karmis, and civilized people, rarely does someone understand the Supreme Absolute Truth, Lord Sri Krishna, and endeavor to serve and surrender to Him.

Let's do a quick recap of what we have so far discussed. The first point we discussed was the right, the right age to start. And we talked about Prahlad Maharaj's instruction which is Kaumaram Acharedh Pragno. From the very childhood we have to begin this process because human life is rarely achieved and we do not know how much time we have at our disposal. It is temporary. It is rarely achieved. So there is a sense of urgency. Before we again fall into the clutches of death. Quickly, quickly, quickly endeavor for perfection. So that's the first point, the right age for, to start. Many people talk about it, this is not the right time, this is too early for you to get into spirituality. So that's the first point. And the second point was rarely achieved. This human form of life. This life is rarely achieved. Many Jnanis are there, many Karmis are there, many civilized people are there, uncivilized people are there. And out of this, there are some Muktas and rarely, rarely somebody will have the understanding of the Supreme Absolute Truth, Lord Sri Krishna and would endeavor to serve that Lord, surrender to that Lord. Sarva Dharmaan Paritya Jaya Maam. And this is the first point. Sarva Dharmaan Paritya Jaya Maam Ekam Sharanam Raja. So that's the second point.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Envious of Lord | BG 16.17 - 16.18 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### demoniac mentality, envy, material desires
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/YcYC73kJBdc?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The root cause of material existence is material desires, lusty desires, ichcha, and dvesha, envy, envious of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the discussion on demoniac mentality, focusing on envy of the Lord as a root cause of material existence, based on Srila Prabhupada's teachings.

We will begin the discussion on chapter 16, text numbers 17 and 18. This verse talks about the demoniac mentality. In this chapter, we have been talking about how the demons think and act. These two particular verses address demoniac tendency, more so from the perspective of being envious of the Lord. Dvesha means envy. We have been discussing in our Gita life classes that the root cause of material existence is material desires, lusty desires, ichcha, and dvesha, envy, envious of the Lord. This is being emphasized in these two verses.

---
#### false prestige, demoniac mentality, scriptural authority
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/YcYC73kJBdc?t=69
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I want to become God, stemming from enviousness of God."
**Summary:** This segment discusses examples of people with demoniac mentalities who perform sacrifices and austerities for personal gain, disregarding scriptural authority and desiring to become God.

We will base this on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada. In this particular discussion, we are going to show some videos I have just collected on God men, present-day God men, some of them. This is the 17th text from the 16th chapter: 'Self-complacent and always impudent, deluded by wealth and false prestige, they sometimes perform sacrifices in name only, without following any rules and regulations.' We have examples of many people who were performing sacrifices, doing austerities, and were also believers in God, worshipping some kind of gods, but they were demons. For example, Ravana, he was a great devotee of Shiva. Another example here is Hiranyakashipu, who created austerities. He did great tapas. He did the tapas. Shakti is there, sacrifices, voluntarily abstaining for a higher cause, all that they did, but the mentality was demoniac. I want to become God, stemming from enviousness of God. Who is God? Hiranyakashipu is telling Prahlad Maharaj, 'I am there, I am the supreme,' and he wanted to be immortal. Next, 'Thinking themselves all in all, not caring and not giving for authority or scripture, the demoniacs sometimes perform so-called religious or sacrificial rites.'

#### scriptural authority, devotion, speculation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/YcYC73kJBdc?t=186
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If one even does devotion to the Lord on his own using his speculation, it creates simply disturbance."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of following scriptural authority (Shruti, Smriti, Puranas) in devotional practices, warning against self-speculation which can create disturbance.

Not caring for authorities of scriptures. There is a verse in our Vedic literature which says, 'Shruti Smriti Puranaadi Pancharatri Ki Vidhi Vinaa.' Shruti means the Vedas, also called Shrutis. Why is it called Shruti? It is coming down through oral reception. It's only 5000 years back it was put into writing. Otherwise, they are called Shrutis. Shruti Smriti Puranaadi Vidhi Vinaa. So they are giving Vidhi. They are giving a process. They are giving a path which we have to tread. Shruti Smriti Puranaadi Pancharatri Ki Vidhi Vinaa Ekantikir Harir Bhakti. If one even does devotion to the Lord on his own using his speculation, Ut Paateva Kalpate. It creates simply disturbance.

#### austerity, deception, spiritual elevation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/YcYC73kJBdc?t=252
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There are so many God men who look God men, talk like God men from their externals... but actually they are not."
**Summary:** This segment uses examples like Jarasandha and Ravana to illustrate how individuals can appear religious but harbor demoniac intentions, and draws an analogy to imitation gold to highlight the deceptive nature of false God men.

Next. So we have examples. For example, of Jarasandha. He was also very austere. He did tapas. He was a believer in Brahminical culture. But he wanted to kill Krishna. He attacked Krishna. So although he was involved in sacrifices, doing rituals, doing worship, believers in Brahminical culture, he is also called a demon. We have another question. Yes. We have an example of Ravana. He was a great devotee of Lord Shiva. Sometimes such people, demons, take up a role of a preacher and mislead people. Actually, in today's world, people are very, very gullible. Gullible means they can easily be cheated. A common man does not know this. I have given this example many times. Prabhupada also gives this example. Like imitation gold and pure gold, they look alike. Yesterday I had gone in Powai market, close to the place where we stay. So, they were selling these bangles, artificial bangles. So then, looks like that lady asked the price was 100 rupees. So then, the lady asked that is existence or not the... within that imitation also it should be real. So then that person said 100 rupais mein heera torna milta hai. So now, actually when I saw that, it was brilliant, it was shining, beautiful bangles, it looked like gold but it was for 100 rupees only. So three hours on site. We went in hmm, it looked like gold, hundred rupees. Obviously it is not pure gold. So now the same example, there are so many God men who look God men, talk like God men from their externals. You feel that they are very spiritually elevated souls but actually they are not.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Escape Cupid's Arrows: Turn to Madan Mohan | Sri Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### lust, attraction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jE9IMGCtwkw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we have experienced real love, real beauty of the Lord, then we will experience that satisfaction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that people are enchanted by Cupid's arrows, which represent lusty desires, but Krishna, or Madan Mohan, is more attractive than Cupid and surrendering to Him can free one from these desires and lead to real love and satisfaction.

World over, people are enchanted by Cupid, arrows of Cupid. Cupid shoots arrows, pierces our heart. We have that picture of a heart being pierced by an arrow, lusty desires. And Cupid is called Madan, and Krishna is called Madan Mohana, who enchants the Cupid, who is much, much more attractive, millions of times more attractive than the Cupid, so-called beauty in this material world. So if we get enchanted by Madan Mohana, if we worship Madan Mohan, if we surrender to Madan Mohan, if we follow the instructions of Krishna, Madan Mohan, which is given in the Bhagavad Gita, then Cupid has no place in our lives, will not be tormented, will not be shot at by the Cupid. If we have experienced real love, real beauty of the Lord, then we will experience that satisfaction. We will experience that higher taste, and the lower taste, false beauty, illusory beauty of this material world will not enchant us.

---

### FACING LIFE'S CHALLENGES I HG Amitasana Das I GITA LIFE Live

#### introduction, Bhagavad Gita, challenges
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=0

**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa welcomes everyone to the Gita Life session and introduces the topic of facing life's challenges through the lens of the Bhagavad Gita.

Hare Krishna. Welcome all of you for today's session. It's been a long time now. I think more than a month ever since we had the regular Gita Live session which we used to have on Sunday evening at 4.30 p.m. I was waiting, maybe once the lockdown gets over, we can resume the sessions. There's always live sessions, sitting across, hearing face to face, always have its own appeal. But now since the lockdown is likely to get extended, let us start and not miss out an opportunity to be in touch with this message. I'm sure many of you have been in touch with the message of Gita by finding time to read Bhagavad Gita and also attend various classes which we have been conducting and our temples have been conducting. We have been sending links to all of you. And I'm sure many of you might be attending the classes regularly. So today we will start the discussion on a very, very pertinent and relevant topic.

---
#### Bhagavad Gita, knowledge, confidentiality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=104
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada says that one can lick the bottle of honey from outside without getting the real taste of honey."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the format of the sessions and emphasizes the importance of approaching the Bhagavad Gita with the right mood and seeking the Lord's blessings to comprehend its essence, using the analogy of licking a honey bottle without opening it.

And the topic for the day is facing life's challenges the Bhagavad Gita way. What we have thought is every week instead of covering Bhagavad Gita, words by words, chapter by chapter, we can take a particular topic. Like, for example, the topic for today is facing life's challenges. So we can take a particular topic. But it will be predominantly from the Bhagavad Gita. I'll be quoting some verses to substantiate what I'm saying. Before we begin today's discussion let's start with prayers it's always important it's always good that we understand these topics the message of Gita in the right mood right frame of mind seeking Lord's blessings Krishna in the Gita says that this knowledge is the most confidential knowledge so if we are not in the right frame of mind right consciousness right mood begging Lord for his mercy so that we can comprehend the subject we can listen to the message but can miss out the essence the essence of the message the gist the real meaning Prabhupada says that one can lick the bottle of honey from outside without getting the real taste of honey one can say yes I'm licking the bottle of honey but he has not opened the lid and actually tasted the honey is licking the bottle of honey but not getting the real taste so quite possible that we are hearing from Bhagwat Gita quite possible that we are reading the Gita but we may miss out the real confidential meaning.

So we'll start with the prayers and immediately after the prayers, we will get into the discussion of the topic what we have planned for the day, which is facing life's challenges, the Bhagavad Gita way. So the topic is facing life's challenges the Bhagavad Gita way. I thought this topic is very appropriate more so because all of us are facing a relatively big challenge, something we had not even thought of, locked up in our houses for many many days, many many weeks and no clarity as to how long this lockdown will be there and when normalcy will return. Let's hope for the best. Let's be optimistic. Let's hope that things will normalize very soon and we can also resume the classes the way we were doing. #### Arjuna, battlefield, dilemma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=623
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And just when the battle is about to begin, Arjuna requests Krishna, Krishna, can you please draw the chariot and place it in between the two armies so that I can have a closer look of my opponents, my enemy with whom we are going to fight."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa recounts the beginning of the Bhagavad Gita, describing Arjuna's dilemma on the battlefield of Kurukshetra as he faces the prospect of fighting his own kinsmen.

So let's see from Bhagavad Gita's perspective. Bhagavad Gita starts, as we all know, the two armies have assembled in the battlefield. In Kurukshetra, on one side it's the Pandavas, the other side is the Kauravas. And just when the battle is about to begin, Arjuna requests Krishna, Krishna, can you please draw the chariot and place it in between the two armies so that I can have a closer look of my opponents, my enemy with whom we are going to fight. So Arjuna, based on the request of Arjuna, Krishna drew the chariot and placed it in between the two armies. And when Arjuna saw the opponents, the enemy against whom he has to fight, it's his own kinsmen, own brothers, own relatives, near and dear ones, Bhishma Pitamah was there, Dronacharya was there, his teacher, spiritual master, Karna, his cousins, his brothers, although they were willing to fight against him, but still they were relatives, they were his own kinsmen. So when Arjuna saw them, he was completely bewildered, and was into a very very confused state of mind. The dilemma he had was, should he really fight this battle? Because if he fights this battle, even if he wins the battle, still he won't be able to enjoy the kingdom because it will be tainted with the blood of his own relatives.

---
#### victory, relatives, stress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=753
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What is the use of having a victory and our near and dear ones are not there to celebrate that moment, to celebrate that victory?"
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa illustrates Arjuna's dilemma by comparing it to the joy of celebrating a victory with loved ones, highlighting the futility of victory tainted by the loss of relatives, and describes Arjuna's resulting stress and confusion.

Generally when we achieve something, we want to enjoy relishing that victory or achievement with our near and dear ones. What is the use of having a victory and our near and dear ones are not there to celebrate that moment, to celebrate that victory? Just like even the World Cup when Indian team wins, they come back and then there are many many functions organized to celebrate the victory. And that really gives the whole victory another wonderful perspective or dimension. So here Arjuna gets into a very big dilemma, in fact a very perplexing situation. Even if he wins, actually it's a loss for him, according to what Arjuna thought. And anyway when he loses the battle, it's anyway it's loss. So just like we have win-win solution, here was a scenario where it was a lose-lose scenario. Even if he wins the battle, he is going to lose. If he loses the battle, anyway he is lost. And seeing his own kinsmen, own relatives, own brothers, elders, his mind starts reeling. He becomes very confused. And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna tells Krishna, Krishna, my mind is reeling. I am unable to stand any longer. My bow, Gandiva, is slipping from my hands. So these are all symptoms of a person who is in a very very stressful situation.

---
#### stress, Arjuna, battle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=882
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna gives up his Gandiva bow and says, Krishna, I will not fight."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa continues to describe Arjuna's symptoms of stress and confusion, culminating in his decision to give up his bow and refuse to fight.

My hairs are standing on end. My mind is reeling. I am unable to think properly. I am not sure what I am supposed to do. My legs are trembling. And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna gives up his Gandiva bow and says, Krishna, I will not fight anymore.

---
#### Krishna, Bhagavad Gita, solution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=990
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And after hearing the message of Gita, the instructions of Lord Sri Krishna, we know what happened. All the doubts, all the confusions, all the dilemmas, all the misgivings, all the fear, all the anxiety which Arjuna had, everything got wiped out."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa contrasts Arjuna's overwhelming challenge with the relatively smaller challenges people face today, and poses the question of how Krishna responded to Arjuna's confusion, revealing that Krishna simply smiled and spoke the Bhagavad Gita, which resolved all of Arjuna's doubts and fears.

Now, a question to all of you. After hearing these statements of Arjuna, after seeing that he is completely confused, what did Krishna do? Did he take him through a crash course on stress management? He simply smiled. And macro level, although he spoke many things without getting into details as to what he spoke right now, macro level, he spoke the Bhagavad Gita. And after hearing the message of Gita, the instructions of Lord Sri Krishna, we know what happened. All the doubts, all the confusions, all the dilemmas, all the misgivings, all the fear, all the anxiety which Arjuna had, everything got wiped out. Arjuna got up, took his bow Gandiva, fought that battle very enthusiastically and won the battle. So, in other words, the message of Gita has the potency, has the power, irrespective of what situation we may face.
